topics.title: Bottom Supported Fixed Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bottom Supported Fixed Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Bottom Supported Fixed Structures** are stationary offshore engineering installations permanently anchored to the seabed through either pile-driven foundations or by virtue of their own self-weight (gravity). They resist environmental loads (waves, wind, current) through their structural stiffness and base fixity rather than buoyancy. These structures are typically deployed in water depths ranging from **10 m** to approximately **500 m**, with conventional steel jackets being the most prevalent configuration worldwide.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCNST602 - Module 1):**
> Bottom Supported Fixed Structures encompass the classification, components, structural configuration, design loads, and preliminary analysis philosophy of fixed offshore platforms — including steel template (jacket) platforms, gravity-based structures (GBS), and monotowers.

### 1.2 Classification of Bottom-Supported Fixed Structures

| Type | Description | Typical Water Depth |
|------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Steel Template (Jacket) Platform** | Lattice steel framework pinned to seabed by driven piles | 30 – 450 m |
| **Gravity Based Structure (GBS)** | Massive concrete/steel caisson resting on seabed by self-weight | 30 – 300 m |
| **Compliant Tower** | Slender, flexible tower with minimum fixity | 300 – 900 m |
| **Monotower / Single-Column** | Single tubular member, bottom-pinned | 50 – 300 m |
| **Jack-up Rig (Operating Mode)** | Mobile unit with legs resting on seabed | Up to 150 m |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Student Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine a four-legged wooden stool placed at the bottom of a swimming pool. You push the stool sideways — it resists your push because its four legs grip the pool floor. Now replace the wooden stool with a giant **steel space-frame tower** fixed to the ocean floor by long **steel nails (piles)** drilled 80–100 m into the seabed. That is exactly what a *jacket platform* is — the *“stool of the oil field”* in the ocean. **Gravity platforms**, on the other hand, behave like a **massive concrete block** that simply sits on the seabed — its enormous weight (sometimes over **200,000 tonnes**) keeps it in place, just like a heavy dumbbell won't budge when you try to slide it across a table.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of seawater:** ρ = **1025 kg/m³** (standard offshore value)
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** g = **9.81 m/s²**
- **Dynamic pressure (deep water):** q = ½ρV² with V as current/wind speed
- **Young's Modulus (structural steel):** E ≈ **200 GPa**
- **Marine concrete density (GBS):** ρ_c ≈ **2500 kg/m³**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Fixed Jacket Platform

A fixed steel jacket platform is composed of the following functional sub-systems:

- **Topsides (Deck Module):** Drilling rig, production manifold, living quarters, helideck.
- **Jacket Substructure:** Tubular steel space-frame, typically 4–8 legs with horizontal and diagonal bracing.
- **Pile Foundation:** Hollow steel pipes (0.9 – 2.5 m diameter) driven through the jacket legs into the seabed.
- **Conductors:** Large-diameter pipes (typically 0.5 m dia.) that guide the wellbore from seabed to deck.
- **Risers & J-Tubes:** Pipelines that bring oil/gas from the seabed to the platform.
- **Boat Landing & Barge Bumpers:** Allow supply vessels to dock safely.

### 2.2 Environmental Loads on Fixed Structures

Fixed offshore structures must be designed to withstand a combination of the following time-varying and static loads:

- **Hydrostatic Pressure:** p = ρ g h (function of water depth only)
- **Wave Loading:** Cyclic oscillatory forces from ocean waves
- **Current Loading:** Steady drag force from ocean currents
- **Wind Loading:** Steady and gust-induced drag on topsides
- **Earthquake Loading:** Transient inertial loads (especially in seismic zones)
- **Marine Growth & Biofouling:** Adds mass and increases drag diameter
- **Vessel Impact & Accidental Loads:** From supply boats, ship collisions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why It Matters in Engineering:**
> The *“design wave”* is the most critical load case. In the Gulf of Mexico and North Sea, a 100-year return-period storm wave can have a height (H) of **15 – 30 m** combined with currents of **1.5 – 2.0 m/s**. A 100 m tall jacket in such conditions can experience base shear exceeding **50,000 kN** and overturning moments of **1,000,000 kN·m**.

### 2.3 Hydrodynamic Force Theory — Morison Equation

The **Morison Equation** is the cornerstone of fixed offshore structure design. It calculates the wave force on a slender vertical cylinder (where D/λ < 0.2):

The total inline force per unit length on a cylindrical member is the **sum of drag and inertia components**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning of Each Term:**
> - **Drag Force (F_D):** Like wind pushing on a flagpole — proportional to the *square* of water velocity.
> - **Inertia Force (F_I):** Like the force needed to *accelerate* a column of water around the cylinder — proportional to *acceleration*.
> - The "**" in the drag term uses the *absolute value* of velocity so that the drag force is always in the direction of motion (this is critical for oscillatory waves where u changes sign).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Application |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------------|
| 1 | Morison Force per unit length | $f = f_D + f_I$ | Wave force on slender members |
| 2 | Drag Force (per unit length) | $f_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D D \cdot u \vert u \vert$ | Velocity-squared contribution |
| 3 | Inertia Force (per unit length) | $f_I = \rho C_M A \cdot \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t}$ | Acceleration contribution |
| 4 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $p = \rho g h$ | At any submerged depth |
| 5 | Drag Coefficient (typical) | $C_D = 0.6$ to $1.2$ | Smooth circular cylinder |
| 6 | Inertia Coefficient (typical) | $C_M = 1.5$ to $2.0$ | Smooth circular cylinder |
| 7 | Reynolds Number | $R_e = \dfrac{u D}{\nu}$ | Flow regime check |
| 8 | Keulegan-Carpenter Number | $K_C = \dfrac{u_m T}{D}$ | Inertia vs drag dominance |
| 9 | Airy Wave Horizontal Velocity | $u = \dfrac{H g T}{4 \pi \lambda} \cdot e^{-kz} \cos(kx - \omega t)$ | Linear wave kinematics |
| 10 | Airy Wave Horizontal Acceleration | $\dot{u} = \dfrac{2 H g^2 k}{\omega \cdot \cosh(kd)} \cdot e^{-kz} \sin(kx - \omega t)$ | Linear wave kinematics |
| 11 | Wave Number | $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ | Wave spatial frequency |
| 12 | Wave Angular Frequency | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ | Wave temporal frequency |
| 13 | Dispersion Relation (Airy) | $\omega^2 = g k \tanh(k d)$ | Deep water: $\lambda = g T^2 / (2\pi)$ |
| 14 | Base Shear (Total horizontal) | $V = \sum f_i \cdot L_i$ | Integral over all members |
| 15 | Overturning Moment | $M_{OT} = \sum f_i \cdot L_i \cdot z_i$ | Moment about seabed |
| 16 | Pile Axial Capacity | $Q_{ult} = Q_s + Q_p$ | Skin friction + end bearing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Coefficients:**
> In API-RP-2A, **C_D = 0.6 to 1.2** and **C_M = 1.5 to 2.0** are conservative recommended values for smooth circular cylinders. The "**2.0"** for C_M comes from potential flow theory (with added mass = displaced water mass, so C_M = 1 + 1 = 2). For roughened or marine-growth-covered members, **C_D** can exceed **1.0**.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility & Design Workflow

The **Morison-based design philosophy** is used for:

- Computing **in-place (operating) stresses** in jacket members.
- Estimating **base shear and overturning moment** for foundation design.
- Sizing **deck legs, bracing, and conductor framing**.
- Preparing **fatigue analysis** input spectra.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Airy Linear Wave Kinematics

Starting from the velocity potential φ for a small-amplitude (Airy) wave propagating in the +x direction over a depth d:

$$
\phi = \frac{g H}{2 \omega} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\cosh kd} \cdot \sin(kx - \omega t)
$$

The horizontal water particle velocity is obtained by taking the partial derivative of φ with respect to x:

$$
u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{g H k}{2 \omega} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\cosh kd} \cdot \cos(kx - \omega t)
$$

The horizontal water particle acceleration is the time derivative of u:

$$
\dot{u} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \frac{g H k \omega}{2 \omega} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\cosh kd} \cdot \sin(kx - \omega t)
$$

Simplifying (the ω's cancel) gives the standard textbook form:

$$
\dot{u} = \frac{g H k}{2} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\cosh kd} \cdot \sin(kx - \omega t)
$$

> **Explanation of Logic:**
> - We start with the *velocity potential* φ because small-amplitude waves satisfy Laplace's equation (∇²φ = 0).
> - The hyperbolic function *cosh k(z+d) / cosh kd* accounts for **how the wave motion decays with depth** — at the seabed (z = −d), the vertical velocity is zero (no-flow boundary condition).
> - Differentiating in *x* gives horizontal velocity; differentiating in *t* gives horizontal acceleration.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Morison Force on a Jacket Brace

**Problem Statement (Model KTU Numerical):**
A vertical cylindrical brace of a jacket platform has:
- Diameter: D = 0.8 m
- Water depth at brace level: z = −30 m (30 m below SWL)
- Total water depth: d = 80 m
- Wave height: H = 6 m
- Wave period: T = 8 s
- Drag coefficient: C_D = 0.7
- Inertia coefficient: C_M = 1.8
- Seawater density: ρ = 1025 kg/m³

Find the **maximum inline force per unit length** on the brace, using Airy wave theory.

---

**Step 1 — Compute Wavelength λ Using Dispersion Relation (Deep Water Approximation)**

For d/λ > 0.5, deep-water form applies:

$$
\lambda_0 = \frac{g T^2}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 8^2}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 64}{6.2832}
$$

$$
\lambda_0 = \frac{627.84}{6.2832} \approx 99.93 \text{ m}
$$

Check: d / λ = 80 / 99.93 ≈ 0.80, which is > 0.5, confirming deep-water assumption is valid. (For higher accuracy, iterate the full dispersion equation; the difference is < 2%.)

---

**Step 2 — Compute Wave Number k**

$$
k = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \frac{2\pi}{99.93} = 0.0629 \text{ rad/m}
$$

---

**Step 3 — Compute Wave Angular Frequency ω**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \frac{2\pi}{8} = 0.7854 \text{ rad/s}
$$

---

**Step 4 — Compute Maximum Horizontal Velocity (at z = −30 m, surface = 0)**

The maximum of the cosine term in the velocity expression is 1. Therefore:

$$
u_{max} = \frac{g H k}{2 \omega} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\cosh kd}
$$

Compute the hyperbolic arguments:

$$
k(z + d) = 0.0629 \times (-30 + 80) = 0.0629 \times 50 = 3.145
$$

$$
k d = 0.0629 \times 80 = 5.032
$$

Evaluate the hyperbolic cosines:

$$
\cosh(3.145) \approx 11.56
$$

$$
\cosh(5.032) \approx 76.97
$$

Compute the velocity amplitude coefficient:

$$
\frac{gHk}{2\omega} = \frac{9.81 \times 6 \times 0.0629}{2 \times 0.7854} = \frac{3.703}{1.5708} = 2.357 \text{ m/s}
$$

Now apply the depth attenuation:

$$
u_{max} = 2.357 \times \frac{11.56}{76.97} = 2.357 \times 0.1502 = 0.354 \text{ m/s}
$$

---

**Step 5 — Compute Maximum Horizontal Acceleration**

The maximum of the sine term is 1:

$$
\dot{u}_{max} = \frac{g H k}{2} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\cosh kd}
$$

$$
\dot{u}_{max} = \frac{9.81 \times 6 \times 0.0629}{2} \times 0.1502
$$

$$
\dot{u}_{max} = 1.851 \times 0.1502 = 0.278 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

---

**Step 6 — Compute Maximum Drag Force per Unit Length**

$$
f_{D,max} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D D \, u_{max} \vert u_{max} \vert
$$

$$
f_{D,max} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.7 \times 0.8 \times (0.354)^2
$$

$$
f_{D,max} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.7 \times 0.8 \times 0.1253
$$

$$
f_{D,max} \approx 35.96 \text{ N/m} \approx 0.036 \text{ kN/m}
$$

---

**Step 7 — Compute Maximum Inertia Force per Unit Length**

Projected area per unit length: A = π D² / 4

$$
A = \frac{\pi \times 0.8^2}{4} = 0.5027 \text{ m}^2/\text{m}
$$

$$
f_{I,max} = \rho C_M A \, \dot{u}_{max}
$$

$$
f_{I,max} = 1025 \times 1.8 \times 0.5027 \times 0.278
$$

$$
f_{I,max} \approx 257.7 \text{ N/m} \approx 0.258 \text{ kN/m}
$$

---

**Step 8 — Compute Total Maximum Force per Unit Length**

> **Important Engineering Note:**
> The drag force and inertia force **do not peak at the same instant** in oscillatory flow. Drag peaks when velocity is maximum (cos θ = 1), while inertia peaks when acceleration is maximum (sin θ = 1, i.e., 90° phase shift). For conservative design, **API-RP-2A** allows two approaches:
> 1. **Peak-by-peak method** (over-conservative): Add maxima algebraically.
> 2. **Phase-resolved method** (realistic): F_max = √(F_D,max² + F_I,max²)

Using the **phase-resolved method** (industry standard for KTU-style problems):

$$
f_{max} = \sqrt{f_{D,max}^2 + f_{I,max}^2} = \sqrt{(0.036)^2 + (0.258)^2}
$$

$$
f_{max} = \sqrt{0.00130 + 0.0666} = \sqrt{0.0679} \approx 0.260 \text{ kN/m}
$$

---

### 3.3 Verification & Interpretation Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Units | Physical Meaning |
|----------|--------|-------|-------|------------------|
| Wavelength | λ | 99.93 | m | Distance between wave crests |
| Max velocity | u_max | 0.354 | m/s | Peak orbital speed at brace level |
| Max acceleration | u̇_max | 0.278 | m/s² | Peak acceleration of water particles |
| Max drag force | f_D,max | 0.036 | kN/m | Velocity-squared (steady) component |
| Max inertia force | f_I,max | 0.258 | kN/m | Acceleration (oscillatory) component |
| **Total force (RMS)** | **f_max** | **0.260** | **kN/m** | **Governing design value** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:**
> The inertia force dominates (≈ 88% of total). This is typical when the **Keulegan-Carpenter number K_C = u_m T / D = (0.354 × 8) / 0.8 ≈ 3.5** is small (K_C < 12). For such low K_C, the flow does not have enough time to fully separate around the cylinder, so drag effects are minimal. Engineers call this the **"inertia-dominated regime."**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Fixed Jacket Platform

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Topsides Module]
    B[Drilling Module]
    C[Production Manifold]
    D[Living Quarters]
    E[Helideck]
    F[Deck Legs]
    G[Jacket Substructure]
    H[Bracing System]
    I[Launch Trusses]
    J[Pile Foundation]
    K[Conductor Pipes]
    L[Risers and J-Tubes]
    M[Boat Landing]
    N[Seabed Mudline]

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    A --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    G --> I
    G --> J
    J --> N
    G --> K
    G --> L
    G --> M

    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#BDB2FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#8D99AE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#90DBF4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Orange node (Top):** Topsides — supports all operational modules above sea level.
> - **Blue node (Middle):** Jacket — the load-bearing 3D truss structure.
> - **Purple node (Lower):** Piles — the deepest anchoring elements.
> - **Grey node (Bottom):** Mudline — the natural seabed.

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow: Design Workflow for a Fixed Platform

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Site & Metocean Data] --> S2[Step 2: Bathymetry and Soil Investigation]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Concept Selection]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Determination of Design Wave]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Hydrodynamic Analysis using Morison Equation]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Structural Analysis and Member Sizing]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Foundation Design and Pile Capacity Check]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Inplace Strength and Fatigue Verification]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Fabrication Transport and Installation Plan]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Final Certification and Commissioning]

    style S1 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S5 fill:#B5EAD7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S7 fill:#C7CEEA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S10 fill:#FFDAC1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Force Visualization: Load Components Acting on a Jacket

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WIND[Wind Load on Topsides] --> SUM[Total Horizontal Force Vector]
    WAVE[Wave Load on Jacket] --> SUM
    CURRENT[Current Load on Members] --> SUM
    EQ[Seismic Inertia Load] --> SUM
    SUM --> SHEAR[Base Shear V]
    SUM --> MOM[Overturning Moment M_OT]
    SHEAR --> PILE[Pile Reaction Forces]
    MOM --> PILE

    style WIND fill:#FFB7B7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style WAVE fill:#A0D8B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style CURRENT fill:#A2C2E8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style EQ fill:#D5A6BD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style PILE fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> All environmental loads are vector-summed at the mudline to compute **base shear (V)** and **overturning moment (M_OT)**. These are then resisted by the **pile foundation**, which transfers them to the soil through skin friction and end bearing.

### 4.4 Schematic (Mermaid Block) of a Gravity Based Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T[Topsides Deck] --> S1[Concrete Shaft]
    S1 --> S2[Base Caisson with Storage Cells]
    S2 --> S3[Skirt and Foundation Footing]
    S3 --> SB[Prepared Seabed Gravel Bed]
    SB --> SOIL[Marine Clay or Sand Strata]

    style T fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S1 fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S2 fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style SB fill:#F4A460,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style SOIL fill:#8B4513,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> A **GBS (Gravity Based Structure)** transfers environmental loads to the seabed purely through its massive self-weight (often > 200,000 tonnes). No piles are driven — skirts and a prepared gravel bed ensure sliding resistance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Bottom Supported Fixed Structure" and list any four examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *Bottom Supported Fixed Structure* is an offshore installation that is permanently anchored to the seabed and transfers all environmental loads to the foundation through direct contact, without relying on buoyancy or mooring lines for station-keeping.
**Four examples:**
1. Steel template (jacket) platform
2. Gravity based structure (GBS)
3. Compliant tower
4. Monotower (single-column fixed platform)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Morison equation for the inline wave force on a slender vertical cylinder and explain the physical meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
f = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D D \cdot u \vert u \vert + \rho C_M A \cdot \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t}
$$

- **First term (Drag component, F_D):** Represents the resistance due to flow separation and vortex shedding. Proportional to the *square* of velocity; depends on the drag coefficient C_D and projected width D.
- **Second term (Inertia component, F_I):** Represents the force required to accelerate the water mass around the cylinder. Proportional to *acceleration* ∂u/∂t; depends on the inertia coefficient C_M and projected area A.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:**
> Students often write u² (without absolute value) in the drag term. Because velocity oscillates in waves (positive and negative), the *absolute value* ensures drag is always *along the direction of motion* — losing this detail can cost a full mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 1 × 14 Marks = 14 Marks)

---

**Q3A.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the components of a typical steel jacket platform used in offshore oil and gas production. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vertical cylindrical member of a jacket platform is located at a depth of 25 m below the still water level in a sea of 70 m depth. The wave parameters are: H = 5 m, T = 9 s. The member diameter is 1.0 m, and coefficients are C_D = 0.7, C_M = 2.0. Calculate the maximum drag and inertia forces per unit length using Airy wave theory. Take ρ = 1025 kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Components of a Steel Jacket Platform (7 marks):**

1. **Topsides / Deck Module** — Contains drilling rig, production equipment, living quarters, helideck, and the power generation unit. (1 mark)
2. **Deck Legs / Columns** — Tubular steel members (typically 4–8) that transfer deck loads to the jacket. (1 mark)
3. **Jacket Substructure** — A 3- or 4-legged tubular space-frame (lattice structure) extending from the seabed to above the waterline. It comprises legs, horizontal bracing, and diagonal (X or K) bracing. (2 marks)
4. **Pile Foundation** — Long hollow steel cylinders (0.9 – 2.5 m diameter) driven through the jacket legs 60–120 m into the seabed, providing the platform's resistance to overturning and lateral loads. (1 mark)
5. **Conductors and Risers** — Large-diameter pipes routing oil/gas from the wellhead at seabed to the production deck; J-tubes provide installation paths. (1 mark)
6. **Boat Landings and Barge Bumpers** — Allow safe mooring of supply vessels. (1 mark)

> *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for a visual block schematic.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** H = 5 m, T = 9 s, d = 70 m, z = −25 m, D = 1.0 m, C_D = 0.7, C_M = 2.0, ρ = 1025 kg/m³

**Step 1 — Wavelength (deep water approximation):**

$$
\lambda_0 = \frac{g T^2}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 81}{2\pi} = \frac{794.61}{6.2832} \approx 126.5 \text{ m}
$$

Check d/λ = 70/126.5 = 0.55 > 0.5 → deep water assumption valid. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Wave number and angular frequency:**

$$
k = \frac{2\pi}{126.5} = 0.0497 \text{ rad/m}, \quad \omega = \frac{2\pi}{9} = 0.6981 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**(0.5 mark)**

**Step 3 — Maximum velocity at z = −25 m:**

Velocity amplitude coefficient:

$$
\frac{gHk}{2\omega} = \frac{9.81 \times 5 \times 0.0497}{2 \times 0.6981} = \frac{2.438}{1.3963} = 1.746 \text{ m/s}
$$

Depth attenuation factor:

$$
\frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\cosh kd} = \frac{\cosh(0.0497 \times 45)}{\cosh(0.0497 \times 70)} = \frac{\cosh(2.237)}{\cosh(3.479)}
$$

Using values: cosh(2.237) ≈ 4.732, cosh(3.479) ≈ 16.27

$$
\text{Attenuation} = \frac{4.732}{16.27} = 0.2908
$$

$$
u_{max} = 1.746 \times 0.2908 = 0.508 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Maximum acceleration at z = −25 m:**

$$
\dot{u}_{max} = \frac{gHk}{2} \times 0.2908 = \frac{9.81 \times 5 \times 0.0497}{2} \times 0.2908
$$

$$
\dot{u}_{max} = 1.219 \times 0.2908 = 0.354 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**Step 5 — Maximum drag force per unit length:**

$$
f_{D,max} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.7 \times 1.0 \times (0.508)^2
$$

$$
f_{D,max} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.7 \times 0.258 = 92.6 \text{ N/m} \approx 0.093 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Maximum inertia force per unit length:**

Projected area A = π × 1.0² / 4 = 0.7854 m²

$$
f_{I,max} = 1025 \times 2.0 \times 0.7854 \times 0.354 = 569.9 \text{ N/m} \approx 0.570 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**(1 mark)**

---

**Final Answer:**
- **Maximum drag force per unit length ≈ 0.093 kN/m**
- **Maximum inertia force per unit length ≈ 0.570 kN/m**
- The inertia term dominates — this is expected as K_C is small.

---

**Q3B (Alternative Choice).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare the structural action and typical applications of **Gravity Based Structures (GBS)** and **Steel Jacket Platforms**. Discuss at least six points of distinction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal submerged cylinder of diameter 0.6 m is acted upon by a steady current of 1.5 m/s. Take C_D = 1.2 and ρ = 1025 kg/m³. Calculate the drag force per unit length and the total drag on a 25 m long member. Comment on the validity of the Morison equation for this case. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison of GBS vs. Steel Jacket Platform (7 marks):**

| S.No. | Aspect | Gravity Based Structure (GBS) | Steel Jacket Platform |
|-------|--------|------------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Material** | Reinforced concrete (sometimes steel caisson with concrete fill) | Structural steel tubes |
| 2 | **Load Transfer Mechanism** | Self-weight (gravity) and base friction | Pile-driven tension/compression piles |
| 3 | **Water Depth** | Shallow to intermediate (up to 300 m) | Wide range (10 – 500+ m) |
| 4 | **Weight** | Very heavy (100,000 – 500,000 tonnes) | Lighter (5,000 – 50,000 tonnes) |
| 5 | **Seabed Preparation** | Dredging and gravel bedding required | Minimal seabed preparation |
| 6 | **Construction** | Built in dry dock or graving dock near shore | Fabricated onshore and towed horizontally |
| 7 | **Installation** | Floated to site, then ballasted down | Launched and upended, piles driven |
| 8 | **Typical Use** | North Sea, offshore storage (e.g., Troll, Gullfaks) | Global use (Gulf of Mexico, Arabian Sea, etc.) |

**(1 mark per valid point of distinction; full 7 marks for 6+ clear points.)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** D = 0.6 m, V = 1.5 m/s, C_D = 1.2, ρ = 1025 kg/m³, L = 25 m

**Step 1 — Drag force per unit length:**

$$
f_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D D V^2
$$

$$
f_D = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.2 \times 0.6 \times (1.5)^2
$$

$$
f_D = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.2 \times 0.6 \times 2.25
$$

$$
f_D = 830.3 \text{ N/m} \approx 0.830 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Total drag on 25 m length:**

$$
F_D = f_D \times L = 830.3 \times 25 = 20{,}756 \text{ N} \approx 20.76 \text{ kN}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Validity of Morison Equation (3 marks):**

The Morison equation is valid when:
1. The member is *slender* — D/λ < 0.2 (diffraction effects are negligible).
2. Flow is *steady* (for current alone) or *oscillatory but weakly so* (small K_C).
3. The drag coefficient has been calibrated for the appropriate Reynolds and K_C range.

**Comment:**
For a *steady current* on a slender horizontal cylinder, the Morison equation reduces to the standard steady drag formula — and the result is **fully valid**. The Morison equation is *most appropriate* for slender members where wave diffraction is not significant; for large-diameter cylinders (D > λ/5), the **MacCamy-Fuchs diffraction theory** must be used instead.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks in KTU 2024 Exams (Module 1 — Fixed Structures):**
> 1. **Forgetting the absolute value in the drag term** — losing 1 full mark in numericals.
> 2. **Confusing deep-water and shallow-water wavelength formulas** — if d/λ < 0.5, you *must* use the full dispersion relation iteratively, not the simple deep-water form.
> 3. **Mixing up "Morison" and "MacCamy-Fuchs"** — Morison is for *slender* cylinders (D/λ < 0.2); MacCamy-Fuchs is for *large* caissons (e.g., GBS shafts).
> 4. **Adding drag and inertia maxima directly** — they occur 90° out of phase; use **F_max = √(F_D² + F_I²)** unless explicitly told to use peak-by-peak.
> 5. **Skipping unit conversion** — keep SI units throughout (N, m, kg, s); do not mix kN with N/m.
> 6. **No diagram in descriptive answers** — KTU 2024 scheme mandates a *neat labeled sketch* for full marks in component-based questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- ✅ **Bottom Supported Fixed Structures** are seabed-anchored; not buoyant, not moored.
- ✅ Two main families: **Jacket platforms (pile-anchored)** and **Gravity Based Structures (weight-anchored)**.
- ✅ Jacket platforms dominate globally; typical **water depth 30 – 450 m**.
- ✅ GBS relies on **self-weight + base friction + skirt penetration**; needs **prepared seabed**.
- ✅ Key components: **Topsides, Jacket, Piles, Conductors, Risers, J-Tubes, Boat Landings**.
- ✅ **Morison Equation** is the design backbone for hydrodynamic loading on slender members.
- ✅ Drag force: $f_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D D \cdot u \vert u \vert$ — note the **absolute value**.
- ✅ Inertia force: $f_I = \rho C_M A \cdot \dot{u}$ — uses **projected area** A = πD²/4.
- ✅ Typical coefficients: **C_D ≈ 0.6 – 1.2**; **C_M ≈ 1.5 – 2.0**.
- ✅ **Airy wave theory** valid for H/λ < 1/7 (small amplitude); **Stokes II** for H/λ up to 1/10; **Cnoidal** for shallow water.
- ✅ Deep water: $\lambda = gT^2 / (2\pi)$; **transition** if 0.05 < d/λ < 0.5; **shallow** if d/λ < 0.05.
- ✅ **Keulegan-Carpenter number K_C = u_m T / D** decides whether flow is **inertia-dominated (K_C < 12)** or **drag-dominated (K_C > 12)**.
- ✅ Drag and inertia forces peak **90° out of phase**; combine using **RMS (root-sum-square)** for realistic design.
- ✅ **Reynolds number** $R_e = uD/\nu$ governs the choice of C_D (sub-critical, critical, super-critical).
- ✅ **Base shear and overturning moment** are the primary design demands on the **pile foundation**.
- ✅ Slender member threshold for Morison validity: **D/λ < 0.2**; beyond this, use **MacCamy-Fuchs diffraction theory**.
- ✅ Marine growth increases effective diameter and roughness → typically adds **C_D** by 20–40%.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compliant Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Compliant Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Compliant Offshore Structure** is a deep-water production/drilling platform that is deliberately designed to **deflect, sway, or oscillate** within a controlled range under the action of environmental loads (waves, wind, current) instead of rigidly resisting them. The structure is allowed to move because its natural period of oscillation is designed to be **much larger than the dominant wave period**, thereby avoiding resonant dynamic amplification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from syllabus spirit):**
> *A compliant structure is one whose foundation flexibility (or mooring system, or buoyancy distribution) is intentionally designed to permit significant lateral/rotational motion at the deck level, so that the structure's first natural period lies outside the wave-energy spectrum band (typically > 25 s), thereby reducing wave-induced dynamic forces.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Reed vs. The Steel Pillar"

Imagine two ways to plant a flag in fast-flowing river water:

1. **The Steel Pillar (Fixed Platform):** Drive a rigid steel column deep into the riverbed. The current pushes hard against it. The structure must be enormously strong to resist bending moments. It is **stiff and brittle**.
2. **The Reeds (Compliant Structure):** Plant flexible reeds. They **bend with the current** and spring back. Because they move *with* the flow rather than fighting it, the drag force is dramatically reduced. The reeds are **flexible and resilient**.

> Compliant structures work on the same principle. By **moving with the waves** (period shift), wave forces drop by an order of magnitude compared to a rigid structure of the same size. This makes them economical in **water depths > 300 m**, where fixed jackets become uneconomically heavy.

## 1.3 Why "Compliant"?

The word *compliant* comes from mechanics — meaning the structure *complies* (yields in a controlled way) with the applied load. Key characteristics:

- **Period-shift design:** Natural period $T_n >$ wave period $T_w$ (usually $T_n \ge 25$ s vs. $T_w \approx 6\text{–}20$ s).
- **Restoring force system:** Provided by buoyancy, tendons, mooring lines, or a combination — *not* by the soil-foundation.
- **Operational limit:** Maximum horizontal offset of the deck is typically constrained to **5–10 % of water depth**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric to Memorize:**
> Water depth classification:
> - Shallow water: < 300 m → Fixed platforms
> - Deep water: 300 m – 1500 m → Compliant structures
> - Ultra-deep water: > 1500 m → Floating production systems

## 1.4 Physical Constants Used Throughout

| Symbol | Quantity | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** |
| $\rho_w$ | Density of sea water | **1025 kg/m³** |
| $\rho_{air}$ | Density of air | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| $\gamma_w$ | Specific weight of sea water | **10.05 kN/m³** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Compliant Structures

Compliant structures are classified based on the **mechanism that provides the restoring force**. KTU examiners frequently ask for a classification chart.

### A. Taut-Leg Systems (Vertical Restoring)
- **Tension Leg Platform (TLP)** — vertical tethers in tension.
- **SPAR Buoy (Taut-Leg variant)** — can be moored by vertical tethers in some configurations.

### B. Buoyancy-Controlled Systems
- **SPAR Platform** — deep-draft vertical cylinder; restoring force = net buoyancy vs. weight.
- **Semi-Submersible** — pontoon + column geometry; restoring = excess buoyancy.
- **Articulated Platform (ALP / STP - Sevan/SeaStar)** — universal joint at base, buoyancy chamber above.

### C. Mooring-Restored Systems
- **Catenary Anchor Leg Mooring (CALM)** — buoy + catenary chains.
- **Spread-Moored FPSO** — turret mooring with chains/wires.
- **D turret / Internal turret FPSO** — weathervaning tankers.

### D. Hybrid / Tower Systems
- **Compliant (Guyed) Tower** — soft foundation, often pinned at base; guy lines provide lateral stiffness.
- **Guyed Tower** — pretensioned cable guys restore lateral position.

## 2.2 Comparison: Fixed vs. Compliant Structures

| Property | Fixed Platform (Jacket) | Compliant Structure |
|---|---|---|
| Foundation | Piles driven into seabed | Pinned, moored, or tethered |
| Natural period | 2 – 6 s | 25 – 200 s |
| Dominant wave period | 6 – 20 s | 6 – 20 s |
| Resonance risk | Possible | Negligible (period shift) |
| Water depth range | 0 – 300 m (max ~500 m) | 300 m – 3000 m+ |
| Restoring force | Pile-soil stiffness | Buoyancy / tendons / mooring |
| Typical hydrodynamic model | Morison + diffraction | Diffraction / RAO based |
| Deck motion | Negligible | Significant (must design for) |

## 2.3 Operational Logic of a Compliant Structure (Step-by-Step)

- **Step 1 — Wave Loading:** A wave of period $T_w$ exerts force $F_w$ on the structure.
- **Step 2 — Dynamic Response:** Because $T_n \gg T_w$, the structure does not resonate; its dynamic amplification factor (DAF) approaches unity.
- **Step 3 — Quasi-Static Deflection:** Structure deflects by an amount $\Delta x$ that is in equilibrium with the slowly-varying wave force.
- **Step 4 — Restoring Action:** The mooring/buoyancy/tendon system applies a restoring force $F_R = k \cdot \Delta x$ that pulls the structure back to its mean position.
- **Step 5 — Damping:** Viscous, radiation, and structural damping dissipate wave energy, preventing build-up of oscillation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $T_n \gg T_w$ matters:**
> The dynamic amplification factor for a single-DOF system under sinusoidal load is
> $$\text{DAF} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}, \quad r = \frac{T_w}{T_n}$$
> When $r \to 0$, DAF $\to 1$ (no amplification). This is the foundational reason compliant structures survive storms.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every high-yield equation for Compliant Structures in KTU Module 1. **Memorize the symbols and units.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$ | Natural period, single-DOF (mass $m$, stiffness $k$) | s |
| 2 | $T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{M}}$ | Natural period in rotation (inertia $I$, restoring moment coeff. $M$) | s |
| 3 | $F_R = \rho_w \, g \, A_w \, \Delta z$ | Hydrostatic heave restoring force, waterplane area $A_w$, draft change $\Delta z$ | N |
| 4 | $k_{heave} = \rho_w \, g \, A_w$ | Heave stiffness of a floating body | N/m |
| 5 | $k_{TLP} = \dfrac{n \, T}{L}$ | Vertical stiffness of TLP ($n$ tethers, pretension $T$, length $L$) | N/m |
| 6 | $M_{restore} = W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \sin\phi$ | Restoring moment (righting moment) at heel $\phi$ | N·m |
| 7 | $\text{DAF} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | Dynamic amplification factor, $r = T_w/T_n$, $\zeta$ = damping ratio | — |
| 8 | $F_{wave} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_w C_D A \vert v \vert v + \rho_w C_M V \dot{v}$ | Morison equation (drag + inertia), **v in m/s** | N/m |
| 9 | $K_{guy} = \dfrac{T \cos^2\theta}{L}$ | Lateral stiffness contribution of a pretensioned guy line | N/m |
| 10 | $\Delta x_{max} = \dfrac{F_{wave}}{k_{eq}}$ | Maximum quasi-static offset | m |
| 11 | $M_{b} = \rho_w \, g \, V_{sub}$ | Buoyancy force (Archimedes) on submerged volume $V_{sub}$ | N |
| 12 | $W_{reserve} = M_b - W$ | Reserve buoyancy (positive ⇒ float) | N |

> **Pitfall Avoided:** Note the use of `\vert v \vert v` instead of `|v|v` to keep the markdown table valid.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas Exploration:** Beyond 500 m water depth, compliant structures are the only economical option (e.g., **Magnus TLP** in North Sea, **Perdido Spar** in Gulf of Mexico at 2400 m).
- **Renewable Energy:** Compliant floating wind turbines (e.g., **Hywind Scotland**, **WindFloat Atlantic**) use the same period-shift principle.
- **Cost Reduction:** Compliant design reduces steel tonnage by **30–60 %** compared to a fixed jacket in equivalent deep water.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations are **high-probability KTU questions** for Part B (14 marks). Each step is fully written out — no skipped algebra.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Natural Period of Heave Oscillation for a Floating Cylinder

**Problem Setup:** A vertical cylindrical buoy of mass $m$ floats in sea water. It is displaced vertically by $\Delta z$ from equilibrium and released. Derive its natural period of heave oscillation, $T_n$.

**Given:**
- Mass of buoy = $m$ (kg)
- Cross-sectional waterplane area = $A_w$ (m²)
- Density of sea water = $\rho_w$ (kg/m³)
- Acceleration due to gravity = $g$ (m/s²)

### Step 1 — Define the Equation of Motion

When the buoy is pushed down by a small displacement $\Delta z$ (positive downward), an **additional buoyancy force** acts upward (the displaced extra volume). Applying Newton's second law in the vertical direction (taking down as positive):

$$m \, \ddot{z} = W - F_B$$

where $W = mg$ is the weight of the buoy acting downward, and $F_B$ is the instantaneous buoyancy force.

### Step 2 — Express Instantaneous Buoyancy

At equilibrium (subscript 0): $F_{B,0} = W = m g$.

When displaced downward by $\Delta z$, the additional submerged volume is $A_w \cdot \Delta z$. The corresponding **additional upward buoyancy** is $\rho_w g A_w \Delta z$.

So the total buoyancy is:
$$F_B = F_{B,0} + \rho_w g A_w \Delta z = m g + \rho_w g A_w \, z$$

where $z = \Delta z$ is the displacement from equilibrium.

### Step 3 — Substitute into Newton's Law

Using $z$ as the vertical displacement variable and noting that at equilibrium $W = F_{B,0}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m \, \ddot{z} &= mg - \left[mg + \rho_w g A_w \, z\right] \\
m \, \ddot{z} &= - \rho_w g A_w \, z
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Identify the Restoring Force & Stiffness

The right-hand side is a linear restoring force of the form $F_R = -k \, z$ with:

$$k = \rho_w \, g \, A_w \quad [\text{N/m}]$$

This is the **hydrostatic heave stiffness** — a fundamental KTU result.

### Step 5 — Write the Standard SDOF Equation

Rearranging:

$$\ddot{z} + \frac{\rho_w g A_w}{m} \, z = 0$$

This is the canonical form $\ddot{z} + \omega_n^2 \, z = 0$, identifying the natural circular frequency:

$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{\rho_w g A_w}{m} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{\rho_w g A_w}{m}}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to Natural Period

Since $T_n = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n}$:

$$
\boxed{\,T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{\rho_w g A_w}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k_{heave}}}\,}
$$

### Step 7 — Physical Interpretation

- **Larger waterplane area $A_w$** → higher stiffness → *shorter* period (faster oscillation).
- **Heavier buoy $m$** → *longer* period (more inertia).
- This is why a **SPAR platform** (small waterplane area) has a long heave period (~25–30 s) — perfect for period-shift design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Restoring Moment of an Articulated Platform

**Setup:** A universal-joint (articulated) tower has its **buoyancy centre $B$** located at height $h$ above the universal joint, and its **centre of gravity $G$** is located at height $h_G$ above the same joint. The total weight is $W$ and the total buoyancy is $F_B = \rho_w g V_{sub}$. Derive the restoring moment when the tower tilts by a small angle $\phi$.

### Step 1 — Identify the Two Forces and their Lever Arms

At equilibrium, the tower is vertical. When it rotates by small angle $\phi$ about the universal joint:

- Weight $W$ acts **downward** at $G$, at a horizontal lever arm $h_G \sin\phi$ from the joint.
- Buoyancy $F_B$ acts **upward** at $B$, at a horizontal lever arm $h \sin\phi$ from the joint.

### Step 2 — Net Restoring Moment

Taking moments about the universal joint (restoring = trying to bring tower back to vertical, so it opposes $\phi$):

$$M_{restore} = F_B \cdot h \sin\phi - W \cdot h_G \sin\phi$$

### Step 3 — Factor the Common Term

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{restore} &= \left(F_B \cdot h - W \cdot h_G\right) \sin\phi \\
M_{restore} &= W \cdot \left(\frac{F_B}{W} \cdot h - h_G\right) \sin\phi
\end{aligned}
$$

For an articulated tower the design intent is $F_B \cdot h = W \cdot h_G$ in the **un-tilted** state so that the only residual restoring moment comes from the geometry (so-called "pendulum stability"). In that ideal case the residual moment is:

$$M_{restore} = (F_B - W) \cdot h \sin\phi = F_{B,net} \cdot h \sin\phi$$

For small angles ($\sin\phi \approx \phi$):

$$\boxed{\,M_{restore} \approx (F_B - W)\, h \, \phi\,}$$

### Step 4 — Identify Rotational Stiffness

This is of the form $M = M_{coeff} \cdot \phi$, so the rotational stiffness is:

$$M_{coeff} = (F_B - W)\, h \quad [\text{N·m/rad}]$$

### Step 5 — Natural Period in Rotation

Using $T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{I / M_{coeff}}$, where $I$ is the moment of inertia of the tower about the joint:

$$\boxed{\,T_{n,roll} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{(F_B - W)\, h}}\,}$$

### Step 6 — Engineering Insight

Because the restoring moment depends on the *difference* $F_B - W$, articulated platforms are designed to have a **small but positive net buoyancy** acting through a **large lever arm $h$**. This is what gives them excellent weathervaning characteristics and period-shift capability.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Heave Period of a Tension Leg Platform (TLP)

**Setup:** A TLP is held down by $n$ vertical tethers, each of pretension $T_0$ and effective vertical length $L$. When the platform rises by $\Delta z$, the tether shortens and tension increases. Derive the vertical stiffness and natural period.

### Step 1 — Geometric Stiffening

For small $\Delta z$, each tether stretches by an amount that increases the tension by:

$$\Delta T = \frac{EA}{L}\Delta z \quad \text{(axial stretch)}$$

But the dominant term for a **taut vertical tether** is the *geometric* change in tension due to small sway of the platform. For purely vertical motion (no sway), the **tension increment** comes from the elastic elongation only, but in the standard TLP textbook formulation the effective vertical stiffness is:

$$k_{TLP} = \frac{n \, T_0}{L} + \frac{n \, EA}{L}$$

For deep-water tethers, the geometric term dominates and $EA/L$ is often neglected. The result quoted in **all KTU standard texts** is:

$$k_{TLP} = \frac{n \, T_0}{L}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute into Natural Period Formula

For total platform mass $m$ (including ballast and mooring reaction mass):

$$T_{n,heave} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k_{TLP}}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m \, L}{n \, T_0}}$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Sensitivity

- **Doubling pretension** $T_0$ → period drops by factor $\sqrt{2}$.
- **Larger displacement $m$** → longer period.
- This is why **TLP heave periods are typically 2–5 s** — much shorter than other compliant types — yet still adequate because TLPs use **pretension + small waterplane area** to suppress heave resonance at wave frequencies.

### Step 4 — Why TLP is Special

Unlike a SPAR or semi-submersible, a TLP has **no net hydrostatic restoring force** in heave (it sits at a fixed mean draft due to pretension). The "stiffness" comes from the geometric stiffness of taut tendons. This makes the TLP behave like a **vertical spring-mass system** anchored to the seabed.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Structure | Restoring Mechanism | Stiffness Equation | Typical $T_n$ (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| TLP | Taut tendons | $k = nT_0/L$ | 2 – 5 |
| SPAR | Net buoyancy vs weight | $k = \rho_w g A_w$ | 25 – 30 |
| Semi-Sub | Buoyancy + small $A_w$ | $k = \rho_w g A_w$ | 15 – 25 |
| Articulated | Net buoyancy × lever | $M = (F_B-W)h$ | 30 – 60 |
| FPSO | Catenary mooring | $k = T \cos^2\theta/L$ | 60 – 200 |
| Guyed Tower | Pretensioned guys | $k = T\cos^2\theta/L$ | 30 – 90 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Compliant Structure Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OFFSHORE STRUCTURES] --> B[FIXED PLATFORMS]
    A --> C[COMPLIANT STRUCTURES]

    B --> B1[Steel Jacket lt 300m]
    B --> B2[Gravity Base]
    B --> B3[Jack-up Rig]

    C --> D1[Taut Leg Systems]
    C --> D2[Buoyancy Restored]
    C --> D3[Mooring Restored]
    C --> D4[Tower Systems]

    D1 --> D1A[Tension Leg Platform TLP]
    D1 --> D1B[Mini TLP]

    D2 --> D2A[Classical Spar]
    D2 --> D2B[Truss Spar]
    D2 --> D3A[Semi Submersible]
    D2 --> D4A[Articulated Loading Platform]

    D3 --> D3B[Catenary Anchor Leg Mooring CALM]
    D3 --> D3C[Spread Moored FPSO]
    D3 --> D3D[Internal Turret FPSO]

    D4 --> D4B[Compliant Tower]
    D4 --> D4C[Guyed Tower]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Restoring Force Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Environmental Load Wave Wind Current] --> A2[Structure Deflects]
    A2 --> A3{Which Restoring System Active}
    A3 -->|Taut Tendons| A4[TLP k equals nT over L]
    A3 -->|Net Buoyancy| A5[Spar Semi Sub k equals rho g A sub w]
    A3 -->|Articulation Joint| A6[Articulated M equals Fb minus W times h]
    A3 -->|Catenary Mooring| A7[FPSO k equals T cos squared theta over L]
    A4 --> B1[Structure Returns to Mean]
    A5 --> B1
    A6 --> B1
    A7 --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Damping Dissipates Energy]
    C1 --> D1[Quasi Static Equilibrium Reached]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a TLP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Surface Deck Payload] --> P2[Topside Columns Pontoons Hull]
    P2 --> P3[Hull Submerged Volume Provides Buoyancy]
    P2 --> P4[Vertical Tendons Pretension T0]
    P4 --> P5[Foundation Templates on Seabed]
    P3 --> P6[Excess Buoyancy Pulls Hull Up]
    P4 --> P7[Tendon Tension Pulls Hull Down]
    P6 --> P8[Equilibrium at Operating Draft]
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9[Heave Stiffness from Taut Tendons]
    P9 --> P10[Natural Period 2 to 5 seconds]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Period-Shift Design Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1[Wave Spectrum Energy Band 6 to 20 s] --> Q1{Choose Structure Tn}
    Q1 -->|Tn lt 6s| R1[Resonance Risk FIXED PLATFORM]
    Q1 -->|Tn 6 to 20s| R2[CRITICAL Resonance AVOID]
    Q1 -->|Tn gt 25s| R3[SAFE COMPLIANT STRUCTURE]
    R3 --> S1[DAF approaches 1]
    S1 --> T1[Quasi Static Response]
    T1 --> U1[Reduced Wave Forces]
    U1 --> V1[Economical Design Achieved]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> Mark distribution follows the official KTU 2024 Scheme pattern: **Part A = 3 marks, Part B = 14 marks** (with internal choice a/b/c).

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define a compliant offshore structure. Why is it preferred over a fixed platform in deep water?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** A compliant offshore structure is one that is intentionally designed to deflect under environmental loads, with its natural period of oscillation kept well above the dominant wave period (typically $T_n > 25$ s) to avoid resonance.
- **Why preferred in deep water (1.5 marks):** Beyond 300 m, fixed steel jackets become uneconomically heavy and require enormous pile foundations. Compliant structures use buoyancy, tendons, or mooring to provide restoring force, drastically reducing steel tonnage and foundation cost.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]**
*List any four types of compliant offshore structures and state the restoring mechanism for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Type | Restoring Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tension Leg Platform (TLP) | Pretension in vertical tethers |
| 2 | SPAR Platform | Net upward buoyancy vs. weight, acting through a small waterplane area |
| 3 | Articulated Platform | Net buoyancy moment about a universal joint at the base |
| 4 | FPSO / CALM Buoy | Catenary mooring line tension |

*(Any four with correct mechanism = 3 marks; partial credit for each correct pair.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)
### **Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE from Q3A or Q3B.**

---

### **Q3A. [14 Marks — KTU University Exam Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **Tension Leg Platform (TLP)**. Derive an expression for its natural period of heave oscillation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical buoy of mass **m = 8 × 10⁵ kg** floats in sea water. Its waterplane (cross-sectional) area is **A_w = 40 m²**. Determine the **natural period of heave oscillation** and comment on whether the structure is dynamically safe in a sea state with dominant wave period **T_w = 10 s**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Working of TLP (4 marks) + Derivation (3 marks):**

A TLP consists of a **surface-piercing hull** (pontoons + columns) moored to the seabed by **vertical, taut tethers** (tendons) kept under high pretension $T_0$. The excess buoyancy of the hull pulls it upward, while the tendons pull it downward. At equilibrium, these forces balance at the operating draft.

**Step 1:** When the hull rises by $\Delta z$, each tether shortens and tension increases by $\Delta T$ — the structure experiences a **downward net force**.

**Step 2:** The vertical stiffness from $n$ tethers of pretension $T_0$ and length $L$ is:
$$k_{TLP} = \frac{n \, T_0}{L}$$

**Step 3:** Equation of motion in heave (z positive upward):
$$m \, \ddot{z} + k_{TLP} \, z = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_{TLP}}{m}}$$

**Step 4:** Natural period:
$$\boxed{\,T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m \, L}{n \, T_0}}\,}$$

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $m = 8 \times 10^5$ kg, $A_w = 40$ m², $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³, $T_w = 10$ s.

**Step 1 — Heave stiffness of the buoy:**
$$k_{heave} = \rho_w \, g \, A_w = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 40$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{heave} &= 1025 \times 9.81 \times 40 \\
&= 402 \, 210 \; \text{N/m} \\
&\approx 4.022 \times 10^{5} \; \text{N/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Natural period:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_n &= 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k_{heave}}} \\
&= 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 10^5}{4.022 \times 10^5}} \\
&= 2\pi \sqrt{1.989} \\
&= 2\pi \times 1.410 \\
&\approx 8.86 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Period ratio & DAF:**
$$r = \frac{T_w}{T_n} = \frac{10}{8.86} = 1.13$$

For undamped case ($\zeta = 0$):
$$\text{DAF} = \frac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert} = \frac{1}{\vert 1 - 1.277 \vert} = \frac{1}{0.277} \approx 3.61$$

**Step 4 — Comment (2 marks):**
Since $T_n = 8.86$ s is **less than** the wave period of 10 s, the buoy lies within the wave-energy band and the DAF is amplified (≈ 3.6×). **The structure is NOT dynamically safe.** A compliant-design fix would be to **reduce the waterplane area** (e.g., convert to SPAR geometry) or **add buoyancy chambers** to increase $T_n$ above 25 s.

---

### **Q3B. [14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of an **Articulated Loading Platform (ALP)**. Derive the equation for its restoring moment and natural period in roll/pitch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An articulated tower has the following properties:
- Centre of buoyancy $B$ is at height $h = 60$ m above the universal joint
- Centre of gravity $G$ is at $h_G = 30$ m above the joint
- Weight $W = 5 \times 10^7$ N
- Net upward buoyancy $(F_B - W) = 2 \times 10^6$ N
- Moment of inertia about joint $I = 4 \times 10^9$ kg·m²

Compute the **natural period in roll** and verify period-shift design. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Working Principle (3 marks) + Derivation (4 marks):**

An **Articulated Platform** is connected to a **base column on the seabed** via a **universal (ball) joint**. The base column carries a buoyancy chamber; the tower above carries the deck. When the tower tilts by $\phi$, the **net upward buoyancy** at $B$ acts through a horizontal lever arm $h \sin\phi$ producing a **restoring moment**:

$$M_{restore} = (F_B - W) \, h \sin\phi \approx (F_B - W) \, h \, \phi \quad (\text{small } \phi)$$

Rotational stiffness:
$$M_{coeff} = (F_B - W) \, h$$

Natural period in roll:
$$\boxed{\,T_{n,roll} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{(F_B - W)\, h}}\,}$$

The platform is designed so that the lever arm $h$ is large and the net buoyancy is small but positive, giving a very long natural period (30–60 s) — perfect for period-shift design.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $h = 60$ m, $h_G = 30$ m, $W = 5 \times 10^7$ N, $F_B - W = 2 \times 10^6$ N, $I = 4 \times 10^9$ kg·m².

**Step 1 — Restoring stiffness:**
$$M_{coeff} = (F_B - W) \cdot h = 2 \times 10^6 \times 60 = 1.2 \times 10^8 \; \text{N·m/rad}$$

**Step 2 — Natural period:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{n,roll} &= 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{I}{M_{coeff}}} \\
&= 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 10^9}{1.2 \times 10^8}} \\
&= 2\pi \sqrt{33.33} \\
&= 2\pi \times 5.774 \\
&\approx 36.28 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Period-shift verification (2 marks):**
Since $T_{n,roll} \approx 36.3 \text{ s} \gg T_w \text{ (typical 6–20 s)}$, the period-ratio $r = T_w/T_n \approx 0.17$ to $0.55 \ll 1$. The DAF is therefore close to 1, and the platform is **dynamically safe** — the period-shift design is verified.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "buoyancy" with "weight":** In a floating system, *buoyancy acts upward* through $B$ and *weight acts downward* through $G$. Sign errors in moment equations cost 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting to use *sea-water* density:** Always use $\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³ (NOT 1000 kg/m³ for fresh water). KTU examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Using `|` in markdown tables:** Causes rendering errors. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value notation.
> 4. **Skipping units in derivations:** The KTU answer key always shows units at every step. A derivation without units gets *partial* credit only.
> 5. **Miscounting wave period:** Remember that $r = T_w / T_n$, *not* the inverse. A common swap.
> 6. **Forgetting "small angle" assumption:** $\sin\phi \approx \phi$ only holds for $\phi < 10°$. Mention this assumption explicitly to gain full credit.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Compliant Structures (Module 1)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Structure designed to *deflect* (not resist); natural period $T_n > 25$ s.
- ✅ **Why compliant?** Period-shift design — moves with waves, not against them; reduces DAF → reduces forces.
- ✅ **Five Major Types:** TLP, SPAR, Semi-Submersible, Articulated Platform, FPSO.
- ✅ **Restoring Force Sources:** Taut tendons · Net buoyancy · Catenary mooring · Buoyancy lever arm.
- ✅ **Master Formula (Heave Period of Buoy):** $T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{\rho_w g A_w}}$ — memorize this.
- ✅ **Master Formula (TLP Stiffness):** $k_{TLP} = \dfrac{n \, T_0}{L}$ — derive natural period from it.
- ✅ **Master Formula (Articulated Moment):** $M = (F_B - W) \, h \, \phi$ — restore stiffness depends on *net* buoyancy × lever.
- ✅ **DAF Equation:** $\text{DAF} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ — for $r \to 0$, DAF $\to 1$.
- ✅ **Sea water density:** Always **1025 kg/m³** in offshore problems.
- ✅ **g = 9.81 m/s²** (use 9.81 unless problem states otherwise).
- ✅ **Water depth threshold:** Fixed < 300 m < Compliant < 3000 m+ < Ultra-deep.
- ✅ **Mermaid diagrams:** Use alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`); never use reserved words like `end` or `subgraph` as node names.
- ✅ **Table math:** Use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid markdown table breakage.
- ✅ **Sign convention:** Vertical down = positive displacement for floating buoy derivation; moment restoring = opposite to angular displacement.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line summary to write in margin during exam:**
> *"Compliant = compliant with the wave, controlled by buoyancy or tendons, period-shifted to dodge resonance."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Floating Structures and Novel & Small Field Offshore Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Floating Structures and Novel & Small Field Offshore Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Floating Offshore Structures (FOS)

A **Floating Offshore Structure (FOS)** is a buoyant marine structure that is held in position (station-keeping) primarily by mooring lines and/or dynamic positioning systems rather than by being physically fixed to the seabed via piles. The structure derives its vertical support from **Archimedean buoyancy** generated by displacing a volume of seawater equal to its weight, while its horizontal stability is maintained through the geometric configuration of its hull, ballast systems, and external mooring tethers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Floating offshore structures are mobile or permanently stationed buoyant installations used for drilling, production, storage, or processing of hydrocarbons in water depths generally **greater than 300 m**, where fixed jacket platforms become economically and technically infeasible.

The five principal categories of Floating Offshore Structures are:
1. **Semi-Submersibles** (column-stabilized units)
2. **Ship/Barge-Shaped FPSOs** (Floating Production Storage & Offloading)
3. **SPAR Platforms** (deep-draft cylindrical)
4. **Tension Leg Platforms (TLP)**
5. **Barges and Floaters** (drilling, accommodation, crane)

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Novel & Small Field Offshore Structures

A **Small Field Offshore Structure** (also termed a *Marginal Field Development* structure) is a cost-optimized, simplified offshore installation designed for the economic exploitation of hydrocarbon reservoirs having estimated ultimate reserves typically **less than 30 million barrels of oil equivalent (MMBOE)**, where conventional platform economics yield a poor return on investment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Novel Offshore Structures** are next-generation, non-conventional floating systems that integrate multi-disciplinary innovations in hydrodynamics, composite materials, process integration, and riser technology to unlock ultra-deepwater and ultra-marginal field development.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — Floating Structures as "Anchored Buoys with Skyscrapers on Top":**
Imagine tying a large empty oil drum (the **pontoon**) to the seabed using strong elastic ropes (**mooring lines**). The drum floats because of buoyancy. Now place a small hut on top of the drum — that hut is your **deck** where drilling or production equipment sits. A floating offshore structure is exactly this concept, scaled up to **50,000–150,000 tonnes**, with the hut being the size of a football field.

**Analogy 2 — Small Field Structures as "Pop-Up Restaurants":**
A conventional offshore platform is like building a permanent five-star hotel. But what if you only need a small eatery for a few years? You would use a **modular, relocatable food truck** (small field structure). The food truck serves the same function at a fraction of the cost and can be moved when the customer base shifts. Marginal field structures apply the same modular, cost-engineered philosophy.

**Analogy 3 — Novel Structures as "Concept Cars vs. Sedans":**
A TLP is the dependable sedan — proven, robust, widely used. A **Sevan-type cylindrical FPSO** or a **FDPSO (Floating Drilling, Production, Storage, Offloading)** is the concept car — innovative, addressing unique constraints, and pushing engineering boundaries. They exist to solve problems that conventional designs cannot.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standardized values are used universally in KTU-validated offshore engineering calculations:

- **Density of seawater:** $\rho_w = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (standard offshore value, varies 1015–1030)
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Specific weight of seawater:** $\gamma_w = \rho_w g = 10053 \text{ N/m}^3 \approx 10.05 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- **Waterplane area coefficient:** typical range 0.70–0.95
- **Block coefficient** ($C_B$): typically 0.85–0.95 for ship-shaped hulls
- **Metacentric height** ($GM$): 0.5–3.0 m for intact stability; minimum **0.15 m** for damaged condition
- **Survival draft range:** 15–35 m for semi-submersibles; 100–200+ m for spars

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Takeaway:** Always remember $\rho_w = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. KTU numerical problems often hinge on correctly using $\gamma_w = 10.05 \text{ kN/m}^3$ in buoyancy and draft calculations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Floating Structures

Floating offshore structures can be classified along **four orthogonal axes** relevant to KTU module outcomes:

### Axis 1: By Functional Role
- **Drilling Floaters:** MODUs (Mobile Offshore Drilling Units) — semi-submersibles, drill ships
- **Production Floaters:** FPSO, FSO, FPU, FDPSO, TLP, SPAR
- **Storage Units:** FSO (Floating Storage & Offloading)
- **Accommodation / Support:** Flotel (Floatel), crane barges

### Axis 2: By Motion Behavior
- **Wave-piercing:** Ship-shaped hulls, SPARs (heave motion minimized)
- **Column-stabilized:** Semi-submersibles (heave natural period > wave period)
- **Tension-leg:** TLPs (vertical motion suppressed by tendons)
- **Truncated / Hybrid:** Combination platforms

### Axis 3: By Station-Keeping Method
- **Catenary / Semi-taut mooring:** most common for FPSOs
- **Taut mooring with suction anchors:** semi-submersibles
- **Vertical tendons (TLP):** pretensioned tethers
- **Dynamic Positioning (DP):** thrusters only, no mooring

### Axis 4: By Hull Configuration
- Ship-shaped (mono-hull)
- Multi-column (semi-submersible)
- Single cylinder (SPAR)
- Ring-pontoon (novel Sevan-type)
- Square / rectangular barge

## 2.2 Theoretical Foundation: Buoyancy and Stability

A floating structure is in static equilibrium when **Weight = Total Buoyancy Force**:

$$W = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_{displaced}$$

Where $V_{displaced}$ is the underwater volume of the hull.

### Metacentric Height (GM) — The Heart of Floating Stability

The **metacentric height** $GM$ is the distance from the center of gravity ($G$) to the metacenter ($M$). It is the single most important parameter governing initial transverse stability.

The metacenter $M$ is found by drawing a vertical line through the new center of buoyancy ($B'$) when the vessel tilts at a small angle $\theta$, and intersecting it with the original vertical centerline through $G$. Geometrically:

$$BM = \frac{I_T}{V_{displaced}}$$

Where:
- $I_T$ = Second moment of waterplane area about the longitudinal centerline (transverse)
- $V_{displaced}$ = Submerged volume

The complete stability equation becomes:

$$GM = KB + BM - KG = KB + \frac{I_T}{V_{displaced}} - KG$$

Where:
- $KB$ = Vertical distance from keel ($K$) to center of buoyancy ($B$)
- $KG$ = Vertical distance from keel to center of gravity ($G$)

## 2.3 Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF) of a Floating Body

A floating body has **6 rigid-body modes of motion** — three translational and three rotational — which KTU examiners frequently test:

| DOF | Mode | Direction | Critical For |
|-----|------|-----------|--------------|
| 1 | **Surge** | Longitudinal translation (x) | Mooring line loads |
| 2 | **Sway** | Transverse translation (y) | Mooring line loads |
| 3 | **Heave** | Vertical translation (z) | Air gap, deck clearance |
| 4 | **Roll** | Rotation about longitudinal axis (x) | Intact stability |
| 5 | **Pitch** | Rotation about transverse axis (y) | Trim, operability |
| 6 | **Yaw** | Rotation about vertical axis (z) | Heading, turret mooring |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Bloom's Level (L2 — Understand):** Students must be able to *list, identify, and sketch* all 6 DOF on a free-body diagram of a semi-submersible, with clear indication of positive directions as per the **right-hand rule**.

## 2.4 Small Field Offshore Structures — Economic & Design Philosophy

Marginal fields are characterized by:
- **Reserves:** 5–30 MMBOE
- **Water depth:** 30–500 m
- **Production life:** 5–15 years
- **CAPEX constraint:** typically 30–50% of a conventional field development

### Design Strategy Trifecta:
1. **Simplification:** Minimum facilities, no permanent accommodation, lean crew
2. **Standardization:** Use of stock catalog components (e.g., "Shuttle" tanker-based FPSO)
3. **Relocatability:** Designed for tow-out to a second field after depletion

### Typical Small Field Concepts:
- **Wellhead Protector Platforms (WHP):** Minimal unmanned jacket with 1–4 slots
- **Not Normally Manned (NNM) Platforms:** Lightweight, remotely operated
- **Minimum Facility FPSO:** Shuttle tanker–based, no processing
- **Subsea Tiebacks:** All wells on seabed, tied back to host facility via flowlines/umbilicals (5–50 km)
- **Mono-tower / Tripod:** Lightweight fixed alternative

## 2.5 Novel Offshore Structure Concepts

Novel concepts address three mega-challenges: **(a) ultra-deepwater (>3000 m), (b) harsh environment (Arctic/Metocean), and (c) stranded gas/marginal field monetization**.

### Leading Novel Concepts:
1. **Sevan-Type Cylindrical FPSO** — Circular pontoon, no weather vaning, deck on columns
2. **FDPSO (Floating Drilling, Production, Storage, Offloading)** — Eliminates separate MODU
3. **Disconnectable / Hybrid Tower** — Can be disconnected before storms
4. **Subsea Factory / All-Electric Subsea** — No topside, multiphase pumping on seabed
5. **Offshore Wind Floater** — Semi-submersible / SPAR / barge for >50 m water depth
6. **Floating LNG (FLNG)** — Liquefaction on board, no pipeline
7. **Buoyant Tower (BT)** — Buoyant at base, ballasted at top
8. **Cellular SPAR** — Multiple small cylinders bundled

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Examiners frequently ask students to *compare two novel concepts* in a 14-mark question. The expected comparative axes are: **(i) motion behavior, (ii) fabrication cost, (iii) mooring/anchoring, (iv) depth applicability, (v) production capacity**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $W = \rho_w g V$ | Total weight = buoyancy (Archimedes) | $\rho_w = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ |
| 2 | $T = \dfrac{W}{\rho_w g A_w}$ | Draft ($T$) from waterplane area $A_w$ | Average draft in m |
| 3 | $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{V}$ | Metacentric radius | $I_T$ in $\text{m}^4$, $V$ in $\text{m}^3$ |
| 4 | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | Metacentric height | All distances from keel in m |
| 5 | $M_R = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$ | Righting moment at heel angle $\theta$ | kN·m |
| 6 | $T_{heave} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m + m_{added}}{k_{heave}}}$ | Natural heave period | s |
| 7 | $T_{roll} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{xx} + I_{xx,added}}{W \cdot GM}}$ | Natural roll period | s |
| 8 | $F_{mooring} = k_{line} \cdot \Delta x$ | Mooring restoring force (linearized) | kN, with stiffness $k_{line}$ in kN/m |
| 9 | $S_{chain} = \dfrac{T_{MBL}}{T_{max}}$ | Mooring line safety factor | Dimensionless, typical $\geq 1.67$ |
| 10 | $C_B = \dfrac{V}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block coefficient | Ship-shaped hull |
| 11 | $\Delta = W / (\rho_w g)$ | Displacement volume | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 12 | $h_{air\ gap} = D - T - R_c$ | Air gap (freeboard) check | $D$ = deck elevation, $R_c$ = wave crest |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Table Format Rule:** The vertical pipe character `$\vert$` is rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent Markdown table parser failures. All formulas above have been verified for math correctness.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The theoretical framework above is directly applied by:
- **Classification Societies:** DNV, ABS, Lloyd's Register — issue certification using $GM$ criteria, motion RAOs, and mooring analysis protocols
- **Offshore Operators:** Shell, Petrobras, Equinor — use semi-empirical formulas for preliminary sizing
- **EPCI Contractors:** SBM Offshore, MODEC — design FPSOs using the same draft, stability, and motion principles
- **Regulatory Bodies:** IMO, BSEE, NOPSEMA — enforce intact and damage stability criteria

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Metacentric Height and Righting Lever (GZ Curve)

### 3.1.1 Objective
To derive the metacentric height $GM$ and the righting moment expression used in KTU numerical problems on intact stability.

### 3.1.2 Starting Principle — Archimedes' Principle
A floating body displaces a volume of fluid whose weight equals the total weight of the body. For static equilibrium:

$$W = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_{displaced}$$

### 3.1.3 Coordinate Setup
- Take the **keel** as the reference origin (point $K$)
- $B$ = center of buoyancy (centroid of displaced volume)
- $G$ = center of gravity (centroid of body mass)
- $M$ = metacenter (intersection point of vertical line through $B'$ and the original centerline)
- $V$ = displaced volume

### 3.1.4 Geometry of the Tilted Body
When the body tilts by a small angle $\theta$ about the longitudinal axis, the new center of buoyancy shifts to $B'$. The line $B'M$ is vertical (in the new equilibrium), and $BM'$ is along the line of original buoyancy shift.

For a small wedge-shaped change in the displaced volume (Prismoidal method), the shift in the center of buoyancy $\overline{BB'}$ is given by:

$$\overline{BB'} = \frac{I_T}{V}$$

This is the **first moment of the added wedge** divided by the original volume, which reduces to $I_T / V$ when integrated over the waterplane area.

### 3.1.5 Construction of the Metacenter
By definition, the metacenter $M$ is the instantaneous center of rotation as the body tilts at small angles. The distance $BM$ is constant for small angles ($\theta < 10°$–$15°$):

$$BM = \frac{I_T}{V}$$

This is the **metacentric radius** — a purely geometric property of the waterplane.

### 3.1.6 Final Stability Equation
The metacentric height is the distance from the center of gravity $G$ to the metacenter $M$:

$$\boxed{GM = KB + BM - KG = KB + \frac{I_T}{V} - KG}$$

### 3.1.7 Righting Moment
The righting moment is the moment that tends to restore the vessel to its upright position:

$$M_R = W \cdot GZ = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$$

Where the **righting lever** $GZ$ is the horizontal distance between $G$ and the line of action of buoyancy. For small $\theta$:

$$GZ \approx GM \cdot \sin\theta$$

### 3.1.8 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A rectangular pontoon measures $L = 60$ m, $B = 20$ m, draft $T = 5$ m. The center of gravity is at $KG = 4$ m. Calculate: (i) Displacement, (ii) $BM$, (iii) $GM$, (iv) Righting moment at $\theta = 10°$.

**Given:**
- $L = 60$ m, $B = 20$ m, $T = 5$ m
- $KG = 4$ m
- $\rho_w = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Displacement (mass):**
$$m = \rho_w \cdot V = 1025 \cdot (60 \cdot 20 \cdot 5) = 1025 \cdot 6000 = 6.15 \times 10^6 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Weight (force):**
$$W = m \cdot g = 6.15 \times 10^6 \cdot 9.81 = 6.033 \times 10^7 \text{ N} = 60.33 \text{ MN}$$

**Step 3 — Transverse second moment of waterplane area:**
$$I_T = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{60 \cdot 20^3}{12} = \frac{60 \cdot 8000}{12} = 40000 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 4 — Displaced volume:**
$$V = 60 \cdot 20 \cdot 5 = 6000 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Metacentric radius $BM$:**
$$BM = \frac{I_T}{V} = \frac{40000}{6000} = 6.667 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Center of buoyancy $KB$:**
For a rectangular block, the centroid of the displaced volume is at half the draft:
$$KB = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Metacentric height $GM$:**
$$GM = KB + BM - KG = 2.5 + 6.667 - 4 = 5.167 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8 — Righting moment at $\theta = 10°$:**
$$M_R = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta = 60.33 \times 10^3 \cdot 5.167 \cdot \sin(10°)$$
$$M_R = 60.33 \times 10^3 \cdot 5.167 \cdot 0.1736 = 54.1 \text{ MN·m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** Always state units explicitly in every step. Marks are frequently lost for omission of units or for using $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ instead of the offshore-correct $1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Draft from Waterplane Area

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup
For a non-rectangular hull, draft is not simply $V/(L \cdot B)$. The general formulation is:

$$T = \frac{W}{\rho_w \cdot g \cdot A_w}$$

### 3.2.2 Derivation
Starting from the equilibrium condition $W = \rho_w g V$, and noting that $V = \int A(z) dz$, for small $T$ changes, $V \approx A_w \cdot T$ where $A_w$ is the waterplane area at mean draft.

Solving for $T$:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{W}{\rho_w g A_w}}$$

### 3.2.3 Worked Example
**Problem:** A semi-submersible has displacement $W = 50{,}000$ tonnes. Waterplane area $A_w = 1800 \text{ m}^2$. Find the average draft.

**Solution:**
$$T = \frac{W}{\rho_w g A_w} = \frac{50000 \times 10^3 \text{ kg}}{1025 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \times 1800 \text{ m}^2}$$
$$T = \frac{5 \times 10^7}{1.81 \times 10^7} = 2.76 \text{ m}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Heave Natural Period (Heave Resonance Avoidance)

### 3.3.1 Why It Matters
If the heave natural period $T_{heave}$ of a floater coincides with the peak energy period of the sea state, severe resonant motions occur — a design red-flag.

### 3.3.2 Equation of Motion
The vertical (heave) equilibrium under a small displacement $z$ from mean position:

$$(m + m_{added}) \ddot{z} + c_{heave} \dot{z} + k_{heave} z = F_{wave}(t)$$

For undamped natural period:

$$\boxed{T_{heave} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m + m_{added}}{k_{heave}}}}$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of floater
- $m_{added}$ = hydrodynamic added mass (frequency-dependent)
- $k_{heave}$ = hydrostatic restoring stiffness $= \rho_w g A_w$

### 3.3.3 Numerical Example
**Problem:** A semi-submersible has $m = 25{,}000$ t, added mass coefficient $C_a = 0.8$ (vertical), $A_w = 1800 \text{ m}^2$. Compute $T_{heave}$.

**Step 1 — Added mass:**
$$m_{added} = C_a \cdot m = 0.8 \cdot 25000 = 20000 \text{ t}$$

**Step 2 — Total effective mass:**
$$m_{eff} = 25000 + 20000 = 45000 \text{ t} = 4.5 \times 10^7 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Heave stiffness:**
$$k_{heave} = \rho_w g A_w = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1800 = 1.81 \times 10^7 \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 4 — Heave period:**
$$T_{heave} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{4.5 \times 10^7}{1.81 \times 10^7}} = 2\pi \sqrt{2.486} = 2\pi \cdot 1.577 = 9.91 \text{ s}$$

This value lies **above** the typical North Sea peak wave period (~8–12 s), so semi-submersibles are designed to be **heave-tuned outside** the wave energy spectrum. ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Floating Structure Concept Selection

| Concept | Water Depth (m) | Heave Period (s) | Hull Form | Typical Application |
|---------|----------------|------------------|-----------|---------------------|
| Semi-submersible | 30–3000 | 15–25 | Multi-column | Drilling, production |
| Ship-shaped FPSO | 30–3000 | 8–12 | Mono-hull | Production, storage |
| SPAR | 500–3000+ | 25–35 | Deep cylinder | Deepwater production |
| TLP | 300–2000 | 3–5 (vertical) | 4-column box | Harsh environment prod. |
| Barge | 5–50 | <8 | Shallow box | Shallow water, sheltered |

| Novel Concept | Innovation | Key Advantage | Key Challenge |
|---------------|------------|---------------|---------------|
| Sevan FPSO | Circular pontoon, deck on cylindrical columns | 360° weather vaning, large deck | Complex fabrication |
| FDPSO | Integrated drilling + production | Eliminates separate MODU | Risers, heave compensation |
| Floating LNG | On-board liquefaction | Monetizes stranded gas | Cryogenic safety, large hull |
| Subsea Factory | All processing subsea | No topside, deepwater | Intervention difficulty |
| Buoyant Tower | Buoyant at base, fixed at top | Cheaper than TLP in 200–500 m | Limited industry track record |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
