topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wind Forces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wind Force (KTU 2024 Definition):** Wind force on an offshore structure is the horizontal aerodynamic load generated by the relative motion of air mass over the exposed surfaces of the structure, including the hull, superstructure, deck modules, helideck, flares, and living quarters. It is evaluated as a **quasi-static drag load** proportional to the dynamic pressure of the wind, the projected area, and a shape-dependent drag coefficient.

For offshore design, wind force is one of the three principal environmental forces (alongside waves and currents). The governing design wind condition is typically a **sustained 1-minute mean wind speed at a reference elevation of 10 m above Mean Sea Level (MSL)**, with a specified **return period (e.g., 100 years for ULS, 10,000 years for abnormal)**.

The classical mathematical expression is given by:

$$F_W = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho_a \, V^2 \, C_S \, A$$

where each term will be rigorously defined in Section 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2:** Students must be able to (i) identify the components of wind force, (ii) derive wind velocity profiles, (iii) compute wind drag on typical offshore components, and (iv) apply API/ISO recommended coefficients to a numerical problem.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a coastal cliff on a stormy day holding a flat umbrella. The harder the wind blows, the harder the umbrella pushes against your hand. Now tilt the umbrella slightly — the force changes direction and magnitude. This is exactly how wind acts on an offshore platform:

- **The umbrella's surface = projected area of the platform deck.**
- **The wind speed = velocity of the air mass.**
- **The tilt of the umbrella = shape and orientation (drag coefficient) of the structure.**

Three intuitive takeaways:

1. **Wind force grows with the SQUARE of wind speed.** Double the wind speed → quadruple the force. This is why a Category 5 hurricane (250 km/h) imposes ~25× the wind load of a gentle breeze (50 km/h) on the same structure.
2. **Wind acts mostly on ABOVE-WATER surfaces.** The hull (submerged) feels negligible wind drag because of the enormous water density shielding it. The deck, helideck, and derrick are the primary receptors.
3. **Wind is NOT uniform with height.** Air near the sea surface is "roughed up" by waves, slowing down. Higher elevations (helideck, flare boom) experience much stronger winds.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air density (sea level, 15°C) | $\rho_a$ | **1.225** | kg/m³ |
| Reference elevation | $z_{ref}$ | **10** | m above MSL |
| Kinematic viscosity of air | $\nu_a$ | $1.46 \times 10^{-5}$ | m²/s |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Von Kármán constant | $k$ | **0.41** | — |
| 100-yr return wind (North Sea, API) | $V_{100}$ | **~36** | m/s @ 10 m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 1.225 kg/m³?** This is the **ISA (International Standard Atmosphere)** air density at 15°C and 101.325 kPa. For tropical offshore basins (e.g., Arabian Sea, Bay of Bengal), engineers often use $\rho_a = 1.20$ to $1.25$ kg/m³. KTU problems will almost always specify the value to use.

## 1.4 Why Wind Matters in Offshore Design

Although wind pressure ($\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2$) is roughly **830× weaker** than hydrostatic pressure at the same depth, the wind-exposed areas of a typical semi-submersible or FPSO can be enormous (10,000+ m²), and the wind moment arm about the waterline can be **30–60 m** long. The resulting **overturning moment** can rival that of extreme waves. Hence, wind is never neglected — it is one of the three orthogonal components (wind + wave + current) that the platform must simultaneously resist.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Wind Loading — Layered Reasoning

Wind force on a body emerges from the momentum exchange between moving air and the body's surface. The mechanism is decomposed into three sequential physical events:

### Layer 1 — The Driving Pressure (Dynamic Pressure)

When a fluid in uniform motion (velocity $V$) is brought to rest against a surface, its kinetic energy converts to pressure energy by Bernoulli's principle. The resulting excess pressure is the **dynamic pressure**:

$$q = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_a V^2$$

> **Plain-English "Why":** Air has mass. When that mass is forced to stop against a wall, the momentum must go somewhere — it becomes pressure pushing the wall.

### Layer 2 — The Shape Factor (Force Coefficient)

Not all the dynamic pressure acts on the structure. The actual force depends on the body's geometry. We use a **force coefficient** $C_S$ (also written $C_F$ or $C_D$ for drag) to scale the ideal dynamic pressure down (or, for some shapes, slightly up):

$$C_S = C_D \quad \text{(drag coefficient, for flow-aligned bodies)}$$

> **Plain-English "Why":** A flat plate facing the wind "catches" more air than a streamlined I-beam. The coefficient simply encodes this shape effect, measured in wind tunnels or CFD.

### Layer 3 — The Effective Catchment Area (Projected Area)

Wind does not act on the *skin* area but on the **shadow (projected) area** the body casts onto a plane perpendicular to the wind direction:

$$A = \text{projected (frontal) area in m}^2$$

> **Plain-English "Why":** Picture a stick held sideways — the wind sees only the cross-section of the stick, not its length.

---

## 2.2 Wind Velocity Profile — How Wind Speed Varies with Height

Offshore wind does **not** blow uniformly. It is slowed near the sea surface by friction with waves. Two mathematical models are used in KTU 2024 problems:

### Model A — Power Law (API / ISO Preferred)

$$\boxed{\,V(z) \;=\; V_{ref}\,\left(\dfrac{z}{z_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha}\,}$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $V(z)$ | Mean wind speed at elevation $z$ (m/s) |
| $V_{ref}$ | Reference wind speed at $z_{ref}$ (m/s) |
| $z$ | Elevation above MSL (m) |
| $z_{ref}$ | Reference elevation (typically **10 m**) |
| $\alpha$ | Power-law exponent (depends on sea state & stability) |

**Typical values of $\alpha$ (offshore):**

| Sea Condition | $\alpha$ |
|---|---|
| Calm sea, stable air | 0.10 |
| Open sea, neutral | **0.14** (most common) |
| Rough sea, unstable | 0.20 |

### Model B — Logarithmic Law (Boundary-Layer Meteorology)

$$\boxed{\,V(z) \;=\; \dfrac{u_{*}}{k}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{z}{z_0}\right)\,}$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $u_{*}$ | Friction velocity (m/s) |
| $k$ | Von Kármán constant = **0.41** |
| $z_0$ | Surface roughness length (m), typically $0.001$ to $0.01$ m for open sea |

> **When to use which?** API RP 2A and ISO 19901-1 specify the **power law** for offshore design. The logarithmic law is theoretically more accurate but requires meteorological input ($u_{*}$, $z_0$) often unavailable at the design stage.

---

## 2.3 Force Components — Drag, Lift, and Moment

For a generic bluff body (like a deck module or derrick), the wind force decomposes into three orthogonal components:

| Component | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Drag (along-wind)** | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2 C_D A$ | Pushes structure downwind |
| **Lift (cross-wind)** | $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2 C_L A$ | Pushes structure sideways (perpendicular) |
| **Overturning moment** | $M = F \times \text{lever arm}$ | Tries to tip the platform over |

For most offshore problems, **drag dominates** and lift is conservatively neglected unless the problem explicitly states otherwise.

---

## 2.4 API RP 2A Wind Force Calculation — Step-by-Step Procedure

The American Petroleum Institute Recommended Practice 2A (API RP 2A) is the industry-standard methodology that KTU follows:

1. Determine the **design wind speed** $V$ at 10 m MSL for the required return period.
2. Compute the wind speed at the centroid of each exposed component using the **power law**.
3. For each component, look up the **shape coefficient** $C_S$ (drag coefficient) from API tables.
4. Compute the wind force on each component: $F_i = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V_i^2 C_{S,i} A_i$.
5. Sum forces and moments about the waterline (or base).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **API Code Tip:** The shape coefficient $C_S$ depends on whether the element is a **beam (truss member), plate (flat surface), or cylinder (tubular).** For tubulars, the coefficient depends on the Reynolds number and the ratio of spacing to diameter. KTU problems frequently give you the $C_S$ value directly.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Formula | Application | Key Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_W = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2 C_S A$ | Total wind force on an exposed area | N (Newtons) |
| 2 | $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2$ | Dynamic pressure (velocity pressure) | Pa (N/m²) |
| 3 | $V(z) = V_{ref}(z/z_{ref})^{\alpha}$ | Power-law wind profile | m/s |
| 4 | $V(z) = (u_{*}/k)\ln(z/z_0)$ | Logarithmic wind profile | m/s |
| 5 | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2 C_D A$ | Drag force on component | N |
| 6 | $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2 C_L A$ | Lift force on component | N |
| 7 | $M_{OT} = \sum F_i \cdot h_i$ | Overturning moment about base | N·m |
| 8 | $V_{z,design} = V_{10m}(z/10)^{\alpha}$ | API design wind at height $z$ | m/s |
| 9 | $A_{proj} = b \times h$ (rectangular) | Projected area (rectangle) | m² |
| 10 | $A_{proj} = L \times D$ (cylinder) | Projected area (cylinder side-on) | m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for Tables:** When writing absolute values or norms (e.g., $\vert V \vert$), always use $\vert\,$ and $\mid$ — never the raw vertical bar character. This protects the markdown table from breaking.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Domain | Application of Wind Force Calculation |
|---|---|
| Fixed offshore platforms (jackets) | Sizing of flare booms, helidecks, derricks |
| FPSOs & semi-submersibles | Mooring line design (wind overturning moment) |
| Offshore wind turbines | Tower & blade aerodynamic design |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wind Force from Bernoulli's Equation

We begin with the steady, incompressible Bernoulli equation along a streamline from a point far upstream (point 0) to a stagnation point on the body (point s):

$$p_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V_0^2 \;=\; p_s + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V_s^2$$

At the stagnation point, the air is brought to rest, so $V_s = 0$:

$$p_s - p_0 \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V_0^2$$

This pressure excess integrated over the projected area $A$ gives the **ideal stagnation force**:

$$F_{ideal} = (p_s - p_0)\cdot A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2 A$$

But real bodies are not flat plates; air slips around them, generating a wake and reducing the actual force. A **shape (force) coefficient** $C_S$ corrects for this:

$$F_W \;=\; C_S \cdot F_{ideal} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_a\, V^2\, C_S\, A \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power-Law Wind Profile from Dimensional / Empirical Reasoning

Empirically, atmospheric boundary-layer measurements show that mean wind speed grows with height in a self-similar manner. The simplest function satisfying $V(z_{ref}) = V_{ref}$ and dimensionless form is a power law. Substituting the boundary condition $V(z_{ref}) = V_{ref}$:

$$V_{ref} = V_{ref}\,\left(\dfrac{z_{ref}}{z_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha} \;=\; V_{ref} \cdot 1^{\alpha} \;=\; V_{ref} \;\;\checkmark$$

This satisfies the reference condition identically. The coefficient $\alpha$ is then fitted from field measurements (e.g., $\alpha = 0.14$ for open sea per API). $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Worked Numerical Example (End-to-End, KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark pattern):**
> An offshore jacket platform has a rectangular deckhouse of plan dimensions **20 m × 15 m** and height **8 m**, located with its base at **EL (+) 15 m** above MSL. The exposed derrick above the deckhouse has a projected area of **12 m²** with centroid at **EL (+) 30 m**. The 100-year return design wind speed at 10 m MSL is **36 m/s**. Take $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\alpha = 0.14$, and $C_S = 1.5$ for the deckhouse and $C_S = 1.2$ for the derrick.
> Compute: (a) the wind force on the deckhouse and derrick separately; (b) the total overturning moment about MSL.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the wind speed at the centroid of the deckhouse.**

The deckhouse extends from 15 m to 23 m. Its centroid is at:

$$z_1 = 15 + \tfrac{8}{2} = 19 \text{ m}$$

Applying the power law:

$$V_1 = V_{ref}\,\left(\dfrac{z_1}{z_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha} = 36 \times \left(\dfrac{19}{10}\right)^{0.14}$$

Evaluate the bracketed term:

$$\left(\dfrac{19}{10}\right)^{0.14} = (1.9)^{0.14}$$

Using logarithms: $\ln(1.9) = 0.6419$, so $0.14 \times 0.6419 = 0.0899$, and $e^{0.0899} = 1.0940$:

$$V_1 = 36 \times 1.0940 = 39.38 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the wind speed at the centroid of the derrick.**

Given as $z_2 = 30$ m:

$$V_2 = 36 \times \left(\dfrac{30}{10}\right)^{0.14} = 36 \times (3.0)^{0.14}$$

$\ln(3) = 1.0986$, $0.14 \times 1.0986 = 0.1538$, $e^{0.1538} = 1.1663$:

$$V_2 = 36 \times 1.1663 = 41.99 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the projected area of the deckhouse.**

The wind hits the **broad face** (20 m wide × 8 m tall) — assume wind comes from the long-side direction:

$$A_1 = 20 \times 8 = 160 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Wind force on deckhouse.**

$$F_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_a\, V_1^2\, C_{S,1}\, A_1$$

$$F_1 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (39.38)^2 \times 1.5 \times 160$$

Compute each piece:

$$V_1^2 = (39.38)^2 = 1550.78 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a = 0.5 \times 1.225 = 0.6125 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\text{Partial} = 0.6125 \times 1550.78 = 949.85 \text{ N/m}^2$$

$$F_1 = 949.85 \times 1.5 \times 160 = 949.85 \times 240 = 227{,}964 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_1 \approx 228.0 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 5 — Wind force on derrick.**

$$F_2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (41.99)^2 \times 1.2 \times 12$$

$$V_2^2 = (41.99)^2 = 1763.04 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a \cdot V_2^2 = 0.6125 \times 1763.04 = 1079.86 \text{ N/m}^2$$

$$F_2 = 1079.86 \times 1.2 \times 12 = 1079.86 \times 14.4 = 15{,}550 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_2 \approx 15.55 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 6 — Total wind force.**

$$F_{total} = F_1 + F_2 = 228.0 + 15.55 = 243.55 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7 — Overturning moment about MSL.**

$$M_{OT} = F_1 \cdot z_1 + F_2 \cdot z_2$$

$$M_{OT} = (227.96 \text{ kN})(19 \text{ m}) + (15.55 \text{ kN})(30 \text{ m})$$

$$M_{OT} = 4331.24 + 466.50 = 4797.74 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$\boxed{M_{OT} \approx 4798 \text{ kN·m}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Insight:** Notice that the deckhouse contributes **~90%** of the total moment despite being lower than the derrick. This is because force scales with area, and the deckhouse is far larger. KTU often asks which component dominates — always quantify!

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Power Law vs Logarithmic Law

| Feature | Power Law | Logarithmic Law |
|---|---|---|
| **Functional form** | Polynomial in $(z/z_{ref})$ | Logarithmic in $(z/z_0)$ |
| **Parameters needed** | $V_{ref}, z_{ref}, \alpha$ | $u_{*}, z_0, k$ |
| **Theoretical basis** | Empirical fit | Boundary-layer theory |
| **Accuracy near surface** | Reasonable | Better |
| **Accuracy at high $z$** | Good | Overpredicts slightly |
| **API/ISO preference** | ✅ **Yes** | ❌ Rarely used in design |
| **KTU exam frequency** | **Very High** | Medium (definition-level) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wind-Force Loading Architecture on an Offshore Platform (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Atmospheric Boundary Layer] --> B[Wind Velocity Profile V-z]
    B --> C[Reference Wind Speed at 10 m MSL]
    C --> D[Power Law Transformation]
    D --> E[Wind Speed at Component Centroid]
    E --> F[Component Geometry]
    F --> G[Projected Area A]
    G --> H[Shape Coefficient Cs Lookup]
    H --> I[Dynamic Pressure q]
    I --> J[Wind Force Calculation Fw]
    J --> K[Drag Force Fd]
    J --> L[Lift Force Fl]
    J --> M[Overturning Moment Mot]
    M --> N[Moment about MSL or Base]
    K --> O[Resultant Environmental Load Vector]
    L --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Combined with Wave and Current Loads]
    P --> Q[Ultimate Limit State Design Check]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Trace the flow from the top (atmosphere) to the bottom (combined design check). Each block represents a *transformation* the wind undergoes before contributing to the final design load.

---

## 4.2 Wind Profile — Height Variation Schematic (Mermaid Visualization)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[MSL — Sea Surface] -->|z = 0 m, V ≈ 0| B[Reference Level]
    B -->|z = 10 m, V = Vref| C[Deck Level]
    C -->|z = 15-25 m, V = V1| D[Deckhouse Centroid]
    D -->|z = 30 m, V = V2| E[Helideck and Derrick]
    E -->|z = 50 m plus, V = V3| F[Flare Boom Tip]
    F --> G[V Increases Monotonically with z]
    F --> H[Power Law V z = Vref times z over zref to alpha]
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — API RP 2A Wind Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputData[Input Parameters]
        I1[Design Wind Speed Vref]
        I2[Air Density rho_a]
        I3[Power Law Exponent alpha]
        I4[Component Geometry and Elevation]
        I5[Shape Coefficients Cs]
    end

    subgraph Processing[Computation Engine]
        P1[Step 1 — Velocity at Centroid]
        P2[Step 2 — Projected Area Calculation]
        P3[Step 3 — Force on Each Component]
        P4[Step 4 — Sum Forces and Moments]
    end

    subgraph Output[Design Output]
        O1[Total Horizontal Wind Force Fw]
        O2[Overturning Moment Mot]
        O3[Base Shear Reaction]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    I4 --> P1
    I4 --> P2
    I2 --> P3
    I5 --> P3
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    I4 --> P4
    P4 --> O1
    P4 --> O2
    P4 --> O3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** In the architecture above, notice the **three independent input streams** (meteorological, geometric, and shape) that converge at the computation engine. KTU problems typically give you 1–2 of these and ask you to derive the rest — be ready to switch between roles.

flowchart TD

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "shape coefficient" used in wind force estimation for offshore structures. Why is its value different for a flat plate, an I-beam, and a tubular member?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **shape coefficient** $C_S$ (also called force or drag coefficient) is a dimensionless factor that scales the ideal stagnation dynamic pressure $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2$ to give the actual wind force on a body:
> $$F_W = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2 C_S A$$
> It accounts for the body's geometry, flow separation, and wake formation. Typical values:
> - **Flat plate (broadside):** $C_S \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$ (maximum blockage)
> - **I-beam (edge-on to wind):** $C_S \approx 1.3$ to $1.6$ (sharp edges, separated flow)
> - **Tubular (cylinder):** $C_S \approx 0.5$ to $1.2$ (curved surface, smooth flow)
> The variation arises because curved surfaces allow the flow to remain attached longer, generating a narrower wake and lower pressure drag, while flat plates cause abrupt flow separation and a wide wake.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the power-law wind velocity profile used in offshore design. What is the typical value of the exponent $\alpha$ for open-sea conditions?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The power-law profile expresses mean wind speed $V(z)$ at elevation $z$ as:
> $$V(z) = V_{ref}\,\left(\dfrac{z}{z_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha}$$
> where $V_{ref}$ is the reference wind speed at elevation $z_{ref}$ (typically 10 m above MSL), and $\alpha$ is the power-law exponent. It reflects the fact that wind speed increases with height due to decreasing frictional drag from the sea surface.
> **Typical value for open-sea, neutral atmospheric stability:** $\alpha = 0.14$ (per API RP 2A / ISO 19901-1). For rough seas and unstable air, $\alpha$ can rise to 0.20.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** "Each module carries 14 marks with internal choice. The question may be split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Both choices must be from the same module." We provide TWO independent 14-mark question choices below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the expression for wind force on an exposed structural component starting from Bernoulli's equation. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A semi-submersible platform has an accommodation block with the following data:
> - Plan dimensions: **30 m × 18 m**, height **12 m**
> - Base of block at **EL (+) 20 m** above MSL
> - 100-year return wind at 10 m MSL: **40 m/s**
> - Power-law exponent: $\alpha = 0.14$
> - Air density: $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³
> - Shape coefficient: $C_S = 1.6$
>
> Compute: (i) the wind speed at the centroid of the accommodation block, (ii) the wind force on the broad face of the block, and (iii) the overturning moment about MSL. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

##### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, incompressible, inviscid flow (Bernoulli valid).
2. Wind blows horizontally; structure is stationary.
3. Quasi-static loading (dynamic gust effects neglected at this stage).
4. Uniform wind speed over the component area.

**Derivation:**

Apply Bernoulli along a streamline from a far-upstream point (0) to a stagnation point (s) on the body:

$$p_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V_0^2 = p_s + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V_s^2$$

At the stagnation point, $V_s = 0$:

$$p_s - p_0 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2$$

This excess pressure acting on projected area $A$ gives the ideal force:

$$F_{ideal} = (p_s - p_0) \cdot A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2 A$$

A real body does not achieve full stagnation due to flow separation and wake formation. Introduce a **shape (force) coefficient** $C_S$ to correct:

$$F_W = C_S \cdot F_{ideal} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2 C_S A \quad \blacksquare$$

##### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**(i) Wind speed at centroid:**

$$z_1 = 20 + \tfrac{12}{2} = 26 \text{ m}$$

$$V_1 = 40 \times \left(\dfrac{26}{10}\right)^{0.14} = 40 \times (2.6)^{0.14}$$

$\ln(2.6) = 0.9555$, $0.14 \times 0.9555 = 0.1338$, $e^{0.1338} = 1.1432$:

$$V_1 = 40 \times 1.1432 = 45.73 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Wind force (broad face = 30 m × 12 m):**

$$A = 30 \times 12 = 360 \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_W = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (45.73)^2 \times 1.6 \times 360$$

$V_1^2 = 2091.2 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$

$F_W = 0.6125 \times 2091.2 \times 1.6 \times 360 = 0.6125 \times 2091.2 \times 576$

$0.6125 \times 2091.2 = 1280.86$

$1280.86 \times 576 = 737{,}775 \text{ N} \approx 737.8 \text{ kN}$

**(iii) Overturning moment about MSL:**

$$M_{OT} = F_W \cdot z_1 = 737.8 \times 26 = 19{,}183 \text{ kN·m} \approx 19.18 \text{ MN·m}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the power-law and logarithmic wind velocity profiles. Compare their applicability to offshore design. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A fixed jacket platform has the following wind-exposed components:
>
> | Component | Centroid Elevation (m) | Projected Area (m²) | Shape Coefficient $C_S$ |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Deckhouse | 18 | 120 | 1.5 |
> | Helideck perimeter | 25 | 40 | 1.3 |
> | Derrick | 32 | 25 | 1.1 |
>
> Given: $V_{ref} = 38$ m/s at 10 m MSL, $\alpha = 0.14$, $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³.
>
> Compute: (i) the wind force on each component, and (ii) the total base shear and overturning moment about MSL. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

##### Part (a) — Theory (7 marks)

| Feature | Power Law | Logarithmic Law |
|---|---|---|
| **Equation** | $V(z) = V_{ref}(z/z_{ref})^{\alpha}$ | $V(z) = (u_{*}/k)\ln(z/z_0)$ |
| **Origin** | Empirical (curve fit to field data) | Theoretical (Monin–Obukhov similarity) |
| **Parameters** | $V_{ref}, z_{ref}, \alpha$ | $u_{*}, z_0, k$ |
| **API/ISO use** | Standard practice | Rarely used in design codes |
| **Advantages** | Simple; needs only $V_{ref}$ and $\alpha$ | More physically grounded |
| **Disadvantages** | Empirical constants; less accurate near surface | Requires $u_{*}, z_0$ from metocean data |

For offshore design, the **power law is preferred** because (i) it requires only a reference wind speed and an exponent, both of which are readily available, and (ii) it is sanctioned by API RP 2A and ISO 19901-1. The logarithmic law is more accurate in the lowest 10–20 m of the boundary layer and is used in detailed wind-resource assessments (e.g., for offshore wind farms) where the additional parameters can be measured.

##### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Wind speed at each centroid:**

For component 1 (deckhouse, $z_1 = 18$ m):

$$V_1 = 38 \times (18/10)^{0.14} = 38 \times (1.8)^{0.14}$$

$\ln(1.8) = 0.5878$, $0.14 \times 0.5878 = 0.0823$, $e^{0.0823} = 1.0858$:

$$V_1 = 38 \times 1.0858 = 41.26 \text{ m/s}$$

For component 2 (helideck, $z_2 = 25$ m):

$$V_2 = 38 \times (25/10)^{0.14} = 38 \times (2.5)^{0.14}$$

$\ln(2.5) = 0.9163$, $0.14 \times 0.9163 = 0.1283$, $e^{0.1283} = 1.1369$:

$$V_2 = 38 \times 1.1369 = 43.20 \text{ m/s}$$

For component 3 (derrick, $z_3 = 32$ m):

$$V_3 = 38 \times (32/10)^{0.14} = 38 \times (3.2)^{0.14}$$

$\ln(3.2) = 1.1632$, $0.14 \times 1.1632 = 0.1628$, $e^{0.1628} = 1.1768$:

$$V_3 = 38 \times 1.1768 = 44.72 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Wind force on each component:**

$$F_i = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times V_i^2 \times C_{S,i} \times A_i$$

Compute $V_i^2$:

- $V_1^2 = (41.26)^2 = 1702.39$
- $V_2^2 = (43.20)^2 = 1866.24$
- $V_3^2 = (44.72)^2 = 1999.88$

Compute each force:

$$F_1 = 0.6125 \times 1702.39 \times 1.5 \times 120 = 0.6125 \times 1702.39 \times 180$$

$0.6125 \times 1702.39 = 1042.71$

$1042.71 \times 180 = 187{,}688 \text{ N} \approx 187.7 \text{ kN}$

$$F_2 = 0.6125 \times 1866.24 \times 1.3 \times 40 = 0.6125 \times 1866.24 \times 52$$

$0.6125 \times 1866.24 = 1143.07$

$1143.07 \times 52 = 59{,}440 \text{ N} \approx 59.4 \text{ kN}$

$$F_3 = 0.6125 \times 1999.88 \times 1.1 \times 25 = 0.6125 \times 1999.88 \times 27.5$$

$0.6125 \times 1999.88 = 1224.93$

$1224.93 \times 27.5 = 33{,}686 \text{ N} \approx 33.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Total base shear:**

$$F_{total} = 187.7 + 59.4 + 33.7 = 280.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Total overturning moment about MSL:**

$$M_{OT} = F_1 z_1 + F_2 z_2 + F_3 z_3$$

$$M_{OT} = (187.7)(18) + (59.4)(25) + (33.7)(32)$$

$$M_{OT} = 3378.6 + 1485.0 + 1078.4 = 5942.0 \text{ kN·m} \approx 5.94 \text{ MN·m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply the power law at the centroid elevation.** Many students use $V_{ref}$ at 10 m for the entire structure. This UNDERESTIMATES wind force on upper decks (helideck, derrick) and loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing broad-face vs narrow-face area.** The "projected area" is the area perpendicular to the wind direction. If the wind blows along the 30 m side, you use 18 m × 12 m, not 30 m × 12 m.
> 3. **Mixing air density with water density.** Air is **1.225 kg/m³**; seawater is **1025 kg/m³**. A factor of ~830 error is fatal.
> 4. **Omitting shape coefficients.** A common 1-mark loss. Always state $C_S$ explicitly.
> 5. **Unit slip-ups.** Wind force should be in **N or kN**; moment in **N·m or kN·m**. Convert before writing the final answer.
> 6. **Wrong reference for the moment arm.** The lever arm is measured from the **moment reference point** (MSL, base, or waterline — problem-specific) to the **centroid of the force**, not to the top of the component.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Wind Forces):**

- ⭐ **Master Formula:** $F_W = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2 C_S A$ — memorize, derive, apply.
- ⭐ **Standard air density:** $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³ (ISA, 15°C, sea level).
- ⭐ **Reference elevation:** 10 m above MSL (per API).
- ⭐ **Power-law exponent for open sea:** $\alpha = 0.14$.
- ⭐ **Von Kármán constant** $k = 0.41$ (logarithmic profile only).
- ⭐ Wind speed **grows with height**; higher decks see higher wind.
- ⭐ Wind force **grows with the square of wind speed** — a 2× speed = 4× force.
- ⭐ The **deckhouse** is typically the dominant contributor to overturning moment due to its large area.
- ⭐ **Shape coefficient $C_S$** depends on geometry: flat plate > I-beam > cylinder.
- ⭐ **Projected area** = shadow area perpendicular to wind direction.
- ⭐ Wind is **one of three orthogonal loads** (wind + wave + current); combine vectorially for ULS.
- ⭐ **Lift force** is conservatively neglected in routine design unless explicitly required.
- ⭐ For TLP / SPAR, wind contributes to **air-gap** analysis (combined with wave crest).
- ⭐ **Gusting** is handled via a **gust factor** $G$ (typically 1.0 to 1.4); beyond KTU scope but good to know.
- ⭐ When summing moments, lever arm = elevation of the **force application point (centroid)** above the chosen reference.
- ⭐ **Common KTU trap:** Using $V_{ref}$ at 10 m for the whole structure — always elevate first.
- ⭐ **API RP 2A** and **ISO 19901-1** are the governing codes — your numerical answers must align with their conventions.

> **End of Module 2 — Wind Forces Notes** ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Earthquake Forces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Earthquake Forces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Earthquake Forces** on offshore structures refer to the **transient dynamic inertial loads** induced in a fixed or floating marine structure due to the propagation of seismic waves through the underlying seabed, the surrounding water column, and the structure itself. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** and **API RP 2A / ISO 19902** design codes, earthquake loading is treated as an **extreme environmental event** that must be combined with other environmental loads (wind, wave, current) using appropriate load combination factors.

The seismic action is fundamentally characterized by the **ground motion time history** $\ddot{u}_g(t)$ at the foundation level, which is conventionally resolved into three orthogonal components: two **horizontal** (H1, H2) and one **vertical** (V). For offshore jackets, gravity platforms, and compliant towers located in seismically active regions (e.g., Bay of Bengal, Sea of Japan, Gulf of California), the design must consider:

- **Inertial forces** from the structure's own mass acceleration,
- **Hydrodynamic forces** from the water surrounding submerged members,
- **Kinematic forces** from the seabed deformation transmitted through the foundation,
- **Soil–Pile–Structure Interaction (SPSI)** effects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **API RP 2A (Section 6.5)** and **ISO 19902 (Clause 13)**, offshore platforms in seismically active zones must be designed for two limit states: **Strength Level Earthquake (SLE)** — with a return period of ~**200–500 years**, and **Ductility Level Earthquake (DLE)** — with a return period of ~**1000–2500 years**, allowing limited inelastic deformations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Anchored Raft in a Tsunami"**
>
> Imagine a heavy wooden raft tied to the seabed by four strong ropes (this is your offshore jacket fixed platform). Now imagine the seabed itself is being violently shaken from below by a giant (the earthquake fault rupture). Three things happen simultaneously:
>
> 1. **The raft itself wants to fly off** because of its inertia (this gives the *inertial forces*).
> 2. **The water sloshing around the ropes and legs** pushes and pulls the structure sideways (this gives the *hydrodynamic forces*).
> 3. **The seabed moves** and tilts the ropes, displacing the base of the raft (this gives the *kinematic / foundation forces*).
>
> The earthquake engineer must design the raft, ropes, and anchors such that during the most violent shaking, the raft does not collapse, overturn, or fail at any joint — and after shaking stops, the platform must still be able to function (post-earthquake operability).

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Parameters

The following **standard seismic metrics** govern the design of offshore structures:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Peak Ground Acceleration | **PGA** | g (or m/s²) | Maximum horizontal ground acceleration |
| Peak Ground Velocity | PGV | cm/s | Maximum ground velocity |
| Peak Ground Displacement | PGD | cm | Maximum permanent ground offset |
| Spectral Acceleration | $S_a$ | g | Peak acceleration of an SDOF oscillator |
| Spectral Velocity | $S_v$ | cm/s | Peak pseudo-velocity |
| Spectral Displacement | $S_d$ | cm | Peak spectral displacement |
| Natural Period | $T_n$ | s | Fixed-base natural period of structure |
| Importance Factor | $I$ | dimensionless | 1.0 (Normal) / 1.25–1.5 (Critical) |
| Response Modification Factor | $R$ | dimensionless | Ductility-dependent (≈ 2–4 for steel jackets) |
| Site Class | A–F | — | Soil stiffness classification (NEHRP) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard gravitational acceleration used in seismic calculations is **g = 9.81 m/s²**, and ground accelerations are typically expressed as a **fraction of g** for ease of comparison (e.g., **0.3 g** for a strong-motion event).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Nature of Earthquake Loading on Offshore Platforms

Unlike wind or wave loads, earthquake loads are **inertial** in nature — they arise because the ground (and hence the structure's base) is suddenly accelerated, and the structure's mass resists this acceleration. The complete seismic analysis proceeds in **five logical stages**:

1. **Hazard Definition**: Identify the seismic zone, fault proximity, and design ground motion (PGA, response spectrum).
2. **Soil–Foundation Modelling**: Quantify site amplification, soil stiffness, and pile–soil interaction.
3. **Structure Idealization**: Develop a 3-D lumped-mass / finite-element model of the jacket, deck, and appurtenances.
4. **Dynamic Analysis**: Perform modal, response spectrum, or time-history analysis.
5. **Load Combination & Design**: Combine seismic forces with operational and environmental loads per code-specified factors.

## 2.2 Equation of Motion for a Multi-Degree-of-Freedom (MDOF) Offshore Structure

The fundamental dynamic equilibrium equation under seismic excitation is:

$$[M]\{\ddot{u}\} + [C]\{\dot{u}\} + [K]\{u\} = -[M]\{r\}\ddot{u}_g(t)$$

Where:
- $[M]$ = Lumped (or consistent) mass matrix including **structural mass + hydrodynamic added mass + rotary inertia of deck modules**
- $[C]$ = Damping matrix (Rayleigh damping typically used: $[C] = \alpha[M] + \beta[K]$)
- $[K]$ = Stiffness matrix (linear or non-linear for DLE analysis)
- $\{u\}$ = Relative displacement vector of structural nodes
- $\{r\}$ = Influence vector (1 for horizontal DOFs excited, 0 otherwise)
- $\ddot{u}_g(t)$ = Ground acceleration time history

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a minus sign?** The right-hand side is the **effective inertial force** acting on the structure when its base accelerates by $\ddot{u}_g$. Since the absolute acceleration is $\{ü\} + \{r\}\ddot{u}_g$, Newton's second law gives the negative sign.

## 2.3 Single-Degree-of-Freedom (SDOF) Idealization — Foundation Concept

For a jack-up or a single tower, the platform is often idealized as an **SDOF system** with an effective period $T_n$ and damping ratio $\xi$:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\xi\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = -\ddot{u}_g(t)$$

The **maximum base shear** $V_b$ is then:

$$V_b = m_{eff} \cdot S_a(T_n, \xi)$$

Where $S_a(T_n, \xi)$ is the **spectral acceleration** read from the design response spectrum at the effective period $T_n$ and damping $\xi$ (typically **5%** for offshore steel structures).

## 2.4 Hydrodynamic Added Mass Effect

Submerged structural members (jacket legs, braces, piles) interact with the surrounding water during seismic shaking. Per **API RP 2A** and **Morison's Equation extended to seismic frequencies**, the **added mass coefficient** $C_M$ for a circular cylinder oscillating in still water at low Keulegan–Carpenter numbers is:

$$C_M = C_m - 1$$

For a smooth circular cylinder in **inviscid, incompressible flow**:

$$C_m = 2.0 \quad \text{(potential flow theory, valid when } KC \to 0 \text{)}$$

For **real offshore conditions** (viscous + finite-frequency effects), API recommends:

$$C_m = \begin{cases} 2.0 & \text{for } KC < 2 \text{ (small oscillation)} \\ 2.0 - 0.044(KC - 2) & \text{for } 2 \le KC \le 12 \\ 1.5 & \text{for } KC > 12 \end{cases}$$

Where $KC$ is the **Keulegan–Carpenter number**:

$$KC = \frac{U_m \cdot T}{D}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **earthquake analysis**, oscillation amplitudes are small ($KC \ll 2$), so **$C_m = 2.0$** is universally used. This effectively **doubles the displaced mass of water per unit length** of submerged member:
> $$m_a = C_M \cdot \rho_w \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

## 2.5 Seismic Base Shear Formula (API / ISO Simplified Method)

For preliminary design of fixed offshore platforms, the **API RP 2A Strength Level Earthquake (SLE)** base shear may be estimated as:

$$V = C_s \cdot W$$

Where:
- $V$ = Total design base shear (kN)
- $C_s$ = Seismic response coefficient, $C_s = \frac{I \cdot S_a}{R \cdot g}$ (in units consistent with $S_a$)
- $W$ = Effective seismic weight (dead load + 25% live load + hydrodynamic added mass contributions)
- $I$ = Importance factor (1.0 for manned, 1.25 for critical, 1.5 for post-earthquake essential)
- $R$ = Response modification factor (typically **2.0–3.5** for steel jackets)
- $S_a$ = Design spectral acceleration at the fundamental period

The base shear is then **distributed vertically** along the structure using an inverted-triangular or modal distribution.

## 2.6 Directional Combination Rules

Since ground motion has **three components** (H1, H2, V), code-prescribed combination rules are:

| Code | Horizontal Combination | Vertical Component |
|---|---|---|
| **API RP 2A** | 100% H1 + 30% H2 (or vice versa) | Added as 0.5 × vertical PGA |
| **ISO 19902** | 100/30 or 100/40 rule | 0.5 × vertical (50% of horizontal) |
| **Square Root of Sum of Squares (SRSS)** | $\sqrt{H_1^2 + H_2^2 + V^2}$ | Included directly |
| **30% Rule (IS 1893, IBC)** | 100% H1 + 30% H2 + 30% V | 30% of horizontal PGA |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme questions, always state the combination rule you are applying. The 30% rule and SRSS rule give different answers (~10–15% difference) and partial credit is lost if the rule is not justified.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $[M]\{ü\} + [C]\{u̇\} + [K]\{u\} = -[M]\{r\}\ddot{u}_g$ | MDOF equation of motion under seismic excitation | N, kg, m |
| 2 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural circular frequency | rad/s |
| 3 | $T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{m/k}$ | Natural period | s |
| 4 | $\xi = c / (2\sqrt{km})$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $V_b = m_{eff} \cdot S_a(T_n, \xi)$ | Spectral base shear | N |
| 6 | $V = C_s \cdot W$ | Code-based base shear | N |
| 7 | $C_s = I \cdot S_a / (R \cdot g)$ | Seismic coefficient | dimensionless |
| 8 | $C_m = 2.0$ (seismic) | Added mass coefficient for circular members | dimensionless |
| 9 | $m_a = C_M \cdot \rho_w \cdot \pi D^2 / 4$ | Added mass per unit length | kg/m |
| 10 | $KC = U_m T / D$ | Keulegan–Carpenter number | dimensionless |
| 11 | $M_u = \mu \cdot M_y$ | Ultimate moment in ductile design | N·m |
| 12 | $\mu = \Delta_u / \Delta_y$ | Ductility factor | dimensionless |
| 13 | $H_{eff} = \frac{\sum w_i h_i^2}{\sum w_i h_i}$ | Effective height for vertical distribution | m |
| 14 | $F_i = V \cdot \frac{w_i h_i}{\sum w_j h_j}$ | Inverted-triangular force distribution | N |
| 15 | $S_{d,i} = \sqrt{\sum_{n} (\Gamma_n \phi_{in} S_{d,n})^2}$ | Modal SRSS combination | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Pseudo-Velocity** and **Pseudo-Acceleration** are related by:
> $$S_a = \omega_n S_v = \frac{2\pi}{T_n} S_v \quad ; \quad S_d = \frac{S_a}{\omega_n^2} = \frac{T_n}{2\pi} S_v$$
> These three spectra are plotted on a **tripartite plot** (also called the **Newmark-Hall diagram**) and any one can be derived from the other two.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Seismic design of offshore structures is critical for hydrocarbon-producing regions like:
- **Northern North Sea** (Statoil Gullfaks, Oseberg platforms)
- **California Offshore** (Hondo, Heritage platforms)
- **B.C. Coast, Canada** (Hecate Strait, Cascadia subduction zone)
- **Bay of Bengal** (KG Basin, ONGC platforms — directly relevant to India)

For Indian offshore engineers, the **Cascadia-type subduction zone** off the Indian coast is now under study, and KTU's inclusion of earthquake forces in PCNST602 reflects the strategic importance of this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equation of Motion for an SDOF Offshore Tower under Horizontal Seismic Excitation

### 3.1.1 System Setup
Consider an offshore tower idealized as a single mass $m$ at height $h$ from the seabed, supported on a fixed base, with lateral stiffness $k$ and damping $c$. The seabed undergoes horizontal acceleration $\ddot{u}_g(t)$.

### 3.1.2 Free-Body Diagram (Analytical)
- Inertial force: $F_I = m \cdot (\ddot{u} + \ddot{u}_g)$ acting on mass
- Damping force: $F_D = c \cdot \dot{u}$ opposing relative velocity
- Spring force: $F_S = k \cdot u$ opposing relative displacement

### 3.1.3 Newton's Second Law Application
Applying $F = ma$ to the mass in the **absolute** coordinate system:

$$\sum F_x = m \cdot a_{absolute}$$

$$-k u - c \dot{u} = m(\ddot{u} + \ddot{u}_g)$$

Rearranging to put all dynamic terms on the left:

$$m \ddot{u} + c \dot{u} + k u = -m \ddot{u}_g(t)$$

### 3.1.4 Standardization to Canonical Form
Dividing through by $m$:

$$\ddot{u} + 2\xi \omega_n \dot{u} + \omega_n^2 u = -\ddot{u}_g(t)$$

Where:
- $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ is the natural circular frequency
- $\xi = c/(2\sqrt{km}) = c/(2m\omega_n)$ is the damping ratio

### 3.1.5 Solution via Duhamel Integral
The particular solution for a given $\ddot{u}_g(t)$ is given by the **Duhamel integral**:

$$u(t) = -\frac{1}{\omega_d} \int_0^t \ddot{u}_g(\tau) \cdot e^{-\xi \omega_n (t-\tau)} \cdot \sin[\omega_d(t-\tau)] \, d\tau$$

Where $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\xi^2} \approx \omega_n$ for offshore steel structures where $\xi \le 0.05$.

The **maximum relative displacement** is the **spectral displacement** $S_d$:

$$S_d = \max_{t} \vert u(t) \vert$$

And the corresponding **pseudo-acceleration** is:

$$S_a = \omega_n^2 S_d = \left(\frac{2\pi}{T_n}\right)^2 S_d$$

### 3.1.6 Result — Base Shear
The maximum base shear (inertia force) is:

$$V_b = k \cdot S_d = m \cdot \omega_n^2 S_d = m \cdot S_a$$

In terms of effective weight $W = mg$:

$$V_b = \frac{W \cdot S_a}{g}$$

This is the **fundamental design equation** for earthquake forces on offshore structures.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hydrodynamic Added Mass of a Submerged Cylinder (Potential Flow Theory)

### 3.2.1 Physical Setup
A vertical circular cylinder of diameter $D$ and unit length is fully submerged in water of density $\rho_w$. The cylinder oscillates horizontally with acceleration $\ddot{x}(t)$ in otherwise still water.

### 3.2.2 Velocity Potential
For incompressible, inviscid, irrotational flow, we define a velocity potential $\phi(x, y, t)$ such that velocity $\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$. The continuity equation is the **Laplace equation**:

$$\nabla^2 \phi = 0$$

### 3.2.3 Boundary Conditions
- **No-penetration on cylinder surface**: At $r = D/2$, the radial velocity equals the radial component of the cylinder's motion:
$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r}\bigg|_{r=D/2} = \dot{x} \cos\theta$$
- **Decay at infinity**: As $r \to \infty$, $\nabla \phi \to 0$
- **Zero initial condition**: $\phi(x, y, 0) = 0$

### 3.2.4 Solution by Separation of Variables
Using polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$ with origin at cylinder center, and assuming simple harmonic motion $\dot{x} = U_0 e^{i\omega t}$, we propose:

$$\phi(r, \theta, t) = -\frac{U_0 D^2}{4r} \cos\theta \cdot e^{i\omega t}$$

**Verification at the cylinder surface** ($r = D/2$):

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r}\bigg|_{r=D/2} = \frac{U_0 D^2}{4 r^2}\cos\theta \cdot e^{i\omega t}\bigg|_{r=D/2} = U_0 \cos\theta \cdot e^{i\omega t} = \dot{x}\cos\theta \checkmark$$

### 3.2.5 Pressure Field via Bernoulli's Equation
The unsteady Bernoulli equation (linearized, neglecting gravity and convective terms):

$$p = -\rho_w \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}$$

Substituting:

$$p(r, \theta, t) = -\rho_w \cdot \left(-\frac{D^2}{4r}\cos\theta\right) \cdot \ddot{x}(t) = \frac{\rho_w D^2 \ddot{x}(t) \cos\theta}{4r}$$

### 3.2.6 Force on Cylinder via Pressure Integration
The horizontal force per unit length is the integral of pressure times the surface normal's x-component:

$$F_x = \int_0^{2\pi} p \cdot \cos\theta \cdot \frac{D}{2} d\theta$$

$$F_x = \int_0^{2\pi} \frac{\rho_w D^2 \ddot{x}(t) \cos\theta}{4r}\cos\theta \cdot \frac{D}{2} d\theta \bigg|_{r=D/2}$$

At the surface $r = D/2$:

$$F_x = \int_0^{2\pi} \frac{\rho_w D^2 \ddot{x}(t) \cos^2\theta}{4 \cdot (D/2)} \cdot \frac{D}{2} d\theta = \frac{\rho_w D \ddot{x}(t)}{2} \int_0^{2\pi} \cos^2\theta \, d\theta$$

Evaluating the integral: $\int_0^{2\pi} \cos^2\theta \, d\theta = \pi$

$$F_x = \frac{\rho_w D \ddot{x}(t)}{2} \cdot \pi = \frac{\pi \rho_w D^2 \ddot{x}(t)}{4}$$

### 3.2.7 Identification of Added Mass
The inertial force on the cylinder from the surrounding water is:

$$F_x = m_a \cdot \ddot{x}(t)$$

Comparing with the derived expression:

$$m_a = \frac{\pi \rho_w D^2}{4}$$

This is exactly the **displaced mass of water** per unit length of cylinder. The **added mass coefficient** is therefore:

$$C_m = \frac{m_a}{\rho_w V_{displaced}} = \frac{\pi \rho_w D^2/4}{\rho_w \pi D^2/4} = 2.0$$

Hence **$C_m = 2.0$** is the theoretical result for inviscid potential flow, which is the basis for the API recommendation during seismic events.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Vertical Force Distribution for an MDOF Jacket

For a **multi-storey** jacket idealized as an $N$-DOF shear-building model with masses $m_i$ at heights $h_i$, the **i-th mode lateral force** is:

$$F_i^{(n)} = \Gamma_n \cdot m_i \cdot \phi_{in} \cdot S_{a,n}$$

Where:
- $\Gamma_n = \frac{\sum m_i \phi_{in}}{\sum m_i \phi_{in}^2}$ is the **modal participation factor**
- $\phi_{in}$ is the **mode shape amplitude** at level $i$ in mode $n$
- $S_{a,n}$ is the **spectral acceleration** at period $T_n$ of mode $n$

The **base shear** in mode $n$ is then:

$$V_b^{(n)} = \sum_i F_i^{(n)} = \Gamma_n S_{a,n} \sum_i m_i \phi_{in} = S_{a,n} \cdot \frac{\left(\sum_i m_i \phi_{in}\right)^2}{\sum_i m_i \phi_{in}^2}$$

The **modal SRSS combination** of base shears from $N$ modes is:

$$V_{total} = \sqrt{\sum_{n=1}^{N} \left(V_b^{(n)}\right)^2}$$

> [!TIP]
> For an **inverted-triangular** mode shape approximation (valid for a slender tower), $\phi_i = h_i/H$. Substituting this simplifies the participation factor to:
> $$\Gamma \approx \frac{\sum m_i h_i}{\sum m_i h_i^2 / H} = \frac{H \sum m_i h_i}{\sum m_i h_i^2}$$
> This is the formula used in **API RP 2A Section 6.5.4** for preliminary seismic design.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example (Step-by-Step)

**Problem:** A 4-legged offshore jacket in a seismic zone has the following properties:
- Total mass (structure + contents): $m = 12{,}000$ tonnes
- Effective weight: $W = mg = 12{,}000 \times 9.81 = 117{,}720$ kN
- Fundamental period: $T_n = 2.5$ s
- Design spectral acceleration: $S_a = 0.20$ g
- Site class D (soft soil), Importance factor $I = 1.25$
- Response modification factor $R = 2.5$

**Find:** Design base shear $V_b$.

**Step 1:** Compute seismic coefficient:
$$C_s = \frac{I \cdot S_a}{R \cdot g} \cdot g = \frac{1.25 \times 0.20g}{2.5} = 0.10g$$

(where the $g$ in numerator and denominator cancels when working in g-units)

**Step 2:** Compute base shear:
$$V_b = C_s \cdot W = 0.10 \times 117{,}720 = 11{,}772 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3:** Distribute to three elevation levels (50 m, 80 m, 110 m) with masses 3000, 5000, 4000 t:

| Level $i$ | $h_i$ (m) | $m_i$ (t) | $m_i h_i$ | $F_i$ (kN) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (lowest) | 50 | 3000 | 150,000 | 11,772 × (150,000 / 990,000) = **1,783** |
| 2 | 80 | 5000 | 400,000 | **4,755** |
| 3 (top) | 110 | 4000 | 440,000 | **5,231** |
| **Sum** | — | 12,000 | 990,000 | 11,769 (≈ 11,772) ✓ |

**Step 4:** Check overturning moment at base:
$$M_{OT} = \sum F_i h_i = 1783 \times 50 + 4755 \times 80 + 5231 \times 110 = 89{,}150 + 380{,}400 + 575{,}410 = 1{,}044{,}960 \text{ kN·m} \approx 1045 \text{ MN·m}$$

This moment must be resisted by pile foundations and checked against sliding/overturning stability per API code.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Seismic Analysis Workflow for Offshore Platform

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Seismic Hazard Definition] --> B[Identify Seismic Zone, PGA, Response Spectrum]
    B --> C[Site Response Analysis]
    C --> D[Soil Stiffness, Amplification Factor]
    D --> E[Structural Idealization]
    E --> F[Lumped Mass 3D Model]
    F --> G[Add Hydrodynamic Added Mass]
    G --> H[Compute Modal Properties T1, T2, Tn]
    H --> I{Analysis Type}
    I --> J[Response Spectrum Analysis]
    I --> K[Time History Analysis]
    I --> L[Pushover for DLE]
    J --> M[Modal Combination SRSS or CQC]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Directional Combination 100 30 Rule]
    N --> O[Compute Member Forces]
    O --> P[Load Combination with Wave Wind]
    P --> Q[Member and Joint Design Check]
    Q --> R[Foundation Pile Check]
    R --> S[Final Design OK]
```

## 4.2 SDOF Idealization of an Offshore Tower

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Seabed Ground Motion] -->|acceleration u_ddot_g| B[Foundation]
    B --> C[Structural Stiffness k]
    C --> D[Lumped Mass m with Added Mass m_a]
    D --> E[Damping c]
    E --> F[Relative Response u t]
    F --> G[Spectral Base Shear V_b = m x S_a]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram: Force Components on Submerged Member

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph EarthquakeForces
        A[Inertial Force from Mass] --> D[Total Lateral Force on Member]
        B[Hydrodynamic Added Mass Force] --> D
        C[Drag Damping Force] --> D
        E[Foundation Kinematic Force] --> D
    end
    D --> F[Design Code API RP 2A / ISO 19902]
    F --> G[Member Capacity Check]
```

## 4.4 Response Spectrum Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Ground Motion Time History a_g t] --> B[Fourier Transform]
    B --> C[Fourier Amplitude Spectrum]
    C --> D[Inverse Transform for Each SDOF]
    D --> E[Plot Peak Response vs T_n]
    E --> F[Design Response Spectrum S_a vs T]
    F --> G[Tripartite Plot S_a, S_v, S_d]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Typical Offshore Jacket under Seismic Loading

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Deck
        D1[Process Module Mass M1]
        D2[Living Quarter Mass M2]
        D3[Helideck Mass M3]
    end
    subgraph Jacket4Legs
        L1[Leg 1]
        L2[Leg 2]
        L3[Leg 3]
        L4[Leg 4]
    end
    subgraph SubmergedMembers
        B1[Horizontal Braces]
        B2[Diagonal Braces]
        P1[Pile Foundation 1]
        P2[Pile Foundation 2]
        P3[Pile Foundation 3]
        P4[Pile Foundation 4]
    end
    subgraph WaterColumn
        W1[Hydrodynamic Added Mass m_a]
        W2[Radiation Damping c_rad]
    end
    D1 --> L1
    D2 --> L1
    D3 --> L1
    L1 --- L2
    L2 --- L3
    L3 --- L4
    L1 --> P1
    L2 --> P2
    L3 --> P3
    L4 --> P4
    L1 -.-> B1
    L1 -.-> B2
    L1 -. Hydrodynamic Interaction .-> W1
    L1 -. Hydrodynamic Interaction .-> W2
    P1 --> Seabed(Seabed Seismic Excitation)
```
