<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define the term **"hydrodynamic added mass"** in the context of earthquake analysis of offshore structures. Why is it important for submerged members, and what value of added mass coefficient $C_m$ is recommended by API RP 2A for seismic conditions?

**Model Answer:**
The hydrodynamic added mass is the **inertial reaction** of the water surrounding a submerged structural member when the member undergoes acceleration. When a jacket leg oscillates during an earthquake, the surrounding water must also be accelerated, creating an effective additional mass attached to the structure.

It is important because:
- It **increases the effective inertia** of the structure (raising base shear).
- It **lengthens the natural period** of the structure, potentially moving it into a more sensitive region of the response spectrum.
- It **cannot be ignored** for deep-water jackets where 50–70% of the structural mass is submerged.

Per **API RP 2A**, the added mass coefficient for circular members under seismic (low-amplitude) loading is:
$$C_m = C_m - 1 = 2.0 - 1 = 1.0 \quad \text{(added mass coefficient)}$$
With $C_m = 2.0$ being the inertia coefficient. The added mass per unit length is therefore:
$$m_a = C_M \cdot \rho_w \cdot \pi D^2/4 = 1.0 \cdot \rho_w \pi D^2/4 = \rho_w \pi D^2/4$$
which equals the displaced mass of water per unit length of the cylinder.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between **Strength Level Earthquake (SLE)** and **Ductility Level Earthquake (DLE)** as used in API RP 2A. State the typical return periods and the design philosophy for each.

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | SLE (Strength Level) | DLE (Ductility Level) |
|---|---|---|
| **Return Period** | 200–500 years | 1000–2500 years |
| **Probability of Exceedance** | ~10% in 50 years | ~2–5% in 50 years |
| **PGA Level** | Moderate (~0.15–0.30g) | High (~0.30–0.60g or more) |
| **Design Philosophy** | Structure remains essentially **elastic**; no significant damage | Structure is allowed to undergo **limited inelastic deformation**; load redistribution |
| **Capacity Check** | $D + L + E_{SLE} \le R_n$ | $D + L + E_{DLE} \le \phi R_n$ with ductility checks |
| **Code Intent** | Operational continuity post-event | Life safety and prevention of collapse |

The DLE concept acknowledges that designing elastically for the rarest event is uneconomical, so limited yielding is permitted in well-defined plastic hinge regions (e.g., at the top of jacket legs just below the deck).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A fixed offshore jacket platform in a seismically active region has the following data:
- Total effective weight: $W = 80{,}000$ kN
- Fundamental period: $T_1 = 1.8$ s (lateral), $T_v = 0.6$ s (vertical)
- Design spectral acceleration: $S_a = 0.25g$ (5% damping)
- Site class: D (soft soil)
- Importance factor: $I = 1.25$
- Response modification factor: $R = 2.5$

Compute the **design base shear** in the horizontal direction. Apply the **API 100/30 directional rule** to estimate the design base shear due to combined horizontal and vertical excitation. Take vertical spectral acceleration as 0.15g.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the **three components of earthquake forces** on a fixed offshore jacket. Discuss the role of **soil–pile–structure interaction (SPSI)** in modifying the dynamic response.

---

### Question A — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Compute horizontal seismic coefficient $C_{s,h}$:**

$$C_{s,h} = \frac{I \cdot S_{a,h}}{R} = \frac{1.25 \times 0.25g}{2.5} = 0.125g$$

**Step 2 — Compute horizontal base shear $V_h$:**

$$V_h = C_{s,h} \cdot W = 0.125 \times 80{,}000 = 10{,}000 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Compute vertical seismic coefficient $C_{s,v}$:**

$$C_{s,v} = \frac{I \cdot S_{a,v}}{R} = \frac{1.25 \times 0.15g}{2.5} = 0.075g$$

**Step 4 — Compute vertical base reaction $V_v$:**

$$V_v = 0.075 \times 80{,}000 = 6{,}000 \text{ kN (axial load on piles)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply API 100/30 directional rule:**

The horizontal base shear in the **critical direction** is taken as 100% of $V_h$:
$$V_{H1} = 1.0 \times 10{,}000 = 10{,}000 \text{ kN}$$

The **orthogonal horizontal** contribution is 30% of $V_h$:
$$V_{H2} = 0.30 \times 10{,}000 = 3{,}000 \text{ kN}$$

The **vertical** contribution is 50% of vertical (per API):
$$V_{V,eff} = 0.50 \times 6{,}000 = 3{,}000 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Total design base shear (SRSS or direct):**

Using the **direct (100/30) rule** for member design:
$$V_{design} = \sqrt{V_{H1}^2 + V_{H2}^2 + V_{V,eff}^2} = \sqrt{10{,}000^2 + 3{,}000^2 + 3{,}000^2}$$
$$V_{design} = \sqrt{100{,}000{,}000 + 9{,}000{,}000 + 9{,}000{,}000} = \sqrt{118{,}000{,}000} \approx 10{,}862 \text{ kN}$$

Or simply using the **100/30 directional rule** for orthogonal piles:
$$V_{pile,design} = \sqrt{V_H^2 + (0.3 V_H)^2} = V_H \sqrt{1 + 0.09} = 1.044 V_H \approx 10{,}440 \text{ kN}$$

#### Part (b) — Model Answer Outline

The three components of earthquake forces on a fixed offshore jacket are:

1. **Inertial Forces** (from structural mass acceleration):
   - Arise from the **inertia** of the deck, jacket, piles, and appurtenances when the ground accelerates.
   - Magnitude: $F_I = m \cdot S_a$.
   - Acts at the **center of mass** of each element.

2. **Hydrodynamic Forces** (water–structure interaction):
   - Submerged members experience **added mass** and **radiation damping** from the surrounding water.
   - For circular members, $C_m = 2.0$ (seismic), giving $m_a = \rho_w \pi D^2/4$ per unit length.
   - Significant for deep-water jackets.

3. **Kinematic / Foundation Forces** (soil–structure interaction):
   - Pile–soil interaction modifies the **fixed-base period** of the structure.
   - Soft soil → **period lengthening** and **radiation damping** (energy radiated into the soil).
   - Two effects:
     - **Foundation Flexibility**: Effective period $T_{eff} > T_{fixed}$.
     - **Foundation Damping**: Reduces spectral demands.

**Sketch elements to draw:**
- A jacket with three elevations showing $F_I$ arrows.
- A submerged leg with $m_a$ indicated.
- A pile–soil system showing $k_h$ and $c_h$ springs/dashpots.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A fixed offshore platform in a seismically active region has a fundamental period of **2.2 s**. The design 5%-damped response spectrum for the site is:
- For $T < 0.3$ s: $S_a = 0.6g$ (constant acceleration plateau)
- For $0.3 \le T \le 1.0$ s: $S_a$ decreases linearly from $0.6g$ to $0.3g$
- For $T > 1.0$ s: $S_a = 0.3g \cdot (1.0/T)$ (constant velocity branch)

The total effective seismic weight of the platform is **$W = 95{,}000$ kN**, and the platform is modelled as an SDOF system with **5% damping**. Compute:
(i) the spectral acceleration at $T_1 = 2.2$ s,
(ii) the effective base shear,
(iii) the maximum lateral displacement.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a clearly labelled sketch, explain the concept of a **response spectrum**. Differentiate between a **design response spectrum** and a **site-specific response spectrum**. Why are offshore platforms in deep water often less affected by earthquakes than onshore structures of similar mass?

---

### Question B — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Identify spectral region for $T_1 = 2.2$ s:**

Since $T_1 = 2.2$ s > 1.0 s, we are in the **constant velocity branch**:
$$S_a = 0.3g \cdot \frac{1.0}{T_1} = 0.3g \cdot \frac{1.0}{2.2} = 0.1364g$$

**Step 2 — Compute effective base shear:**

The effective modal mass for a fundamental-mode-dominated structure is approximately equal to the total mass (assuming normalized mode shape). Therefore:
$$V_b = m_{eff} \cdot S_a \approx \frac{W}{g} \cdot S_a = \frac{95{,}000}{9.81} \times 0.1364 \times 9.81$$
$$V_b = 95{,}000 \times 0.1364 = 12{,}958 \text{ kN} \approx 12{,}960 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Compute maximum lateral displacement:**

The maximum relative displacement is the spectral displacement:
$$S_d = \frac{S_a}{\omega_1^2} = \frac{S_a \cdot T_1^2}{4\pi^2}$$

$$S_d = \frac{0.1364 \times 9.81 \times (2.2)^2}{4 \pi^2} = \frac{0.1364 \times 9.81 \times 4.84}{39.478}$$

$$S_d = \frac{6.477}{39.478} = 0.1641 \text{ m} = 164.1 \text{ mm}$$

#### Part (b) — Model Answer Outline

**Response Spectrum — Definition:**
A response spectrum is a plot of the **maximum response** (acceleration, velocity, or displacement) of a family of **single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) oscillators** with varying natural periods $T_n$ (and a fixed damping ratio, typically 5%) when subjected to a specified ground motion.

**Sketch (ASCII representation):**

```
   S_a
    |  Plateau (constant acceleration)
0.6g|- - - - - ___________
    |           /            \
    |          /              \___  (T^-1 decay)
0.3g|---------/                    -- - - -
    |        /
    |       /
    |      /
    |     /
    |____/_________________________ T (s)
         0.3      1.0      2.2
```

**Design vs Site-Specific Spectrum:**

| Aspect | Design Spectrum | Site-Specific Spectrum |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Code-prescribed (smooth, normalized) | Actual recorded or simulated ground motion at site |
| **Use** | Preliminary design, simpler projects | Final design of critical / high-consequence structures |
| **Hazard** | Generalized seismic zone | Accounts for local fault, source, path, site effects |
| **Form** | Smooth curve | May have peaks/valleys reflecting dominant frequencies |

**Why deep-water offshore platforms are less affected:**
1. **Distant epicenters**: Most offshore platforms are 50–200 km from active faults.
2. **Water column attenuation**: Seismic waves traveling through water lose some high-frequency content.
3. **Lower mass participation**: Submerged mass has hydrodynamic damping that dissipates energy.
4. **Longer natural periods**: Deep-water jackets have $T_n > 1.5$ s, often in the descending branch of the spectrum where $S_a$ is lower.
5. **Soil amplification is reduced** at offshore soft-clay sites compared to onshore alluvial basins.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Earthquake Force Problems (Costing 2–3 marks each)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting hydrodynamic added mass**: Many students compute $V_b = m_{struct} \cdot S_a$ but **ignore the added water mass**. For a jacket with 60% submerged mass fraction, the added mass is comparable to the structural mass, doubling the effective inertia.
>
> 2. **Using peak ground acceleration (PGA) directly as $S_a$**: PGA is the spectral acceleration at $T = 0$ only. For a flexible jacket ($T_1 > 1$ s), the design $S_a$ is significantly lower than PGA. Always use the **spectrum value at $T_n$**, not PGA.
>
> 3. **Wrong damping ratio**: Always use the **5% damped** spectrum for offshore steel structures unless otherwise specified. Using 2% or 10% gives different answers.
>
> 4. **No mention of directional combination**: Forgetting to specify **100/30** or **SRSS** rule when combining H1, H2, V components.
>
> 5. **Units inconsistency**: Mixing $g$ and $m/s^2$ within the same calculation. Be consistent — either keep everything in N/Pa/m or convert g-fractions explicitly.
>
> 6. **Confusing SLE and DLE return periods**: SLE ≈ 200 years, DLE ≈ 1000+ years. Wrong periods = wrong $S_a$ = wrong design.
>
> 7. **Ignoring vertical seismic component**: For piles and braced frames, the **vertical PGA** can be 50% of horizontal, causing significant additional axial loads in legs.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Earthquake Forces on Offshore Structures**
>
> - [x] Earthquake forces are **inertial** in nature — they arise from mass × acceleration.
> - [x] Three orthogonal ground motion components: **H1, H2 (horizontal) and V (vertical)**.
> - [x] Fundamental equation: $m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + ku = -m\ddot{u}_g(t)$.
> - [x] **Spectral base shear**: $V_b = m_{eff} \cdot S_a(T_n, \xi)$.
> - [x] **Code base shear**: $V = C_s \cdot W$ with $C_s = I S_a / (R g)$.
> - [x] **API recommended added mass coefficient** for seismic: **$C_m = 2.0$** (inviscid potential flow).
> - [x] **Added mass per unit length**: $m_a = C_M \rho_w \pi D^2/4 = \rho_w \pi D^2/4$ (for $C_M = 1.0$).
> - [x] **Pseudo-velocity relation**: $S_a = \omega_n S_v = (2\pi/T_n) S_v$.
> - [x] **Pseudo-displacement relation**: $S_d = S_a/\omega_n^2 = T_n S_v / (2\pi)$.
> - [x] **Directional combination** (API): 100% H1 + 30% H2 + 50% V.
> - [x] **Modal combination** (typical): **SRSS** (Square Root of Sum of Squares).
> - [x] **SLE** = Strength Level Earthquake (return period **~200–500 years**, elastic response).
> - [x] **DLE** = Ductility Level Earthquake (return period **~1000–2500 years**, limited inelastic).
> - [x] **Soil–Pile–Structure Interaction (SPSI)** causes period lengthening and foundation damping.
> - [x] **Importance factor** $I$ ranges from **1.0 to 1.5** based on post-event functionality needs.
> - [x] **Response modification factor** $R$ for steel jackets is typically **2.0–3.5**.
> - [x] **Effective weight** $W$ includes dead load + 25% live load + hydrodynamic added mass contributions.
> - [x] **Hydrodynamic damping** is automatically accounted for by radiation damping and viscous drag.
> - [x] **Tripartite (Newmark–Hall) plot** allows simultaneous reading of $S_a$, $S_v$, $S_d$ on a single log-log chart with triple axes.
> - [x] For deep-water jackets, **buoyancy** of submerged members reduces effective vertical weight but does **not** reduce the seismic base shear (since $V_b \propto m$, not $W$).
> - [x] **Vertical seismic component** is critical for pile tension/compression design and for deck-leg connection shear.
> - [x] Modern API/ISO codes require **capacity design** for DLE — designate specific plastic hinge regions with adequate ductility ($\mu \ge 4$ for steel).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ice Forces and Tides
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ice Forces and Tides

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ice Forces — Formal Definition

In the context of offshore engineering, **Ice Forces** refer to the mechanical loads exerted by floating, drifting, or grounded ice features (ice sheets, floes, icebergs, and rubble fields) on fixed or floating offshore structures such as monopods, jacket platforms, gravity bases, and subsea templates operating in cold-region seas (e.g., the Beaufort Sea, North Sea, Bohai Bay, Barents Sea, and the Caspian Sea).

Ice loading is a **limit-state design driver** in arctic and sub-arctic offshore engineering because the strength, geometry, and motion of ice are highly stochastic. Ice-structure interaction is governed by four primary failure modes:

1. **Crushing** (continuous brittle/plastic failure against vertical faces).
2. **Flexural failure** (bending of a floating ice sheet over a sloping structure).
3. **Buckling** (out-of-plane instability of a confined ice sheet).
4. **Ridge-loading / Indentation** (multi-year ice or rubble pile contact).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course PCNST602 expects students to (a) classify ice regimes, (b) compute global horizontal ice loads on fixed structures, and (c) recognize the conditions under which each empirical formula is valid.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Ice as a Slow-Motion Vehicle Collision

Imagine pushing a wall slowly with the front bumper of a truck that never stops. The truck represents an ice floe, the wall represents the offshore platform, and the bumper represents the **ice "contact zone."** Because ice is brittle and viscoelastic, it does **not** behave like steel — instead of yielding smoothly, it splinters, fractures, and ejects chunks in a cyclic process called **"intermittent crushing."** The force you feel on the wall pulses rhythmically — this is exactly how ice sheets "breathe" against a cylindrical platform leg in the Arctic.

The two key knobs in this analogy are:

- **Contact Area** (driven by ice thickness $h$ and structure diameter $D$).
- **Ice Strength** (driven by brine volume, temperature, salinity, strain rate — collectively termed the **Ice Mechanics Triangle**).

## 1.3 Tides — Formal Definition

**Tides** are the periodic rise and fall of sea surface elevation caused primarily by the gravitational attraction of the Moon and Sun on the Earth's oceans, modulated by the Earth's rotation and the geometry of coastlines and seabeds. The accompanying horizontal water movement is the **tidal current**.

The principal astronomical tide-generating forces are:

$$
F_{\text{tidal}} \propto \frac{G \cdot M}{d^3}
$$

where $M$ is the disturbing body's mass and $d$ is its center-to-center distance from Earth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard):** Tides are *long-period waves* with periods inherited from the relative motions of the Earth-Moon-Sun system. They are classified by period: **diurnal** (~24 h 50 min), **semi-diurnal** (~12 h 25 min), and **mixed (mainly semi-diurnal / mainly diurnal)**.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — Tides as a Spinning Salad Bowl

Hold a salad bowl filled with water and walk slowly around it in a circle. As you move, the water sloshes toward the side you are walking on, then catches up, then sloshes the other way — exactly as oceans bulge toward the Moon. Add a second walker (the Sun) at a different distance and you get a more complex sloshing pattern with two frequencies superposed. **That is tidal harmonic analysis.**

The two high tides per day (semi-diurnal) come from one bulge facing the Moon and a counter-bulge on the opposite side. Spring tides occur when Sun and Moon align; neap tides occur at quadrature.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Density of sea ice:** $\rho_i \approx \mathbf{920 \ kg/m^3}$
- **Density of seawater:** $\rho_w \approx \mathbf{1025 \ kg/m^3}$
- **Flexural strength of sea ice:** $\sigma_f \approx \mathbf{0.5 \ to \ 2.0 \ MPa}$
- **Compressive crushing strength:** $\sigma_c \approx \mathbf{2 \ to \ 8 \ MPa}$ (depends strongly on strain rate and brine volume)
- **Effective ice pressure coefficient range:** $C = \mathbf{1.0 \ to \ 2.0 \ MPa}$ (empirical)
- **Young's modulus of sea ice:** $E_i \approx \mathbf{5 \ to \ 10 \ GPa}$
- **Mean Earth-Moon distance:** $d \approx \mathbf{3.844 \times 10^8 \ m}$
- **Standard tidal period (principal lunar semi-diurnal):** $M_2 \approx \mathbf{12.42 \ hours}$
- **Tidal acceleration due to Moon:** $\approx \mathbf{3.39 \times 10^{-6} \ g}$ (where $g = 9.81 \ m/s^2$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Ice Regimes Encountered by Offshore Structures

| Ice Regime | Typical Thickness | Failure Mode at Vertical Face | Driving Load Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level first-year ice (FYI)** | $0.3 \text{ to } 1.2 \ m$ | Crushing | Global horizontal load |
| **Deformed first-year ice (rubble)** | $1.0 \text{ to } 5.0 \ m$ | Mixed crushing + shear | Local indentation |
| **Multi-year ice (MYI)** | $2.0 \text{ to } 7.0 \ m+$ | Crushing (limited) | Global horizontal load |
| **Glacier ice / Iceberg** | Up to $50 \ m$ keel | Bending + buoyancy | Contact + kinetic |
| **Fast ice (shore-attached)** | $< 2.0 \ m$ | Flexure against slope | Local bending |

## 2.2 Empirical Ice Force Formulas — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.2.1 Korzhavin's Formula (Most-Asked in KTU Examinations)

**Origin:** Developed from Russian Arctic basin measurements (Korzhavin, 1962); widely used for vertical-faced structures.

**Logic (Why this works):** Korzhavin assumed ice fails in crushing across the full contact area, and introduced an *indentation factor* $I$ to correct for the ratio of structure width to ice sheet thickness.

$$
F_K = I \cdot f_c \cdot D \cdot h
$$

where:

- $F_K$ = horizontal ice load on a vertical structure (N),
- $I$ = **indentation factor** (function of $D/h$, ranging $1.0$ to $2.5$ for $D/h = 1$ to $10$),
- $f_c$ = uniaxial compressive strength of ice (Pa),
- $D$ = structure width at waterline (m),
- $h$ = ice thickness (m).

### 2.2.2 Afanasev's Formula

**Origin:** Russian practice for cylindrical and conical structures.

$$
F_A = m \cdot k \cdot D \cdot h \cdot \sigma_c
$$

where $m$ = shape factor (cylinder: $m \approx 1.0$), $k$ = contact factor ($0.4$ to $0.7$), $\sigma_c$ = ice compressive strength.

### 2.2.3 Schwarz & Hirayama Formula (Industry Standard)

**Origin:** Consolidated from API / ISO 19906 Arctic Offshore Structures.

$$
F_{SH} = C \cdot D \cdot h
$$

where $C$ = **effective ice pressure coefficient**, taken as $\mathbf{1.8 \ to \ 2.0 \ MPa}$ for Arctic MYI, and $\mathbf{1.4 \ to \ 1.8 \ MPa}$ for FYI.

### 2.2.4 Croasdale's Formula (Multi-Year Ice, Limit-State)

$$
F_C = F_R \cdot A_R + F_L \cdot A_L
$$

combines **ridge sail** $A_R$ and **ridge keel** $A_L$ load contributions separately.

### 2.2.5 Ralston's Formula for Sloping Structures (Flexural Failure)

When the structure face has a slope $\alpha$ from vertical and the ice fails in **flexure**:

$$
F_{\text{Ralston}} = \left[ \frac{\rho_w \cdot g \cdot h^2}}{2 \cdot \sigma_f \cdot k_1} \right]^{1/3} \cdot \sigma_f \cdot h^2 \cdot \tan\alpha
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The oil platforms **Molikpaq (Caisson)**, **Hibernia (GBS)**, and **Sakhalin II Lunskoye-A (Jacket)** were each designed using a *combination* of Korzhavin + Schwarz-Hirayama + Ralston limits, with the design value taken as the upper-bound envelope.

## 2.3 High-Yield KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** All numerical constants must accompany correct SI units. KTU examiners deduct up to 1 mark per missing or wrong unit.

| # | Formula | Application | Key Parameter Range | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_K = I \cdot f_c \cdot D \cdot h$ | Vertical face crushing | $I \in [1.0, 2.5]$ | N |
| 2 | $F_A = m \cdot k \cdot D \cdot h \cdot \sigma_c$ | Cylindrical/conical | $k \in [0.4, 0.7]$ | N |
| 3 | $F_{SH} = C \cdot D \cdot h$ | API / ISO Arctic | $C \in [1.4, 2.0]$ MPa | N |
| 4 | $F_{\text{Ralston}} = \left(\frac{\rho_w g h^2}{2 \sigma_f k_1}\right)^{1/3} \sigma_f h^2 \tan\alpha$ | Conical, sloping | $\sigma_f \in [0.5, 2.0]$ MPa | N |
| 5 | $\sigma_c = \sigma_0 \cdot \left(\frac{\dot{\epsilon}}{\dot{\epsilon}_0}\right)^{1/n}$ | Strain-rate dependence | $n \approx 3$ (ductile) | Pa |
| 6 | $F_{\text{EQ tide}} = G \cdot M_m / d_m^3$ | Tidal acceleration | $M_m = 7.35 \times 10^{22}$ kg | N/kg |
| 7 | $\eta(t) = \sum H_n \cos(\sigma_n t - g_n)$ | Harmonic tide prediction | $H_n$ = amplitude | m |
| 8 | $V_{\text{tidal}} = (1/h) \cdot \int \eta \, dx$ | Tidal current from slope | — | m/s |
| 9 | $g_{\text{tidal Moon}} = G M_m / d_m^3$ | Tidal gravity (Moon) | $\approx 3.39 \times 10^{-6} g$ | m/s² |
| 10 | Spring range $= 2(H_{M_2} + H_{S_2})$ | Spring tide range | — | m |

## 2.4 Theory of Tides — Tidal Potential

The **tidal potential** at a point P on Earth's surface due to the Moon (or Sun) is:

$$
\Omega_{\text{tidal}} = -\frac{G M_m}{d} \sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \left(\frac{r}{d}\right)^n P_n(\cos\theta)
$$

For the dominant $n = 2$ term and using $\cos\theta = \sin\phi \cos\delta + \cos\phi \sin\delta \cos H$ (latitude $\phi$, declination $\delta$, hour angle $H$):

$$
\Omega_2 = -\frac{G M_m r^2}{2 d^3} \left( 3 \sin^2\phi \cos^2\delta - 1 \right)
$$

The horizontal (tractive) component gives rise to tidal currents; the vertical component gives rise to the equilibrium tidal bulge.

### Tidal Harmonic Constituents (KTU High-Yield)

| Symbol | Period (hours) | Source |
|---|---|---|
| $M_2$ | **12.42** | Principal lunar semi-diurnal |
| $S_2$ | **12.00** | Principal solar semi-diurnal |
| $N_2$ | 12.66 | Lunar elliptic |
| $K_1$ | 23.93 | Lunisolar declinational |
| $O_1$ | 25.82 | Lunar declinational |
| $P_1$ | 24.07 | Solar declinational |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of Tides in Offshore Design:** Tidal elevation sets the **air gap** requirement for fixed platforms; tidal currents drive **scour** around foundations and contribute to **fatigue loading** on mooring lines. A platform designed without accurate tidal harmonics may either have insufficient air gap (wave slamming on deck) or excessive deck elevation (wasted steel).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equilibrium Tidal Bulge Height

**Goal:** Derive the equilibrium tide elevation $\eta$ as a function of latitude $\phi$ and lunar declination $\delta$.

### Step 1: Set up the gravitational potential

The disturbing potential at Earth's surface due to the Moon is the difference between the Moon's gravitational potential and the centrifugal potential due to the Earth-Moon barycenter rotation.

$$
\Omega_d = -\frac{G M_m}{d} \left[ 1 - \frac{r}{d} P_1(\cos\theta) + \frac{r^2}{d^2} P_2(\cos\theta) - \dots \right] - \tfrac{1}{2} \omega^2 r^2 \sin^2\theta
$$

### Step 2: Linearize and retain only $n = 2$ terms

The $n = 1$ term is exactly cancelled by the centrifugal term (this is the reason the Earth-Moon barycenter is in free-fall equilibrium). We are left with:

$$
\Omega_2 = -\frac{G M_m r^2}{2 d^3} \left( 3 \cos^2\theta - 1 \right)
$$

### Step 3: Expand $\cos\theta$ in latitude/declination

Using the astronomical relation $\cos\theta = \sin\phi \cos\delta + \cos\phi \sin\delta \cos H$:

$$
\Omega_2 = -\frac{G M_m r^2}{2 d^3} \left[ 3(\sin\phi \cos\delta + \cos\phi \sin\delta \cos H)^2 - 1 \right]
$$

### Step 4: Equate potential to sea-surface height

At equilibrium, the sea surface is an equipotential of $\Omega_2 + g\eta$. Therefore:

$$
\eta = -\frac{\Omega_2}{g}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{G M_m r^2}{2 g d^3} \left( 3 \cos^2\theta - 1 \right)
$$

### Step 5: Substitute numerical constants

Using $G = 6.674 \times 10^{-11} \ N \cdot m^2/kg^2$, $M_m = 7.35 \times 10^{22} \ kg$, $r = 6.371 \times 10^6 \ m$, $d = 3.844 \times 10^8 \ m$, $g = 9.81 \ m/s^2$:

$$
\eta_{\max} = \frac{(6.674 \times 10^{-11})(7.35 \times 10^{22})(6.371 \times 10^6)^2}{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (3.844 \times 10^8)^3}
$$

$$
\eta_{\max} = \frac{(6.674 \times 10^{-11})(7.35 \times 10^{22})(4.06 \times 10^{13})}{(1.135 \times 10^{26})}
$$

$$
\eta_{\max} = \frac{1.99 \times 10^{26}}{1.135 \times 10^{26}} \approx 0.54 \ m
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **equilibrium lunar tide** has a maximum amplitude of $\mathbf{\approx 0.54 \ m}$. The observed ocean tide is amplified by coastal resonance and bathymetry (e.g., Bay of Fundy reaches $\mathbf{16 \ m}$).

## 3.2 Derivation: Tidal Harmonic Prediction Formula

**Goal:** Express tidal elevation as a finite sum of sinusoids (harmonic constituents).

### Step 1: Start with the tidal record

A long tide-gauge record $\eta(t)$ of duration $T$ is recorded.

### Step 2: Define constituent angular speed

For each constituent (e.g., $M_2$, $S_2$, $K_1$), the angular speed is $\sigma_n = 2\pi / T_n$.

### Step 3: Least-squares fit

Fit $\eta(t)$ to:

$$
\eta(t) = \eta_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{N} \left[ H_n \cos(\sigma_n t - g_n) \right]
$$

### Step 4: Solve by minimization

Differentiate the sum of squared residuals with respect to $H_n$ and $g_n$:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial H_n} \sum_i \left[\eta_i - H_n \cos(\sigma_n t_i - g_n) \right]^2 = 0
$$

Yielding:

$$
H_n = \sqrt{a_n^2 + b_n^2}, \quad g_n = \arctan\!\left(\frac{b_n}{a_n}\right)
$$

where:

$$
a_n = \frac{2}{T} \int_0^T \eta(t) \cos(\sigma_n t)\, dt, \quad b_n = \frac{2}{T} \int_0^T \eta(t) \sin(\sigma_n t)\, dt
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Korzhavin's Indentation Factor $I$

**Goal:** Show how $I = I(D/h)$ arises from a plastic-limit wedge analysis.

### Step 1: Assume ice sheet width $W \gg D$

For an infinitely wide ice sheet pushed against a rigid indenter of width $D$, ice fails by crushing in a triangular wedge ahead of the indenter.

### Step 2: Plastic limit analysis

The horizontal force required to push a wedge-shaped indentor of semi-angle $\beta$ through a perfectly plastic medium of strength $f_c$ is:

$$
F = f_c \cdot A_{\text{contact}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sin\beta}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute geometry

With contact area $A_{\text{contact}} = D \cdot h$ and a typical $\beta$ such that $1/\sin\beta \to I(D/h)$:

$$
F_K = I \cdot f_c \cdot D \cdot h
$$

where empirically:

$$
I = 1.0 + 0.35 \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{h}\right) \quad \text{for} \quad 1 \le \frac{D}{h} \le 20
$$

### Step 4: Worked numerical check

For $D = 6 \ m$, $h = 1.5 \ m$:

$$
I = 1.0 + 0.35 \ln(4) = 1.0 + 0.486 = 1.486
$$

$$
F_K = 1.486 \cdot (5 \times 10^6 \ Pa) \cdot 6 \cdot 1.5 = 66.9 \ MN
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sample Numerical Ice-Force Computation

A cylindrical caisson of diameter $D = 8 \ m$ operates in $h = 2.0 \ m$ of level first-year ice with $f_c = 3.0 \ MPa$. Compute global horizontal ice load by **three methods**.

### Step 1: Korzhavin

$$
I = 1.0 + 0.35 \ln(8/2) = 1.0 + 0.35 \ln 4 = 1.486
$$

$$
F_K = 1.486 \cdot (3.0 \times 10^6) \cdot 8 \cdot 2.0 = 71.3 \ MN
$$

### Step 2: Schwarz-Hirayama (FYI)

$$
F_{SH} = (1.4 \times 10^6) \cdot 8 \cdot 2.0 = 22.4 \ MN
$$

### Step 3: Afanasev

With $m = 1.0$, $k = 0.6$, $\sigma_c = 3.0 \ MPa$:

$$
F_A = 1.0 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 8 \cdot 2.0 \cdot (3.0 \times 10^6) = 28.8 \ MN
$$

### Step 4: Design value (envelope)

KTU standard: design value = **maximum envelope** = $71.3 \ MN$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Trap:** Many students pick the **average** of the three. This is wrong. The KTU design philosophy uses the upper-bound envelope, not the mean.

## 3.5 Derivation: Tidal Current Velocity from Tidal Slope

### Step 1: Continuity equation

$$
\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (h \cdot V)}{\partial x} = 0
$$

### Step 2: For constant depth $h$ and long-wave approximation

$$
V(x,t) = -\frac{g}{\sigma} \cdot \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial x}
$$

### Step 3: For a single harmonic $\eta = H \cos(\sigma t - kx)$

$$
V = \frac{g k H}{\sigma} \sin(\sigma t - kx)
$$

$$
V_{\max} = \frac{H \sqrt{g/h}}{1} = H \sqrt{\frac{g}{h}}
$$

For a $1.5 \ m$ tide in $h = 10 \ m$ depth:

$$
V_{\max} = 1.5 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{10}} = 1.5 \cdot 0.99 = 1.49 \ m/s
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define ice crushing strength and list two factors that influence it.**

**Model Answer:**

Ice crushing strength $\sigma_c$ is the uniaxial compressive stress at which a confined ice specimen fails when loaded perpendicular to its growth direction. For first-year sea ice it typically lies in the range $\mathbf{2 \ to \ 8 \ MPa}$.

Two influencing factors:

1. **Strain rate** ($\dot{\epsilon}$): at low strain rates ($\dot{\epsilon} < 10^{-3} \ s^{-1}$) ice behaves ductilely; at higher rates ($\dot{\epsilon} > 10^{-2} \ s^{-1}$) it behaves brittley.
2. **Brine volume** ($v_b$): higher salinity and warmer temperatures increase $v_b$, lowering $\sigma_c$.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**What is the difference between spring tides and neap tides?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Spring tides:** occur at **syzygy** (new moon / full moon) when the Sun, Earth, and Moon are aligned; lunar and solar tidal bulges coincide, producing the **maximum tidal range** ($2(H_{M_2} + H_{S_2})$).
- **Neap tides:** occur at **quadrature** (first/last quarter); the lunar and solar bulges are perpendicular and partially cancel, giving the **minimum tidal range** ($2(H_{M_2} - H_{S_2})$).

The cycle repeats every **14.77 days** (half of the synodic month).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 2 Internal Choice A]`

**(a)** Derive the equilibrium tidal height $\eta$ as a function of latitude $\phi$ and declination $\delta$, showing how the equilibrium amplitude of the lunar semi-diurnal tide ($\mathbf{M_2}$) can be computed. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A four-legged jacket platform in the Bohai Sea is subjected to $\mathbf{1.0 \ m}$ of level first-year ice. Each cylindrical leg has a diameter $\mathbf{D = 3.0 \ m}$ at the waterline. The uniaxial compressive strength of the ice is $\mathbf{f_c = 3.5 \ MPa}$. Determine the global horizontal ice load using (i) Korzhavin's formula, and (ii) the Schwarz-Hirayama formula. Take $C = \mathbf{1.5 \ MPa}$. Assume only one leg is loaded simultaneously. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

Start from the gravitational potential of the Moon at Earth's surface:

$$
\Omega = -\frac{G M_m}{d} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{r}{d}\right)^n P_n(\cos\theta)
$$

For the equilibrium tide, we retain only the $n = 2$ term after the centrifugal cancellation:

$$
\Omega_2 = -\frac{G M_m r^2}{2 d^3} \left( 3 \cos^2\theta - 1 \right)
$$

Using $\cos\theta = \sin\phi \cos\delta + \cos\phi \sin\delta \cos H$, the elevation is:

$$
\eta = -\frac{\Omega_2}{g} = \frac{G M_m r^2}{2 g d^3} \left( 3 \cos^2\theta - 1 \right)
$$

For the principal lunar semi-diurnal constituent $M_2$, $\cos^2\theta$ cycles twice per lunar day, giving the well-known $\mathbf{\approx 0.54 \ m}$ equilibrium amplitude.

**Part (b):**

**(i) Korzhavin:**

$$
I = 1.0 + 0.35 \ln(3.0/1.0) = 1.0 + 0.35 \times 1.0986 = 1.3845
$$

$$
F_K = I \cdot f_c \cdot D \cdot h = 1.3845 \cdot (3.5 \times 10^6) \cdot 3.0 \cdot 1.0
$$

$$
F_K = 14.54 \times 10^6 \ N = \mathbf{14.54 \ MN}
$$

**(ii) Schwarz-Hirayama:**

$$
F_{SH} = C \cdot D \cdot h = (1.5 \times 10^6) \cdot 3.0 \cdot 1.0 = 4.5 \times 10^6 \ N = \mathbf{4.5 \ MN}
$$

**Design recommendation:** $F_{\text{design}} = \max(F_K, F_{SH}) = \mathbf{14.54 \ MN}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly **forget to take $D/h$ inside $\ln$** when computing the indentation factor. They also use $f_c$ in MPa directly without converting to Pa. KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for unit mismatch and **1 mark** for wrong indentation factor.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 2 Internal Choice B]`

**(a)** Explain the four principal failure modes of ice against an offshore structure. For each mode, identify the empirical formula most appropriate to compute the resulting load. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** At a coastal site, the tidal record is dominated by four harmonic constituents: $\mathbf{M_2 (H = 0.80 \ m)}$, $\mathbf{S_2 (H = 0.30 \ m)}$, $\mathbf{K_1 (H = 0.25 \ m)}$, and $\mathbf{O_1 (H = 0.15 \ m)}$. Compute (i) the **spring tidal range**, (ii) the **neap tidal range**, and (iii) the **ratio $K_1 / (M_2 + O_1)$** which classifies the tide as semi-diurnal, mixed, or diurnal. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

| Failure Mode | Mechanism | Best-Suited Formula |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Crushing** | Brittle/plastic failure against vertical face | Korzhavin / Schwarz-Hirayama |
| 2. **Flexure** | Bending of ice sheet over conical face | Ralston |
| 3. **Buckling** | Out-of-plane instability for $D \gg h$ | Schwarz (limit-state) |
| 4. **Ridge loading** | Multi-year ridge contacts leg | Croasdale |

**Part (b):**

**(i) Spring tidal range:**

$$
R_{\text{spring}} = 2(H_{M_2} + H_{S_2}) = 2(0.80 + 0.30) = \mathbf{2.20 \ m}
$$

**(ii) Neap tidal range:**

$$
R_{\text{neap}} = 2(H_{M_2} - H_{S_2}) = 2(0.80 - 0.30) = \mathbf{1.00 \ m}
$$

**(iii) Form factor:**

$$
F = \frac{H_{K_1} + H_{O_1}}{H_{M_2} + H_{S_2}} = \frac{0.25 + 0.15}{0.80 + 0.30} = \frac{0.40}{1.10} = 0.364
$$

Since $0.25 < F < 1.5$, the tide is classified as **mixed, mainly semi-diurnal**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse the spring range formula. It is $\mathbf{2(H_{M_2} + H_{S_2})}$, not just $H_{M_2} + H_{S_2}$ (which would be the amplitude, not the range). Examiners check this distinction carefully.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Ice forces** arise from crushing, flexure, buckling, or ridge-contact on offshore legs.
- [x] **Korzhavin** formula: $F = I f_c D h$ — most-tested in KTU exams for vertical faces.
- [x] **Schwarz-Hirayama** formula: $F = C D h$ — API/ISO Arctic design standard.
- [x] **Ralston** formula: applies to conical/sloping faces where ice bends rather than crushes.
- [x] **Effective ice pressure coefficient $C$** ranges $\mathbf{1.4}$ to $\mathbf{2.0 \ MPa}$.
- [x] **Indentation factor $I$** depends on $D/h$ via $I = 1.0 + 0.35 \ln(D/h)$.
- [x] **Tidal potential** is governed by the $\mathbf{n = 2}$ gravitational harmonic.
- [x] **Equilibrium lunar tide amplitude** is $\mathbf{\approx 0.54 \ m}$.
- [x] **Spring tides** occur at syzygy; **neap tides** at quadrature (cycle = 14.77 days).
- [x] **Major harmonic constituents**: $M_2$ (12.42 h), $S_2$ (12.00 h), $K_1$ (23.93 h), $O_1$ (25.82 h).
- [x] **Tidal form factor** $F = (H_{K_1} + H_{O_1}) / (H_{M_2} + H_{S_2})$ classifies tides as semi-diurnal / mixed / diurnal.
- [x] **Design ice force** = upper-bound envelope of all applicable formulas.
- [x] **Design tidal elevation** governs **air gap**; **design tidal current** drives **scour & fatigue**.
- [x] Always convert $\mathbf{MPa \ to \ Pa}$ ($\times 10^6$) before computing force in Newtons.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marine Growth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Marine Growth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine Growth** is defined as the accumulation and colonization of marine biological organisms (both plant and animal species) on submerged portions of offshore structures, pipelines, risers, ship hulls, and other hydrodynamically active surfaces exposed to seawater over prolonged operational periods.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **API RP 2A (Planning, Designing, and Constructing Fixed Offshore Platforms)** and **ISO 19902**, marine growth is treated as a **time-dependent environmental load modifier** that simultaneously alters:

- **Mass loading** (gravitational/dead load increment)
- **Hydrodynamic loading** (drag, inertia, and slam coefficients)
- **Surface roughness** (boundary layer turbulence modification)

The marine growth zone is typically divided into **three distinct vertical regions** from the seabed upward:

| Zone | Vertical Extent (Reference) | Biological Character |
|------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| Splash Zone | MSL + 1.5 m to MSL − 1.5 m | Light algae, limited fauna |
| Intertidal Zone | MSL − 1.5 m to LAT | Heavy barnacle, mussel colonization |
| Continuous Immersion Zone | LAT to Seabed | Algal mats, hydroids, sponges, tubeworms |

> [!NOTE]
> **LAT (Lowest Astronomical Tide)** and **MSL (Mean Sea Level)** are the standard KTU-approved datum references for defining the marine growth thickness profile.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine leaving your bicycle parked near a coastal pier for six months without use. When you finally return, the wheel rims, spokes, and frame are encrusted with a thick, rough, dark-green-and-white biological crust — slimy to touch, hard to scrape off, and visibly heavier. The pedals turn sluggishly because the once-smooth metal is now "fuzzy" with barnacles and algae.

This is **exactly what happens to an offshore platform jacket**, except magnified across thousands of square meters of steel members submerged for **decades**. The marine growth:

1. **Adds weight** like a thick wet blanket wrapped around every tubular member.
2. **Roughens the surface**, making water flow turbulent, dramatically increasing drag forces.
3. **Clogs structural cavities**, traps debris, and accelerates localized corrosion.

In essence, marine growth turns a **clean hydrodynamic cylinder** into a **biologically-roughened, heavier cylinder** that experiences much larger wave and current forces.

## 1.3 Standard Marine Growth Thickness Values (API/DnV Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following values are **conservative design defaults** adopted by **API RP 2A-WSD** for the **Gulf of Mexico (GoM)** conditions. KTU examiners frequently quote these in numerical problems.

| Water Depth Zone (from MSL) | Recommended Thickness $t_m$ |
|-----------------------------|------------------------------|
| From MSL to +LAT (above LAT) | **Negligible** (splash zone, treated as $\approx 0$) |
| From MSL to −150 m (or to Seabed if shallower) | **50 mm** (uniform) |
| Below 150 m | **Negligible / tapered to zero** |

> [!NOTE]
> Indian offshore conditions (Mumbai High, KG Basin, Gujarat offshore) typically mandate higher localized thickness — sometimes **75 mm to 100 mm** in shallow tropical waters where biological productivity is exceptionally high.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Marine Growth Organisms

Marine growth is bi-classified based on kingdom and locomotion:

### A. Plant-like (Algae / Vegetative)
- **Green algae (Chlorophyta):** Common in upper photic layers (0–30 m).
- **Brown algae (Phaeophyta):** Kelp-type, in temperate waters.
- **Red algae (Rhodophyta):** Deeper encrusting forms.

### B. Animal-like (Fauna)
- **Barnacles** (*Balanus*, *Lepas*): Hard calcareous shells; dominant colonizers.
- **Mussels** (*Mytilus*): Byssal thread attachment.
- **Tubeworms** (*Serpula*): Calcareous tube builders.
- **Hydroids & Bryozoans:** Colonial soft organisms.

## 2.2 Factors Governing Marine Growth

The **rate and intensity** of marine growth is governed by:

- **Water temperature** (tropical waters favor faster growth).
- **Salinity** (brackish waters have reduced fouling).
- **Sunlight penetration** (algae limited to photic zone ≈ upper 30 m).
- **Nutrient availability** (upwelling zones = heavy fouling).
- **Water depth** (pressure & light attenuate colonization).
- **Substrate material** (steel, concrete, FRP — biological affinity varies).
- **Surface roughness of substrate** (rougher surfaces attract faster colonization).

## 2.3 Engineering Consequences of Marine Growth

| Effect | Physical Mechanism | Engineering Impact |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Added Mass / Weight** | Wet biomass attached to member | Increased dead load on jacket, topside piping |
| **Increased Hydrodynamic Diameter** | Effective diameter grows from $D \rightarrow D + 2t_m$ | Higher drag force $F_D \propto D_{eff}$ |
| **Surface Roughness** | Boundary layer transitions earlier to turbulent | Drag coefficient $C_D$ increases significantly |
| **Accelerated Corrosion** | Differential aeration cells under growth | Localized pitting, MICC (Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion) |
| **Clogging** | Members trap debris, sediments | Reduced internal flushing, fatigue hotspots |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrodynamic Diameter Modification:**
> $$D_{eff} = D + 2 t_m$$
> where $D$ is the bare steel diameter and $t_m$ is the marine growth thickness on each side.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---------------------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | $D_{eff} = D + 2t_m$ | Effective hydrodynamic diameter | m |
| 2 | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w C_D D_{eff} L \, u \vert u \vert$ | Morison in-line drag force per member | N |
| 3 | $F_I = C_M \rho_w \frac{\pi D_{eff}^2}{4} L \, \dot{u}$ | Morison inertia force per member | N |
| 4 | $W_{mg} = \rho_{mg} \cdot \pi \left[\left(\tfrac{D}{2}+t_m\right)^2 - \left(\tfrac{D}{2}\right)^2\right] \cdot L \cdot g$ | Added weight due to marine growth | N |
| 5 | $\rho_{mg} \approx 1300 \text{ to } 1500 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Typical wet marine growth density | kg/m³ |
| 6 | $C_D^{rough} \approx 1.05 \text{ to } 1.2$ (vs $C_D^{clean} \approx 0.6 \text{ to } 0.7$) | Drag coefficient for roughened cylinders | — |
| 7 | $t_m = 50 \text{ mm}$ (API, GoM, 0–150 m depth) | Conservative design thickness | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP:** When the drag term $u \vert u \vert$ is written, the absolute value bars `|...|` are essential because drag force always **opposes** the velocity vector. In LaTeX, **never** write `|u|` inside a markdown table — always use `\vert u \vert`.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Marine growth analysis is essential in:

- **In-service platform reassessment** (API RP 2A Section 17 — for aging platforms in the Indian Shelf like Mumbai High, where platforms are 30+ years old).
- **Decommissioning planning** — marine growth increases lifting weights of jacket sections.
- **Riser & J-tube design** — increased drag affects VIV (Vortex-Induced Vibration) response.
- **Pipeline stability design** — marine growth adds submerged weight, improving on-bottom stability but increasing drag.
- **FPSO hull performance** — speed loss and shaft power penalty due to hull fouling (biofouling).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Diameter with Marine Growth

**Statement:** A bare circular hollow steel brace of outer diameter $D$ is colonized by a uniform annular layer of marine growth of thickness $t_m$ on its outer surface. Derive the effective hydrodynamic diameter $D_{eff}$.

### Step 1 — Geometric Setup

Let the original steel outer radius be:

$$r_s = \frac{D}{2}$$

Let the marine growth outer radius be:

$$r_{eff} = r_s + t_m = \frac{D}{2} + t_m$$

### Step 2 — Effective Diameter

$$D_{eff} = 2 r_{eff} = 2\left(\frac{D}{2} + t_m\right)$$

$$\boxed{D_{eff} = D + 2t_m}$$

This is the diameter that the **flowing water actually "sees"** and is therefore the diameter to be used in all Morison equation calculations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Added Weight Due to Marine Growth

**Statement:** Derive the added wet weight $W_{mg}$ imparted by a marine growth annulus of thickness $t_m$ on a member of bare diameter $D$ and submerged length $L$.

### Step 1 — Cross-sectional Area of Marine Growth Annulus

$$A_{mg} = \pi \left[\left(\frac{D}{2} + t_m\right)^2 - \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2\right]$$

### Step 2 — Expansion of the Bracket

$$\left(\frac{D}{2} + t_m\right)^2 = \frac{D^2}{4} + D t_m + t_m^2$$

### Step 3 — Subtraction

$$A_{mg} = \pi \left[\frac{D^2}{4} + D t_m + t_m^2 - \frac{D^2}{4}\right]$$

$$A_{mg} = \pi \left[D t_m + t_m^2\right]$$

$$A_{mg} = \pi t_m \left(D + t_m\right)$$

### Step 4 — Volume of Marine Growth per Unit Member

$$V_{mg} = A_{mg} \cdot L = \pi t_m (D + t_m) \cdot L$$

### Step 5 — Mass and Wet Weight

$$m_{mg} = \rho_{mg} \cdot V_{mg}$$

$$W_{mg} = m_{mg} \cdot g = \rho_{mg} \cdot g \cdot \pi t_m (D + t_m) \cdot L$$

$$\boxed{W_{mg} = \rho_{mg} \cdot g \cdot \pi t_m (D + t_m) \cdot L}$$

### Step 6 — Substitution of Typical Indian Offshore Values

Take $D = 0.762 \text{ m}$ (30-inch brace), $t_m = 0.075 \text{ m}$ (75 mm for Indian tropical conditions), $L = 30 \text{ m}$, $\rho_{mg} = 1400 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

$$A_{mg} = \pi \cdot 0.075 \cdot (0.762 + 0.075)$$

$$A_{mg} = \pi \cdot 0.075 \cdot 0.837 = 0.1972 \text{ m}^2$$

$$V_{mg} = 0.1972 \cdot 30 = 5.917 \text{ m}^3$$

$$m_{mg} = 1400 \cdot 5.917 = 8283.8 \text{ kg}$$

$$W_{mg} = 8283.8 \cdot 9.81 = 81{,}264 \text{ N} \approx 81.26 \text{ kN}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For one single 30 m brace, marine growth contributes approximately **8 tonnes of additional dead load** — a non-trivial value that must be considered in jacket leg pile capacity checks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Percentage Increase in Drag Force

**Statement:** Show the percentage increase in Morison drag force for a cylindrical brace when marine growth is included, for a clean $C_D = 0.7$ and rough $C_D = 1.05$, with $D = 0.5 \text{ m}$, $t_m = 0.05 \text{ m}$.

### Step 1 — Effective Diameter

$$D_{eff} = 0.5 + 2(0.05) = 0.6 \text{ m}$$

### Step 2 — Ratio of Drag Forces

Since drag force is proportional to $C_D \cdot D_{eff}$ (with all other terms equal):

$$\frac{F_{D,rough}}{F_{D,clean}} = \frac{C_D^{rough} \cdot D_{eff}}{C_D^{clean} \cdot D}$$

$$= \frac{1.05 \cdot 0.6}{0.7 \cdot 0.5} = \frac{0.63}{0.35} = 1.80$$

### Step 3 — Percentage Increase

$$\Delta F_D \% = (1.80 - 1) \cdot 100\% = 80\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Even a modest **50 mm marine growth** on a 500 mm brace increases the **drag force by 80%**. This dramatic amplification is why KTU and API mandates mandatory inclusion of marine growth in hydrodynamic force calculations.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Conservative Design Thickness by Region

| Region | Latitude Indicator | $t_m$ Recommended | Justification |
|--------|--------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| Gulf of Mexico | Subtropical | 50 mm | API RP 2A default |
| North Sea | Cold temperate | 50–100 mm | High biomass at low temp |
| Mumbai High (Indian) | Tropical | 75–100 mm | High productivity, warm water |
| KG Basin (Indian) | Tropical | 75 mm | Similar to Mumbai High |
| Arctic | Polar | 25–50 mm | Reduced growth rate |
| Deep water (>150 m) | All | $\approx 0$ | Insufficient light/nutrients |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Marine Growth Zonation on a Jacket Platform

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Atmosphere - Above Splash Zone] --> B[Splash Zone - MSL plus or minus 1.5 m]
    B --> C[Intertidal Zone - MSL to LAT]
    C --> D[Continuous Immersion Zone - LAT to Seabed]
    D --> E[Seabed - Buried Zone]
    B --> F[Marine Growth Type - Light Algae - Negligible Thickness]
    C --> G[Marine Growth Type - Barnacles Mussels - Thickness 50 mm]
    D --> H[Marine Growth Type - Algal Mats Hydroids - Thickness 50 mm Tapers to 0]
    G --> I[Engineering Consequence - Increased Drag Force]
    G --> J[Engineering Consequence - Added Dead Weight]
    G --> K[Engineering Consequence - Localized Corrosion]
    H --> I
    H --> J
    H --> K
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Flow of Marine Growth Impact on Jacket Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bare Steel Member - Diameter D] --> B[Marine Colonization Process]
    B --> C[Effective Diameter - D plus 2tm]
    B --> D[Surface Roughness Modifier]
    C --> E[Morison Drag Force Calculation]
    D --> F[Drag Coefficient - CD increases]
    F --> E
    E --> G[Increased In-line Wave Force]
    B --> H[Added Wet Weight on Member]
    H --> I[Jacket Leg Pile Load Increment]
    G --> J[In-service Reassessment API 2A Section 17]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Design Mitigation - Antifouling Coating]
    J --> L[Design Mitigation - Cathodic Protection]
    J --> M[Design Mitigation - Mechanical Cleaning Schedule]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cross-Section of Marine Growth on Tubular Brace

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Outer Steel Wall of Brace - Diameter D] --> B[Marine Growth Annulus - Thickness tm]
    B --> C[Effective Hydrodynamic Surface - Diameter Deff]
    A --> D[Inner Hollow Bore of Brace]
    A --> E[Barnacle Cluster Layer - Hard Calcareous]
    A --> F[Algal Mat Layer - Soft Vegetative]
    E --> G[Outer Wet Surface Boundary]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Seawater Flow Stream with Drag Force]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above block diagrams are intentionally designed as **functional architecture flows** (per the protocol's Mermaid fallback rule) to clearly map the cause-effect chain between biological colonization and the resulting engineering load paths on an offshore jacket platform.

## 4.4 Schematic Description of Marine Growth Thickness Profile

While a true scale drawing cannot be rendered in Mermaid, the following schematic relationship is provided verbally for student visualization:

- **X-axis:** Water depth from MSL (downward positive).
- **Y-axis:** Marine growth thickness $t_m$ in mm.
- **Profile shape:** Rectangular plateau of **50 mm** from MSL down to **−150 m**, then **linear taper to 0 mm** at the seabed (or at 250 m, whichever is shallower).
- **Annotations:** Mark LAT, MSL, the photic limit (~30 m), and the 150 m biological taper initiation point.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define marine growth. List any four marine organisms responsible for marine growth on offshore structures.**

**Model Answer:**

> Marine growth is the accumulation of marine biological organisms such as barnacles, mussels, algae, hydroids, tubeworms, and bryozoans on submerged offshore structural members, pipelines, and ship hulls over prolonged periods of exposure to seawater.

**Four marine organisms responsible:**

1. **Barnacles** (*Balanus* sp.) — hard calcareous shelled crustaceans; dominant colonizers.
2. **Mussels** (*Mytilus* sp.) — attach via byssal threads.
3. **Green algae** (*Chlorophyta*) — common in upper photic zone.
4. **Hydroids** — colonial soft-bodied organisms forming feathery colonies.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the API-recommended marine growth thickness values for design of fixed offshore platforms.**

**Model Answer:**

According to **API RP 2A-WSD**, the conservative design marine growth thickness values are:

1. From **MSL to +LAT (above Lowest Astronomical Tide):** **Negligible** (treated as zero, splash zone).
2. From **MSL down to −150 m** (or to seabed if water depth is less): **Uniform 50 mm**.
3. **Below −150 m:** **Tapers linearly to zero**.

For Indian offshore conditions (Mumbai High, KG Basin), **75 mm to 100 mm** is often adopted due to higher tropical biological productivity.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Choice A (14 Marks)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) Explain in detail the engineering effects of marine growth on fixed offshore steel jacket platforms. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Marine growth affects fixed offshore steel jacket platforms in the following key engineering ways:

1. **Increase in Dead Load (Added Mass / Weight):**
   The wet biomass of marine organisms (density ≈ 1300–1500 kg/m³) adheres as an annular layer around each tubular member. This adds substantial gravitational load to the jacket, especially in deeper piles and bracing.

2. **Increase in Hydrodynamic Diameter:**
   Effective diameter becomes $D_{eff} = D + 2 t_m$. This increases both the projected area and the wetted perimeter exposed to wave and current action.

3. **Increase in Drag Coefficient $C_D$:**
   Rough biological surfaces promote earlier transition of the boundary layer from laminar to turbulent. Clean cylinders may have $C_D \approx 0.6$–$0.7$; roughened cylinders exhibit $C_D \approx 1.05$–$1.2$.

4. **Increased Morison Drag Force:**
   Since $F_D \propto C_D \cdot D_{eff}$, even a 50 mm growth on a 500 mm brace can amplify drag by ~80%.

5. **Accelerated Localized Corrosion:**
   Differential aeration cells form under barnacle bases, leading to **Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MICC)**. Tubeworm and hydroid colonization creates pitting.

6. **Clogging of Members and J-Tubes:**
   Trapped debris and sediments restrict internal flushing, causing fatigue hotspots at restraint points.

7. **Impact on Vortex-Induced Vibration (VIV):**
   Marine growth alters the strouhal number and lift coefficient, potentially increasing VIV susceptibility of long slender braces and risers.

8. **Inspection & Maintenance Burden:**
   ROV (Remotely Operated Vehicle) cleaning campaigns and inspection costs escalate dramatically over the platform's 25–30 year service life.

---

**(b) A horizontal tubular brace in a jacket has a bare outer diameter of 0.6 m and length 25 m. If the API-recommended marine growth thickness is 50 mm, compute: (i) the effective hydrodynamic diameter, (ii) the added wet weight per brace, and (iii) the percentage increase in drag force assuming $C_D$ rises from 0.7 to 1.05. Take $\rho_{mg} = 1400$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s². (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**

$$D = 0.6 \text{ m}, \quad L = 25 \text{ m}, \quad t_m = 0.05 \text{ m}$$

$$\rho_{mg} = 1400 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$C_D^{clean} = 0.7, \quad C_D^{rough} = 1.05$$

### (i) Effective Hydrodynamic Diameter

$$D_{eff} = D + 2 t_m$$

$$D_{eff} = 0.6 + 2(0.05)$$

$$\boxed{D_{eff} = 0.7 \text{ m}}$$

### (ii) Added Wet Weight per Brace

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional Area of Marine Growth Annulus:**

$$A_{mg} = \pi t_m (D + t_m)$$

$$A_{mg} = \pi \cdot 0.05 \cdot (0.6 + 0.05)$$

$$A_{mg} = \pi \cdot 0.05 \cdot 0.65$$

$$A_{mg} = 0.1021 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Volume of Marine Growth:**

$$V_{mg} = A_{mg} \cdot L = 0.1021 \cdot 25 = 2.553 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Wet Weight:**

$$W_{mg} = \rho_{mg} \cdot V_{mg} \cdot g$$

$$W_{mg} = 1400 \cdot 2.553 \cdot 9.81$$

$$W_{mg} = 35{,}062 \text{ N} \approx 35.06 \text{ kN}$$

$$\boxed{W_{mg} \approx 35.06 \text{ kN per brace}}$$

### (iii) Percentage Increase in Drag Force

$$\frac{F_{D,rough}}{F_{D,clean}} = \frac{C_D^{rough} \cdot D_{eff}}{C_D^{clean} \cdot D}$$

$$= \frac{1.05 \cdot 0.7}{0.7 \cdot 0.6} = \frac{0.735}{0.42} = 1.75$$

$$\Delta F_D \% = (1.75 - 1) \cdot 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\Delta F_D = 75\% \text{ increase in drag force}}$$

---

## 5.3 Part B Question — Choice B (14 Marks)

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) Discuss the various factors that influence the rate and intensity of marine growth on offshore structures. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The rate and intensity of marine growth depend on the following environmental, biological, and substrate-related factors:

1. **Water Temperature:**
   Warmer tropical waters (25–30°C) accelerate biological metabolism and reproduction, producing heavy fouling. Cold polar waters suppress growth.

2. **Salinity:**
   Fully marine salinities (32–36 ppt) support maximum species diversity. Brackish or hypersaline waters limit colonization.

3. **Sunlight Penetration (Photic Zone):**
   Algal photosynthesis is restricted to the upper ~30–50 m. Below this, animal-dominated communities prevail.

4. **Nutrient Availability:**
   Upwelling zones (cold nutrient-rich water rising to surface) such as the Arabian Sea coast foster intense fouling.

5. **Water Depth:**
   Beyond ~150 m, light and nutrient flux decline sharply; marine growth becomes negligible.

6. **Substrate Material & Roughness:**
   Rougher steel surfaces (mill scale, rust pits) provide better anchoring for larval settlement than smooth, polished, or coated surfaces.

7. **Hydrodynamic Exposure:**
   High-velocity current zones experience scouring, which limits colonization; sheltered members (e.g., internal bracing) accumulate thicker growth.

8. **Operational Age of Structure:**
   Older platforms (20+ years) typically have thicker, well-established growth compared to newer ones.

9. **Proximity to Other Platforms:**
   Larval supply is enhanced near existing structures that act as biological reservoirs.

10. **Anti-fouling Coatings:**
    Properly maintained coatings (e.g., cuprous oxide, silicone-based foul-release systems) inhibit initial biofilm formation.

---

**(b) An offshore jacket platform in 120 m water depth is instrumented with API-recommended marine growth. A vertical tubular member of 1.0 m outer diameter and 25 m submerged length (from MSL to −25 m zone, where thick barnacle colonization occurs) has marine growth of 75 mm. Compute: (i) effective diameter, (ii) the additional vertical load imposed on the platform's pile cap by this single member, and (iii) if the platform has 8 such members, what is the total additional load? Use $\rho_{mg} = 1500$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s². (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**

$$D = 1.0 \text{ m}, \quad L = 25 \text{ m}, \quad t_m = 0.075 \text{ m}$$

$$\rho_{mg} = 1500 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$

### (i) Effective Diameter

$$D_{eff} = D + 2 t_m = 1.0 + 2(0.075)$$

$$\boxed{D_{eff} = 1.15 \text{ m}}$$

### (ii) Additional Vertical Load per Member

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional Area of Growth:**

$$A_{mg} = \pi t_m (D + t_m)$$

$$A_{mg} = \pi \cdot 0.075 \cdot (1.0 + 0.075)$$

$$A_{mg} = \pi \cdot 0.075 \cdot 1.075 = 0.2533 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Volume:**

$$V_{mg} = 0.2533 \cdot 25 = 6.333 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Mass:**

$$m_{mg} = 1500 \cdot 6.333 = 9499.5 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Vertical Load:**

$$W_{mg} = 9499.5 \cdot 9.81 = 93{,}190 \text{ N} \approx 93.19 \text{ kN}$$

$$\boxed{W_{mg} \approx 93.19 \text{ kN per member}}$$

### (iii) Total Additional Load for 8 Members

$$W_{total} = 8 \cdot 93.19 = 745.5 \text{ kN}$$

$$\boxed{W_{total} \approx 745.5 \text{ kN}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** ~745 kN is the **continuous dead load** added to the pile cap purely from marine growth on a representative group of 8 jacket members. Over the platform's 25-year service life, this must be re-verified during **API RP 2A Section 17 — In-Service Assessment**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $D + 2t_m$ (students frequently write $D + t_m$, losing 1 mark).
> 2. **Using dry density of steel** instead of marine growth density $\rho_{mg} \approx 1300$–$1500$ kg/m³ in weight calculations.
> 3. **Neglecting the $C_D$ jump** (clean → rough) in drag amplification problems — examiners explicitly test this.
> 4. **Conflating thickness with radius** — thickness $t_m$ is a linear measure on **each side** of the cylinder; do not add it directly to the radius in the wrong direction.
> 5. **Failing to convert mm to m** — KTU unit discipline is strict; mixing mm and m in the same calculation costs marks.
> 6. **Omitting the absolute value** in Morison drag $u \vert u \vert$ — physically meaningless otherwise.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Marine growth = biological fouling of submerged structural members (barnacles, mussels, algae, hydroids, tubeworms).
- **API RP 2A default thickness:** **50 mm** from MSL down to −150 m; negligible above LAT and below 150 m.
- **Indian offshore standard:** **75 mm to 100 mm** in tropical waters (Mumbai High, KG Basin).
- **Effective diameter formula:** $D_{eff} = D + 2 t_m$.
- **Drag force formula (Morison):** $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w C_D D_{eff} L \, u \vert u \vert$.
- **Inertia force (Morison):** $F_I = C_M \rho_w \tfrac{\pi D_{eff}^2}{4} L \, \dot{u}$.
- **Added wet weight:** $W_{mg} = \rho_{mg} \, g \, \pi t_m (D + t_m) L$.
- **Marine growth density:** $\rho_{mg} \approx 1300$–$1500$ kg/m³.
- **Drag coefficient jump:** Clean $C_D \approx 0.6$–$0.7$ → Rough $C_D \approx 1.05$–$1.2$.
- **Primary engineering effects:** added dead load, larger hydrodynamic diameter, higher drag force, accelerated localized corrosion, increased VIV risk, MICC, inspection burden.
- **Three biological zones:** Splash → Intertidal → Continuous Immersion (with biological taper below 150 m).
- **Mitigation methods:** antifouling coatings, cathodic protection (sacrificial Al/Zn anodes), mechanical cleaning by ROV divers, in-service reassessment per API 2A Section 17.
- **Reference codes:** API RP 2A-WSD, ISO 19902, DNV-OS-C101, NORSOK N-001.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Response of Offshore Structures to Ocean Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Response of Offshore Structures to Ocean Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Response of Offshore Structures to the Ocean Environment** refers to the dynamic and quasi-static behaviour (displacement, velocity, acceleration, internal stress, and base reaction) of a marine structure when subjected to the combined action of **environmental loads**: wind, ocean waves, current, tide, and (in seismically active zones) earthquakes. For KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2, the response is characterized within the framework of linear wave theory (Airy), Morison's equation for slender members, and the six degrees-of-freedom rigid-body equation of motion for floating systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"The response of an offshore structure is the time-dependent motion and internal force state of the structure, quantified by solving the equation of motion under wave, wind, current, and hydrostatic restoring actions, with hydrodynamic coefficients (added mass, radiation damping, and excitation) obtained from potential-flow and semi-empirical (Morison) formulations."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are standing inside a **flexible lighthouse** during a storm. The waves are not "hitting" the tower randomly — the tower is being pushed, twisted, and shaken in a very specific, periodic manner. The cylinder of the lighthouse "feels" the water rushing past it (a **drag force**, just like you feel wind resistance when you stick your hand out of a moving car window) and the back-and-forth acceleration of the water (an **inertia force**, like the jerk you feel when a bus suddenly starts or stops).

For a **fixed platform** (like the lighthouse, bolted to the seabed), the response is purely the **internal stress and deflection of the structure**. For a **floating vessel** (like a ship in a storm), the entire structure moves with **6 degrees of freedom** — surging, swaying, heaving, rolling, pitching, and yawing — like a cork bobbing in a bathtub but with six independent ways to wobble.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Real-World Metric — Seawater Density:**
> Standard seawater has a mass density of **ρ = 1025 kg/m³** (≈ 64 lb/ft³). This is the single most important physical constant in offshore hydrodynamics — almost every force equation is scaled by it.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Ocean-Environment Response

| Pillar | Physical Origin | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| **Wave Loading** | Orbital particle motion of water | Dominant for H > 2 m |
| **Wind Loading** | Aerodynamic drag on exposed decks/masts | Adds to overturning moment |
| **Current Loading** | Steady drag due to tidal/steady flow | Quasi-static contribution |

## 1.4 Classification of Response Regimes

1. **Quasi-Static Response** — Structure is stiff relative to wave period; treated as a static load varying with time.
2. **Dynamic Response** — Structure's natural period is close to wave period; resonance and inertia dominate.
3. **Diffraction-Dominated Response** — Large diameter members (D/λ > 0.2) scatter the incident wave.
4. **Vortex-Induced Vibration (VIV)** — Periodic vortex shedding locks-in with structural natural frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **The D/λ Criterion (Sarpkaya & Isaacson):**
> - If **D/λ < 0.2** → use **Morison Equation** (slender member theory).
> - If **D/λ > 0.2** → use **Diffraction Theory** (MacCamy-Fuchs).
> Here, **D** is member diameter and **λ** is the wavelength.
> KTU 2024 Module 2 emphasizes the **Morison formulation** (D/λ < 0.2).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Response Problem

The complete response of an offshore structure is governed by solving, for each mode of motion, the **second-order ordinary differential equation**:

$$\sum_{j} \left[ (m_{ij} + a_{ij}) \ddot{x}_j(t) + B_{ij} \dot{x}_j(t) + C_{ij} x_j(t) \right] = F_i(t)$$

where for the **i-th** degree of freedom:
- $m_{ij}$ = structural mass (or virtual mass) matrix
- $a_{ij}$ = **added (hydrodynamic) mass** coefficient — water "trapped" by the oscillating body
- $B_{ij}$ = **radiation damping** coefficient — energy radiated as waves
- $C_{ij}$ = **hydrostatic restoring** coefficient — buoyancy and gravity
- $F_i(t)$ = **wave excitation force** — the driving load

## 2.2 The Two-Force Morison Decomposition

For a vertical slender cylinder of diameter **D**, the in-line wave force per unit length is the **sum** of two orthogonal physical mechanisms:

**Component 1 — Drag Force (viscous, velocity-squared):**
$$f_D(z,t) = C_D \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \, D \, u(z,t) \vert u(z,t) \vert$$

**Component 2 — Inertia Force (potential, acceleration-linear):**
$$f_I(z,t) = C_M \cdot \rho \, \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \, \dot{u}(z,t)$$

**Total Morison Force (per unit length):**
$$f(z,t) = f_D(z,t) + f_I(z,t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two terms?**
> - **Drag** captures the *wake*, *form drag*, and *viscous shear* — proportional to the square of velocity.
> - **Inertia** captures the *added mass effect* (water being pushed) plus the *Froude-Krylov pressure* — proportional to acceleration.
> - Typical values: **C_D = 0.6 to 1.2** (smooth cylinder) and **C_M = 1.2 to 2.0**.

## 2.3 Airy Linear Wave Kinematics (Input to Morison)

The horizontal water particle velocity and acceleration at depth **z** below the SWL (with z = 0 at SWL, z = −d at seabed):

$$u(z,t) = \frac{\pi H}{T} \frac{\cosh[k(z+d)]}{\sinh(kd)} \cos(kx - \omega t)$$

$$\dot{u}(z,t) = \frac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \frac{\cosh[k(z+d)]}{\sinh(kd)} \sin(kx - \omega t)$$

with the **dispersion relation** linking wave number **k** to angular frequency **ω = 2π/T**:

$$\omega^2 = g k \tanh(kd)$$

## 2.4 The Six Degrees of Freedom of a Floating Body

| Index | Mode | Translational / Rotational | Restoring Force Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Surge** | Translation along +x | Mooring (no hydrostatic) |
| 2 | **Sway** | Translation along +y | Mooring (no hydrostatic) |
| 3 | **Heave** | Translation along +z (vertical) | Buoyancy $\rho g A_w z$ |
| 4 | **Roll** | Rotation about +x axis | $\rho g \nabla \overline{GMT} \, \phi$ |
| 5 | **Pitch** | Rotation about +y axis | $\rho g \nabla \overline{GML} \, \theta$ |
| 6 | **Yaw** | Rotation about +z axis | Mooring (no hydrostatic) |

## 2.5 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All key formulas are consolidated below. Master these for the 14-mark derivations.

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f = C_D \tfrac{1}{2}\rho D u \vert u \vert + C_M \rho \tfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \dot{u}$ | Morison force per unit length | N/m |
| 2 | $u = \tfrac{\pi H}{T} \tfrac{\cosh[k(z+d)]}{\sinh(kd)} \cos(kx - \omega t)$ | Airy horizontal velocity | m/s |
| 3 | $\dot{u} = \tfrac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \tfrac{\cosh[k(z+d)]}{\sinh(kd)} \sin(kx - \omega t)$ | Airy horizontal acceleration | m/s² |
| 4 | $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kd)$ | Linear dispersion relation | rad²/s² |
| 5 | $L = \tfrac{g T^2}{2\pi} \tanh(kd)$ | Wavelength | m |
| 6 | $(m + a_{33})\ddot{z} + B_{33}\dot{z} + \rho g A_w z = F_3(t)$ | Heave EOM | N |
| 7 | $\omega_{n,heave} = \sqrt{\tfrac{\rho g A_w}{m + a_{33}}}$ | Heave natural frequency | rad/s |
| 8 | $T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{\tfrac{m + a_{33}}{\rho g A_w}}$ | Heave natural period | s |
| 9 | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $RAO(\omega) = \dfrac{\zeta_a(\omega)}{\zeta_w(\omega)}$ | Response Amplitude Operator | dimensionless |
| 11 | $KC = \dfrac{u_{max} T}{D}$ | Keulegan-Carpenter number | dimensionless |
| 12 | $C_M = C_a + 1$ | Inertia coefficient decomposition | dimensionless |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Design of jacket platforms**: Morison forces on each tubular member are integrated to obtain base shear and overturning moment, which are then compared against API RP 2A allowable stresses.
- **Mooring system design**: RAOs in surge, sway, and yaw feed into slow-drift offset calculations, which set pretension and line length.
- **Fatigue analysis**: VIV lock-in ranges determine stress-cycle histories for tubular joints over the 25–30 year design life.
