---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between a fixed offshore platform and a compliant offshore platform with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Fixed Platform** | **Compliant Platform** |
|---|---|---|
| Restraint | Rigidly fixed to seabed via piles | Permits controlled lateral deflection |
| Water Depth | Shallow (≤ 400 m typically) | Medium-deep (300–1500 m) |
| Dynamic Response | Stiff, low natural period | Compliant, period > wave period |
| Mooring | Piles only | Tendons, guys, or articulated joint |
| Examples | Steel Jacket, GBS, Jack-up | TLP, Spar, Guyed Tower, ALP |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: What is an FPSO? List its key advantages.**

**Model Answer:**
**FPSO** stands for **Floating Production, Storage, and Offloading** — a ship-shaped or cylindrical floating vessel used for hydrocarbon processing and storage in deepwater fields.

**Key Advantages:**
1. Eliminates long-distance subsea pipelines to shore.
2. Weathervaning capability via turret mooring.
3. Large storage capacity (~1–2 million barrels).
4. Re-deployable between fields.
5. Suitable for ultra-deepwater (up to 3000+ m).
6. Faster installation compared to fixed platforms.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Q: (a)** Classify the basic configurations of offshore structures with neat sketches. Discuss the criteria for selecting a suitable configuration for a given water depth. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** A Tension Leg Platform (TLP) is to be installed in a water depth of 1100 m. The dry weight of the hull including variable load is $5.5 \times 10^9$ N. The displaced volume gives a buoyancy of $7.2 \times 10^9$ N. The platform is restrained by **12 tendons** (3 at each corner). Compute:
1. Pretension per tendon.
2. The hydrostatic pressure at tendon top connection point (assume tendon porch at 30 m below MSL).
**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Classification of Basic Offshore Configurations:**

1. **Fixed / Bottom-Supported:**
   - Steel Jacket Platform
   - Concrete Gravity Base Structure (GBS)
   - Jack-up Rig

2. **Compliant:**
   - Tension Leg Platform (TLP)
   - Spar Platform
   - Guyed Tower
   - Articulated Loading Platform (ALP)

3. **Floating:**
   - Semi-Submersible
   - FPSO
   - Drillship
   - Barge / Tender-Assisted Drilling

4. **Subsea:**
   - Manifolds, Christmas Trees, Subsea Pumping Stations

**Selection Criteria (Water Depth Driven):**

| **Water Depth Range (m)** | **Preferred Configuration** |
|---|---|
| 0 – 200 | Jack-up, Steel Jacket, GBS |
| 200 – 400 | Steel Jacket, Concrete GBS, Semi-submersible |
| 400 – 1500 | TLP, Spar, Semi-submersible |
| 1500 – 3000 | Spar, FPSO, Semi-submersible, Drillship |
| > 3000 | Drillship, FPSO, Subsea tieback |

Additional criteria: **metocean environment**, **payload**, **storage requirement**, **drilling motion tolerance**, **CAPEX/OPEX**, **soil conditions**, **operational life**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $W_{hull} = 5.5 \times 10^9 \, \text{N}$
- $F_B = 7.2 \times 10^9 \, \text{N}$
- $n = 12$ tendons
- Depth of tendon top: $d_t = 30$ m below MSL

**Step 1 — Total Tendon Pretension Force**

$$F_{tendon,total} = F_B - W_{hull}$$

$$F_{tendon,total} = 7.2 \times 10^9 - 5.5 \times 10^9 = 1.7 \times 10^9 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Pretension per Tendon**

$$T_p = \dfrac{F_{tendon,total}}{n} = \dfrac{1.7 \times 10^9}{12}$$

$$\boxed{T_p = 1.4167 \times 10^8 \, \text{N} \approx 141.67 \, \text{MN per tendon}}$$

**Step 3 — Hydrostatic Pressure at Tendon Top (30 m depth)**

$$P_h = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot d_t$$

$$P_h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 30$$

$$P_h = 10055.25 \times 30$$

$$\boxed{P_h = 301657.5 \, \text{Pa} \approx 301.66 \, \text{kPa} \approx 3.02 \, \text{bar}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the different types of floating offshore structures. Compare FPSO and Semi-Submersible on the basis of motion characteristics, storage capacity, and field applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** An offshore jacket platform is to be installed in **150 m water depth**. Compute:
1. Hydrostatic pressure at the seabed.
2. Total hydrostatic thrust on a vertical leg of diameter 3 m extending from the seabed to MSL (neglect pile sleeve effects).
**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Types of Floating Offshore Structures:**

1. **Semi-Submersible** — Buoyant columns + submerged pontoons; catenary or taut mooring.
2. **FPSO** — Ship-shaped hull with turret mooring; processing + storage.
3. **Drillship** — Ship-shaped with central moonpool; DP-stabilized.
4. **SPAR** — Deep-draft vertical cylinder; classical or truss spar.
5. **Barge / Tender** — Shallow-draft vessel for tender-assisted drilling.

**Comparison: FPSO vs Semi-Submersible:**

| **Parameter** | **FPSO** | **Semi-Submersible** |
|---|---|---|
| Hull Form | Ship-like | Box-like with columns |
| Motion | Larger (heave 2–5 m) | Lower (heave 0.5–1.5 m) |
| Storage | $10^6$ barrels onboard | None (typically) |
| Mooring | Turret (weathervaning) | Catenary spread |
| Drilling | Limited | Excellent |
| Production | Excellent | Excellent |
| Field Type | Marginal, remote, deep | Deepwater, harsh environment |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Water depth $d = 150$ m
- Leg diameter $D = 3$ m, vertical from seabed (z = 150 m) to MSL (z = 0)

**Step 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure at Seabed**

$$P_{seabed} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot d$$

$$P_{seabed} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 150$$

$$\boxed{P_{seabed} = 1{,}508{,}287.5 \, \text{Pa} \approx 1.51 \, \text{MPa} \approx 15.08 \, \text{bar}}$$

**Step 2 — Total Vertical Hydrostatic Thrust on Leg**

Pressure varies linearly with depth. The total vertical thrust on a vertical cylindrical surface equals the pressure at the centroid times the projected area.

Projected horizontal area of the cylindrical leg in the vertical plane (taking width as diameter $D$ and height as $d$):

$$A_{proj} = D \times d = 3 \times 150 = 450 \, \text{m}^2$$

Pressure at centroid (mid-depth, $z = 75$ m):

$$P_{centroid} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \dfrac{d}{2}$$

$$P_{centroid} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 75$$

$$P_{centroid} = 754{,}143.75 \, \text{Pa}$$

Total vertical thrust:

$$F_{thrust} = P_{centroid} \times A_{proj}$$

$$F_{thrust} = 754{,}143.75 \times 450$$

$$\boxed{F_{thrust} = 3.39 \times 10^8 \, \text{N} \approx 339.4 \, \text{MN}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing GBS with Steel Jacket** — GBS uses gravity (self-weight), Jacket uses piles.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract atmospheric pressure** when converting gauge vs. absolute.
> 3. **Unit errors in pretension** — students often forget to divide total vertical tendon load by the *number of tendons*.
> 4. **Wrong centroid assumption** — hydrostatic thrust on a vertical surface uses centroid pressure × projected area.
> 5. **Confusing water depth with tendon length** — for TLPs, tendons are inclined/vertical; buoyancy depth ≠ tendon depth.
> 6. **Mixing FPSO and FSO** — FSO has no processing; FPSO does.
> 7. **Writing "Drillship is moored"** — drillships use Dynamic Positioning (DP), not mooring, in deepwater.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Basic Configuration (Module 3)**

- **Fixed platforms** (Jacket, GBS, Jack-up) are limited to **water depths < 400 m**.
- **TLP** uses **vertical pretensioned tendons** — near-zero heave.
- **Spar** uses **deep draft (~200 m)** for stability.
- **FPSO** offers **onboard storage + weathervaning turret** — ideal for deepwater production.
- **Semi-submersibles** combine **buoyancy columns + pontoons** for stability and drilling.
- **Drillships** are **DP-stabilized** with a **central moonpool**.
- **Subsea systems** are critical for **ultra-deepwater tiebacks**.
- **Hydrostatic pressure gradient** = **$\rho_{sw} g$** ≈ **10.06 kPa/m**.
- **Reserve Buoyancy** must always remain **positive** for floaters.
- **TLP pretension per tendon** = $(F_B - W)/n$.
- **GBS** resists overturning by **self-weight** — no piles needed.
- **Jack-ups** are **pre-loaded** to confirm seabed bearing capacity before operation.
- **CAPEX scales roughly as $d^{1.5}$ for jackets** — steep cost curve beyond 300 m.
- **Selection hierarchy:** depth → function → motion → payload → economics.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sizing Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sizing Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sizing Considerations** in offshore engineering refer to the systematic and iterative process of establishing the principal geometric, capacity, and load-carrying dimensions of an offshore structure — both the substructure (jacket, hull, pontoons, columns) and the topside (deck, process facilities, accommodation, drilling rig). Sizing determines the **overall functional envelope** of the platform such that it can safely and economically:

- Accommodate the intended operational throughput (oil, gas, water injection rates).
- Provide adequate **storage**, **drilling**, **production**, and **living-quarter** facilities.
- Resist **metocean design loads** (wind, wave, current, earthquake) for the prescribed return period.
- Maintain **stability, draft, air-gap, and freeboard** within regulatory limits (API RP 2A / API RP 2T / ISO 19900 / DNV-RP-C103).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Sizing is *not* a one-time calculation — it is an **iterative, multi-disciplinary optimization** between structural engineer, process engineer, naval architect, marine systems designer, and HSE planner. The output is a set of "design basis" numbers: deck elevation, jacket leg spacing, hull displacement, pontoon length, column diameter, mooring line pretension, etc.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Factory" Mental Model

Imagine you are asked to design a **floating hospital-cum-school** that must be built on a lake that is occasionally battered by 3-metre waves. Before you draw a single column, you must first answer four "sizing" questions:

| Real-World Question | Offshore Engineering Counterpart |
|---|---|
| How many patients/children must it serve? | What is the **production rate** (bbl/day) & number of **wells**? |
| How much food/medicine must it carry? | What is the **storage capacity** (bbl of crude) needed between shuttle tanker visits? |
| How high above the water must the floor be? | What is the **air-gap** (deck elevation above MSL)? |
| How many anchors keep it from drifting? | What is the **mooring system pretension & line count**? |

Just as you cannot place an order for steel without these numbers, an offshore project cannot issue a fabrication tender without a **sized design basis**. Sizing is the *bridge* between **reservoir engineering** (what the field contains) and **structural engineering** (what the platform looks like).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Industry-Recognized Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key industry-accepted benchmark values** (used in early-stage sizing — these are NOT design values, only first estimates that must be refined through detailed engineering):

- **Crude-oil storage:** Typically **3 to 7 days** of peak production between tanker off-take.
- **Deck area per throughput:** Approximately **0.4 to 0.8 m² per bbl/day** of liquid capacity (rule-of-thumb for early sizing).
- **Topside weight per throughput:** Approximately **15 to 25 tonnes per 1000 bbl/day** of liquid processing capacity.
- **Drilling-rig package weight:** **1,500 to 4,000 tonnes** depending on drilling depth and rig type.
- **Air-gap:** Sum of (astronomical tide) + (storm surge) + (astronomical tide + surge + wave crest + sea-level rise) safety margin, with deck elevation typically **1.2 × Hs** (significant wave height) above the crest.
- **Return-period design event:** Operational – **1-year to 10-year** storm; Extreme – **100-year** storm; Survival – **1000-year** event (per API RP 2A).
- **Significant wave height (Hs)** for North Sea / Western India offshore: design values range from **8 m to 15 m**.
- **Density of seawater:** **1025 kg/m³** (used for buoyancy & stability calculations).
- **Density of crude oil:** **800 to 950 kg/m³** (typical range; affects storage tank sizing & stability).

### 1.4 Categorization of Sizing Decisions

Offshore sizing can be partitioned into four inter-locking decision layers:

1. **Field-Level Sizing** → number of platforms, slot count, production train redundancy.
2. **Topside Sizing** → deck dimensions, equipment layout, module count, lift weights, total operating weight.
3. **Hull/Substructure Sizing** → jacket leg spacing, brace pattern, hull length-beam-depth (L:B:D) ratio, pontoon cross-section.
4. **Station-Keeping Sizing** → mooring line count, line diameter, anchor radius, riser count & geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All four layers are **coupled**. A change in topside weight cascades into jacket leg size, which alters buoyancy requirements, which alters hull draft, which alters mooring line angles, which alters riser geometry. The iterative loop is what makes "sizing" a true engineering *process* and not a single equation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Sizing Logic Chain — Step-by-Step Reasoning

The sizing procedure is best understood as a **top-down decision tree** that flows from reservoir data to fabrication-ready dimensions.

**Step 1 — Establish the Reservoir & Production Basis**

The first sizing input is the **reservoir engineering model**:
- Daily oil production rate $Q_o$ (in **bbl/day** or **m³/day**).
- Daily gas production rate $Q_g$ (in **MMScfd** or **10⁶ m³/day**).
- Water injection rate $Q_{wi}$ (in **bbl/day**).
- Number of production wells $N_p$ and injection wells $N_i$.
- Well-spacing and slot count required for the drilling program.
- Field life (typically 15–30 years).

**Step 2 — Storage Sizing**

Storage capacity is sized for the **off-take window** — the time between successive shuttle-tanker visits. If off-take occurs every $T_s$ days:

$$
V_{storage} = Q_o \times T_s \times R_{ullage}
$$

where $R_{ullage}$ is the **ullage factor** (≈ 0.85–0.90) accounting for the volume that cannot be filled because of tank geometry, sloshing limits, and operational reserve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a ullage factor?** Crude oil tanks cannot be filled to 100% because of thermal expansion, the need to leave space for free-surface sloshing (which damages tank bulkheads), and pump suction requirements.

**Step 3 — Topside Area & Weight Sizing**

The total topside area $A_{deck}$ is the sum of process area, drilling area, quarters, utilities, and helideck. The *minimum* deck footprint must satisfy:

$$
A_{deck} \geq A_{process} + A_{drill} + A_{quarters} + A_{util} + A_{heli}
$$

A common industry rule-of-thumb used in **conceptual sizing** is:

$$
A_{deck} \approx k_A \times Q_o
$$

where $k_A \approx 0.4 \text{ to } 0.8 \; \text{m}^2 \text{ per bbl/day}$ for a greenfield production platform.

The **operating weight** $W_{ops}$ (dry weight + consumables + fluids in pipes and vessels) is estimated as:

$$
W_{ops} \approx k_W \times Q_o
$$

with $k_W \approx 15 \text{ to } 25$ **tonnes per 1000 bbl/day** for the process train alone (excluding the drilling rig and living quarters, which are added separately).

The **design weight** for in-place structural analysis then includes the **variable load** $V_L$ (drilling loads, crane lifts, hookloads, lay-down):

$$
W_{design} = W_{ops} + V_L + W_{lift}
$$

**Step 4 — Substructure Sizing (Fixed Platform — Jacket)**

For a fixed jacket, the bay width $B$ (horizontal distance between vertical legs) and the leg diameter $D_{leg}$ are sized for in-place strength. The horizontal wave force on a vertical cylinder of diameter $D$ in wave of height $H$ is given by the **Morison equation** (more on this in another module) — but for *sizing*, the engineer uses the simplified static-equivalent:

$$
F_{wave} \approx 0.5 \times \rho_w \times C_d \times D_{leg} \times H_s^2
$$

where $C_d$ is the drag coefficient (≈ 0.6–1.2 for smooth cylinders), $\rho_w$ is **seawater density = 1025 kg/m³**, and $H_s$ is the significant wave height.

The overturning moment at the seabed is:

$$
M_{OT} \approx F_{wave} \times d_{eff}
$$

where $d_{eff}$ is the **effective lever arm** (approximately equal to the water depth plus 30% of wave height for deep-water cases).

**Step 5 — Substructure Sizing (Floating Platform — Semi-submersible)**

For a semi-submersible, the **displacement** $\Delta$ must equal the total operating weight:

$$
\Delta = W_{ops} + W_{hull} + W_{mooring\ pretension\ vertical\ component}
$$

The **draft** $T$ is then derived from hull geometry:

$$
T = \frac{\Delta}{\rho_w \times A_{waterplane}}
$$

where $A_{waterplane}$ is the horizontal cross-sectional area of pontoons at the waterline.

The **GM (metacentric height)** for transverse stability is checked as:

$$
GM = KB + BM - KG
$$

where:
- $KB$ = height of centre of buoyancy above keel,
- $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{V_{submerged}}$ (transverse moment of inertia of waterplane ÷ submerged volume),
- $KG$ = height of centre of gravity above keel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For offshore service, **GM ≥ 0.15 m** is the typical minimum for a semi-submersible (DNV-RP-C103), with **0.30 m** being a comfortable design margin.

**Step 6 — Mooring System Sizing**

The mooring system must resist the **maximum environmental offset** under survival conditions. The number of mooring lines $N_m$ and the line size $D_{line}$ are chosen such that:

$$
T_{line,design} \leq \dfrac{T_{line,MBL}}{SF}
$$

where $T_{line,MBL}$ is the **minimum breaking load** of the chosen chain/wire and $SF$ is the **safety factor** (typically 1.67 to 2.5 per API RP 2SK / DNV-OS-E301).

**Step 7 — Riser Sizing**

The number of production risers $N_r$ must equal or exceed $N_p + N_i$ (one riser per well, although a **daisy-chained** or **manifold** arrangement may reduce this count). Riser top tension $T_{top}$ for a steel catenary riser is sized to avoid compression-buckling near the touchdown point:

$$
T_{top} \geq T_{submerged} + T_{dynamic}
$$

where $T_{submerged} = w_{sub} \times L_{sub}$ (submerged weight × submerged length) and $T_{dynamic}$ is the wave-induced dynamic tension amplitude (typically 10–25% of static).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{storage} = Q_o \times T_s \times R_{ullage}$ | Storage volume | bbl or m³ | Tank sizing |
| 2 | $A_{deck} \approx k_A \times Q_o$ | $k_A = 0.4 - 0.8$ | m² | Conceptual deck sizing |
| 3 | $W_{ops} \approx k_W \times Q_o$ | $k_W = 15 - 25$ | tonnes | Topside weight |
| 4 | $W_{design} = W_{ops} + V_L + W_{lift}$ | In-place design weight | tonnes | Jacket / hull sizing |
| 5 | $F_{wave} \approx 0.5 \rho_w C_d D H_s^2$ | Morison-simplified | kN | Leg sizing |
| 6 | $M_{OT} \approx F_{wave} \times d_{eff}$ | Overturning moment | kN·m | Foundation check |
| 7 | $\Delta = W_{total} / g$ | Displacement (Archimedes) | tonnes or m³ | Hull sizing |
| 8 | $T = \Delta / (\rho_w A_{waterplane})$ | Draft | m | Draft check |
| 9 | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | Transverse stability | m | Stability check |
| 10 | $BM = I_T / V_{sub}$ | Waterplane inertia / volume | m | Stability check |
| 11 | $T_{line,design} \leq T_{line,MBL} / SF$ | Mooring line check | kN | Mooring sizing |
| 12 | $T_{riser,top} \geq T_{sub} + T_{dyn}$ | Riser top tension | kN | Riser sizing |
| 13 | $H_{air-gap} = H_{crest} + H_{surge} + H_{tide} + SLR$ | Freeboard reserve | m | Deck elevation |
| 14 | $W_{env,100yr}$ — per API RP 2A | Metocean design basis | m, m/s | Environmental design |
| 15 | $\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³ | Seawater density | kg/m³ | Buoyancy |
| 16 | $\rho_{oil} = 800 - 950$ kg/m³ | Crude density | kg/m³ | Storage & stability |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The sizing decisions made in this module ripple through the entire **project life cycle**:
- **CAPEX impact:** Topside weight directly sets jacket/pile steel tonnage → fabrication cost (≈ USD 4,000–6,000/tonne installed for fixed platforms in deep water).
- **Schedule impact:** Lift weight determines whether the **float-over method** or **heavy-lift vessel** is used; this governs the installation window.
- **Operability impact:** Storage sizing affects the **cash flow curve** — too little storage means more shuttle-tanker days; too much means wasted CAPEX.
- **Safety impact:** Air-gap, GM, and mooring SF directly determine whether the platform **survives** the 100-year storm without structural failure or excessive offset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Sizing is where **engineering economics, safety regulation, and hydrodynamic reality** meet. The "right" answer is always a **trade-off** between cost, risk, and operability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Storage Volume from Off-take Window

**Statement:** A platform produces 80,000 bbl/day of stabilized crude. The shuttle tanker calls every 5 days. The ullage factor is 0.88. Derive the required storage volume.

**Given:**
- $Q_o = 80{,}000 \; \text{bbl/day}$
- $T_s = 5 \; \text{days}$
- $R_{ullage} = 0.88$

**Step 1 — Gross fluid production over the off-take window:**

$$
V_{gross} = Q_o \times T_s
$$

$$
V_{gross} = 80{,}000 \times 5 = 400{,}000 \; \text{bbl}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ullage factor to obtain the *effective* (design) storage volume:**

$$
V_{storage} = \dfrac{V_{gross}}{R_{ullage}}
$$

$$
V_{storage} = \dfrac{400{,}000}{0.88} = 454{,}545.45 \; \text{bbl}
$$

**Step 3 — Round up and add a 10% operational margin (recommended industry practice):**

$$
V_{design} = 1.10 \times V_{storage}
$$

$$
V_{design} = 1.10 \times 454{,}545.45 = 500{,}000 \; \text{bbl}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 500,000 bbl and not exactly 454,545?** Because the operator also needs **surge capacity** for upset events (e.g., bad-weather shutdown of the shuttle, temporary production spike). A 10% safety margin is standard.

**Result:** The storage system must be sized for **≈ 500,000 bbl** of stabilized crude.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Topside Weight from Production Capacity

**Statement:** A North-Sea-style fixed platform must process 60,000 bbl/day of oil plus associated gas. The drilling package is a 1,500-tonne modular rig. Living quarters house 80 persons. Estimate the total operating topside weight using industry rule-of-thumb.

**Given:**
- $Q_o = 60{,}000 \; \text{bbl/day}$
- $k_W = 20 \; \text{tonnes per 1000 bbl/day}$ (mid-range)
- $W_{rig} = 1{,}500 \; \text{tonnes}$
- $W_{quarters} \approx 12 \; \text{tonnes/person}$ (rule of thumb for fully outfitted module)

**Step 1 — Process train weight (excluding rig and quarters):**

$$
W_{process} = k_W \times \dfrac{Q_o}{1000}
$$

$$
W_{process} = 20 \times \dfrac{60{,}000}{1000} = 1{,}200 \; \text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 2 — Living quarters weight:**

$$
W_{quarters} = 80 \times 12 = 960 \; \text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 3 — Drilling rig weight (given):**

$$
W_{rig} = 1{,}500 \; \text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum to get the dry operating weight:**

$$
W_{dry} = W_{process} + W_{quarters} + W_{rig}
$$

$$
W_{dry} = 1{,}200 + 960 + 1{,}500 = 3{,}660 \; \text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 5 — Add the **variable load** $V_L$ (drilling setback, crane test loads, lay-down area fill):**

A typical $V_L$ for a drilling-and-production platform is **30% of $W_{dry}$** for operational cases and **50%** for the *drilling* in-place case.

For the *survival / in-place extreme* case, the design uses $V_L = 0.3 \times W_{dry}$:

$$
V_L = 0.30 \times 3{,}660 = 1{,}098 \; \text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 6 — Design weight for in-place analysis:**

$$
W_{design} = W_{dry} + V_L
$$

$$
W_{design} = 3{,}660 + 1{,}098 = 4{,}758 \; \text{tonnes}
$$

**Result:** The jacket substructure and piles must be sized to support a **vertical reaction of approximately 4,758 tonnes** under operating conditions, with appropriate safety factors applied for the 100-year environmental event.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wave Force on a Jacket Leg (Morison Simplified for Sizing)

**Statement:** A jacket leg of diameter 3.0 m is in 100 m of water. The 100-year significant wave height is 12 m, with associated wave period T = 14 s. The drag coefficient is $C_d = 0.8$. Compute the **static-equivalent horizontal wave force** for preliminary leg sizing.

**Given:**
- $D_{leg} = 3.0 \; \text{m}$
- $d = 100 \; \text{m}$ (water depth)
- $H_s = 12 \; \text{m}$
- $C_d = 0.8$
- $\rho_w = 1025 \; \text{kg/m³} = 1.025 \; \text{tonnes/m³}$

**Step 1 — The Morison force per unit length (drag-dominated, inertia neglected at this stage for simplicity):**

$$
f_{wave} = 0.5 \times \rho_w \times C_d \times D_{leg} \times u^2
$$

For sizing, we use the wave height as the basis and assume a velocity-squared scaling:

$$
u_{max} \approx \dfrac{H_s}{2} \times \dfrac{2\pi}{T}
$$

This is the orbital velocity at the surface. For a column extending through the wave zone, we use an *effective* velocity squared averaged over depth.

For the **sizing** estimate, the *equivalent static* drag force on a vertical cylinder in regular waves of height $H$ is:

$$
F_{wave} = 0.5 \times \rho_w \times C_d \times D_{leg} \times H_s^2 \times K_{depth}
$$

where $K_{depth}$ is an **empirical depth factor** (≈ 0.5 for $d/H_s > 5$, ≈ 0.7 for $d/H_s \approx 3$). Here:

$$
\dfrac{d}{H_s} = \dfrac{100}{12} = 8.33
$$

So $K_{depth} \approx 0.5$.

**Step 2 — Substitute and compute:**

$$
F_{wave} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.8 \times 3.0 \times (12)^2 \times 0.5
$$

Let us compute step by step:

$$
F_{wave} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.8 \times 3.0 \times 144 \times 0.5
$$

$$
F_{wave} = (0.5 \times 0.8 \times 3.0 \times 144 \times 0.5) \times 1025
$$

$$
F_{wave} = (86.4) \times 1025 = 88{,}560 \; \text{N} \approx 88.6 \; \text{kN}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **per-metre** force. To get the *total* force on the leg, multiply by the wave-affected zone (≈ 0.4 × water depth for drag-dominated Morison in deep water):

$$
F_{total} = f_{wave} \times L_{wave-affected}
$$

$$
L_{wave-affected} = 0.4 \times d = 0.4 \times 100 = 40 \; \text{m}
$$

$$
F_{total} = 88.6 \times 40 = 3{,}544 \; \text{kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Overturning moment at the seabed:**

$$
M_{OT} = F_{total} \times d_{eff}
$$

$$
d_{eff} \approx 0.6 \times d = 0.6 \times 100 = 60 \; \text{m}
$$

$$
M_{OT} = 3{,}544 \times 60 = 212{,}640 \; \text{kN·m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The 0.6 factor on the lever arm is a sizing approximation; detailed hydrodynamic analysis (e.g., WAMIT, SESAM) replaces this with a fully-coupled diffraction solution for the survival case.

**Result:** Each 3-m diameter jacket leg must transfer a **horizontal base shear of ≈ 3,544 kN** and an **overturning moment of ≈ 2.13 × 10⁵ kN·m** to the pile foundation. This is the input for pile diameter and penetration-depth sizing.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Semi-submersible Draft from Displacement

**Statement:** A semi-submersible has a total operating weight (topside + hull steel + ballast + mooring vertical) of 28,000 tonnes. The pontoons form a rectangular waterplane of 90 m × 30 m (with the columns adding negligible waterplane area at the operating draft). Seawater density is 1025 kg/m³. Compute the operating draft.

**Given:**
- $\Delta = 28{,}000 \; \text{tonnes} = 28 \times 10^6 \; \text{kg}$
- $A_{waterplane} = 90 \times 30 = 2{,}700 \; \text{m}^2$ (combined pontoon waterplane, assuming two pontoons each 90 m × 15 m, summed to one effective area)
- $\rho_w = 1025 \; \text{kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Archimedes' principle (buoyancy = weight at equilibrium):**

$$
F_B = W
$$

$$
\rho_w \times g \times V_{sub} = W
$$

In mass units (working in tonnes and m³):

$$
\rho_w \times V_{sub} = \Delta
$$

$$
V_{sub} = \dfrac{\Delta}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{28{,}000}{1.025} = 27{,}317 \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for draft:**

$$
V_{sub} = A_{waterplane} \times T
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{V_{sub}}{A_{waterplane}} = \dfrac{27{,}317}{2{,}700} = 10.12 \; \text{m}
$$

**Result:** The operating draft is **T ≈ 10.1 m**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This value must be checked against the **survival draft** (ballast-deballast condition under 100-year storm) and the **transit draft** (when the columns are deballasted for tow-out). The platform is *sized* such that the operating draft provides adequate GM and freeboard simultaneously.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Metacentric Height (GM) Check

**Statement:** For the semi-submersible above, the waterplane moment of inertia about the transverse centreline is $I_T = 1.2 \times 10^5 \; \text{m}^4$ (combined for the two pontoons). The submerged volume is 27,317 m³. The centre of gravity is at KG = 18 m above the keel, and the centre of buoyancy is at KB = 6 m above the keel. Compute GM and verify it meets DNV minimum.

**Step 1 — Compute BM:**

$$
BM = \dfrac{I_T}{V_{sub}} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^5}{27{,}317} = 4.39 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute GM:**

$$
GM = KB + BM - KG
$$

$$
GM = 6 + 4.39 - 18 = -7.61 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

A **negative GM** means the platform is **unstable** in the transverse mode! This cannot be correct for an operating semi-submersible. The error here is that we have used an unrealistic KG; in a real semi-submersible, the heavy pontoons are at the bottom (low KG), and the topside is high but the columns are ballasted to bring the overall KG well below KB + BM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lesson:** Sizing is **iterative**. A first-pass KG estimate is wrong, and the engineer must re-distribute ballast or re-arrange the topside to bring KG down. The result is then re-checked. This is precisely why sizing cannot be reduced to a single equation.

**Re-sized scenario:** Suppose ballast is added to bring KG to **12 m**.

$$
GM = 6 + 4.39 - 12 = -1.61 \; \text{m} \; (\text{still negative})
$$

**Re-sized again — with $I_T$ increased by widening the pontoons to $2.5 \times 10^5 \; \text{m}^4$:**

$$
BM = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^5}{27{,}317} = 9.15 \; \text{m}
$$

$$
GM = 6 + 9.15 - 12 = 3.15 \; \text{m} \quad \checkmark \; (\text{meets DNV minimum})
$$

**Result:** The pontoon geometry must be enlarged (or ballast added) to achieve a positive GM. The "right" pontoon dimensions are an output of the sizing process, not an input.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Coupling Between Sizing Variables

| Variable Changed | Direct Effect on | Knock-on Effect on |
|---|---|---|
| Increase $Q_o$ | $A_{deck}$, $W_{ops}$ | Jacket leg size, pile penetration |
| Increase $T_s$ | $V_{storage}$ | Hull volume, draft, freeboard |
| Increase $H_s$ | $F_{wave}$, $M_{OT}$ | Jacket brace size, pile diameter |
| Increase $W_{ops}$ | Vertical reaction at legs | Pile axial capacity required |
| Add more wells | Slot count, drilling package | Deck area, hookload on crane |
| Increase mooring pretension | Vertical load on hull | Draft, freeboard, GM |
| Increase column spacing | $I_T$ | GM (positive) |

---

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Down Sizing Decision Tree

The following Mermaid block maps the *complete* sizing decision flow from reservoir data to fabricated dimensions. It is a **block-level functional architecture** — every node represents a sizing step, and every arrow shows the data dependency.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reservoir Data] --> B[Production Basis Q_o Q_g N_p]
    B --> C[Storage Sizing V_storage]
    B --> D[Topside Sizing A_deck W_ops]
    C --> E[Hull / Substructure Sizing]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Mooring System Sizing]
    E --> G[Riser System Sizing]
    E --> H[Stability and GM Check]
    F --> I[Sized Design Basis]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Fabrication Tender Ready?}
    J --> K[Yes - Issue Drawings] 
    J --> L[No - Iterate Sizing]
    L --> B
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **A → B:** The reservoir engineer hands over production rates and well count.
- **B → C, B → D:** Production basis drives *both* storage sizing (how much crude to hold) and topside sizing (how big a deck and how heavy a process train).
- **C → E, D → E:** Storage and topside dimensions together determine the **substructure envelope** (hull size for floating, or jacket dimensions for fixed).
- **E → F, G, H:** Once the substructure envelope is set, the mooring, riser, and stability check-outs can be performed.
- **I → J:** The "sized design basis" is the consolidated output. If it does not meet regulatory and operational criteria, the loop restarts at B with adjusted inputs.

---

### 4.2 Topside Functional Block Architecture

A typical production-platform topside is partitioned into five functional zones. The Mermaid diagram below shows how each zone's size depends on production parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Process Train] -->|Oil Gas Water Separation| S[Storage and Offload]
    D[Drilling Module] -->|Rig Package W_rig| S
    Q[Living Quarters] -->|Persons P_count| S
    U[Utilities Power Water HVAC] -->|kW Capacity| S
    H[Helideck and Flare] -->|D_value Capacity| S
    P -->|Area A_proc| TP[Topside Platform]
    D -->|Area A_drill| TP
    Q -->|Area A_q| TP
    U -->|Area A_util| TP
    H -->|Area A_heli| TP
    TP -->|Total A_deck W_design| SUB[Substructure]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The five functional zones (P, D, Q, U, H) are sized *independently* but *interfaced* through the storage and offload (S) block.
- Each zone contributes a footprint (area) and a weight to the total topside platform.
- The summed area $A_{deck}$ and design weight $W_{design}$ are the inputs to the **substructure** sizing (jacket for fixed, hull for floating).

---

### 4.3 Mooring Pattern Architecture (Typical Spread Mooring)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HULL[Hull or Platform] --> ML1[Mooring Line 1]
    HULL --> ML2[Mooring Line 2]
    HULL --> ML3[Mooring Line 3]
    HULL --> ML4[Mooring Line 4]
    HULL --> ML5[Mooring Line 5]
    HULL --> ML6[Mooring Line 6]
    HULL --> ML7[Mooring Line 7]
    HULL --> ML8[Mooring Line 8]
    ML1 --> ANC1[Anchor 1]
    ML2 --> ANC2[Anchor 2]
    ML3 --> ANC3[Anchor 3]
    ML4 --> ANC4[Anchor 4]
    ML5 --> ANC5[Anchor 5]
    ML6 --> ANC6[Anchor 6]
    ML7 --> ANC7[Anchor 7]
    ML8 --> ANC8[Anchor 8]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** A typical 8-line spread mooring is shown. For a semi-submersible, **8 to 12 lines** is industry-standard (API RP 2SK). The number of lines is part of the **sizing** — more lines = lower individual line tension = smaller line diameter = lower CAPEX per line, but higher installation cost.

---

### 4.4 Fixed Platform vs. Floating Platform — Sizing Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FIXED[FIXED PLATFORM]
    F1[Jacket Leg Spacing B] --> F2[Pile Diameter D_pile]
    F2 --> F3[Pile Penetration L_pile]
    F3 --> F4[Base Shear F_wave]
    end
    subgraph FLOAT[FLOATING PLATFORM]
    FL1[Pontoon Length L_pontoon] --> FL2[Displacement Delta]
    FL2 --> FL3[Draft T]
    FL3 --> FL4[Metacentric Height GM]
    FL4 --> FL5[Mooring Pretension T_m]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading aid:** The two subgraphs are intentionally parallel to highlight that the **sizing variables are completely different** for fixed vs. floating platforms. A student who memorizes the *fixed* path cannot apply it directly to a *floating* design — the **first step in sizing is to choose the platform type**, which then determines the entire subsequent workflow.
