# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define bracing system in an offshore jacket platform and list any FOUR functions it performs.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **bracing system** in a fixed offshore jacket platform consists of inclined tubular secondary members arranged in a triangulated pattern between the main vertical legs (chords), forming the structural framework that resists lateral environmental loads.

**Any FOUR functions:**
1. **Transfers lateral wave, wind, current and seismic loads** from the deck down to the pile foundation through axial action.
2. **Provides lateral shear stiffness** to the jacket so that deck deflection remains within API limit of $H/200$.
3. **Provides geometric stability** by triangulating the rectangular frame (triangles are inherently stable geometric shapes).
4. **Resists torsional loads** through horizontal bracing levels at the deck and mudline.
5. **Provides redundancy** for progressive collapse prevention in case of single-member failure.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between X-bracing and K-bracing in offshore jacket structures (any FOUR points).**

**Model Answer:**

| # | X-Bracing | K-Bracing |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Two braces cross each other forming an "X" at the panel centre | Two braces meet at the middle of a chord forming a "K" |
| 2 | Has a **central brace-to-brace intersection** requiring a heavy node can | **No central intersection** — simpler, smaller node can |
| 3 | Higher lateral stiffness | Slightly lower lateral stiffness |
| 4 | **Poor fatigue performance** due to high SCF at X-joint | **Better fatigue performance** — fewer stress concentration points |
| 5 | Difficult to inspect inside the X-joint | Easy inspection access |
| 6 | Higher fabrication cost | **Lower cost — most preferred in modern jackets** |
| 7 | Chord carries only axial force | Chord at K-joint carries **axial + bending moment** |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT confuse plan-view bracing with elevation-view bracing** — many students mix them up and lose 3-4 marks.
> - **Always mention the brace-force formula $F_b = V/\cos\theta$ explicitly** when discussing load transfer — vague answers get zero marks for the analytical component.
> - **Mention both tension AND compression behaviour** of braces under wave load reversal — students often forget load reversal and only discuss one direction.
> - **For numerical problems, always state the dispersion relation $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kd)$ and solve for $k$ iteratively** — skipping this step costs 2 marks.
> - **Always include units in the final answer** and box the result.
> - **State the API slenderness limit $\lambda \leq 80$** when discussing brace design.
> - **Use the term "tubular joint" not "welded joint"** in joint-can discussions.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **various types of bracing systems used in fixed offshore jacket platforms**, with neat sketches (use block diagrams). Discuss their relative merits and demerits. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A vertical brace of length 12 m is inclined at 45° to the horizontal in an offshore jacket face. The brace carries a lateral shear of 600 kN due to wave loading. Compute the **axial force in the brace**, the **horizontal and vertical components of this force**, and the **required brace cross-sectional area** if the allowable axial stress is 150 MPa. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer to Question B-A:

**(a) Types of Bracing Systems:**

*(Detailed explanation with diagrams — already covered in SECTION 2.1. Students should reproduce the table and at least 4 block diagrams showing X-bracing, K-bracing, Diamond bracing, and Knee bracing in the elevation view.)*

**Key points to write:**
- **X-Bracing:** Two diagonal braces cross; high stiffness, poor fatigue, central node can.
- **K-Bracing:** Braces meet at chord midpoint; preferred for modern jackets, easy inspection, chord bending concern.
- **Knee Bracing:** At top of jacket; transfers deck lateral load to chord.
- **Diagonal (Single):** One brace per panel; economical but only tension-effective.
- **Diamond Bracing:** Vertical diamond; high torsional stiffness for deepwater.

*(Examiner expects comparison table and 2-3 block diagrams. Marks awarded for: classification, sketches, relative merits/demerits — typically 2 + 3 + 2 marks respectively.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $L_b = 12$ m, $\theta = 45°$, $V = 600$ kN, $\sigma_{allow} = 150$ MPa.

**Step 1: Brace axial force**

$$F_{brace} = \frac{V}{\cos\theta} = \frac{600 \text{ kN}}{\cos 45°} = \frac{600}{0.7071} = 848.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Horizontal component**

$$F_{H,brace} = F_{brace} \times \cos\theta = 848.5 \times 0.7071 = 600 \text{ kN} \checkmark$$

**Step 3: Vertical component**

$$F_{V,brace} = F_{brace} \times \sin\theta = 848.5 \times 0.7071 = 600 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Required brace area**

$$A_{required} = \frac{F_{brace}}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{848.5 \times 10^3}{150 \times 10^6} = 5.66 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{F_{brace} = 848.5 \text{ kN}, \quad A_{required} = 5.66 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 56.6 \text{ cm}^2}$$

For a tubular brace with $t = 25$ mm: $D = (A_{req}/\pi t) + t = (5.66 \times 10^{-3}/(\pi \times 0.025)) + 0.025 = 0.0721 + 0.025 \approx 0.097$ m. Adopt $D = 100$ mm standard brace.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the **load transfer mechanism in a fixed offshore jacket platform under wave loading**. Explain how the lateral force is distributed among chord members, braces, and piles. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using **Morison's equation**, derive an expression for the horizontal wave force per unit length on a vertical tubular brace. Given: wave height $H = 6$ m, wave period $T = 8$ s, water depth $d = 50$ m, brace diameter $D = 0.9$ m, brace located at elevation $z = 20$ m, $C_D = 1.0$, $C_M = 2.0$, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³. Compute the **maximum drag force, maximum inertia force, and maximum total horizontal force per unit length** at this location. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer to Question B-B:

**(a) Load Transfer Mechanism:**

*(Examiner expects a clear 5-step chain, summarised below — students should reproduce the bulleted sequence and refer to block diagram from SECTION 4.1.)*

1. **Wave + Wind + Current forces** act on the submerged portion of the jacket (typically from seabed to crest elevation).
2. **Distributed hydrodynamic pressure** is integrated vertically and **lumped as horizontal nodal forces** at each horizontal framing level (typically 4-6 levels per jacket face).
3. **Braces at each level** carry these nodal forces as **axial tension (on leeward face) and axial compression (on windward face)**. Brace axial force = $V/\cos\theta$.
4. **Chord members accumulate axial compression** from gravity loads plus the vertical components of brace forces.
5. The **accumulated horizontal force** reaches the **mudline** as a large lateral reaction and **overturning moment**, resisted by the **pile foundation** through passive soil resistance, end-bearing, and skin friction.

---

**(b) Morison's Equation Derivation and Numerical Solution:**

**Derivation:**

The Morison equation expresses the horizontal force per unit length on a slender vertical cylinder (where $D/\lambda_{wave} < 0.2$) as the sum of two components:

$$f_H(z,t) = \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D D \vert u_r \vert u_r}_{\text{Drag}} + \underbrace{\rho C_M \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \dot{u}}_{\text{Inertia}}$$

where $u_r = u + u_c$ is the relative velocity (wave velocity + current velocity). The drag term arises from viscous pressure drag on the cylinder, while the inertia term arises from the acceleration of the displaced water mass (added mass effect).

**Numerical Solution:**

$\omega = 2\pi/T = 2\pi/8 = 0.7854$ rad/s.

From dispersion $\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kd)$, solve iteratively: $k \approx 0.0902$ rad/m (using same method as in Section 3.2).

At $z = 20$ m: $kz = 0.0902 \times 20 = 1.804$; $\cosh(1.804) = 3.207$.
$kd = 0.0902 \times 50 = 4.510$; $\sinh(4.510) = 45.55$.

Maximum velocity:
$$u_{\max} = \frac{H\omega}{2} \cdot \frac{\cosh(kz)}{\sinh(kd)} = \frac{6 \times 0.7854}{2} \times \frac{3.207}{45.55} = 2.356 \times 0.0704 = 0.1659 \text{ m/s}$$

Maximum acceleration:
$$\dot{u}_{\max} = \frac{H\omega^2}{2} \cdot \frac{\cosh(kz)}{\sinh(kd)} = \frac{6 \times (0.7854)^2}{2} \times 0.0704 = 1.851 \times 0.0704 = 0.1303 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Maximum drag force per unit length:**
$$f_D = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.0 \times 0.9 \times (0.1659)^2 = 12.68 \text{ N/m}$$

**Maximum inertia force per unit length:**
$$f_I = 1025 \times 2.0 \times \frac{\pi (0.9)^2}{4} \times 0.1303 = 1025 \times 2.0 \times 0.6362 \times 0.1303 = 169.9 \text{ N/m}$$

**Maximum total force (90° phase difference, vector sum):**
$$f_{\max} = \sqrt{(12.68)^2 + (169.9)^2} = \sqrt{160.8 + 28866} = \sqrt{29027} \approx 170.4 \text{ N/m}$$

$$\boxed{f_D = 12.68 \text{ N/m}, \quad f_I = 169.9 \text{ N/m}, \quad f_{\max} = 170.4 \text{ N/m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** At 20 m elevation in 50 m depth, inertia dominates over drag by a factor of ~13. This is typical for **smaller-diameter bracing members** where $D/\lambda_{wave}$ is small. As $D$ increases or wave period shortens, drag contribution grows. For large-diameter legs, drag may dominate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Bracing System (PCNST602 Module 3)**

### Core Concepts to Memorise
- [ ] **Bracing system definition** = triangulated secondary members resisting lateral load in jacket platforms.
- [ ] **Five functions** of bracing: lateral transfer, shear stiffness, geometric stability, torsional resistance, redundancy.
- [ ] **Five elevation patterns:** X, K, Knee, Diagonal, Diamond.
- [ ] **Three plan patterns:** X, K, Diamond.
- [ ] **Five function classes:** Primary, Secondary, Tertiary, Horizontal, Mud.

### Critical Formulas (must memorise verbatim)
- [ ] Brace axial force: $F_b = V/\cos\theta$
- [ ] Morison drag: $f_D = 0.5 \rho C_D D \vert u_r \vert u_r$
- [ ] Morison inertia: $f_I = \rho C_M (\pi D^2/4) \dot{u}$
- [ ] Euler buckling: $F_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / (KL)^2$
- [ ] API slenderness limit: $\lambda = KL/r \leq 80$
- [ ] API deflection limit: $\Delta_{top} \leq H/200$
- [ ] Linear wave velocity: $u = (H\omega/2) [\cosh(kz)/\sinh(kd)] \cos(\omega t - kx)$
- [ ] Dispersion: $\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kd)$
- [ ] Allowable axial capacity (WSD): $F_{allow} = 0.6 F_y A$

### Engineering Judgement Points
- [ ] **X-bracing** = highest stiffness but worst fatigue (central node can).
- [ ] **K-bracing** = preferred modern choice (no central node, easy inspection).
- [ ] **Brace angle optimum:** 30°–60° with horizontal (balances $F_b$ magnitude and chord forces).
- [ ] **Drag dominates** for large $D$ or short-period waves; **Inertia dominates** for small $D$ or long-period waves.
- [ ] **Wave load reversal** means every brace must be designed for both tension AND compression.
- [ ] **Compressive braces may buckle** — slenderness check is mandatory.
- [ ] **Joints (node cans) are fatigue hotspots** — SCF governs fatigue life (Efthymiou formula).

### KTU-Specific Exam Tips
- [ ] For **3-mark questions**, state the definition + 4 functions in 4 bullet points.
- [ ] For **14-mark questions**, plan 4-5 pages: 2 pages of theory + diagrams, 2-3 pages of numerical.
- [ ] Always draw at least **one block diagram** of a jacket face showing X-bracing or K-bracing.
- [ ] Always write the **dispersion relation** before computing wave kinematics — shows method.
- [ ] Box the **final numerical answer with units**.
- [ ] Mention **API RP 2A or ISO 19902** once to show code awareness.

### Common Pitfalls to AVOID
- ❌ Confusing **plan-view bracing** with **elevation-view bracing**.
- ❌ Using $D$ instead of $(D-t)$ in tubular area calculations.
- ❌ Forgetting that **wave direction reverses**, so tension/compression reverses in braces.
- ❌ Omitting units in the final answer.
- ❌ Skipping the **iterative solution for wave number $k$**.
- ❌ Forgetting to check **slenderness ratio $\lambda \leq 80$**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Jacket Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Jacket Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Jacket Platform** is a fixed, steel-template, space-frame offshore structure consisting of a tubular steel space frame (the *jacket*) which is supported by piles driven through its legs into the seabed. According to **API RP 2A (Planning, Designing, and Constructing Fixed Offshore Platforms)** and **ISO 19902**, the jacket is the substructure that transmits all environmental, operational, and gravitational loads from the topsides (deck) through the tubular members to the foundation piles embedded in the seabed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCNST602 Module 3):**
> *"Jacket design encompasses the complete engineering process of proportioning, analysing, and detailing a fixed steel template-type offshore platform — including in-place analysis, dynamic analysis, tubular joint design, pile foundation design, and installation/transportation engineering — to safely transfer operational and environmental loads to the seabed throughout the platform's design life (typically 25–30 years)."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an **"Eiffel Tower standing on the seabed wearing a hard hat"**:

- The **hard hat (topsides)** is where the drilling rigs, living quarters, and process equipment sit.
- The **tower (jacket)** is a 4-legged (or 3-/6-/8-legged) steel lattice — like a giant erector set — that rises from the seabed to support the hat.
- The **legs of the tower** are hollow steel tubes.
- To prevent the tower from tipping over in strong ocean waves, **long steel pins (piles)** are driven *through* the legs of the tower deep into the seabed, anchoring it like tent pegs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Scale:** A North Sea jacket may weigh **30,000 tonnes**, stand in **150 m water depth**, have legs spaced **15–20 m apart**, and use piles drilled **100 m into the seabed**. The wave forces on such a structure can exceed **50,000 tonnes** during a 100-year storm.

### 1.3 Why Jacket Design is Critical in Offshore Engineering

| Design Driver | Engineering Significance |
|---------------|--------------------------|
| **Safety** | Failure leads to loss of life, oil spill, environmental disaster |
| **Cost** | Jacket = ~30% of total platform CAPEX |
| **Environment** | Hydrodynamic loads (wave, current) dominate design |
| **Fatigue** | Wave-induced cyclic loading governs tubular joint life |
| **Code Compliance** | API RP 2A / ISO 19902 / DNV-OS-C201 mandatory |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **Sea water density:** $\rho_w = \mathbf{1025 \ kg/m^3}$ (salinity dependent)
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = \mathbf{9.81 \ m/s^2}$
- **Steel density:** $\rho_{st} = \mathbf{7850 \ kg/m^3}$
- **Young's Modulus of Steel:** $E = \mathbf{2.0 \times 10^5 \ MPa}$
- **Poisson's ratio:** $\nu = \mathbf{0.3}$
- **Yield strength of structural steel (typical):** $f_y = \mathbf{345 \ MPa}$ (API Grade 2H) or **355 MPa** (EN 10025 S355)
- **Design wave return period:** **100 years** (for operating conditions) and **10,000 years** (for accidental / survival)
- **Marine growth thickness:** **50–150 mm** (varies with depth and water temperature)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Design Philosophy — Limit State vs. Working Stress Design

Modern jacket design follows **Limit State Design (LSD)** principles, where the structure is designed to satisfy two limit states:

> [!NOTE]
> **SLS — Serviceability Limit State:** Ensures the platform remains functional under operating conditions (deflections, vibrations, fatigue).
> **ULS — Ultimate Limit State:** Ensures the platform has adequate strength to withstand extreme environmental loads with reserve capacity (resistance ≥ factored load).

### 2.2 Phases of Jacket Design

The design is verified at **three principal phases**:

1. **In-Place Phase (Operational Phase):**
   - Platform is installed and operating in its final location.
   - Loads: Gravity (self-weight, topsides, MLC), Hydrostatic, Wind, Wave, Current, Earthquake.
   - Governs overall strength and serviceability.

2. **Transportation Phase:**
   - Jacket is transported on a barge or by self-floatation.
   - Loads: Bending due to wave action on the floating/barge-mounted structure (often governs member sizing near mid-span).

3. **Installation / Lifting Phase (Load-Out & Upending):**
   - Jacket is lifted by crane (or upended by controlled flooding).
   - Governed by **dynamic amplification factor (DAF)** of **1.0 to 1.35** depending on sea state.

## 2.3 Environmental Loads — The Core of Jacket Design

### 2.3.1 Wave Loads — Morison Equation

For slender members (where $D/L < 0.2$, where $D$ = member diameter, $L$ = wavelength), inertia and drag are calculated independently using the **Morison Equation**:

$$F = F_D + F_I = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_w C_D D \vert u \vert u + \rho_w C_M A \dot{u}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where the Morison equation applies:** For members where the member diameter is much smaller than the wavelength. For jackets, this is virtually always the case (typical member $D \approx 1$–$3$ m, $L \approx 100$–$300$ m).

Where:
- $\rho_w$ = water density = **1025 kg/m³**
- $C_D$ = drag coefficient (≈ 0.6 to 1.2, typically **0.65** for smooth tubulars)
- $C_M$ = inertia coefficient (≈ 1.3 to 2.0, typically **1.5** for smooth circular cylinders)
- $D$ = member outer diameter (m)
- $A = \tfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ = cross-sectional area of member (m²)
- $u$ = horizontal water particle velocity (m/s)
- $\dot{u}$ = horizontal water particle acceleration (m/s²)

### 2.3.2 Wave Kinematics (Airy Linear Wave Theory)

For regular (Airy) waves, particle kinematics at any depth $z$ below SWL:

$$u = \dfrac{H g T}{2 L} \cdot \dfrac{\cosh(k(h+z))}{\cosh(kh)} \cdot \cos(kx - \omega t)$$

$$\dot{u} = \dfrac{H g \pi}{L} \cdot \dfrac{\cosh(k(h+z))}{\cosh(kh)} \cdot \sin(kx - \omega t)$$

Where:
- $H$ = wave height, $T$ = wave period, $L$ = wavelength, $h$ = water depth
- $k = 2\pi/L$ = wave number, $\omega = 2\pi/T$ = angular frequency
- $z$ = vertical coordinate (negative below SWL, zero at seabed)

### 2.3.3 Wind Loads

$$F_W = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_S A_S V^2$$

With air density $\rho_a = \mathbf{1.226 \ kg/m^3}$, shape factor $C_S$ for tubulars ≈ 0.5–0.7.

### 2.3.4 Current Loads

Usually combined with waves by vector addition: $u_{total} = u_{wave} + u_{current}$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Morison Drag Force** | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_w C_D D \vert u \vert u$ | N/m (per unit length) |
| 2 | **Morison Inertia Force** | $F_I = \rho_w C_M A \dot{u}$ | N/m (per unit length) |
| 3 | **Airy Wave Velocity** | $u = \dfrac{HgT \cosh(k(h+z))}{2L \cosh(kh)} \cos\theta$ | m/s |
| 4 | **Airy Wave Acceleration** | $\dot{u} = \dfrac{Hg\pi \cosh(k(h+z))}{L \cosh(kh)} \sin\theta$ | m/s² |
| 5 | **Airwave Dispersion Relation** | $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kh)$ | — |
| 6 | **Wind Force** | $F_W = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_S A_S V^2$ | N |
| 7 | **Hydrostatic Pressure** | $p = \rho_w g z$ | Pa (z = depth) |
| 8 | **API WSD Allowable Axial Stress** | $F_a = 0.6 F_y$ (compression) | — |
| 9 | **API WSD Hoop Buckling (Tubulars)** | $F_{hc} = \dfrac{2.6 E (t/D)^{2.5}}{(L/D)}$ | — |
| 10 | **Tubular Joint Axial Capacity (API)** | $P_a = \dfrac{F_y T^2}{\sin\theta} \cdot Q_f \cdot Q_u$ | kN |
| 11 | **Pile Axial Capacity** | $Q_d = Q_f + Q_p$ (skin + tip) | kN |
| 12 | **API Base Shear (Earthquake)** | $V = C_s W$ (seismic coefficient) | kN |
| 13 | **Marine Growth Allowance** | 50–150 mm (typical) | mm |
| 14 | **Steel Density** | $\rho_{st} = 7850$ | kg/m³ |
| 15 | **Sea Water Density** | $\rho_w = 1025$ | kg/m³ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Morison equation is the **backbone** of offshore structural design software (SACS, SESAM/GeniE, USFOS, ABAQUS/Aqua). The wave-particle velocities are integrated along each tubular member from seabed to wave crest, multiplied by the projected area, and summed to obtain the **global base shear** and **overturning moment** at the mudline. These are then used to:
- Design tubular member cross-sections (diameter-to-thickness ratio check).
- Design tubular joints (chord can thickness, brace-to-chord angle).
- Determine pile penetration and cross-section.
- Assess fatigue life under cyclic wave loading (S-N curve approach with Miner's rule).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Morison Equation (Step-by-Step)

The Morison equation represents the **total horizontal wave force per unit length** on a slender vertical cylinder. It is derived by superposing two physical mechanisms:

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual basis:** As a wave passes a vertical pile, it does two things: (1) it pushes the pile sideways (drag from the moving water), and (2) it accelerates the water mass around the pile (inertia).

### Step 1 — Define the Control Volume

Consider a vertical cylinder of diameter $D$ in a flow of velocity $u$ and acceleration $\dot{u}$.

### Step 2 — Express Drag (Empirical, Dimensional)

Drag force is proportional to the **dynamic pressure** of the fluid times the **projected area**:

$$F_D \propto \rho_w u^2 D$$

Introducing a dimensionless drag coefficient $C_D$ to account for flow separation, wake formation, and Reynolds number effects:

$$F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w C_D D \vert u \vert u$$

(The $\vert u \vert$ is required to keep the sign of $F_D$ opposite to motion direction.)

### Step 3 — Express Inertia (from Potential Flow + Added Mass)

For an inviscid, incompressible, irrotational flow past a cylinder, the force per unit length on the cylinder is:

$$F_I = \rho_w \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \dot{u}$$

This is essentially **Newton's second law** for the displaced water mass. Adding the empirical inertia coefficient $C_M$ (which corrects for viscous and 3-D effects; for a smooth cylinder $C_M \approx 1.5$–$2.0$):

$$F_I = \rho_w C_M A \dot{u}, \quad A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

### Step 4 — Superpose the Two Effects

The total in-line force per unit length is the sum of the drag and inertia components:

$$\boxed{F(z,t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_w C_D D \vert u(z,t) \vert u(z,t) + \rho_w C_M A \dot{u}(z,t)}$$

This is the **Morison Equation** (1950).

## 3.2 Worked Example: Computing Base Shear in a Jacket

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This problem type is a KTU favourite — typical 7 to 14 mark question.**

### Problem Statement

A 4-legged jacket stands in **water depth h = 80 m**. The legs have diameter **D = 1.5 m**. A regular Airy wave has height **H = 12 m** and period **T = 10 s**. Compute the **maximum horizontal force on a single leg** at elevation $z = -10$ m below SWL. Use $C_D = 0.7$, $C_M = 1.6$, $\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

### Step 1 — Compute the Wavelength

Solve the dispersion relation iteratively:

$$\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kh), \quad \omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{T} = 0.6283 \ \text{rad/s}$$

Assume deep-water approximation $L_0 = gT^2 / (2\pi) = 78.5$ m. Iterate using Fenton & McKee (1990) explicit formula or trial. Final value: $L \approx \mathbf{91.4 \ m}$ (for $h/L \approx 0.875$, finite-depth regime).

### Step 2 — Compute Wave Number

$$k = \dfrac{2\pi}{L} = \dfrac{2\pi}{91.4} = 0.0688 \ \text{m}^{-1}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Hyperbolic Factors

$$kh = 0.0688 \times 80 = 5.50, \quad k(h+z) = 0.0688 \times 70 = 4.81$$

$$\cosh(5.50) \approx 122.0, \quad \cosh(4.81) \approx 61.9$$

### Step 4 — Compute Particle Velocity at Crest Passage

For Airy wave, the maximum horizontal velocity at $z = -10$ m occurs when $\cos(kx - \omega t) = 1$:

$$u_{max} = \dfrac{H g T \cosh(k(h+z))}{2 L \cosh(kh)} = \dfrac{12 \times 9.81 \times 10 \times 61.9}{2 \times 91.4 \times 122.0}$$

$$u_{max} = \dfrac{7288}{22302} = 0.327 \ \text{m/s}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Particle Acceleration

The maximum acceleration occurs when $\sin(kx - \omega t) = 1$:

$$\dot{u}_{max} = \dfrac{H g \pi \cosh(k(h+z))}{L \cosh(kh)} = \dfrac{12 \times 9.81 \times \pi \times 61.9}{91.4 \times 122.0}$$

$$\dot{u}_{max} = \dfrac{22877}{11151} = 2.052 \ \text{m/s}^2$$

### Step 6 — Compute Drag Force per Unit Length

$$F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(0.7)(1.5)(0.327)^2 = 57.6 \ \text{N/m}$$

### Step 7 — Compute Inertia Force per Unit Length

$$F_I = (1025)(1.6)\left(\dfrac{\pi (1.5)^2}{4}\right)(2.052) = 2976 \ \text{N/m}$$

### Step 8 — Total Force per Unit Length

$$F = 57.6 + 2976 = \mathbf{3034 \ N/m \approx 3.03 \ kN/m}$$

### Step 9 — Note: Maximum Combined Force

Since drag is non-linear, the absolute maximum does not occur when both peaks align. Numerical maximum (scan $0 \le \theta \le 2\pi$):

$$F_{max} \approx 1.05 \times (F_D^{max} + F_I^{max}) \approx \mathbf{3.18 \ kN/m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inertia-dominated regime:** Note that $F_I \gg F_D$ (≈ 50× larger) — this is typical for **small-to-medium diameter members in deep water** (Keulegan-Carpenter number $KC < 10$ → inertia-dominated). Drag dominates for **large diameter** members.

### Step 10 — Total Force on One Leg (per wavelength, integrating along depth)

By integrating $F(z,t)$ from $z = -80$ m to $z = 0$ m at the instant of maximum base shear (typically $\theta = 90°$ for inertia-dominated), the total horizontal force on a single leg is approximately:

$$F_{leg} \approx 300 \ \text{kN (per leg, at instant of maximum load)}$$

For 4 legs, base shear $V \approx \mathbf{1.2 \ MN}$ (excluding bracing contributions).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Wave Theory

| Water Depth / Wavelength Ratio | Applicable Theory | Application |
|----------------------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| $h/L > 0.5$ (deep) | **Airy / Linear** | Deep water, low-steepness waves |
| $0.05 < h/L < 0.5$ (intermediate) | **Stokes 2nd or 5th Order** | Most jackets in 50–200 m water |
| $h/L < 0.05$ (shallow) | **Cnoidal / Solitary** | Shallow Gulf of Mexico, South China Sea |
| $H/gT^2 > 0.06$ (steep) | **Stream Function** | Steep storm waves |
| Breaking wave zone | **Hydraulic / Boussinesq** | Nearshore platforms |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Jacket Platform — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ENVIRONMENTAL LOADS] --> A1[Wind on Topside]
    A --> A2[Wave and Current on Jacket]
    A --> A3[Earthquake Inertial]
    B[GRAVITY LOADS] --> B1[Topside Equipment]
    B --> B2[Jacket Self-Weight]
    B --> B3[Pile and Grout]
    C[JACKET FRAME] --> C1[Legs 4 or 6 nos]
    C --> C2[Horizontal Braces]
    C --> C3[Diagonal X Braces]
    C --> C4[Conductor Guides]

    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[Embedment 5D to 8D in Soil]
    A2 --> C1
    A2 --> C2
    A2 --> C3
    B1 --> C1
    C1 --> E[BASE SHEAR AND OVERTURNING TO SEABED]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    D1 --> E
```

## 4.2 Jacket Design Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[INPUT DATA: Metocean Soil Bathymetry] --> P2[CONCEPTUAL DESIGN]
    P2 --> P3[GLOBAL MODEL SETUP]
    P3 --> P4[LOAD COMPUTATION: Wave Wind Current EQ]
    P4 --> P5[INPLACE ANALYSIS]
    P5 --> P6[MEMBER SIZING]
    P6 --> P7[TUBULAR JOINT CHECK]
    P7 --> P8[PILE DESIGN]
    P8 --> P9[TRANSPORTATION CHECK]
    P9 --> P10[INSTALLATION LIFT CHECK]
    P10 --> P11[FATIGUE ANALYSIS]
    P11 --> P12[FINAL DETAILING]
    P12 --> P13[APPROVAL AND ISSUE FOR CONSTRUCTION]
```

## 4.3 Tubular Joint Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J[TUBULAR JOINTS] --> J1[T Joints: 90 deg brace to chord]
    J --> J2[Y Joints: 45 deg brace to chord]
    J --> J3[K Joints: Load bearing gap or overlap]
    J --> J4[DT Joints: Double T configuration]
    J1 --> JT[Simple Joints]
    J2 --> JT
    J3 --> JC[Complex or Multiplanar Joints]
    J4 --> JC
```

## 4.4 Schematic Cross-Section of a Typical Jacket Leg (Mudline Region)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Topside Deck Elevation +30 m] --> L2[Waterline 0 m]
    L2 --> L3[Wave Zone 0 to minus 20 m]
    L3 --> L4[Splash Zone minus 20 m to minus 40 m]
    L4 --> L5[Mudline minus 80 m]
    L5 --> L6[Pile Sleeve Grouted Connection]
    L6 --> L7[Pile Tip minus 130 m in Soil]
    L4 --> LF[Fatigue Critical Zone]
    L3 --> LC[Corrosion and Marine Growth Zone]
    L2 --> LD[Wave Slam Zone]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **splash zone** (alternately wet and dry) is the most corrosion-prone and is protected by **monopile corrosion clamps** or **cathodic protection (CP) anodes** (typically Al-In-Zn alloy). The **mudline region** is the fatigue-critical zone for jacket legs.

## 4.5 Pile-to-Jacket Connection (Grouted Connection)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pile Outer Dia 2.0 m] --> G1[Grout Annulus 50 to 150 mm]
    G1 --> J1[Jacket Leg Inner Dia 2.3 m]
    J1 --> S1[Shear Keys Welded]
    S1 --> L1[Load Transfer by Bearing and Friction]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Jacket" in the context of offshore structures. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **jacket** is a welded steel space-frame substructure of a fixed offshore platform. It typically consists of **4 to 8 tubular legs** interconnected by horizontal and diagonal bracing, and is supported on **driven steel piles** that pass through its legs into the seabed. The jacket transmits all **environmental loads** (wave, wind, current, seismic) and **operational loads** (gravity from topsides, MLC, drilling) from the deck to the foundation piles. According to API RP 2A, jackets are designed for a service life of **25–30 years**, with operating conditions using a 100-year return period storm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always mention the pile connection — students often forget the piles and lose 1 mark.

---

### Question 2: What is the Morison equation? State its applicability. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Morison equation** is a semi-empirical formula for calculating the **horizontal wave force per unit length on a slender vertical cylinder** (typical of jacket bracing and legs). It superposes two force components: a **drag force** (proportional to $u^2$) and an **inertia force** (proportional to $\dot{u}$):

$$F = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_w C_D D \vert u \vert u + \rho_w C_M A \dot{u}$$

**Applicability:** Valid for **slender members** where the diameter $D$ is much smaller than the wavelength $L$ (specifically $D/L < 0.2$). It assumes the **flow remains attached** and that **wave scattering** is negligible (no diffraction). Typical values: $C_D \approx 0.6$ to $1.2$, $C_M \approx 1.3$ to $2.0$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has TWO sub-parts: (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) With neat sketches, explain the different types of jacket platforms based on configuration. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

Jacket platforms are classified based on **leg configuration** and **bracing arrangement**:

1. **Four-Legged Jacket (Conventional):**
   - Most common; 4 main legs at the corners of a square or rectangular plan.
   - Typical spacing: 10–20 m at top, tapering at bottom (battered).
   - Used in water depths 30–250 m.

2. **Three-Legged Jacket (Tripod):**
   - 3 legs, more efficient material use.
   - Used in shallow water (≤ 100 m) or for satellite wellhead platforms.
   - Less stability against torsion; more cost-effective for small topsides.

3. **Six- or Eight-Legged Jacket:**
   - Used for very heavy topsides (e.g., production platforms with > 30,000 t topsides).
   - Provides redundancy and lower leg loads.

4. **Bracing Types:**
   - **X-bracing:** Most efficient, but joints are complex.
   - **K-bracing:** Reduces joint complexity, lower effective bracing length.
   - **Diagonal-only bracing:** Used in deep water where member length governs.

5. **Battered vs. Vertical Legs:**
   - **Battered (tapered):** Wider at base → better overturning resistance.
   - **Vertical:** Simpler pile connection, used in deep water (≥ 200 m).

**Sketch (Mermaid representation):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    J4[Four Legged Jacket] --> S1[Square Plan 15 by 15 m]
    J3[Three Legged Jacket] --> S2[Triangular Plan]
    J6[Six Legged Jacket] --> S3[Rectangular Plan 30 by 20 m]
    S1 --> B1[Battered Legs]
    S1 --> B2[Vertical Legs]
    B1 --> X1[X Bracing]
    B1 --> K1[K Bracing]
    B2 --> X1
    B2 --> K1
```

#### (b) Explain the various phases of design of a jacket platform. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

The design of a jacket platform is verified at **three critical phases**:

**Phase 1 — In-Place (Operational) Phase:**
- Platform is installed in its final location and is operating.
- Loads: Self-weight, topside weight, MLC, hydrostatic, wind, wave, current, earthquake.
- Members are checked for strength, stability, fatigue, and tubular joint capacity.
- **This is the most critical phase for long-term integrity.**

**Phase 2 — Transportation Phase:**
- Jacket is transported on a **cargo barge** or by **self-floatation** (using temporary buoyancy tanks).
- For self-floating jackets, wave-induced bending and hogging/sagging stresses are checked.
- For barge-transported jackets, **sea-fastenings** are designed to transfer jacket weight to the barge during tow.
- **Period of return period storm considered:** typically 10 to 25 years (shorter exposure = lower return period).

**Phase 3 — Installation (Lifting & Upending) Phase:**
- Jacket is lifted from the transport vessel by **crane(s)** or upended by **controlled ballasting** (flooding of buoyancy legs).
- **Dynamic Amplification Factor (DAF) = 1.0 to 1.35** is applied to account for crane motion, sea state, and impact.
- For single-crane lifts: **DAF = 1.0 to 1.15** (calm sea).
- For tandem lifts: **DAF = 1.15 to 1.35**.
- Lifting trunnions, pad-eyes, and slings are designed with adequate redundancy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention **fatigue analysis** as a separate check. Fatigue must be done for the in-place phase under **cyclic wave loading** using **S-N curves** and **Miner's rule** with a **100-year fatigue life** assumption. Marks lost: ~1 to 2.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Derive the Morison equation for wave force on a vertical pile. State the assumptions and range of validity. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The member is **slender** ($D/L < 0.2$), so the wave does not diffract significantly around the member.
2. The flow is **incompressible** and **two-dimensional**.
3. The **drag and inertia** components are linearly superposable.
4. The **drag coefficient $C_D$** and **inertia coefficient $C_M$** are constants (empirical).

**Derivation:**

Consider a vertical cylinder of diameter $D$ submerged in a flow with horizontal water particle velocity $u(z,t)$ and acceleration $\dot{u}(z,t)$.

**Component 1 — Drag Force (Empirical):**
By dimensional analysis, drag is proportional to the dynamic pressure and the projected area per unit length ($D \times 1$):

$$F_D \propto \rho_w u^2 D \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_w C_D D \vert u \vert u$$

The factor ½ comes from the standard drag formulation (dynamic pressure $= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho u^2$). The $\vert u \vert$ ensures the force opposes motion.

**Component 2 — Inertia Force (from potential flow):**
From inviscid potential flow past a circular cylinder, the force per unit length on a stationary cylinder in an accelerating flow is:

$$F_I = \rho_w A \dot{u}, \quad A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

This is the force required to accelerate the **displaced water mass**. Adding the empirical correction $C_M$:

$$F_I = \rho_w C_M A \dot{u}$$

**Superposition:**

$$\boxed{F = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_w C_D D \vert u \vert u + \rho_w C_M A \dot{u}}$$

**Range of Validity:**
- $D/L < 0.2$ (slender member assumption).
- Reynolds number $Re > 10^3$ (turbulent flow regime).
- Keulegan-Carpenter number $KC = u_{max} T / D < 30$ (for constant $C_D$, $C_M$).
- Member is vertical or near-vertical; for inclined members, normal and tangential components are computed separately.

#### (b) A vertical pile of diameter 1.2 m is subjected to a regular Airy wave of height 6 m and period 8 s in 40 m water depth. Calculate the maximum total wave force per unit length on the pile at the still water level (SWL). Use $C_D = 0.7$, $C_M = 1.5$, $\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Wavelength (L):**
From dispersion: $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kh)$, with $\omega = 2\pi/T = 0.7854$ rad/s and $h = 40$ m.

Iterate (or use Fenton-McKee explicit formula). For $h = 40$ m, $T = 8$ s:
$$L \approx 71.5 \ \text{m} \quad (\text{final iterated value})$$

**Step 2 — Compute Wave Kinematics at SWL ($z = 0$):**

$$k = 2\pi/L = 0.0879 \ \text{m}^{-1}, \quad kh = 0.0879 \times 40 = 3.515$$

$$\cosh(0) = 1, \quad \cosh(3.515) = 17.84$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Horizontal Velocity at SWL:**

$$u_{max} = \dfrac{H g T \cosh(kh)}{2 L \cosh(kh)} = \dfrac{6 \times 9.81 \times 8}{2 \times 71.5} = \dfrac{470.9}{143} = 3.293 \ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Horizontal Acceleration at SWL:**

$$\dot{u}_{max} = \dfrac{H g \pi \cosh(kh)}{L \cosh(kh)} = \dfrac{6 \times 9.81 \times \pi}{71.5} = \dfrac{184.9}{71.5} = 2.586 \ \text{m/s}^2$$

**Step 5 — Drag Force per Unit Length at SWL:**

$$F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(0.7)(1.2)(3.293)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(0.7)(1.2)(10.84)$$

$$F_D = 4665 \ \text{N/m} \approx 4.67 \ \text{kN/m}$$

**Step 6 — Inertia Force per Unit Length at SWL:**

$$F_I = (1025)(1.5)\left(\dfrac{\pi (1.2)^2}{4}\right)(2.586) = (1025)(1.5)(1.131)(2.586)$$

$$F_I = 4496 \ \text{N/m} \approx 4.50 \ \text{kN/m}$$

**Step 7 — Maximum Total Force (numerical search over wave phase):**

Scan $F(\theta) = F_D(\theta) + F_I(\theta)$ for $\theta \in [0, 2\pi]$. Because drag is non-linear ($\propto u^2$), the absolute maximum is approximately:

$$F_{max} \approx 1.10 \times (F_D^{max} + F_I^{max}) \approx 1.10 \times (4.67 + 4.50)$$

$$\boxed{F_{max} \approx 10.07 \ \text{kN/m} \ \text{at the SWL}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Errors in KTU Exam:**
> 1. Forgetting the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ factor in drag → error of 50%.
> 2. Using $A = \pi D$ (incorrect; should be $\pi D^2/4$).
> 3. Confusing **maximum velocity** with **maximum force** — they do not occur at the same wave phase because of the non-linear drag term.
> 4. Forgetting the $\vert u \vert$ on velocity in drag term (loses sign consistency).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3, Jacket Design**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- [x] Jacket = tubular steel space frame on driven steel piles.
- [x] Designed for **3 phases**: In-Place, Transportation, Installation.
- [x] Code basis: **API RP 2A**, **ISO 19902**, **DNV-OS-C201**.

### ✅ Key Equations
- [x] **Morison equation:** $F = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_w C_D D \vert u \vert u + \rho_w C_M A \dot{u}$
- [x] **Airy wave velocity** and **acceleration** (cosh / cosh factors).
- [x] **API WSD allowable axial stress:** $F_a = 0.6 F_y$.
- [x] **Pile axial capacity:** $Q_d = Q_f + Q_p$.

### ✅ Critical Numbers
- [x] $\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³, $\rho_{st} = 7850$ kg/m³, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa.
- [x] $C_D \approx 0.65$ (smooth), $C_M \approx 1.5$ (smooth).
- [x] 100-year return period (operating), 10,000-year (survival).
- [x] DAF = 1.0 to 1.35 (installation).

### ✅ Hot-Spot for KTU Questions
- [x] **Derivation of Morison equation** (favourite 7-mark question).
- [x] **Numerical base shear / force on a leg** (compute $u$, $\dot{u}$, $F_D$, $F_I$).
- [x] **Phases of design** + **loads in each phase**.
- [x] **Tubular joints** (T, Y, K types) and API punching shear check.
- [x] **Pile design** (skin friction + end bearing).

### ✅ Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- [x] Confusing drag and inertia dominance (small D = inertia; large D = drag).
- [x] Forgetting to apply **marine growth** allowance (50–150 mm) on diameter.
- [x] Ignoring **fatigue** in in-place design.
- [x] Missing the **1.05–1.10** combined force factor when drag and inertia peak together.

> **End of Notes — PCNST602 Module 3: Jacket Design**

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Offshore Pile Design** is the engineered process of determining the geometry, embedment depth, material grade, and structural dimensions of a long, slender structural member (a pile) that is driven, drilled, or suction-installed into the seabed to transfer superstructure loads from a jacket, monopile, or gravity platform safely into the deeper, competent subsoil strata.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCNST602 - Module 3):**
> *Pile design* in the offshore context involves establishing the **axial (tensile and compressive) capacity**, **lateral (transverse) capacity**, and **structural buckling resistance** of foundation piles used to anchor fixed offshore platforms, compliant towers, and subsea structures. The design is governed primarily by **API RP 2A (Planning, Designing, and Constructing Fixed Offshore Platforms)** and **ISO 19902** working stress design philosophy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Underground Tent Peg" Intuition

Imagine driving a long steel peg into the soft ground to anchor a large beach umbrella against strong winds.

- The **umbrella** = the offshore platform (jacket or topside)
- The **wind pushing sideways** = wave, current, and wind-induced lateral forces
- The **rope tying the umbrella to the peg** = the structural connection at the pile head
- The **soft topsoil** = the weak marine clay at the seabed
- The **firm sand layer below** = the competent bearing stratum

Just as the tent peg must be deep enough that:
1. The **side friction** along its surface (skin friction) resists being pulled out.
2. The **pointed tip** resting on firm ground resists being pushed down.
3. The **embedded length** prevents the peg from bending sideways in the wind.

The offshore engineer must satisfy exactly these three modes of failure for a pile under combined **axial + lateral + moment** loading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Philosophy:**
> Unlike onshore piles, offshore piles are subjected to **cyclic storm loading** (100-year return period hurricane/wave), **scour around the pile**, **seismic loading** in active zones, and **installation stresses** from hammer driving. Hence, design is governed by a **multiple safety factor** approach: 2.0 for axial compression, 3.0 for axial tension (uplift), and 1.5 for combined loading under operating conditions.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Offshore Value |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------------|
| Pile outer diameter | $D$ | 0.6 m – 3.0 m (24" – 118") |
| Wall thickness | $t$ | 25 mm – 100 mm |
| Embedment length | $L$ | 30 m – 150 m |
| Length-to-diameter ratio | $L/D$ | 20 – 60 |
| Submerged unit weight of soil | $\gamma'$ | 5 – 11 kN/m³ |
| Soil-pile adhesion factor | $\alpha$ | 0.3 – 1.0 |
| API soil-bearing factor | $N_q$ | 8 – 50 (sands) |
| Design wave return period | $T_R$ | 100 years |
| Steel yield strength | $f_y$ | 250 – 450 MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test the distinction between **API RP 2A-WSD** (Working Stress Design, traditional) and **API RP 2A-LRFD** (Load and Resistance Factor Design). Module 3 focuses on **WSD methodology**, where allowable stresses are obtained by dividing ultimate capacity by a factor of safety.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Modes of Failure for an Offshore Pile

An offshore pile under combined loading can fail in **five distinct modes**. The design must check all of them:

1. **Axial Compression Failure** — Insufficient skin friction + end bearing → pile punches downward into the soil.
2. **Axial Tension (Uplift) Failure** — Insufficient skin friction → pile is pulled out of the seabed.
3. **Lateral (Transverse) Failure** — Soil yields around the pile → pile translates horizontally, causing platform overturning.
4. **Structural Buckling** — Long, slender pile in soft soil column buckles under compressive load.
5. **Combined Load Failure** — Interaction of axial and lateral loads exceeds the failure envelope.

## 2.2 Axial Pile Capacity — The Two-Component Model

The total axial capacity $Q$ of a driven offshore pile is the **sum** of:
1. **Skin Friction (Shaft Resistance):** $Q_s$ — resistance along the cylindrical pile-soil interface.
2. **End Bearing (Point Resistance):** $Q_p$ — resistance at the pile tip.

### 2.2.1 Skin Friction in Cohesive Soils (API RP 2A)

$$Q_s = f_s \cdot A_s = (\alpha \cdot c_u) \cdot (\pi \cdot D \cdot L)$$

Where:
- $c_u$ = undrained shear strength of clay (kPa)
- $\alpha$ = adhesion factor (function of $c_u$, from API Chart)
- $D$ = pile outer diameter
- $L$ = embedded length
- $A_s = \pi D L$ = lateral surface area

> [!NOTE]
> **API Adhesion Factor Rule:**
> $\alpha = 0.5 \cdot \psi^{-0.5}$ for $\psi = c_u / p_o' \le 1.0$, where $p_o'$ is the effective overburden pressure. Capped at $\alpha = 1.0$.

### 2.2.2 Skin Friction in Cohesionless Soils (API RP 2A)

$$Q_s = \int_0^L f_s \cdot \pi D \cdot dz, \quad f_s = K \cdot p_o' \cdot \tan\delta$$

Where:
- $K$ = coefficient of lateral earth pressure (0.8 for driven piles, displacement type)
- $p_o'$ = effective vertical overburden pressure
- $\delta$ = soil-pile friction angle ($\approx 0.8 \phi'$ for steel piles)

### 2.2.3 End Bearing

$$Q_p = q_p \cdot A_p$$

- **For clays:** $q_p = 9 \cdot c_u$ (API recommendation for driven piles)
- **For sands:** $q_p = p_o' \cdot N_q$ (where $N_q$ from API bearing capacity chart, function of $\phi'$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Limit:** The API code limits $f_s$ to a maximum of **150 kPa** for clays and the unit end bearing to **9 c_u** to prevent over-conservative design in stiff soils.

## 2.3 Lateral Pile Capacity — The p-y Curve Method

For lateral loading (which dominates offshore jacket design under storm waves), the **p-y curve method** is the industry standard.

- **p** = soil reaction per unit length (kN/m)
- **y** = lateral pile deflection (m)
- The p-y curve is a **nonlinear soil spring** whose shape depends on:
  - Soil type (sand, soft clay, stiff clay)
  - Depth below mudline
  - Static vs. cyclic loading

For **soft clay under static loading (Matlock, 1970):**

$$\frac{p}{p_u} = \begin{cases} 0.5 \left( \dfrac{y}{y_c} \right)^{1/3} & \text{for } y \le 8 y_c \\ 1.0 & \text{for } y > 8 y_c \end{cases}$$

Where:
- $p_u$ = ultimate soil reaction = $N_p \cdot c_u \cdot D$ (with $N_p$ from 3 to 9)
- $y_c$ = characteristic deflection = $2.5 \varepsilon_{50} D$
- $\varepsilon_{50}$ = strain at 50% stress in UU triaxial test

For **sand under static loading (Reese, 1974):** A trilinear p-y curve is constructed using:

$$p_u = \min \left[ (C_1 z + C_2 D)\gamma' z, \; C_3 D \gamma' z \right]$$

Where $C_1, C_2, C_3$ are empirical coefficients from API tables (function of $\phi'$ and depth $z$).

## 2.4 Pile Buckling Capacity

For long piles in soft marine clay, the critical buckling load is:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(K L_e)^2}$$

Where:
- $E$ = Young's modulus of steel ($\approx 200$ GPa)
- $I$ = moment of inertia of pile cross-section
- $L_e$ = unsupported length (in soft clay, often taken as 0 for fully embedded case)
- $K$ = effective length factor (0.5 to 1.0 for fixed-free conditions)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Insight:**
> The actual buckling check uses a **P-δ** (beam-column) analysis with soil springs (p-y curves) representing the lateral support. This is typically run using software like **LPILE**, **FB-Pier**, or **OpenSees**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---------|----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Skin friction (clay) | $Q_s = \alpha c_u \pi D L$ | kN | Axial tension/compression |
| 2 | Skin friction (sand) | $f_s = K p_o' \tan\delta$ | kPa | Unit skin friction |
| 3 | End bearing (clay) | $q_p = 9 c_u$ | kPa | Tip resistance |
| 4 | End bearing (sand) | $q_p = N_q p_o'$ | kPa | Tip resistance |
| 5 | Total axial capacity | $Q_u = Q_s + Q_p$ | kN | Compression only |
| 6 | Tension capacity | $Q_t = Q_s$ (no end bearing) | kN | Uplift |
| 7 | Allowable load | $Q_a = Q_u / FS$ | kN | WSD check |
| 8 | Ultimate lateral resistance (clay) | $p_u = N_p c_u D$ | kN/m | p-y curve |
| 9 | Critical buckling | $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / (K L_e)^2$ | kN | Slender pile check |
| 10 | Scour depth (API) | $S/D = 1.3$ (rivers), 0 (deep sea) | m | Local scour |
| 11 | API clay adhesion | $\alpha = 0.5(c_u/p_o')^{-0.5}$, capped at 1.0 | – | Soft clay |
| 12 | Broms ultimate moment (free head) | $M_u = 2.25 D \gamma' L^3$ (sand) | kN·m | Quick check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Use of this System:**
> These equations form the **backbone of every offshore platform pile design** submitted to certifying authorities such as **DNV, ABS, Lloyd's, or Bureau Veritas**. A single jacket platform may have **4 to 16 piles**, each designed using these exact formulas iteratively until allowable stresses are not exceeded under the design environmental load combinations.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: API Cohesionless Soil Skin Friction Limit

The API RP 2A code imposes an **upper bound** on unit skin friction for sands to prevent over-prediction in very dense or very loose sands. The limit is based on the **effective stress concept** but capped empirically:

**Step 1: Define the basic unit skin friction**
$$f_s = K \cdot p_o' \cdot \tan\delta$$

**Step 2: Apply the API limit curve**
API provides a tabulated maximum $f_s$ as a function of depth, fitting a curve:
$$f_{s,\max}(z) = k \cdot z \quad \text{(linear in shallow zone)}$$
$$f_{s,\max}(z) = f_{s,L} \quad \text{(constant at greater depths)}$$

Where $k$ is the slope (kPa/m) and $f_{s,L}$ is the limiting value (typically 115 kPa for non-displacement piles, 200 kPa for displacement piles).

**Step 3: Construct the piecewise limiting function**

$$
f_{s,\text{API}}(z) = \begin{cases}
K \cdot p_o'(z) \cdot \tan\delta, & \text{calc} < f_{s,\max}(z) \\
f_{s,\max}(z), & \text{calc} \ge f_{s,\max}(z)
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4: Integrate along pile length**
$$
Q_s = \int_0^L f_{s,\text{API}}(z) \cdot \pi D \cdot dz
$$

**Step 5: Final design check**
$$
Q_{\text{allow}} = \frac{Q_s + Q_p}{FS}, \quad FS = 2.0 \text{ (compression)}, \quad 3.0 \text{ (tension)}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Critical Embedment Length for Lateral Stability (Broms Simplified Method)

For a **short, rigid pile in sand** under a lateral head load $H$ and moment $M$, Broms derived the ultimate load:

**Step 1: Geometry of failure**
The pile rotates about a point at depth $z_0$. Passive earth pressure mobilises on the front face below the rotation point.

**Step 2: Passive earth pressure distribution**
The Rankine passive pressure is:
$$p_p = K_p \cdot \gamma' \cdot z, \quad K_p = \tan^2(45 + \phi'/2)$$

**Step 3: Resultant of passive wedge below $z_0$**
$$P_p = \frac{1}{2} K_p \gamma' D (L - z_0)^2$$

**Step 4: Equilibrium of horizontal forces**
$$H = P_p = \frac{1}{2} K_p \gamma' D (L - z_0)^2$$

**Step 5: Equilibrium of moments about the rotation point**
Taking moments about the pile head:
$$H(e + z_0) = M + \frac{1}{2} P_p (L - z_0) / 3$$

**Step 6: Solve for ultimate load $H_u$ (free-head condition)**
Substituting and solving the quadratic:

$$
H_u = \frac{K_p \gamma' D L^3}{2(e + L)} \quad \text{(simplified Broms free-head formula)}
$$

**Step 7: Derive the maximum bending moment location**
Differentiating the moment equation and setting it to zero gives the depth of maximum moment at:
$$z_{M,\max} = 0.74 L \quad \text{(for free-head, short pile)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:**
> This Broms derivation is one of the **most frequently asked 14-mark questions** in Module 3, as it combines soil mechanics, statics, and code application in a single problem.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Axial Capacity Calculation

**Given:**
- Pile: $D = 1.0$ m, $L = 30$ m, open-ended steel pipe pile
- Soil profile: Soft clay from 0–15 m, dense sand from 15–30 m
- Clay: $c_u = 50$ kPa (avg), $\gamma' = 7$ kN/m³
- Sand: $\phi' = 35°$, $\gamma' = 10$ kN/m³
- API factors: $\alpha = 0.8$ in clay, $N_q = 40$ at tip, $K = 0.8$, $\delta = 0.8 \phi' = 28°$

**Find:** Allowable axial compression capacity with FS = 2.0.

**Step 1: Skin friction in clay (0 to 15 m)**
$$
Q_{s,\text{clay}} = \alpha \cdot c_u \cdot \pi D L_1 = 0.8 \times 50 \times \pi \times 1.0 \times 15
$$
$$
Q_{s,\text{clay}} = 0.8 \times 50 \times 3.1416 \times 15 = 1884.96 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2: Skin friction in sand (15 to 30 m)**
First, compute average overburden at mid-depth of sand layer (z = 22.5 m):
$$
p_o' = (7 \times 15) + (10 \times 7.5) = 105 + 75 = 180 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
Q_{s,\text{sand}} = K p_o' \tan\delta \cdot \pi D L_2 = 0.8 \times 180 \times \tan(28°) \times \pi \times 1.0 \times 15
$$
$$
Q_{s,\text{sand}} = 0.8 \times 180 \times 0.5317 \times 3.1416 \times 15
$$
$$
Q_{s,\text{sand}} = 0.8 \times 180 \times 0.5317 \times 47.124 = 3606.2 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3: End bearing at tip (z = 30 m)**
$$
q_p = N_q \cdot p_o'(z=30) = 40 \times [7 \times 15 + 10 \times 15] = 40 \times 255 = 10200 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
Q_p = q_p \cdot A_p = 10200 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(1.0)^2 = 10200 \times 0.7854 = 8010.9 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4: Total ultimate capacity**
$$
Q_u = Q_{s,\text{clay}} + Q_{s,\text{sand}} + Q_p = 1884.96 + 3606.2 + 8010.9 = 13502.1 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5: Allowable capacity (FS = 2.0)**
$$
Q_a = \frac{Q_u}{2.0} = \frac{13502.1}{2.0} = 6751.0 \text{ kN} \approx 6.75 \text{ MN}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Interpretation:**
> This single 1.0 m diameter, 30 m long pile can safely carry approximately **6.75 MN** in compression. For a 4-pile jacket group, the total jacket foundation capacity is roughly $4 \times 6.75 = 27$ MN — enough to anchor a small fixed platform in moderate water depths.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Failure Modes

| Failure Mode | Governing Equation | Critical Soil Property | Typical Factor of Safety | KTU Frequency |
|--------------|--------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|----------------|
| Axial compression | $Q_u = Q_s + Q_p$ | $c_u$ (clay), $\phi'$ (sand) | 2.0 | Very High |
| Axial tension (uplift) | $Q_t = Q_s$ | $c_u$, $\delta$ | 3.0 | High |
| Lateral (short pile) | Broms, $H_u \propto \gamma' D L^3$ | $\phi'$, $\gamma'$ | 1.5 | Very High |
| Lateral (long pile) | p-y curve, beam-column | $p_u$, $k_h$ | 1.5 | High |
| Buckling | $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / (K L_e)^2$ | $E$, soft clay | 1.67 | Medium |
| Cyclic degradation | $f_{s,\text{cyc}} = 0.5 f_{s,\text{stat}}$ | Cyclic test data | 2.0 | Medium |

## 4.1 Offshore Pile-Soil Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wave and Wind Loads on Platform] --> B[Platform Jacket Substructure]
    B --> C[Structural Connection at Pile Head]
    C --> D[Offshore Steel Pipe Pile]
    D --> D1[Above Mudline Free Length]
    D --> D2[Embedded Length in Soil]
    D1 --> E1[Lateral Loads Bending the Pile]
    D1 --> E2[Axial Loads Transferred to Soil]
    D2 --> F1[Skin Friction Resistance]
    D2 --> F2[End Bearing Resistance]
    D2 --> F3[Lateral Soil Reaction p-y Springs]
    E1 --> G1[Bending Moment Diagram]
    E2 --> G2[Axial Force Diagram]
    F1 --> H[Combined Resistance Model]
    F2 --> H
    F3 --> H
    G1 --> H
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[Compare with Allowable Capacity]
    I --> J1[Pile Design OK]
    I --> J2[Increase Length or Diameter]
    J1 --> K[Issue Construction Drawings]
    J2 --> C
```

## 4.2 Pile Design Calculation Flowchart (API WSD Methodology)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Pile Design Problem] --> B[Step 1: Define Platform Loads]
    B --> C[Step 2: Determine Soil Profile and Properties]
    C --> D[Step 3: Select Trial Pile Diameter D and Wall Thickness t]
    D --> E[Step 4: Compute Skin Friction Qs]
    E --> F1[Clay: Qs = alpha times cu times pi D L]
    E --> F2[Sand: Qs = integral of K p0 tan delta pi D dz]
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G[Step 5: Compute End Bearing Qp]
    G --> H1[Clay: Qp = 9 cu times Ap]
    G --> H2[Sand: Qp = Nq p0 times Ap]
    H1 --> I
    H2 --> I[Step 6: Calculate Ultimate Axial Capacity Qu]
    I --> J[Step 7: Check Axial Compression FS greater than 2.0]
    J --> K[Step 8: Check Axial Tension FS greater than 3.0]
    K --> L[Step 9: Perform p-y Lateral Analysis using LPILE]
    L --> M[Step 10: Check Maximum Bending Stress less than Allowable]
    M --> N[Step 11: Check Pile Buckling for Slender Piles]
    N --> O[Step 12: Apply Scour Reduction and Recompute]
    O --> P{All Checks Passed?}
    P --> Q1[Yes: Finalize Design and Drawings]
    P --> Q2[No: Increase D, t, or L and Restart from Step 3]
```

## 4.3 Pile-Soil Force Transfer Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pile Head: Axial P, Shear V, Moment M] --> B[Pile Cross-Section: Steel Pipe with D and t]
    B --> C[Soil Layer 1: Soft Clay 0 to L1]
    B --> D[Soil Layer 2: Dense Sand L1 to L2]
    C --> C1[Unit Skin Friction fs clay]
    C --> C2[Lateral Spring p clay]
    D --> D1[Unit Skin Friction fs sand]
    D --> D2[Lateral Spring p sand]
    D --> D3[End Bearing qp at Tip]
    C1 --> E[Total Shaft Resistance Qs]
    D1 --> E
    D3 --> F[Total Tip Resistance Qp]
    E --> G[Total Axial Resistance Qu = Qs + Qp]
    F --> G
    C2 --> H[Lateral Resistance per Unit Length]
    D2 --> H
    H --> I[Maximum Pile Deflection y and Moment Mmax]
    G --> J[Check Qa = Qu / FS greater than Applied P]
    I --> K[Check Mmax / Section Modulus less than Allowable Stress]
```

## 4.4 Cyclic Degradation Conceptual Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Static Skin Friction fs static] --> B[Apply Cyclic Storm Loading]
    B --> C[Soil-Pile Interface Degrades]
    C --> D[Cyclic Skin Friction fs cyc less than fs static]
    D --> E[Degradation Factor Df: 0.5 to 0.9]
    E --> F[Compute Post-Cyclic Capacity]
    F --> G[Verify: Post-Cyclic Qu greater than Maximum Cyclic Demand]
    G --> H1[Satisfied: Pile Safe]
    G --> H2[Not Satisfied: Increase Pile Length]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:**
> The flowcharts above abstract the physical reality of a pile in the seabed into logical engineering checks. In practice, these are implemented using **finite element software** (ABAQUS, SACS, LPILE) where the soil is represented as a series of nonlinear **Winkler springs** (p-y, t-z, and Q-z springs for lateral, shaft, and tip behaviour, respectively).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define skin friction and end bearing as they apply to offshore pile design. Which component contributes more to the axial capacity of a long driven pile in dense sand?**

**Model Answer:**
Skin friction, $Q_s$, is the resistance developed along the cylindrical surface of the pile due to the shear stress transferred between the pile wall and the surrounding soil. It is computed as $Q_s = f_s \cdot \pi D L$ where $f_s$ is the unit skin friction. End bearing, $Q_p$, is the resistance at the pile tip due to the soil's bearing capacity failure beneath the pile, computed as $Q_p = q_p \cdot A_p$.

For a long driven pile (e.g., $L/D > 30$) in dense sand, **skin friction contributes the majority (typically 70-90%) of the total axial capacity**, while end bearing contributes only 10-30%. This is why open-ended pipe piles are preferred offshore — the soil plugging inside the pile provides additional shaft resistance along the inner surface as well.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample]
**What is a p-y curve? Why is it preferred over simple subgrade reaction (constant k_h) for offshore pile design?**

**Model Answer:**
A **p-y curve** is a graphical representation of the nonlinear relationship between the soil reaction per unit length of pile (p, in kN/m) and the corresponding lateral pile deflection (y, in m) at a specific depth below the mudline. It is constructed from empirical correlations (Matlock for clay, Reese for sand) that capture the strain-softening, strain-hardening, and depth-dependent behaviour of marine soils.

It is preferred over a constant subgrade reaction (Winkler foundation with constant $k_h$) because:
1. Soil stiffness and ultimate resistance **vary significantly with depth** below the mudline.
2. The soil exhibits **nonlinear and inelastic** behaviour, especially at large deflections.
3. Cyclic storm loading causes **degradation** of stiffness, which p-y curves (e.g., cyclic Matlock) capture but a constant $k_h$ cannot.
4. API RP 2A explicitly mandates the use of p-y methods for offshore pile design certification.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Question A (a) [7 Marks]:** A driven open-ended steel pipe pile of diameter $D = 1.2$ m and embedded length $L = 40$ m is installed in a uniform soft marine clay with undrained shear strength $c_u = 80$ kPa and submerged unit weight $\gamma' = 8$ kN/m³. The pile wall thickness is $t = 38$ mm. Using API RP 2A methodology, compute:
1. The unit skin friction profile,
2. The total skin friction capacity $Q_s$,
3. The end bearing capacity $Q_p$, and
4. The allowable axial compression load with FS = 2.0.

**Question A (b) [7 Marks]:** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the construction of a p-y curve for soft clay under **static** loading as recommended by Matlock (1970). Define all terms used.

---

**Model Solution to Question A (a):**

**Given:**
- $D = 1.2$ m, $L = 40$ m, $t = 38$ mm
- $c_u = 80$ kPa (uniform)
- $\gamma' = 8$ kN/m³
- $\gamma_w = 10$ kN/m³ (for effective stress)
- API recommends $\alpha = 0.5 \psi^{-0.5}$ where $\psi = c_u / p_o' \le 1.0$

**Step 1: Compute effective overburden at the pile tip (z = 40 m)**
$$p_o'(z=40) = \gamma' \times z = 8 \times 40 = 320 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\psi$ at mid-depth (z = 20 m, for average adhesion)**
$$p_o'(20) = 8 \times 20 = 160 \text{ kPa}$$
$$\psi = \frac{c_u}{p_o'} = \frac{80}{160} = 0.5$$

**Step 3: Compute adhesion factor $\alpha$**
$$\alpha = 0.5 \times (0.5)^{-0.5} = 0.5 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.5}} = 0.5 \times 1.4142 = 0.7071$$

Check: $\alpha \le 1.0$ ✓

**Step 4: Unit skin friction**
$$f_s = \alpha \cdot c_u = 0.7071 \times 80 = 56.57 \text{ kPa}$$

Check API limit: $f_s \le 150$ kPa ✓

**Step 5: Total skin friction**
$$Q_s = f_s \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot L = 56.57 \times \pi \times 1.2 \times 40$$
$$Q_s = 56.57 \times 3.1416 \times 48 = 8529.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: End bearing capacity**
For driven pile in clay (API):
$$q_p = 9 \cdot c_u = 9 \times 80 = 720 \text{ kPa}$$
$$Q_p = q_p \cdot A_p = 720 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(1.2)^2 = 720 \times 1.1310 = 814.3 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7: Total ultimate axial capacity**
$$Q_u = Q_s + Q_p = 8529.6 + 814.3 = 9343.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 8: Allowable axial compression**
$$Q_a = \frac{Q_u}{FS} = \frac{9343.9}{2.0} = 4671.95 \text{ kN} \approx 4.67 \text{ MN}$$

**Final Answer:** $Q_a \approx 4.67$ MN (compression)

---

**Model Solution to Question A (b):**

**Matlock (1970) p-y Curve for Soft Clay under Static Loading:**

The Matlock p-y curve construction proceeds as follows:

**Step 1: Compute ultimate soil resistance $p_u$**
$$p_u = N_p \cdot c_u \cdot D$$
where $N_p$ is the ultimate lateral resistance coefficient (function of depth $z$):
- $N_p = 3 + \frac{\gamma' z}{c_u} + 0.5 \frac{z}{D}$ (for $z < z_R$)
- $N_p = 9$ (constant, for $z \ge z_R$)
where $z_R = 6D / (\gamma' D / c_u)$ is the depth below which $p_u$ becomes constant at $9 c_u D$.

**Step 2: Compute characteristic deflection $y_c$**
$$y_c = 2.5 \cdot \varepsilon_{50} \cdot D$$
where $\varepsilon_{50}$ is the strain at 50% of maximum principal stress difference in a UU triaxial test (typically 0.005 to 0.02 for soft clays).

**Step 3: Construct the p-y curve piecewise**
$$
\frac{p}{p_u} = \begin{cases}
0.5 \left( \dfrac{y}{y_c} \right)^{1/3}, & y \le 8 y_c \\[6pt]
1.0, & y > 8 y_c
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4: Sketch description**
- X-axis: lateral deflection $y$
- Y-axis: soil reaction $p$ per unit length
- Origin at (0, 0)
- Initial cubic-shaped curve: $p/p_u = 0.5 (y/y_c)^{1/3}$ from origin to $(8y_c, p_u)$
- Plateau: $p = p_u$ for $y > 8y_c$
- The curve is symmetric in shape (it works the same in both +y and -y directions)
- Different depths $z$ give different $p_u$ values, producing a **family of curves**, deeper curves have higher $p_u$

**Diagram (ASCII representation):**
```
   p
   |
 p_u|-----------------____________________
   |              __/
   |           _/
   |         /
   |       /  (cubic, slope decreasing)
   |    _/
   |  _/
   | /
   |/_____________________________________ y
   0           8y_c
```

**Key Terms Defined:**
- $p$ = Soil reaction force per unit length of pile (kN/m)
- $y$ = Lateral deflection of pile (m)
- $c_u$ = Undrained shear strength of clay (kPa)
- $D$ = Pile diameter (m)
- $N_p$ = Lateral bearing capacity factor
- $\varepsilon_{50}$ = Strain at 50% maximum deviatoric stress in UU test
- $y_c$ = Reference deflection defining transition from elastic to plastic behaviour

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Question B (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **Broms method** for computing the ultimate lateral resistance of a **short rigid pile in sand** under a free-head condition. Derive the equation for the ultimate lateral load $H_u$ and identify the location of maximum bending moment.

**Question B (b) [7 Marks]:** A jacket platform is supported by **four piles**, each of diameter $D = 1.5$ m and length $L = 50$ m, driven into a uniform soft clay with $c_u = 60$ kPa and $\gamma' = 7$ kN/m³. The platform imposes a **maximum storm tensile load of 12 MN** at the base. Check whether the pile group has adequate uplift capacity using FS = 3.0. Use API $\alpha = 0.7$.

---

**Model Solution to Question B (a):**

**Broms Method — Short Rigid Pile in Sand (Free-Head):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Pile is **short and rigid** (rotation dominates over bending)
2. Failure occurs by **rotation about a point** at depth $z_0$ below mudline
3. Passive earth pressure fully mobilises on the front face of the embedded portion
4. Soil is cohesionless (sand) with $\phi'$ and $\gamma'$

**Step 1: Passive earth pressure distribution**
Using Rankine's passive pressure:
$$p_p(z) = K_p \cdot \gamma' \cdot z, \quad K_p = \tan^2\left(45° + \frac{\phi'}{2}\right)$$

**Step 2: Force equilibrium (horizontal)**
The net passive resistance below rotation point $z_0$ equals the applied lateral load:
$$H_u = \frac{1}{2} K_p \gamma' D (L - z_0)^2$$

**Step 3: Moment equilibrium (about rotation point)**
$$H_u (e + z_0) = M + \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2} K_p \gamma' D (L - z_0)^2 \cdot (L - z_0)$$

For free-head ($M = 0$ at top, but applied moment $M_a$ at distance $e$ above ground):
$$H_u (e + z_0) = M_a + \frac{1}{6} K_p \gamma' D (L - z_0)^3$$

**Step 4: Solve for $H_u$ (free-head, $e$ is load eccentricity above ground)**
Broms' final formula for free-head, short pile in sand:
$$
\boxed{H_u = \frac{0.5 \cdot K_p \cdot \gamma' \cdot D \cdot L^3}{e + L}}
$$

**Step 5: Maximum bending moment location**
For free-head, the maximum moment occurs at:
$$z_{M,\max} = 0.74 L \quad \text{(below mudline)}$$
$$M_{\max} = H_u \cdot \left( e + \frac{0.32 L}{\sqrt[3]{H_u / (K_p \gamma' D L^2)}} \right)$$

In many simplified texts:
$$M_{\max} \approx 0.21 H_u L \quad \text{(for free-head, short rigid pile)}$$

**Sketch (described in words):**
The pile rotates about a point at $0.74L$ below the mudline. The active wedge develops behind the pile in the upper portion, and the passive wedge develops in front of the pile in the lower portion. The maximum moment occurs at the rotation point.

---

**Model Solution to Question B (b):**

**Given:**
- 4 piles, $D = 1.5$ m, $L = 50$ m
- Soft clay, $c_u = 60$ kPa, $\gamma' = 7$ kN/m³
- Applied uplift (tension) load: $P_{\text{tension}} = 12$ MN
- $\alpha = 0.7$, FS = 3.0

**Step 1: Unit skin friction**
$$f_s = \alpha \cdot c_u = 0.7 \times 60 = 42 \text{ kPa}$$
Check API cap: $f_s \le 150$ kPa ✓ (OK)

**Step 2: Skin friction capacity per pile**
$$Q_{s,\text{per pile}} = f_s \cdot \pi D L = 42 \times \pi \times 1.5 \times 50 = 42 \times 3.1416 \times 75 = 9896.0 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Total skin friction capacity (4 piles)**
$$Q_{s,\text{total}} = 4 \times 9896.0 = 39584.0 \text{ kN} \approx 39.58 \text{ MN}$$

**Step 4: Tension capacity = skin friction only (no end bearing in uplift)**
$$Q_t = Q_{s,\text{total}} = 39.58 \text{ MN}$$

**Step 5: Allowable tension capacity**
$$Q_{t,\text{allow}} = \frac{Q_t}{FS} = \frac{39.58}{3.0} = 13.19 \text{ MN}$$

**Step 6: Compare with applied load**
$$Q_{t,\text{allow}} = 13.19 \text{ MN} \quad \text{vs} \quad P_{\text{tension}} = 12.0 \text{ MN}$$
$$13.19 > 12.0 \quad \therefore \text{ADEQUATE} \checkmark$$

**Step 7: Margin of safety**
$$\text{Utilization ratio} = \frac{12.0}{13.19} = 0.91 = 91\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Marks Lost Here:**
> 1. Students often **include end bearing in uplift capacity calculation**. This is WRONG — end bearing cannot resist tension. The KTU key deducts full 2 marks for this.
> 2. Students often **forget to use FS = 3.0 for tension** (using 2.0 like compression). The KTU key deducts 1 mark.
> 3. Students often **use the wrong $\alpha$ formula** without checking the API cap of 1.0.
> 4. Students must **explicitly state the unit of final answer** (MN) and **conclude "ADEQUATE" or "INADEQUATE"** to get the final 1 mark in the conclusion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Pile Design: One-Page Cheat Sheet for KTU Exam Day**

- ☐ **API RP 2A-WSD** is the governing code; **FS = 2.0 (compression), 3.0 (tension), 1.5 (combined).**
- ☐ **Axial capacity** = skin friction $Q_s$ + end bearing $Q_p$ (compression only).
- ☐ **Tension capacity** = skin friction only ($Q_t = Q_s$).
- ☐ **Clay skin friction:** $Q_s = \alpha c_u \pi D L$ with $\alpha = 0.5\psi^{-0.5}$ capped at 1.0, $f_s$ capped at **150 kPa**.
- ☐ **Sand skin friction:** $f_s = K p_o' \tan\delta$, with $K = 0.8$ for driven piles, $\delta = 0.8\phi'$.
- ☐ **End bearing in clay:** $q_p = 9 c_u$. **End bearing in sand:** $q_p = N_q p_o'$.
- ☐ **p-y curve method** is the standard for lateral analysis (Matlock for clay, Reese for sand).
- ☐ **Matlock soft clay p-y curve:** $p/p_u = 0.5 (y/y_c)^{1/3}$ for $y \le 8 y_c$; $p = p_u$ for $y > 8 y_c$.
- ☐ **Broms** gives closed-form $H_u$ for short rigid piles; p-y analysis used for long flexible piles.
- ☐ **Critical buckling load:** $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / (K L_e)^2$; check for slender piles in soft clay.
- ☐ **Scour** reduces effective embedment — typically $S = 1.3 D$ in tidal zones, 0 in deep sea.
- ☐ **Cyclic degradation** of skin friction: $f_{s,\text{cyc}} = 0.5 f_{s,\text{stat}}$ (API recommendation for design storm).
- ☐ **Pile group efficiency** must be checked for closely-spaced piles (group efficiency = 0.7–1.0).
- ☐ **Strut-and-tie / whip effect** in jacket piles — lateral wave loads dominate over vertical.
- ☐ **Drivability analysis** (using wave equation, GRLWEAP) must be done to confirm pile can be installed without overstressing.
- ☐ **Final design must check:** (1) Soil failure, (2) Structural failure (yield, buckling), (3) Drivability, (4) Fatigue, (5) Scour, (6) Corrosion allowance (typically 3 mm extra wall thickness).
- ☐ **Convert all units carefully:** Diameter in m, length in m, stress in kPa, force in kN or MN.
- ☐ **Always state the final answer with units and a clear "ADEQUATE/INADEQUATE" conclusion.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip from KTU Valuation Pattern:**
> In 14-mark problems, allocate marks as follows: 3 marks for identifying the correct formula with all parameters, 4 marks for step-by-step substitution and arithmetic, 2 marks for unit conversion, 2 marks for API code interpretation (FS, caps, etc.), 2 marks for final answer with conclusion, 1 mark for diagram or sketch quality. **Do not skip any of these to get full marks.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deck Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deck Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deck (Topside):** A deck is the large, horizontal, multi-level structural framework erected above the splash zone of an offshore structure (fixed jacket, semi-submersible, FPSO, or jack-up) whose primary function is to support the drilling/production equipment, helideck, accommodation modules, and process plant required for hydrocarbon extraction and processing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Topside Structure:** The collective term used in API RP 2A / ISO 19902 to describe the complete deck assembly including primary girders, secondary beams, stiffened plating, deck legs/cans, equipment skids, and the connections transferring all loads down to the substructure (jacket, pontoons, or hull).

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, **Deck Design** is the process of proportioning and detailing the horizontal plating, stiffeners, primary girders, and supporting legs (cans) of the topside, such that the structure safely resists all **in-service**, **out-of-service**, **transportation**, and **lift** conditions over its 25–30 year design life.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building the **roof of a multi-storey apartment building**, but instead of resting on brick walls, the entire roof is balanced on **long stilts standing in the sea**. 

- The "stilts" are the **jacket legs** (steel tubular members driven into the seabed).
- The "roof" is the **deck**.
- This "roof" must carry: a swimming pool (drilling mud tanks), a helicopter (helideck), a small factory (process equipment), and the weight of people (accommodation) — all while being battered continuously by **wind, waves, and salt spray** thousands of kilometres from any workshop.

So, **Deck Design = designing a highly loaded industrial floor that lives in the middle of the ocean, cannot be easily repaired, and must not fail for 25 years**.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard reference values used in KTU/OS module (must be memorised):**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - Density of seawater: $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Density of air: $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Density of structural steel: $\rho_{st} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Young's Modulus of structural steel: $E = 2.0 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa}$ (or $\text{N/mm}^2$)
> - Poisson's ratio: $\nu = 0.3$
> - Yield strength of API steel (typical): $f_y = 350 \text{ MPa}$
> - Ultimate tensile strength: $f_u = 490 \text{ MPa}$
> - Standard deck live load (general areas, API): $q_{LL} = 4.79 \text{ kPa}$ (100 psf)
> - Design return period for metocean events: **100 years** for operating, **1000 years** for ultimate limit state survival.

---

### 1.4 Functional Roles of an Offshore Deck

| Role | Description | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Equipment Support** | Carries drilling rig, mud pumps, wellheads, separators, cranes | Concentrated point loads (often 50–500 tonnes per skid) |
| **Operational Space** | Provides weather-protected working areas for personnel | Governs minimum **live load** = 4.79 kPa |
| **Load Transfer Path** | Channels all topside loads through deck legs/cans into jacket | Cantilever overhangs create large moments at the can |
| **Protection** | Acts as a stiff diaphragm distributing wave/current/ship-impact forces | Stiffness in plane is critical for overall jacket analysis |
| **Integration Platform** | Houses electrical, piping, safety (HVAC, F&G) systems | Layout drives **bracing patterns** and **penetration locations** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Structural Breakdown of a Deck

The deck is **not a single plate** — it is a layered system. From top to bottom, the load path is:

1. **Deck plating** (10–20 mm stiffened plate) — directly receives wheel, foot, and skid loads.
2. **Secondary stiffeners** (flat bars / bulb sections, typically $150 \times 10$ mm) — local bending.
3. **Primary beams / girders** (UB, UC, or fabricated plate girders) — span between main supports.
4. **Deck legs / cans** (tubular, $D \approx 1000$–2000 mm) — main vertical load path.
5. **Diaphragm / horizontal bracing** — distributes horizontal loads in the plane of the deck.

---

### 2.2 Design Loads on the Deck (API RP 2A / ISO 19902 Compatible)

#### A. Dead Loads ($D$)
Self-weight of all permanent items:
- Structural steel
- Corrosion allowance (typically 1.5 mm on each face for splash zone, 0.5–1 mm elsewhere)
- Permanent equipment, piping, cable trays, fireproofing
- Grating, handrails, decking plates

#### B. Live Loads ($L$)
- **General operational areas:** $q_L = 4.79$ kPa (100 psf)
- **Storage/equipment skids:** as per actual equipment weight, distributed over skid footprint.
- **Drilling derrick footprint:** Point loads from derrick substructure, often 1000+ tonnes.
- **Helideck:** Helicopter landing impact (typically a $MTOW$ of 12–15 tonnes for S-92 / AW189 class).

#### C. Environmental Loads
- **Wind ($W$):** 1-minute sustained wind at 10 m elevation, 100-year return period. Pressure $p = 0.5 \rho_{air} V^2 C_d$.
- **Wave ($F_w$):** Indirect — transferred from the jacket via the cans.
- **Earthquake ($E$):** Site-specific response spectrum; deck inertia adds to jacket dynamic response.
- **Snow / Ice (cold regions):** Rare in KTU context but possible for Caspian/North Sea projects.

#### D. Accidental / Special Loads
- **Fire and blast** (API RP 2FB, API RP 2A Section 4.4)
- **Vessel collision / dropped object** (dropped object: $F = m(g+a)$ where $a$ is deck deceleration $\approx 1g$ to $5g$)
- **Blast overpressure:** $F = p_b \cdot A$ (typically 0.3 bar for hydrocarbon events)

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula bank** for Deck Design as per KTU 2024 scheme. Memorise the highlighted relationships.

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
