| 1 | $w = \gamma_{steel} \cdot t_{plate}$ | Self-weight of deck plating per unit area | kPa |
| 2 | $M_{sd} = \dfrac{q L^2}{8}$ (UDL on simple beam) | Max bending moment in stiffener/beam | kN·m |
| 3 | $V_{sd} = \dfrac{q L}{2}$ | Max shear force | kN |
| 4 | $M_{pl} = Z_p \cdot f_y$ | Plastic moment capacity (for ductile sections) | kN·m |
| 5 | $M_{el} = Z_e \cdot f_y$ | Elastic moment capacity | kN·m |
| 6 | $Z_{req} = \dfrac{M_{sd}}{f_y / \gamma_{M0}}$ | Required section modulus (limit state) | $\text{cm}^3$ |
| 7 | $I_{req} = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 E \delta_{lim}}$ | Required moment of inertia for deflection | $\text{cm}^4$ |
| 8 | $b_{eff} = \dfrac{L}{3}$ (Orthotropic plate) | Effective width of plating acting with stiffener | m |
| 9 | $\sigma_{bx} = \dfrac{M}{Z} + \dfrac{N}{A}$ | Combined bending + axial stress | MPa |
| 10 | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{A_{web}}$ | Shear stress in web | MPa |
| 11 | $\lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{\sigma_{cr}}}$ | Lateral-torsional slenderness (beam check) | – |
| 12 | $P_{drop} = m \cdot (g + a)$ | Dropped object impact force | kN |
| 13 | $q_w = 0.5 \rho_{air} V^2$ | Dynamic wind pressure | Pa |
| 14 | $S_{deck} = \dfrac{V_{total}}{\Delta_{story}}$ | In-plane deck stiffness (for jacket global analysis) | kN/m |
| 15 | $\dfrac{s}{t} \leq k \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{\sigma_{com}}}$ | Plate slenderness check (stiffened plate) | – |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key partial safety factors (Eurocode 3 / API LRFD hybrid commonly used in KTU):**
> - $\gamma_{M0} = 1.05$ (resistance of member)
> - $\gamma_{M1} = 1.10$ (resistance to buckling)
> - $\gamma_{M2} = 1.25$ (resistance of net section / bolts)
> - $\gamma_{G} = 1.30$ (dead load, unfavourable)
> - $\gamma_{Q} = 1.50$ (live load, unfavourable)

---

### 2.4 Why These Formulas Matter in Real Engineering

- **Equations (2)–(3)** are the *daily workhorse* of every deck beam/stiffener design in industry.
- **Equations (8) and (15)** separate a working design from a "paper design" — most KTU exam problems specifically test effective width and plate slenderness.
- **Equation (12)** is critical for **drilling decks** where drill bits, tongs, and pipe joints are frequently dropped; API mandates dynamic amplification factors of 2.0 to 5.0.
- **Equation (14)** is the linkage between topside and substructure — without adequate in-plane stiffness, jacket analysis yields incorrect natural periods and dynamic responses.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Width of Plating Acting with a Stiffener

When a stiffened plate bends, the plate does **not** act over its full geometric width. Due to **shear lag**, only a portion (the "effective width", $b_{eff}$) effectively contributes to the section modulus of the combined T-section.

**Starting principle:** Assume parabolic distribution of longitudinal stress across the plate width $b$ (spacing between stiffeners).

**Step 1 — Geometric compatibility:**
The mid-surface strain is linear along the beam axis. Because the plate in the plane of the cross-section deforms by shear, the longitudinal stress $\sigma_x$ is not uniform but follows a parabolic law:

$$
\sigma_x(y) = \sigma_{max} \left( 1 - \left( \dfrac{2y}{b}\right)^2 \right)
$$

where $y$ is measured from the centreline of the stiffener, and $b$ is the stiffener spacing.

**Step 2 — Equivalent uniform stress block:**
Define $b_{eff}$ such that the **total axial force** in the plate is preserved at the peak stress $\sigma_{max}$:

$$
\int_{-b/2}^{b/2} \sigma_x(y) \, dy = b_{eff} \cdot \sigma_{max}
$$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration:**

$$
\int_{-b/2}^{b/2} \sigma_{max} \left( 1 - \dfrac{4y^2}{b^2} \right) dy
= \sigma_{max} \left[ y - \dfrac{4y^3}{3b^2} \right]_{-b/2}^{b/2}
$$

$$
= \sigma_{max} \left[ \left( \dfrac{b}{2} - \dfrac{4(b/2)^3}{3b^2} \right) - \left( -\dfrac{b}{2} + \dfrac{4(b/2)^3}{3b^2} \right) \right]
$$

$$
= \sigma_{max} \left[ b - \dfrac{b}{3} \right] = \dfrac{2b}{3} \cdot \sigma_{max}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate to uniform block:**

$$
b_{eff} \cdot \sigma_{max} = \dfrac{2b}{3} \cdot \sigma_{max}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,b_{eff} = \dfrac{2b}{3} \approx 0.67\, b\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Codes refine this:** API RP 2A and Eurocode 3 use $b_{eff} = b/3$ (more conservative) when full shear lag is assumed, or up to $b_{eff} = L/3$ where $L$ is the stiffener span — whichever is **smaller**. This is the KTU-favoured form.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Design of a Deck Stiffener

**Problem (typical 7-mark KTU part-a style):**
> A deck stiffener on an offshore platform spans $L = 4.0$ m between primary girders. The stiffener spacing is $s = 600$ mm. The plate thickness is $t = 12$ mm. The total uniformly distributed load (dead + live, factored) on the stiffener is $w = 18$ kN/m. The stiffener is a flat bar $150 \times 12$ mm. Steel grade is $f_y = 350$ MPa. Take $\gamma_{M0} = 1.10$. Check the adequacy of the section.

#### Step 1 — Bending moment and shear

$$
M_{sd} = \dfrac{w L^2}{8} = \dfrac{18 \times 4^2}{8} = 36.0 \text{ kN·m}
$$

$$
V_{sd} = \dfrac{w L}{2} = \dfrac{18 \times 4}{2} = 36.0 \text{ kN}
$$

#### Step 2 — Section properties of the effective T-section

**Plate contribution (effective width $b_{eff} = s/3 = 200$ mm):**
- Area of plate: $A_p = 200 \times 12 = 2400 \text{ mm}^2$
- Distance from NA (assuming NA at plate-stiffener junction for a quick check): use parallel-axis properly.

**Stiffener (flat bar $150 \times 12$):**
- Area: $A_s = 150 \times 12 = 1800 \text{ mm}^2$
- Depth: $d_s = 150$ mm
- $I_{xx,s} = \dfrac{12 \times 150^3}{12} = 3.375 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$

**Combined section (assume NA at the interface for a conservative quick check, then refine):**

Total area $A = A_p + A_s = 2400 + 1800 = 4200 \text{ mm}^2$.

Distance of NA from the **bottom of the stiffener** (taking $y=0$ at bottom):
- Plate centroid: $y_p = 150 + 6 = 156$ mm
- Stiffener centroid: $y_s = 75$ mm
- $\bar{y} = \dfrac{(2400)(156) + (1800)(75)}{4200} = \dfrac{374400 + 135000}{4200} = 121.3$ mm

**Moment of inertia about NA:**

$$
I_{xx} = \left[ \dfrac{200 \times 12^3}{12} + 2400 (156 - 121.3)^2 \right] + \left[ 3.375 \times 10^6 + 1800 (75 - 121.3)^2 \right]
$$

$$
I_{xx} = [28800 + 2400 (34.7)^2] + [3.375 \times 10^6 + 1800 (46.3)^2]
$$

$$
I_{xx} = 28800 + 2.89 \times 10^6 + 3.375 \times 10^6 + 3.86 \times 10^6
$$

$$
I_{xx} \approx 10.13 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4
$$

#### Step 3 — Section modulus

Distance to extreme fibre (top of plate): $y_{top} = 162 - 121.3 = 40.7$ mm
Distance to extreme fibre (bottom of stiffener): $y_{bot} = 121.3$ mm

$$
Z_{top} = \dfrac{I_{xx}}{y_{top}} = \dfrac{10.13 \times 10^6}{40.7} = 248{,}900 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
Z_{bot} = \dfrac{I_{xx}}{y_{bot}} = \dfrac{10.13 \times 10^6}{121.3} = 83{,}510 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Governing (smaller): $Z = 83{,}510 \text{ mm}^3 = 83.5 \text{ cm}^3$

#### Step 4 — Bending stress check

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M_{sd}}{Z} = \dfrac{36 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}}{83{,}510 \text{ mm}^3} = 431 \text{ MPa}
$$

#### Step 5 — Allowable / design capacity

$$
\sigma_{Rd} = \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{M0}} = \dfrac{350}{1.10} = 318 \text{ MPa}
$$

#### Step 6 — Verdict

$$
\sigma_{max} = 431 \text{ MPa} > \sigma_{Rd} = 318 \text{ MPa} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{NOT OK}
$$

**Conclusion:** The flat bar $150 \times 12$ mm is **inadequate** as a deck stiffener for the given load. Upgrade to a deeper section, e.g., **UB $203 \times 133 \times 25$ kg/m** ($Z_{xx} \approx 258$ cm³) or **increase plate thickness to 16 mm** and recheck.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Limit State Checks to be Performed

| Limit State | Equation | Typical Failure | Code Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ultimate Bending (ULS) | $\sigma \leq f_y / \gamma_{M0}$ | Yield of extreme fibre | EC3 §6.2.5 / API 4.3.3 |
| Shear Yielding | $\tau \leq f_y / (\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{M0})$ | Web yielding | EC3 §6.2.6 |
| Web Buckling | $\tau \leq \chi_w f_y / (\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{M1})$ | Web crippling under patch load | EC3 §6.2.6(2) |
| Lateral-Torsional Buckling | $M_{sd} \leq \chi_{LT} M_{Rd}$ | Beam rolls over | EC3 §6.3.2 |
| Plate Buckling | $s/t \leq$ limit (function of $\psi$) | Stiffened plate dimples | EC3 Part 1-5 |
| Deflection (SLS) | $\delta \leq L/200$ to $L/300$ | Excessive vibration / ponding | API 4.4.1 |
| Fatigue | $\Delta\sigma \leq \Delta\sigma_{allow}$ | Crack initiation in welded joint | API RP 2A, EC3 Part 1-9 |
| Vibration (walking) | $f_n \geq 5$ Hz for offices | Resonance with footfall | AISC DG-11 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Deck Design Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Functional Layout of Topside] --> B[Define Equipment Footprint and Skid Weights]
    B --> C[Establish Gridlines: Primary Beam Spacing 6-10 m]
    C --> D[Compute Loads: Dead, Live, Wind, Dropped Object]
    D --> E[Compute Load Combinations ULS SLS ALS]
    E --> F[Preliminary Member Sizing: Plating Stiffeners Girders]
    F --> G[Effective Width: Plate Acting with Stiffener]
    G --> H[ULS Check: Bending Shear Axial Combined]
    H --> I[Buckling Checks: LTB Web Plate]
    I --> J[SLS Check: Deflection Vibration]
    J --> K{All Checks Pass?}
    K -- No --> F
    K -- Yes --> L[Connection Design: Welded Bolted Shear Studs]
    L --> M[Detail Drawings: Can Girder Splices Bracing]
    M --> N[Fabrication Lifting and Sea Fastening]
    N --> O[End: Issued for Construction IFC]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Hierarchical Load Path in a Typical Fixed Platform Deck

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Top Surface: Helideck Equipment Skids Personnel] --> B[Deck Plating 10-20 mm]
    B --> C[Secondary Stiffeners 150x10 Flat Bar]
    C --> D[Primary Girders UB UC or Plate Girder]
    D --> E[Deck Cans Tubular 1-2 m Diameter]
    E --> F[Top of Jacket Legs]
    F --> G[Substructure Piles to Seabed]
    H[In-plane Bracing and Diaphragm] --> D
    I[Lateral Loads Wind Wave Earthquake] --> H
    J[Dropped Object] --> B
    K[Fire Blast Accidental] --> D
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Stiffened Plate Cross-Section (T-Beam Action)

```
  +----------------------------------------------+   <-- Effective plate (b_eff)
  |xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx|       acts as flange
  +----------------------^-----------------------+
                             |
                             |   Stiffener (web)
                             |
                             v
        +--------------------+--------------------+
        |                                         |
        +-----------------------------------------+   <-- Toe of stiffener
        ===========================================
                          Neutral Axis (NA)
                          
   y_top   = distance from NA to top of plate
   y_bot   = distance from NA to bottom of stiffener
   b_eff   = effective width of plating (typically s/3)
   s       = stiffener spacing (600-900 mm typical)
   t_plate = plating thickness (10-16 mm typical)
```

### 4.4 Mermaid: Typical Layout Plan of a Production Deck (Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Module1 [Wellbay Module]
        A1[Wellhead A] --- A2[Wellhead B] --- A3[Wellhead C]
    end
    subgraph Module2 [Process Module]
        B1[Separator] --- B2[Heat Exchanger] --- B3[Pump Skid]
    end
    subgraph Module3 [Drilling Module]
        C1[Derrrick Substructure] --- C2[Mud Tank]
    end
    subgraph Module4 [Accommodation]
        D1[Living Quarters LQ]
    end
    subgraph Module5 [Helideck]
        E1[Helideck 25T]
    end
    Module1 --- Module2
    Module2 --- Module3
    Module3 --- Module4
    Module4 --- Module5
    F1[Cantilever North] --- Module3
    F2[Cantilever South] --- Module3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*List the major design loads that must be considered in the design of an offshore platform deck. Mention the relevant code/standard for each load type.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — keyword-mapped for valuation):**

1. **Dead loads ($D$):** self-weight of structure, permanent equipment, fireproofing, corrosion allowance. [1 mark] → Reference: API RP 2A Section 4.3 / ISO 19902.
2. **Live loads ($L$):** general operational $4.79$ kPa, equipment skids, helideck impact. [1 mark] → Reference: API RP 2A Section 4.4.2.
3. **Environmental loads ($W$ + $E$):** wind, wave (via jacket), earthquake, snow. [0.5 mark] → Reference: API RP 2A Sections 4.4.3 / 4.4.4.
4. **Accidental loads:** dropped object, fire, blast, vessel collision. [0.5 mark] → Reference: API RP 2FB / NORSOK N-001.

(Examiner allocates 0.5 mark per correct item; the **code reference** is the differentiator for full 3 marks.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
*What is "effective width" in a stiffened plate? Why is it used in deck design?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **effective width** $b_{eff}$ of plating acting with a stiffener is the reduced width over which the plate is assumed to carry the peak longitudinal bending stress, in lieu of the full geometric stiffener spacing $s$. It is used because of the **shear lag phenomenon**: in a wide plate, the longitudinal stress is not uniform across the width; it is maximum at the stiffener-plate junction and decays parabolically toward the mid-span between stiffeners. [2 marks]

For deck design, the conservatively adopted value is $b_{eff} = s/3$ (or $b_{eff} = L/3$, whichever is **smaller**), per API RP 2A and Eurocode 3 Part 1-5. [1 mark]

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Adapted]`

**Q. A fixed offshore jacket platform has a production deck. As the design engineer:**
**(a)** Sketch and explain the typical structural hierarchy of a deck, naming the functions of each member. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A primary deck girder of span $L = 8$ m carries a uniformly distributed load of $w = 60$ kN/m (factored ULS) including self-weight, equipment load, and live load. The girder is to be selected from standard rolled I-sections (steel grade $f_y = 350$ MPa, $\gamma_{M0} = 1.10$). Determine the **required elastic section modulus** and the **minimum moment of inertia for a deflection limit of $L/300$**. Take $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ MPa. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Structural hierarchy [7 marks — 1 mark per member type × 5, plus 2 marks for the explanatory diagram and load path description]:**

| Member | Function | Typical Size |
|---|---|---|
| Deck plating | Resists local wheel/foot loads; acts as flange of T-beam | 10–20 mm stiffened plate |
| Secondary stiffeners | Spans between primary beams; reduces plate slenderness | $150 \times 10$ flat bar |
| Primary beams/girders | Main load-carrying members between deck legs | UB 533×210×82 or plate girder |
| Deck legs/cans | Transfer all vertical loads to the jacket | Tubular $\phi 1000$–$2000$ mm |
| Horizontal bracing/diaphragm | Resists in-plane lateral loads; provides diaphragm action | CHS / flat-bar bracing |

The **load path** is: equipment → plating → stiffeners → primary girders → cans → jacket legs → piles.

**(b) Numerical design [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Design bending moment [2 marks]**

$$
M_{sd} = \dfrac{w L^2}{8} = \dfrac{60 \times 8^2}{8} = 480 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 2: Design shear [1 mark]**

$$
V_{sd} = \dfrac{w L}{2} = \dfrac{60 \times 8}{2} = 240 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3: Required elastic section modulus [2 marks]**

$$
Z_{req} = \dfrac{M_{sd}}{f_y / \gamma_{M0}} = \dfrac{480 \times 10^6}{350 / 1.10} = \dfrac{480 \times 10^6}{318.18}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Z_{req} \approx 1{,}509 \times 10^3 \text{ mm}^3 = 1509 \text{ cm}^3\,}
$$

**Step 4: Required moment of inertia for deflection [2 marks]**

Limit: $\delta_{lim} = L/300 = 8000/300 = 26.67$ mm

For a simply supported beam under UDL:

$$
\delta = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 E I} \leq \delta_{lim}
$$

Convert $w$ to N/mm: $w = 60$ kN/m $= 60$ N/mm.

$$
I_{req} = \dfrac{5 \times 60 \times 8000^4}{384 \times 2.0 \times 10^5 \times 26.67}
$$

$$
I_{req} = \dfrac{5 \times 60 \times 4.096 \times 10^{15}}{2.048 \times 10^{9}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,I_{req} \approx 6.0 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4 = 60{,}000 \text{ cm}^4\,}
$$

**Selection:** A **UB $610 \times 229 \times 113$ kg/m** ($Z_{xx} = 1{,}580$ cm³, $I_{xx} = 48{,}600$ cm⁴) satisfies $Z$ but **fails** the deflection check. Up-size to **UB $610 \times 305 \times 179$ kg/m** ($Z_{xx} = 2{,}560$ cm³, $I_{xx} = 78{,}700$ cm⁴) which satisfies **both** checks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Adapted]`

**Q. While reviewing a deck design for a North-Sea production platform:**
**(a)** Describe the **three critical limit states** that must be checked for a deck girder. For each, state the governing failure mode and the acceptance criterion. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A cantilever deck overhang of length $L_c = 5$ m is to be designed to carry a uniform dead + live load of $w = 25$ kN/m. The supporting cantilever beam is proposed as a welded plate girder with web depth $d_w = 800$ mm, web thickness $t_w = 10$ mm, flange width $b_f = 250$ mm, flange thickness $t_f = 20$ mm, $f_y = 350$ MPa. Check whether the section is adequate in **bending and shear** at ULS. Neglect self-weight of girder (already included in $w$). Take $\gamma_{M0} = 1.10$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three critical limit states [7 marks — ~2.3 marks each]:**

1. **Ultimate Limit State (ULS) — Bending** [2.3 marks]
   - Failure mode: yielding of extreme fibre (or plastic hinge formation).
   - Criterion: $M_{sd} \leq M_{Rd} = W_{pl} f_y / \gamma_{M0}$ (or $W_{el} f_y / \gamma_{M0}$ for elastic design).
2. **Ultimate Limit State (ULS) — Shear** [2.3 marks]
   - Failure mode: shear yielding of the web.
   - Criterion: $V_{sd} \leq V_{Rd} = A_v f_y / (\sqrt{3} \gamma_{M0})$, where $A_v = d_w t_w$ for unstiffened webs.
3. **Serviceability Limit State (SLS) — Deflection / Vibration** [2.4 marks]
   - Failure mode: excessive sag, ponding, or human-induced vibration.
   - Criterion: $\delta_{actual} \leq \delta_{lim}$, typically $L/200$ to $L/300$ for decks; $f_n \geq 5$ Hz to avoid walking-induced resonance.

**(b) Plate-girder check [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Design moment and shear [1 mark]**

$$
M_{sd} = \dfrac{w L_c^2}{2} = \dfrac{25 \times 5^2}{2} = 312.5 \text{ kN·m}
$$

$$
V_{sd} = w L_c = 25 \times 5 = 125 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2: Section properties of the plate girder [2 marks]**

- $I_{xx} = \dfrac{t_w d_w^3}{12} + 2 \left[ \dfrac{b_f t_f^3}{12} + b_f t_f \left( \dfrac{d_w + t_f}{2} \right)^2 \right]$
- $I_{xx} = \dfrac{10 \times 800^3}{12} + 2 \left[ \dfrac{250 \times 20^3}{12} + 250 \times 20 \times (410)^2 \right]$
- $I_{xx} = 4.267 \times 10^8 + 2 \left[ 1.667 \times 10^5 + 8.405 \times 10^8 \right]$
- $I_{xx} = 4.267 \times 10^8 + 1.681 \times 10^9 = 2.108 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4$

- Depth to extreme fibre: $h/2 = (d_w + 2 t_f)/2 = (800 + 40)/2 = 420$ mm
- Elastic section modulus: $Z_{el} = I_{xx} / (h/2) = 2.108 \times 10^9 / 420 = 5.019 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3 = 5019$ cm³

**Step 3: Bending check [2 marks]**

$$
M_{Rd} = \dfrac{Z_{el} f_y}{\gamma_{M0}} = \dfrac{5.019 \times 10^6 \times 350}{1.10} = 1597 \text{ kN·m}
$$

$$
M_{sd} = 312.5 \text{ kN·m} < M_{Rd} = 1597 \text{ kN·m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{OK in bending (UR = 0.196)}
$$

**Step 4: Shear check [2 marks]**

$$
A_v = d_w \times t_w = 800 \times 10 = 8000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
V_{Rd} = \dfrac{A_v f_y}{\sqrt{3} \, \gamma_{M0}} = \dfrac{8000 \times 350}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.10} = 1470 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
V_{sd} = 125 \text{ kN} < V_{Rd} = 1470 \text{ kN} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{OK in shear (UR = 0.085)}
$$

**Verdict:** Both bending and shear capacities are well above the design demand. The section is **adequate**; however, deflection and lateral-torsional buckling must still be checked separately in a complete design.

---

### 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students LOSE marks in Deck Design problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the corrosion allowance (1–1.5 mm on each face).** The examiner routinely deducts 1 mark for "no corrosion allowance" on the plate thickness used in the section modulus.
> 2. **Using $b_{eff} = s$ instead of $b_{eff} = s/3$.** Full plate width ignores shear lag — this is a classic KTU trap.
> 3. **Mixing up partial safety factors.** For LRFD: $\gamma_M = 1.10$ (steel). For WSD/ASD: no factor, but stresses are compared to **0.6 $f_y$**. Examiners check that the student has used a *consistent* set.
> 4. **Ignoring cantilever moments.** Decks frequently have **5–10 m overhangs** beyond the jacket centreline; the resulting negative moment at the can governs the design.
> 5. **Confusing SLS deflection limits.** Office / accommodation: $L/300$; process areas: $L/200$; helideck: $L/400$ under landing impact.
> 6. **Not checking plate slenderness $s/t$.** API requires $s/t \leq 60 \sqrt{355/f_y}$ for primary stiffeners. Many KTU answers skip this.
> 7. **Forgetting to convert units.** A 14-mark numerical problem where the moment is in kN·m and the section modulus in mm³ requires the factor of $10^6$ — omitting it loses 2 marks outright.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deck Design — KTU 2024 Module 3: Final Revision Bullets**

- ✅ Deck is the **topside structure** of an offshore platform; supports drilling/production/accommodation.
- ✅ **Five-tier load path:** Plating → Stiffeners → Girders → Cans → Jacket legs.
- ✅ **Design loads:** Dead ($D$), Live ($L$), Wind ($W$), Earthquake ($E$), Dropped object, Fire/blast.
- ✅ **Live load for general areas:** $4.79$ kPa (API). **Helideck:** helicopter MTOW as point load.
- ✅ **Effective width of plating:** $b_{eff} = s/3$ or $L/3$, whichever is **smaller** (shear lag).
- ✅ **Stiffener as T-beam:** Plate (flange) + flat bar/UB (web) — combined $I_{xx}$ to be computed.
- ✅ **Limit states to check:** ULS bending, ULS shear, ULS buckling (LTB, web, plate), SLS deflection, Fatigue.
- ✅ **Partial safety factors (LRFD):** $\gamma_{M0} = 1.05$ (EC3) or $1.10$ (API); $\gamma_G = 1.30$; $\gamma_Q = 1.50$.
- ✅ **Deflection limit:** $L/300$ for offices, $L/200$ for process areas.
- ✅ **Plate slenderness:** $s/t \leq 60 \sqrt{355/f_y}$ for primary stiffeners.
- ✅ **Cantilever overhangs** create large negative moments — must check **both** positive and negative regions.
- ✅ **Corrosion allowance:** 1.5 mm (splash zone), 0.5–1 mm elsewhere — **deduct from plate thickness before strength check**.
- ✅ **In-plane deck stiffness** affects jacket dynamic analysis — provide adequate horizontal bracing.
- ✅ **Dropped object load:** $F = m(g + a)$ with $a = 1g$ to $5g$ dynamic amplification.
- ✅ **Connections:** Welded (typical) for shop; bolted for site splice; shear studs (studs) for composite action with grating.
- ✅ **Codes to cite in KTU answers:** API RP 2A, ISO 19902, NORSOK N-001, Eurocode 3 (EN 1993-1-1, 1993-1-5, 1993-1-9), AISC 360.
- ✅ **Always draw a free-body diagram (FBD)** and a **section sketch** — KTU awards 1–2 marks for clear diagrams even in numerical problems.
- ✅ **Units discipline:** Convert $w$ to N/mm before using in $\delta$ formulas; convert $M$ to N·mm before dividing by $Z$ in mm³.
- ✅ **Final check before writing the answer:** Have you addressed (i) effective width, (ii) corrosion, (iii) limit state factors, (iv) deflection, (v) buckling?
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Top Sides
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Top Sides

# Topside of Offshore Structures — Module 3 Lecture Notes

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PCNST602 / Module 3):**
> *Topside – functional and operational requirements, layout, weight estimation, integrated topside design, transportation, and installation concepts.*

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The **Topside** of an offshore platform is the complete **above-water superstructure** that rests on the supporting substructure (jacket, monopile, or floating hull). It houses every facility required for **hydrocarbon production, drilling, processing, accommodation, and safe operation** of the platform.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the topside is treated as a **functional-deck assembly** consisting of multiple **deck levels** (e.g., Cellar Deck, Production Deck, Mezzanine Deck, Helideck, and Derrick), supported on the **jacket legs / columns** through the **deck legs** and connected via the **top-of-jacket interface**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box:**
> *"The topside is the integrated process-and-utility complex mounted above the splash zone. Its structural skeleton (deck framing), its operational payload (modules), and its environmental exposure (wind, wave-splash) collectively define the platform's production capacity, manning limit, and survivability."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Apartment-Factory"

Imagine a **multi-storey building sitting on stilts in the ocean**. The stilts are the *jacket*, and the building is the *topside*. Inside this building:

- The **ground floor (cellar deck)** receives oil and gas from underwater wells (like a basement receiving goods).
- The **middle floors (production decks)** act like a **kitchen of a factory** — separating oil, gas, and water.
- The **upper floor (accommodation block)** is like an **apartment** housing workers.
- The **roof (helideck + flare boom)** is like an **emergency exit and waste burner**.

Every kilogram of weight, every square meter of wind-facing wall, and every piece of equipment must be carefully counted — because the entire "apartment-factory" floats above deep water on slender legs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metrics:**
> - **Fixed platform topside weight:** typically **2,000 – 25,000 tonnes** (≈ **2 × 10⁶ – 2.5 × 10⁷ kg**).
> - **Deck elevation above MSL (Mean Sea Level):** **15 m – 40 m** (to clear storm waves).
> - **Topside plan dimensions:** up to **~80 m × 80 m** for large production platforms.
> - **Number of deck levels:** **3 – 5** typical.

### 1.3 Why Topside Engineering Matters (KTU Exam Perspective)

In KTU board examinations, the topside topic is high-yield because it tests the student's ability to:
1. **Identify functional requirements** (5-mark / 3-mark short questions).
2. **Compute topside weight and center of gravity** (14-mark numerical).
3. **Apply environmental loading** (wind on elevated decks).
4. **Explain modularization and float-over installation logic.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional & Operational Requirements of the Topside

The topside must satisfy **four functional mandates**, each tied to a structural design driver:

| Functional Mandate | Engineering Output | Design Driver |
|---|---|---|
| **Hydrocarbon Processing** | Separators, pumps, heat exchangers, compressors | Weight + Plot area + Vibration |
| **Drilling (if drilling rig)** | Derrick, drawworks, mud pumps, BOPs | High concentrated load + Dynamic impact |
| **Accommodation (Manning)** | Living quarters, galley, hospital, helideck | Fire safety + Crew load (~150 persons typical) |
| **Utilities & Safety** | Power generators, crane, flare boom, ESD systems | Wind loads + Explosion relief |

### 2.2 Typical Topside Layout (Hierarchical Deck Arrangement)

```
┌──────────────────────────────┐  ← HELIDECK + FLARE BOOM (highest elevation)
├──────────────────────────────┤
│  PRODUCTION DECK (Level 3)   │  ← High-pressure separators, manifolds
├──────────────────────────────┤
│  PROCESS / MEZZANINE DECK    │  ← Pumps, heat exchangers, chemical injection
├──────────────────────────────┤
│  CELLAR DECK (Level 1)       │  ← Wellheads, Christmas trees, incoming flowlines
└──────────────────────────────┘
       ↓ supported on ↓
    JACKET LEGS / DECK LEGS
```

### 2.3 Topside Structural Framing

The topside skeleton is typically a **steel space frame** comprising:
- **Primary trusses** (girders spanning between jacket legs).
- **Secondary beams** (supporting deck plating and modules).
- **Deck legs** (vertical columns transferring load to jacket cans).
- **Module support frames** (skids for plug-in process modules).
- **Bracing** (X, K, or diagonal types for lateral stiffness).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pearl:** The topside is *not* a single rigid block. It behaves as an **assembly of rigidly connected deck modules**, each with its own weight, CG, and wind area. This drives the **integrated weight & CG estimation** problem that frequently appears in KTU exams.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Weight, CG, Wind, Module Lifts)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU-board-tested. Memorize the units alongside the symbols.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Topside Weight (Dry) | $W_{dry} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i$ | kN or tonnes | Sum of all module, structural, and equipment weights |
| 2 | Total Operational Weight (Live + Dry) | $W_{op} = W_{dry} + W_{live} + W_{consumables}$ | kN | Live load includes personnel, fluids in vessels |
| 3 | Vertical Centre of Gravity (VCG) | $Z_{cg} = \dfrac{\sum W_i \, z_i}{\sum W_i}$ | m above baseline | $z_i$ measured from a common datum (e.g., cellar deck base) |
| 4 | Horizontal Centre of Gravity | $X_{cg} = \dfrac{\sum W_i \, x_i}{\sum W_i}$ | m | Same logic along x-axis |
| 5 | Wind Force on Topside (API/ISO) | $F_w = \dfrac{1}{2} \, \rho_a \, V^2 \, C_d \, A$ | N | $\rho_a = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$; $C_d \approx 1.6$–$2.0$ for bluff shapes |
| 6 | Wind Overturning Moment | $M_w = F_w \cdot h_{cg}$ | N·m | $h_{cg}$ = height of wind centroid above waterline |
| 7 | Wind Pressure (design) | $q = 0.613 \, V_d^2$ | Pa | $V_d$ = design wind speed in m/s (used in ISO 19901-1) |
| 8 | Module Lift Weight (Hook Load) | $W_{hook} = W_{module} + W_{rigging} + W_{lift\_beam}$ | kN | Rigging ≈ 5–10 % of module weight |
| 9 | Crane Capacity Check | $W_{hook} \le 0.75 \cdot W_{crane\,rated}$ | kN | 75 % safety factor (API RP 2D) |
| 10 | Topside Period (for dynamic analysis) | $T_n \approx 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{W_{op}}{g \cdot K}}$ | s | $K$ = stiffness of jacket in translation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table syntax safeguard:** All absolute values use $\vert \cdot \vert$ LaTeX form so the markdown table renders without breaking.

### 2.5 Engineering Significance of Topside Design

| Design Aspect | Real-World Implication |
|---|---|
| **Weight Control** | Each tonne of topside increases jacket leg size, foundation pile diameter, and offshore installation vessel cost. |
| **Center of Gravity (CG)** | A high VCG amplifies **wind overturning moment** and **dynamic response** under wave loading. |
| **Modularization** | Enables **parallel onshore fabrication** → reduces offshore hook-up time by 30–50 %. |
| **Topside Float-Over Installation** | For >25,000 t topsides, the deck is floated above the jacket on a barge and lowered — saves expensive heavy-lift vessels. |
| **Layout Discipline** | Hazardous areas (flare, wellhead) must be separated from accommodation → a **HSE-driven layout** problem. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Topside Weight Estimation by Module Aggregation

The KTU-standard method to estimate topside weight is **module-by-module summation**, then adjusting with contingency.

**Step 1 — List every functional group:**

$$
W_{topside} = W_{structure} + W_{process} + W_{utilities} + W_{accommodation} + W_{drilling} + W_{safety}
$$

**Step 2 — Add live (operational) loads:**

$$
W_{live} = W_{personnel} + W_{fluids} + W_{consumables\,(diesel, chemicals)}
$$

$$
W_{total} = W_{dry} + W_{live}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply engineering contingency (typically 10–15 %):**

$$
W_{design} = 1.10 \text{ to } 1.15 \cdot W_{total}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Vertical Centre of Gravity (VCG):**

$$
Z_{cg} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \, z_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i}
$$

where $z_i$ is the elevation of group $i$ above the chosen datum (usually the **top of jacket elevation**).

**Step 5 — Compute Horizontal CG (for in-place analysis):**

$$
X_{cg} = \dfrac{\sum W_i \, x_i}{\sum W_i}, \qquad Y_{cg} = \dfrac{\sum W_i \, y_i}{\sum W_i}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Insight:** The CG formula emerges from the **first moment of mass principle** — the resultant of parallel forces equals the algebraic sum of individual moments about any axis. This is identical in form to the centroid of a composite area in mechanics.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style — 14 Marks)

**Problem:**
A fixed offshore platform topside consists of the following modules. Compute the **(a)** Total Dry Weight, **(b)** Total Operational Weight, and **(c)** Vertical Centre of Gravity (VCG) above the cellar deck base.

| Module | Weight $W_i$ (kN) | Elevation $z_i$ (m) |
|---|---|---|
| Deck structure (steel framing) | 6,000 | 0 (datum) |
| Cellar deck equipment (wellheads) | 2,500 | 2 |
| Process module (separators) | 8,000 | 8 |
| Power generation | 3,500 | 12 |
| Accommodation block | 4,000 | 18 |
| Helideck + crane | 1,500 | 25 |
| Live load (personnel + fluids) | 2,000 | 10 (assumed average) |

**Solution:**

**(a) Total Dry Weight (excluding live load):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{dry} &= 6{,}000 + 2{,}500 + 8{,}000 + 3{,}500 + 4{,}000 + 1{,}500 \\
W_{dry} &= 25{,}500 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Total Operational Weight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{total} &= W_{dry} + W_{live} \\
&= 25{,}500 + 2{,}000 \\
&= 27{,}500 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Vertical Centre of Gravity:**

First, compute $\sum W_i \, z_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum W_i z_i &= (6{,}000)(0) + (2{,}500)(2) + (8{,}000)(8) \\
&\quad + (3{,}500)(12) + (4{,}000)(18) + (1{,}500)(25) + (2{,}000)(10)
\end{aligned}
$$

Evaluating term by term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum W_i z_i &= 0 + 5{,}000 + 64{,}000 + 42{,}000 + 72{,}000 + 37{,}500 + 20{,}000 \\
&= 240{,}500 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{cg} &= \dfrac{\sum W_i z_i}{\sum W_i} = \dfrac{240{,}500}{27{,}500} \\
Z_{cg} &\approx 8.75 \text{ m above cellar deck base}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result interpretation:** A VCG of ~8.75 m above the cellar deck is **typical** for a 4-level production platform. If VCG exceeds ~12 m, the structure becomes dynamically "soft" and wave-wind coupling must be revisited in Module 4 (Dynamic Analysis).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Wind Force on the Topside (ISO 19901-1 Form)

**Step 1 — Reference wind velocity at 10 m above sea level:**

$$
V_{ref} = \text{site-specific 100-year return wind speed (m/s)}
$$

**Step 2 — Wind velocity at topside elevation $z$ (power law):**

$$
V_z = V_{ref} \left(\dfrac{z}{10}\right)^{\alpha}, \qquad \alpha \approx 0.10 \text{–} 0.14
$$

**Step 3 — Dynamic wind pressure:**

$$
q_z = 0.613 \, V_z^2 \quad [\text{Pa}]
$$

**Step 4 — Total wind force on topside:**

$$
F_w = \sum q_z \cdot C_{d,i} \cdot A_i
$$

where $A_i$ is the projected area of element $i$ in the wind direction and $C_{d,i}$ is the drag coefficient.

**Step 5 — Wind overturning moment at the jacket base:**

$$
M_w = \sum F_{w,i} \cdot h_i
$$

**Numerical illustration** (using the above example):
If the topside presents a projected wind area $A = 1{,}500 \text{ m}^2$ at mean elevation $z = 20$ m, with design 3-sec gust $V_d = 50$ m/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{20} &= 50 \cdot \left(\dfrac{20}{10}\right)^{0.12} = 50 \cdot (2)^{0.12} \\
&\approx 50 \cdot 1.087 \approx 54.4 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{20} &= 0.613 \cdot (54.4)^2 \approx 0.613 \cdot 2{,}959 \approx 1{,}814 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_w &= q_{20} \cdot C_d \cdot A = 1{,}814 \cdot 1.8 \cdot 1{,}500 \\
&\approx 4{,}898 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_w &= F_w \cdot 20 = 97{,}960 \text{ kN·m} \approx 98 \text{ MN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This overturning moment becomes a **critical load case** when checking jacket leg compression and foundation pile pull-out capacity.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Topside Installation Method

| Topside Weight Range | Recommended Method | Vessel Required |
|---|---|---|
| < 2,500 t | **Crane Lift** | Heavy-lift vessel (e.g., SSCV Thialf) |
| 2,500 – 15,000 t | **Crane Lift (dual)** | Two-crane sheerlegs |
| 15,000 – 25,000 t | **Float-Over (skid-on)** | Cargo barge + strand jacks |
| > 25,000 t | **Float-Over (balanced)** | Specialized Semi-Submersible Crane Vessel (SSCV) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Topside Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["HELIDECK AND FLARE BOOM"] --> nodeB["ACCOMMODATION BLOCK 100 PAX"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["PRODUCTION DECK L3 SEPARATORS MANIFOLDS"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["PROCESS DECK L2 PUMPS HEAT EXCHANGERS"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["CELLAR DECK L1 WELLHEADS XMAS TREES"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["DECK LEGS TRANSFER TO JACKET"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["JACKET SUBSTRUCTURE"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["PILE FOUNDATION SEABED"]

    nodeI["POWER GENERATION DIESEL GAS TURBINES"] -.powers.-> nodeC
    nodeJ["SAFETY SYSTEMS ESD FIRE FIGHTING"] -.protects.-> nodeB
    nodeK["CRANE 1500T HEAVY LIFT"] -.services.-> nodeE
    nodeL["UTILITIES HVAC WATER CHEMICAL INJECTION"] -.supports.-> nodeD
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Topside Weight Estimation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["Identify all functional modules"] --> s2["Weigh each module Wi"]
    s2 --> s3["Note module elevation zi"]
    s3 --> s4["Compute sum Wi"]
    s3 --> s5["Compute sum Wi times zi"]
    s4 --> s6["Total weight W total"]
    s5 --> s7["VCG Zcg"]
    s6 --> s8["Apply contingency 10 to 15 percent"]
    s7 --> s9["Feed into global in place analysis"]
    s8 --> s9
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Topside Installation Methods Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q{"Topside weight?"}
    q --> r1["Less than 2500 t"]
    q --> r2["2500 to 15000 t"]
    q --> r3["15000 to 25000 t"]
    q --> r4["Greater than 25000 t"]

    r1 --> m1["Single crane lift"]
    r2 --> m2["Dual crane lift"]
    r3 --> m3["Float over skid on barge"]
    r4 --> m4["Float over balanced SSCV"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram rationale:** Mermaid is used to render **functional block architecture** rather than physical drawings, in compliance with the V10 protocol when topics involve complex structural geometry that cannot be cleanly represented in flowcharts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any **six** functional components of an offshore platform topside.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Production process module** — separators, manifolds, pumps.
2. **Drilling module** — derrick, drawworks, BOPs.
3. **Power generation module** — gas turbines, diesel generators.
4. **Accommodation block** — quarters, galley, hospital, helideck.
5. **Crane** — for material handling and supply boat operations.
6. **Flare boom** — for safe disposal of excess hydrocarbons.
7. *(bonus)* Safety systems — ESD, fire-fighting, life-saving appliances.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** ½ mark per correct item, capped at 6 items × ½ = **3 marks**. Examiners look for clear functional naming, not vague terms.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define **integrated topside design** and state **two advantages** of modularization.*

**Model Answer:**
**Definition:** *Integrated topside design* is a methodology in which the deck structure, process modules, utilities, and accommodation are **engineered as a single coordinated system** from concept stage — optimizing weight, layout, and constructability simultaneously rather than designing each in isolation.

**Advantages of modularization:**
1. **Parallel fabrication** — multiple modules fabricated simultaneously onshore, reducing project schedule by up to **40 %**.
2. **Quality control** — modules built in sheltered yards yield better weld quality than in-situ offshore construction.

*(Optional third point for credit)* — Easier **transportation** as discrete plug-in units to the offshore site.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Show the summation table explicitly** — students who write only the final number for VCG lose **3 marks**.
> - **State the datum clearly** before writing the CG formula (jacket top? cellar deck? MSL?).
> - **Apply and state the contingency factor** when computing design weight.
> - **Sign convention** for CG: explicitly mark positive "above datum" direction.
> - **Units consistency**: never mix tonnes (mass) with kN (force) without the $g$ conversion.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice Set**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat **labeled layout sketch**, describe the **typical arrangement of a topside** for a fixed offshore production platform. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The topside of a fixed platform has the following modules. Calculate **(i)** Total Dry Weight, **(ii)** Total Operational Weight with 10 % contingency, and **(iii)** Vertical Centre of Gravity above the cellar deck. *(7 marks)*

| Module | Weight (kN) | Elevation (m) |
|---|---|---|
| Deck steel structure | 4,500 | 0 |
| Cellar deck piping & wellheads | 3,000 | 3 |
| Production module | 9,000 | 9 |
| Power module | 4,200 | 13 |
| Accommodation | 3,800 | 20 |
| Helideck | 1,200 | 27 |
| Live load (operational) | 2,500 | 11 (average) |

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The topside layout follows a **vertical hierarchy of hazard zoning**, from hazardous low deck to safe upper accommodation:

| Level | Elevation (m) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Helideck | 25–30 | Personnel transfer, helicopter landing |
| Accommodation | 18–22 | Crew quarters, control room |
| Production Deck (L3) | 10–14 | High-pressure separators, manifolds |
| Process Deck (Mezzanine) | 6–10 | Pumps, exchangers, chemical injection |
| Cellar Deck (L1) | 2–5 | Wellheads, Xmas trees, riser pull-in |

**Mark split (7 marks):**
- 2 marks — clear sketch with all levels labelled.
- 2 marks — identification of hazard zoning principle.
- 2 marks — naming of major equipment on each level.
- 1 mark — presentation neatness.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Total Dry Weight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{dry} &= 4{,}500 + 3{,}000 + 9{,}000 + 4{,}200 + 3{,}800 + 1{,}200 \\
&= 25{,}700 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total Operational Weight (with 10 % contingency):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{op} &= 1.10 \times (W_{dry} + W_{live}) \\
&= 1.10 \times (25{,}700 + 2{,}500) \\
&= 1.10 \times 28{,}200 \\
&= 31{,}020 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Vertical Centre of Gravity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum W_i z_i &= (4{,}500)(0) + (3{,}000)(3) + (9{,}000)(9) \\
&\quad + (4{,}200)(13) + (3{,}800)(20) + (1{,}200)(27) + (2{,}500)(11)
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing each product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum W_i z_i &= 0 + 9{,}000 + 81{,}000 + 54{,}600 + 76{,}000 + 32{,}400 + 27{,}500 \\
&= 280{,}500 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{cg} &= \dfrac{280{,}500}{28{,}200} \\
&\approx 9.95 \text{ m above cellar deck base}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mark split (7 marks):**
- 1 mark — correctly summed dry weights.
- 2 marks — correctly applied contingency factor with statement.
- 3 marks — full moment-sum computation shown step-by-step.
- 1 mark — final VCG with correct units and sign.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **functional requirements** of an offshore topside under the categories of **(i) Production, (ii) Drilling, (iii) Utilities, and (iv) Accommodation**. Mention at least two items under each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A topside is subjected to a design 3-second gust wind speed of **V = 55 m/s** at 10 m elevation. The topside projected area in the wind direction is **A = 1,800 m²**, mean elevation **z = 22 m**, drag coefficient **C_d = 1.8**, and density of air $\rho_a = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Calculate **(i)** wind velocity at z = 22 m using power-law exponent α = 0.12, **(ii)** wind force on the topside, and **(iii)** overturning moment at the jacket base. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

| Category | Functional Items |
|---|---|
| (i) Production | Oil-gas-water separators; manifolds & flowlines; pumping units |
| (ii) Drilling | Derrick; drawworks; mud pumps; blow-out preventer (BOP) |
| (iii) Utilities | Power generators; HVAC; water makers; chemical injection skid |
| (iv) Accommodation | Living quarters; galley & mess; helideck; hospital; control room |

**Mark split (7 marks):** 1.5 marks per category × 4 = 6 marks + 1 mark for clarity/structure.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Wind velocity at z = 22 m:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{22} &= V_{10} \cdot \left(\dfrac{22}{10}\right)^{0.12} \\
&= 55 \cdot (2.2)^{0.12} \\
&\approx 55 \cdot 1.094 \\
&\approx 60.2 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Wind force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_w &= \dfrac{1}{2} \, \rho_a \, V^2 \, C_d \, A \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot (60.2)^2 \cdot 1.8 \cdot 1{,}800 \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 3{,}624 \cdot 1.8 \cdot 1{,}800 \\
&\approx 7{,}184 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Overturning moment at jacket base:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_w &= F_w \cdot z \\
&= 7{,}184 \cdot 22 \\
&\approx 158{,}050 \text{ kN·m} \\
&\approx 158 \text{ MN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mark split (7 marks):**
- 2 marks — power-law velocity profile with stated exponent.
- 3 marks — correct drag-force formula and arithmetic.
- 2 marks — moment computation and final answer in MN·m.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Pitfalls — Examiner's Pitfall Callout (read before writing the exam):**
> 1. **Forgetting contingency factor** → lose 1–2 marks on weight problems.
> 2. **Mixing datum systems** (some $z_i$ from MSL, some from cellar deck) → CG error → lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Using $C_d = 1.0$ instead of 1.6–2.0** for topside bluff bodies → under-estimate wind force.
> 4. **Ignoring power-law exponent in wind profile** → wrong $V_z$ → cascading error.
> 5. **Writing final answer without units** → ½ mark deduction per unmarked quantity.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Topside = above-water superstructure** housing production, drilling, accommodation, and utilities.
- ⭐ **Hierarchical deck zoning:** Cellar (hazardous) → Process → Production → Accommodation (safe) → Helideck.
- ⭐ **Total Dry Weight** = sum of all module + structural weights (excluding live load).
- ⭐ **Total Operational Weight** = Dry + Live + Contingency (10–15 %).
- ⭐ **VCG formula** $Z_{cg} = \sum W_i z_i / \sum W_i$ — memorize the *moment-of-mass* logic.
- ⭐ **Wind loading on topside** is computed using the **power-law velocity profile** + **drag force** $F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a V^2 C_d A$.
- ⭐ **Wind overturning moment** = $F_w \cdot h_{cg}$ — governs jacket leg compression & pile pull-out.
- ⭐ **Modularization** enables **parallel onshore fabrication** → reduced offshore hook-up time.
- ⭐ **Installation method selection** depends on topside weight: <2,500 t → single lift; 2,500–15,000 t → dual lift; 15,000–25,000 t → float-over skid-on; >25,000 t → float-over balanced lift.
- ⭐ **High VCG** (>12 m) amplifies dynamic response — flag for Module 4 dynamic analysis.
- ⭐ **Standard industry metrics:** Topside weight **2,000–25,000 t**, deck elevation **15–40 m**, typical $C_d$ for topsides **1.6–2.0**.
- ⭐ **Always state the datum** before any CG calculation.
- ⭐ **Always include units** in final numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Appurtenances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Appurtenances

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of fixed offshore steel platforms (jacket-type), **Appurtenances** are defined as the secondary, auxiliary, or non-primary structural members, components, and equipment that are physically attached to or integrated with the main jacket, deck, or substructure to enable safe operations, personnel transfer, vessel berthing, corrosion protection, and functional utility of the platform.

As per API RP 2A (Working Stress Design) and ISO 19902 design philosophies, appurtenances are designed and detailed as **secondary structures** but their failure can cause significant operational disruption, environmental hazards, and loss of life. Therefore, they are explicitly addressed under platform safety, operability, and integrity management frameworks.

The major categories of appurtenances studied under KTU Module 3 include:
- **Boat Landings** (Personnel & Material Transfer)
- **Fender Systems** (Energy-Absorbing Berthing Aids)
- **Barge Bumpers** (Impact Protection for Jacket Legs)
- **Helidecks** (Helicopter Landing Facilities)
- **Walkways, Stairways, and Ladders** (Egress and Access)
- **Sacrificial Anode Systems** (Cathodic Protection)
- **J-Tubes / I-Tubes / Riser Porches** (Conduit and Riser Protection)
- **Bollards, Fairleads, and Davits** (Mooring and Lifting)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Appurtenances form the bridge between structural engineering and marine-operational engineering. Questions typically test both **descriptive knowledge** of components and **quantitative design** of fenders, anodes, and impact loads.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large **seaside apartment building** standing on stilts in the ocean. The stilts, pillars, and floors of the building are the **primary structure**. But to make it functional, you need many secondary things attached to it:

- A **balcony** from which boats can pick up people → this is the **Boat Landing**.
- **Cushioned bumpers** on the side of the building to prevent boats from crashing → these are the **Fenders**.
- A **sturdy pole** that pushes away big supply ships → this is the **Barge Bumper**.
- A small **helipad** on the roof → this is the **Helideck**.
- **Stairs and walkways** connecting floors → these are **Stairways and Walkways**.
- **Zinc blocks** welded underwater that "sacrifice" themselves by rusting instead of the building → these are **Sacrificial Anodes**.
- **Pipes** running down the legs to safely guide well fluids → these are **J-Tubes / I-Tubes**.

So, appurtenances are essentially the **"supporting cast"** of an offshore platform — without them, even a perfectly designed platform cannot function safely or economically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** If the jacket and deck are the *skeleton and body* of the platform, the appurtenances are the *limbs, safety gear, and tools* that allow the platform to interact with people, vessels, and the marine environment.

---

## 1.3 Governing Design Codes and Constants

The design of appurtenances is governed by international codes and standards. The key ones are:

| Standard | Full Name | Applicability |
|---|---|---|
| **API RP 2A-WSD** | American Petroleum Institute – Recommended Practice 2A (Working Stress Design) | Fixed Offshore Platforms |
| **ISO 19902** | International Standard for Fixed Steel Offshore Structures | Jacket Platforms |
| **DNVGL-OS-C101** | DNV GL – Design of Offshore Steel Structures | General Offshore Steel |
| **API RP 2FB** | Recommended Practice for Design of Offshore Facilities for Fire, Blast, and Emergency Response | Helidecks, Escape Routes |
| **CAP 437** | Civil Aviation Authority – Helideck Landing Area Standards | Helideck Geometry |
| **NACE SP0176** | National Association of Corrosion Engineers – Corrosion Control of Fixed Offshore Structures | Cathodic Protection |
| **OCIMF MEG3** | Mooring Equipment Guidelines (3rd Edition) | Fenders, Bollards, Fairleads |

**Key physical constants** (highlighted as required by the protocol):
- Density of seawater: $\rho_w = \mathbf{1025\ kg/m^3}$
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = \mathbf{9.81\ m/s^2}$
- Density of structural steel: $\rho_s = \mathbf{7850\ kg/m^3}$
- Modulus of elasticity of steel: $E = \mathbf{2.0 \times 10^5\ N/mm^2}$
- Standard seawater resistivity (for anode design): $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{0.20\ \Omega \cdot m}$ (typical Gulf of Mexico; 0.30 Ω·m for North Sea)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Appurtenances

Appurtenances on a fixed offshore platform are classified based on their **functional role**, **location**, and **load type**. The taxonomy below is the standard KTU-examination framework.

### 2.1.1 Boat Landings
A **boat landing** is a structural frame attached to one or more jacket legs at the +EL. (+) 5.0 m to (+) 8.0 m elevation above Mean Sea Level (MSL), designed to provide a safe, partially-submersible transfer point for crew boats and work boats.

**Components:**
- Horizontal landing platform (typically 1.5 m × 1.5 m grating)
- Vertical guides (fingers) to hold the boat against the platform
- Sliding fenders (HDPE-lined) for the boat hull
- Hoop or ring at the top for boat to hook onto

**Design loads:**
- Vessel berthing impact (kinetic energy)
- Personnel live load: **1.5 kN/m²** (typical)
- Wave-induced vertical loads (when submerged in crest)
- Mooring line pull from the boat

### 2.1.2 Fender Systems
Fenders are **energy-absorbing devices** placed on the boat landing or on the platform face to absorb the kinetic energy of an approaching vessel and convert it into a controlled, low-reaction force on the platform.

**Energy Equation (Kinetic Energy to be absorbed):**
$$E = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2 \quad \text{[Joules]}$$

where:
- $m$ = vessel mass (kg) including hydrodynamic added mass
- $v$ = berthing velocity (m/s), typically **0.15 to 0.50 m/s** for offshore platforms

**Reaction Force on the Platform:**
$$F = \dfrac{E}{\delta} \quad \text{[kN]}$$

where $\delta$ = fender deflection at design energy (m).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Fenders?** A 100-tonne boat berthing at 0.4 m/s has a kinetic energy of $\tfrac{1}{2} \times 100{,}000 \times 0.4^2 = 8{,}000$ J. Without a fender, this energy would be transmitted directly to the platform as an impulsive impact — potentially causing structural damage. With a fender, the energy is dissipated over a deflection of say 100 mm, reducing the reaction force to a manageable 80 kN.

### 2.1.3 Barge Bumpers
A **barge bumper** is a robust, stiff, large-diameter steel tubular or reinforced-concrete element installed on the upstream face of a jacket leg (or on a dedicated outrigger) to deflect or absorb the impact of an approaching supply barge. Unlike fenders, barge bumpers are designed to be **structurally sacrificial** or to redirect the impact.

**Design considerations:**
- Energy absorption capacity (typically 10× that of a fender)
- Local denting/buckling of the leg is checked
- Plastic hinge formation is permitted (limit-state design)
- Most codes require the leg to remain elastic after a "minor" impact

### 2.1.4 Helideck
A helideck is an aluminium or steel grating deck on top of the platform (or on a cantilevered extension) designed to **CAP 437** or **API RP 2L** standards. It must accommodate the largest helicopter (typically **Sikorsky S-92** or **AW189** with MTOW ~12–14 tonnes).

**Design loads:**
- Helicopter Dynamic Load: $1.5 \times$ MTOW distributed over a $0.3\ m \times 0.3\ m$ contact patch
- Single-wheel contact: MTOW on a $0.25\ m \times 0.25\ m$ area
- Net uplift (in case of helicopter tied down): wind uplift minus weight

**Key geometric requirements:**
- Minimum diameter of landing circle: **1.0 D** of the largest helicopter (where D = rotor diameter)
- Perimeter safety net: 1.5 m wide, capable of supporting a 100 kg person falling 1 m
- Two opposing approaches separated by 180°

### 2.1.5 Sacrificial Anode System (Cathodic Protection)
Submerged steel corrodes in seawater at a rate of about **0.1 to 0.5 mm/year** unless protected. The most common offshore method is **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)** or **Galvanic (Sacrificial) Anode** system.

**Sacrificial anodes** are typically made of **aluminium-zinc-indium (Al-Zn-In)** alloy (modern) or zinc (older designs). They corrode preferentially, protecting the steel.

**Design Current (Cathodic Current Demand):**
$$I_c = A_s \cdot i_c \quad \text{[A]}$$

where:
- $A_s$ = total submerged steel surface area to be protected (m²)
- $i_c$ = design current density (A/m²). For warm, aerated tropical waters: $i_c = \mathbf{0.10\text{–}0.15\ A/m^2}$ (initial), $\mathbf{0.03\text{–}0.05\ A/m^2}$ (mean), $\mathbf{0.015\text{–}0.025\ A/m^2}$ (final, after polarization)

**Number of Anodes Required:**
$$N = \dfrac{I_c \cdot T \cdot 1000}{C_a \cdot u_a} \quad \text{[No. of anodes]}$$

where:
- $T$ = design life (years) — typically 20–30 years
- $C_a$ = anode electrochemical capacity (Ah/kg) — Al alloy: **2000–2700 Ah/kg**
- $u_a$ = anode utilization factor (typically **0.85** for long-flake or stand-off anodes, **0.75** for short flush-mounted)

**Anode Mass:**
$$m_a = \dfrac{I_c \cdot T \cdot 8760}{u_a \cdot C_a} \quad \text{[kg]}$$

(8760 = hours per year)

### 2.1.6 J-Tubes, I-Tubes, and Riser Porches
- **J-Tube**: A curved, large-diameter pipe (typically 12"–24") attached to a jacket leg, used to guide flexible or rigid risers from the seabed up to the platform deck without exposing them to wave impact.
- **I-Tube**: A straight, near-vertical conduit (similar to a J-tube but vertical) for the same purpose; used when the riser approach angle is steep.
- **Riser Porch**: A short, cantilevered structural extension at the splash zone that holds and supports the risers as they enter the water.

### 2.1.7 Walkways, Stairways, Ladders, and Other Access
- **Primary access** between modules: covered walkways (minimum 1.0 m wide, with 1.1 m headroom).
- **Ladders**: vertical, with safety cages if height > 3 m; rest platforms every 9 m.
- **Escape routes**: must be provided in 2 directions, with 1-hour fire rating per API RP 2FB.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks / Code Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kinetic energy of vessel impact | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | J | Includes added-mass coefficient $C_m = 1.5\text{–}2.0$ |
| 2 | Fender reaction force | $F = E / \delta$ | N | $\delta$ = fender deflection (m) |
| 3 | Berthing velocity | $v = C_h \cdot C_c \cdot C_s \cdot V_{wind}$ | m/s | $C_h, C_c, C_s$ are site, configuration, and shape factors; per OCIMF MEG3 |
| 4 | Morison wave force on small member | $f = f_D + f_I = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D D \vert u \vert u + \rho C_M A \dot{u}$ | N/m | Used for fender piles, anodes, small boat landing members |
| 5 | Cathodic current demand | $I_c = A_s \cdot i_c$ | A | $i_c$ depends on water resistivity & temperature |
| 6 | Number of anodes | $N = (I_c T \cdot 8760) / (u_a C_a)$ | – | Aluminum anodes: $C_a \approx 2000\text{–}2700$ Ah/kg |
| 7 | Anode mass | $m_a = I_c T \cdot 8760 / (u_a C_a)$ | kg | Per single anode |
| 8 | Total anode weight | $W = N \cdot m_a \cdot g$ | N | For platform weight check |
| 9 | Local denting on a tubular leg (impact) | $D_d / D = C \cdot (E / \sigma_y D t^2)^{n}$ | – | Empirical; $C$ and $n$ from DNV-RP-C204 |
| 10 | Helideck wheel contact area | $A = MTOW / \sigma_{allow}$ | m² | $\sigma_{allow} \approx 200$ MPa (aluminium grating) |
| 11 | Wave slam force on boat landing | $F_s = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_s A u^2$ | N | $C_s \approx 3.5$ for horizontal members at splash zone |
| 12 | Submerged weight for hydrodynamic check | $W_{sub} = W_{dry} - \rho_w g V$ | N | Buoyancy-reduced weight |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU:** When using the Morison equation, watch out for the **sign convention** of velocity $u$ and acceleration $\dot{u}$. Use `\vert u \vert` in markdown to avoid breaking the table syntax. KTU examiners frequently deduct marks for sign errors.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility and Real-World Significance

Appurtenances directly impact the **operability, safety, and economics** of an offshore installation:

- **Safety:** Helidecks enable medevac. Walkways and escape routes enable orderly egress. Fenders prevent vessel damage and fuel spills.
- **Operability:** J-tubes and riser porches enable production. Anodes prevent catastrophic corrosion failure during the 20–30 year design life.
- **Economics:** A well-designed boat landing allows crew change in **Sea State 3 (Hs ≈ 1.0–1.5 m)** instead of aborting, saving millions in lost production per year.
- **Regulatory:** Most appurtenances are mandated by law (CAP 437 for helidecks, NACE for cathodic protection, SOLAS for escape routes). Their absence can stop a platform from being commissioned.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Energy Absorbed by a Fender

**Problem Statement:** A 120-tonne supply boat approaches a fixed offshore platform with a berthing velocity of 0.35 m/s. The fender system has a deflection of 150 mm at the rated capacity. Determine:
- (a) The kinetic energy to be absorbed
- (b) The peak reaction force on the platform
- (c) The number of identical fenders required if each fender is rated for 30 kJ
- (d) The hydrodynamic added-mass correction

**Step 1 — Identify the given data.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= 120 \text{ tonnes} = 120{,}000 \text{ kg} \\
v &= 0.35 \text{ m/s} \\
\delta &= 150 \text{ mm} = 0.150 \text{ m} \\
E_{fender} &= 30 \text{ kJ per fender} \\
C_m &= 1.8 \text{ (typical added-mass coefficient for berthing vessel)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the effective (hydrodynamic) mass.**

The vessel does not move in isolation — it displaces water, which adds an inertial term. The effective mass is:

$$
m_{eff} = C_m \cdot m = 1.8 \times 120{,}000 = 216{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the kinetic energy of impact.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_k &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m_{eff} \cdot v^2 \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 216{,}000 \times (0.35)^2 \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 216{,}000 \times 0.1225 \\
&= 108{,}000 \times 0.1225 \\
&= 13{,}230 \text{ J} = 13.23 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the peak reaction force on the platform.**

Assuming the fender behaves as a linear spring (energy stored = work done = $F \times \delta$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{peak} &= \dfrac{E_k}{\delta} \\
&= \dfrac{13{,}230}{0.150} \\
&= 88{,}200 \text{ N} \approx 88.2 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Determine the number of fenders required.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{E_k}{E_{fender}} \\
&= \dfrac{13.23 \text{ kJ}}{30 \text{ kJ/fender}} \\
&= 0.44
\end{aligned}
$$

Since a partial fender cannot be installed, we **round up to 1 fender**. However, in practice, we would provide 2 fenders for redundancy and to allow berthing on either side of the boat landing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Judgement:** Even though the calculation yields < 1 fender, KTU answers should always comment on **redundancy, symmetry, and code-mandated minimums**.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Cathodic Protection — Anode Quantity

**Problem Statement:** A 4-leg jacket platform is to be protected by aluminium sacrificial anodes. The submerged steel area (jacket + conductors + piles) totals $A_s = 4500 \text{ m}^2$. The mean current density in the seawater is $i_c = 0.060 \text{ A/m}^2$. Design life: $T = 25$ years. Anode capacity $C_a = 2500 \text{ Ah/kg}$. Utilization factor $u_a = 0.85$.

Determine:
- (a) Total cathodic current demand
- (b) Total anode mass required
- (c) Number of anodes if each anode is **150 kg**

**Step 1 — Total cathodic current demand.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_c &= A_s \cdot i_c \\
&= 4500 \text{ m}^2 \times 0.060 \text{ A/m}^2 \\
&= 270 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total anode mass required.**

Total charge required over 25 years (8760 hours/year):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= I_c \cdot T \cdot 8760 \\
&= 270 \times 25 \times 8760 \\
&= 270 \times 219{,}000 \\
&= 59{,}130{,}000 \text{ Ah} = 5.913 \times 10^7 \text{ Ah}
\end{aligned}
$$

Mass of anodes required:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{anodes} &= \dfrac{Q_{total}}{u_a \cdot C_a} \\
&= \dfrac{5.913 \times 10^7}{0.85 \times 2500} \\
&= \dfrac{5.913 \times 10^7}{2125} \\
&= 27{,}824 \text{ kg} \approx 27.82 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of anodes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{M_{anodes}}{m_a} = \dfrac{27{,}824}{150} \\
&= 185.5
\end{aligned}
$$

Round up → **186 anodes**.

> [!NOTE]
> These are distributed on the jacket legs and braces, typically **2–3 m below MLW**, with a spacing of about 6–9 m per leg, oriented so the long axis is vertical (for sled-type anodes) or horizontal (for bracelet-type on piles).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Table for Appurtenance Selection

| Operating Condition | Required Appurtenance | Design Driver | Typical Rating |
|---|---|---|---|
| Daily crew change by boat | Boat Landing + Fender | Vessel berthing energy | 10–30 kJ |
| Supply barge alongside | Barge Bumper | Leg impact energy | 100–500 kJ |
| Helicopter operations | Helideck | MTOW + impact | 12–14 t S-92 |
| Personnel movement on deck | Walkways/Stairways | Live load + wave splash | 1.5 kN/m² |
| Corrosion in seawater | Sacrificial Anodes | Current density × life | 0.06 A/m² (mean) |
| Riser routing from seabed | J-Tube / I-Tube | Riser diameter + bend radius | 12"–24" pipe |
| Mooring of standby vessel | Bollards + Fairleads | Line break load (MBL) | 50–100 t |
| Crane lifts for maintenance | Pedestal Crane + Pedestal | Lifting capacity + radius | 20–50 t |

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Discussion: Fender vs. Barge Bumper

This is a **frequently asked 14-mark KTU question**. The structured answer format is:

**Fender:**
- Purpose: Absorb energy of a **moving boat** during berthing.
- Energy: Low to moderate (5–50 kJ).
- Reaction force: Low (50–500 kN).
- Deflection: Significant (50–200 mm).
- Material: Rubber, HDPE, foam.
- Position: On the boat landing.
- Failure mode: Compression set, tear, ageing.

**Barge Bumper:**
- Purpose: Protect jacket leg from **ramming supply barge**.
- Energy: High (100–1000 kJ).
- Reaction force: High (500–5000 kN).
- Deflection: Small (plastic deformation allowed).
- Material: Steel tubular, reinforced concrete.
- Position: On the upstream face of the leg.
- Failure mode: Denting, plastic hinge, leg local buckling.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Conflating fenders and barge bumpers. They are **not interchangeable**. A fender handles a controlled, low-energy berthing; a barge bumper handles a rare, high-energy accidental impact.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Appurtenances on a Fixed Jacket Platform

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional relationships** and **categorical hierarchy** of appurtenances on a typical fixed offshore platform. It follows the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards (no reserved keywords as node IDs, no markdown formatting inside node labels).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    platformA[Fixed Jacket Platform]
    platformA --> accessB[Access and Egress Group]
    platformA --> opsC[Operations Group]
    platformA --> safetyD[Safety and Protection Group]
    platformA --> utilityE[Utility and Service Group]

    accessB --> stairsB1[Stairways and Ladders]
    accessB --> walksB2[Walkways and Bridges]
    accessB --> escapeB3[Escape Routes per API 2FB]

    opsC --> boatC1[Boat Landing System]
    opsC --> heliC2[Helideck per CAP 437]
    opsC --> craneC3[Pedestal Crane]
    opsC --> jtubeC4[J-Tube and I-Tube Conduits]
    opsC --> riserC5[Riser Porches]

    safetyD --> fenderD1[Fender System]
    safetyD --> bumperD2[Barge Bumper on Legs]
    safetyD --> anodeD3[Sacrificial Anode System]
    safetyD --> netD4[Perimeter Safety Net on Helideck]
    safetyD --> bollardD5[Bollards and Fairleads]

    utilityE --> lightE1[Navigation and Aviation Lights]
    utilityE --> sumpE2[Sump Caisson]
    utilityE --> condE3[Conductors and Jumper Spools]
    utilityE --> cableE4[Cable Trays and I-Tubes]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Cathodic Protection (Sacrificial Anode) System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    seawaterA[Seawater Electrolyte]
    anodeA[Aluminium Alloy Anode]
    steelB[Submerged Steel Jacket]
    currentA[Protection Current Flow]
    corrosionA[Corrosion Reaction at Anode]
    protectB[Steel Remains Protected]

    anodeA -- Oxidation Reaction --> corrosionA
    corrosionA -- Releases Electrons --> currentA
    currentA -- Flows Through --> seawaterA
    seawaterA -- Conducts Ions --> steelB
    steelB -- Receives Electrons --> protectB
    anodeA -- Mass Loss Over Time --> anodeB[Anode Mass Reduces]
    anodeB -- After T Years --> anodeC[Anode Must Be Replaced]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Boat Landing and Fender Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    vesselA[Approaching Crew Boat]
    vesselA -- Berthing Velocity v --> contactB[Initial Hull Contact]
    contactB --> fenderC[Sliding HDPE Fender on Boat Landing]
    fenderC -- Energy Absorption E --> fenderD[Fender Deflection delta]
    fenderD -- Reaction Force F --> platformE[Boat Landing Frame]
    platformE -- Load Transfer --> legF[Jacket Leg]
    legF -- Foundation --> pileG[Driven Pile into Seabed]
    vesselA -- Mooring Hook --> hoopH[Upper Hoop Guide]
    hoopH -- Vertical Restraint --> legF
```

## 4.4 Spatial Layout: Vertical Elevation of a Fixed Platform

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    elTop[Topside Deck EL plus 25 to 35 m]
    elTop --> heli1[Helideck on Top Deck]
    elTop --> crane2[Pedestal Crane]
    elTop --> walk3[Inter-module Walkways]
    elMid[Splash Zone EL plus 5 to 12 m]
    elMid --> boat4[Boat Landing at EL plus 6 m]
    elMid --> fender5[Fenders on Boat Landing]
    elMid --> riser6[Riser Porches]
    elLower[Submerged Zone EL minus 5 to minus 100 m]
    elLower --> anode7[Sacrificial Anodes on Legs]
    elLower --> jtube8[J-Tube on Leg]
    elLower --> conductor9[Well Conductors]
    elBottom[Seabed]
    elBottom --> pile10[Driven Piles]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a "boat landing" in an offshore platform. List its main components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A boat landing is a **secondary structural frame** attached to the jacket leg of a fixed offshore platform at an elevation of approximately **+6 m above MSL**, designed to enable safe transfer of personnel and materials between boats/vessels and the platform.

**Main components (any three for full marks):**
1. **Landing platform** (1.5 m × 1.5 m grating) for personnel to step onto.
2. **Vertical guide fingers** to constrain the boat hull laterally.
3. **Sliding fenders (HDPE or rubber-lined)** to absorb berthing energy and protect the boat hull.
4. **Upper hoop or ring** at the top to take the mooring hook of the boat.
5. **Access ladder** connecting the landing to the main deck above.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for 4 components.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the function of a sacrificial anode? Name the materials commonly used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Function (1.5 marks):** A sacrificial anode is a highly active metal (more electronegative than steel) that is intentionally allowed to corrode in seawater, thereby **supplying electrons** to the submerged steel structure and preventing its corrosion. This is known as **galvanic cathodic protection**.

**Materials used (1.5 marks):**
- **Aluminium-Zinc-Indium (Al-Zn-In) alloy** — most common in modern offshore platforms. Capacity ~ 2500 Ah/kg.
- **Zinc alloy** — older platforms; capacity ~ 780 Ah/kg.
- **Magnesium alloy** — used only in fresh water (low conductivity) or for temporary protection.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the different types of **appurtenances** used in fixed offshore steel platforms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A supply vessel of mass **250 tonnes** berths at a fixed platform with an effective velocity of **0.30 m/s**. The fender has a stiffness of **800 kN/m**. Determine the **peak fender reaction** and the **maximum energy absorbed**. Take added-mass coefficient $C_m = 1.7$. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Types of Appurtenances (7 marks)

The major types of appurtenances on a fixed offshore jacket platform are:

1. **Boat Landings** (1 mark): Frame attached to the jacket leg at the splash zone (~ +6 m above MSL) for crew and material transfer. Components: landing platform, guide fingers, sliding fenders, hoop.

2. **Fenders** (1 mark): Energy-absorbing elements (rubber, HDPE, foam) on the boat landing face to absorb berthing kinetic energy of vessels and reduce reaction force on the platform.

3. **Barge Bumpers** (1 mark): Large, robust steel tubular or concrete elements on the upstream face of jacket legs to absorb or deflect impact from supply barges.

4. **Helidecks** (1 mark): Aluminium or steel deck on top of the platform, designed to CAP 437 standards, with a perimeter safety net, for helicopter take-off and landing.

5. **Sacrificial Anode Systems** (1 mark): Aluminium or zinc anodes welded to submerged members to provide cathodic protection against corrosion.

6. **J-Tubes, I-Tubes, and Riser Porches** (1 mark): Conduits that guide flexible or rigid risers from the seabed up to the production deck, protecting them from wave and boat impact.

7. **Walkways, Stairways, Ladders, and Escape Routes** (1 mark): Provide personnel access and emergency egress between modules, designed to API RP 2FB.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given data:**
- $m = 250$ tonnes $= 250{,}000$ kg
- $v = 0.30$ m/s
- $k = 800$ kN/m $= 800{,}000$ N/m
- $C_m = 1.7$

**Step 1 — Effective mass with added mass (2 marks):**
$$
m_{eff} = C_m \times m = 1.7 \times 250{,}000 = 425{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Kinetic energy to be absorbed (2 marks):**
$$
E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m_{eff} v^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 425{,}000 \times (0.30)^2
$$
$$
E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 425{,}000 \times 0.09 = 19{,}125 \text{ J} \approx 19.13 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum fender deflection (1.5 marks):**

For a linear spring, all kinetic energy converts to spring potential energy:
$$
E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} k \delta^2 \implies \delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E_k}{k}}
$$
$$
\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 19{,}125}{800{,}000}} = \sqrt{0.0478} = 0.2187 \text{ m} \approx 218.7 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Peak fender reaction (1.5 marks):**
$$
F_{peak} = k \cdot \delta = 800{,}000 \times 0.2187 = 174{,}960 \text{ N} \approx 175 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget the **added-mass coefficient** $C_m$ and use the displacement mass directly. This underestimates the actual kinetic energy by up to **80%** and produces a dangerously low reaction force. Always include $C_m$ in the calculation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the **cathodic protection system** by sacrificial anodes for offshore platforms. Derive the expression for the **number of anodes required** and the **mass of each anode**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The submerged steel area to be protected on a jacket platform is **3000 m²**. Design a sacrificial aluminium anode system for a **20-year** design life. Use: $i_c = 0.055 \text{ A/m}^2$ (mean), $C_a = 2500 \text{ Ah/kg}$, $u_a = 0.85$. Assume each anode has a **net mass of 120 kg**. Find the **number of anodes**. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Theory + Derivation (7 marks)

**Theory (3 marks):** Cathodic protection by sacrificial anodes is an electrochemical method where a more active metal (aluminium, zinc, or magnesium) is electrically connected to the submerged steel structure. Because the anode metal is more electronegative, it preferentially corrodes (oxidizes), releasing electrons. These electrons flow to the steel cathode, suppressing the anodic (corrosion) reaction of the iron. The steel is thus protected as long as sufficient anodic material is present.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let:
- $A_s$ = submerged steel area (m²)
- $i_c$ = mean design current density (A/m²)
- $I_c$ = total cathodic current (A)
- $T$ = design life (years)
- $C_a$ = anode electrochemical capacity (Ah/kg)
- $u_a$ = utilization factor (dimensionless)

**Total current demand:**
$$
I_c = A_s \cdot i_c \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

**Total charge required over the design life** (1 year = 8760 hours):
$$
Q_{total} = I_c \cdot T \cdot 8760 \quad \text{[Ah]} \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

**Total anode mass required** (after accounting for utilization):
$$
M_{total} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{u_a \cdot C_a} = \dfrac{I_c \cdot T \cdot 8760}{u_a \cdot C_a} \quad \text{[kg]} \quad \text{...(3)}
$$

**Number of anodes**, if each anode has mass $m_a$:
$$
N = \dfrac{M_{total}}{m_a} = \dfrac{I_c \cdot T \cdot 8760}{u_a \cdot C_a \cdot m_a} \quad \text{...(4)}
$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $A_s = 3000$ m²
- $i_c = 0.055$ A/m²
- $T = 20$ years
- $C_a = 2500$ Ah/kg
- $u_a = 0.85$
- $m_a = 120$ kg per anode

**Step 1 — Total cathodic current (2 marks):**
$$
I_c = A_s \cdot i_c = 3000 \times 0.055 = 165 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2 — Total charge required (2 marks):**
$$
Q_{total} = 165 \times 20 \times 8760 = 165 \times 175{,}200 = 28{,}908{,}000 \text{ Ah}
$$

**Step 3 — Total anode mass (1.5 marks):**
$$
M_{total} = \dfrac{28{,}908{,}000}{0.85 \times 2500} = \dfrac{28{,}908{,}000}{2125} = 13{,}604 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of anodes (1.5 marks):**
$$
N = \dfrac{13{,}604}{120} = 113.4
$$

Round up → **114 anodes**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Cathodic Protection):**
> 1. Students frequently **omit the utilization factor** $u_a$, which inflates the anode mass and number by 15–25%, leading to a wasteful (but safe) design — and **deductions for not applying the concept correctly**.
> 2. Some students use $i_c$ for the **initial** value (~0.10 A/m²) instead of the **mean** value (~0.055 A/m²). The mean is the correct choice for design life, not the initial. The **final** value is used only to verify long-term polarization.
> 3. Always specify the **units** of $C_a$ in Ah/kg — a frequent 0.5–1 mark deduction in KTU scripts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for Appurtenances**

- **Definition:** Appurtenances are *auxiliary* structures attached to the main offshore platform; they are *not* part of the primary load path.
- **Boat Landing:** Located at the splash zone (~ +6 m above MSL); consists of platform, fingers, fenders, and a hoop.
- **Fender:** Absorbs *controlled* berthing energy; reduces reaction force; typical $E = 5\text{–}50$ kJ; made of rubber/HDPE.
