- **Barge Bumper:** Handles *accidental* high-energy impact; located on the jacket leg; $E = 100\text{–}1000$ kJ; allows plastic deformation.
- **Helideck:** Designed to CAP 437; net uplift and dynamic load check; perimeter safety net mandatory.
- **Sacrificial Anode:** Aluminium alloy most common; $C_a \approx 2500$ Ah/kg; $u_a \approx 0.85$.
- **Key formula 1:** $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} C_m m v^2$ (energy of berthing vessel).
- **Key formula 2:** $F_{peak} = E_k / \delta$ (linear-fender reaction).
- **Key formula 3:** $I_c = A_s \cdot i_c$ (cathodic current demand).
- **Key formula 4:** $N = (I_c \cdot T \cdot 8760) / (u_a \cdot C_a \cdot m_a)$ (number of anodes).
- **J-Tube vs I-Tube:** J-Tube is curved (used for shallow-angle risers); I-Tube is straight (used for steep-angle risers).
- **Codes to Remember:** API RP 2A, API RP 2FB, CAP 437, NACE SP0176, OCIMF MEG3, ISO 19902.
- **Physical Constants:** $\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³; $g = 9.81$ m/s²; seawater resistivity ~ 0.20–0.30 Ω·m.
- **Always include the added-mass coefficient** $C_m$ (1.5–2.0) in impact energy calculations.
- **Always round up** the number of anodes and fenders; partial units are not allowed.
- **Examiner's pet trick:** Ask you to compare fenders vs. barge bumpers — keep the four parameters (energy, force, deflection, material) ready.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Floating Structures: Basic Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Floating Structures: Basic Configurations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Floating Offshore Structure** is a buoyant marine vessel or platform that derives its primary vertical support from the buoyancy force exerted by the displaced water (Archimedes' Principle), rather than from fixed structural members penetrating the seabed. These structures are typically moored or dynamically positioned to maintain station over a wellhead and are deployed in water depths where fixed (jacket) platforms become economically or technically unfeasible (usually beyond **300 m / 1000 ft**).

A **Basic Configuration** refers to the geometric arrangement of the buoyant hull (or hulls), columns, pontoons, and ballast systems that define how the structure interacts with the wave environment, governs its stability, and dictates its heave, pitch, and roll responses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Floating Structures — Basic configurations are classified on the basis of **(i) Geometry of the buoyant hull**, **(ii) Mooring/Station-keeping philosophy**, and **(iii) Stability mechanism (shifting ballast, fixed ballast, mooring line tension)**.

### 1.2 The Four Canonical Configurations

| S.No. | Configuration | Stability Mechanism | Typical Operating Depth |
|:-----:|:--------------|:--------------------|:-----------------------:|
| 1 | **Ship / Barge (Drillship / FPSO)** | Shaped hull form (high metacentric height) | Ultra-deep (3000 m+) |
| 2 | **Semi-Submersible** | Submerged pontoons + column spacing (large waterplane area & low VCG) | 300 m – 3000 m |
| 3 | **Spar Platform** | Low centre of gravity via fixed ballast in deep draft hull | 500 m – 3000 m |
| 4 | **Tension Leg Platform (TLP)** | Vertical pretension in taut mooring tethers (compliant) | 300 m – 1500 m |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Floating Lemon, the Floating Table, the Floating Pencil & the Floating Umbrella"

Imagine four everyday objects placed in a bathtub of water to keep a small toy upright:

1. **A floating lemon (Ship/Barge):** It sits on top because its own shape is wide and round at the bottom — if you tilt it, it rights itself. *Analogy:* A drillship uses its wide, ship-shaped hull. The shape alone provides stability — no extra ballast, no taut lines.

2. **A floating table (Semi-Submersible):** Only the legs and a flat top sit above water; most of the table (the columns + pontoons) are below the waves. *Analogy:* The semi-sub uses its widely-spaced columns and submerged pontoons to "stand wide" on the sea, minimizing wave-driven motion.

3. **A floating pencil (Spar):** Imagine a long pencil weighted at the bottom (with blu-tack), floating vertically like a buoy. It is super stable because the heavy weight is **far below** the waterline. *Analogy:* A spar has a deep-draft cylindrical hull fixed-ballasted so its centre of gravity is way below its centre of buoyancy.

4. **An inverted umbrella pulled by strings (TLP):** Imagine an upside-down umbrella held underwater by taut strings anchored to the bottom of the bath. The strings pull it down, so the floats *want* to rise but the strings hold it. *Analogy:* A TLP has excess buoyancy; vertical tethers (taut leg tendons) pull the platform downward, providing a stiff vertical restoring force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Ship and Semi-Submersible are *positive-stability* systems (righting moment comes from shape/ballast). Spar is a *pendulum-stabilized* system (low VCG creates a large metacentric height). TLP is a *tension-stabilized* system (the mooring lines provide the heave restoring force, NOT the hull form).

### 1.4 Hydrostatic Definitions Every KTU Student Must Memorize

- **Displacement ($\Delta$):** Weight of water displaced by the hull = Total weight of the structure (floating condition). Units: **tonnes (t)** or **kN** (where $1 \text{ t} \approx 9.81 \text{ kN}$).
- **Draft (T):** Vertical distance from waterline to the lowest point of the hull. Units: **metres (m)**.
- **Freeboard (F):** Vertical distance from waterline to the main deck. Units: **m**. Relation: $F = \text{Air Gap} - T$ (approximate).
- **Centre of Gravity (G):** Point through which the weight $W$ of the structure acts.
- **Centre of Buoyancy (B):** Centroid of the displaced volume of water.
- **Metacentre (M):** Point about which the hull rotates when tilted by a small angle; for small angles, it is the **intersection of the vertical line through the new B with the original vertical axis**.
- **Metacentric Radius (BM):** $BM = I/V$, where $I$ = second moment of waterplane area about the tilt axis, $V$ = displaced volume.
- **Metacentric Height (GM):** $GM = KB + BM - KG = KM - KG$. Units: **m**.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Logic — Why Four Types and Not One?

The four canonical configurations arise because **no single hull form is optimal across all water depths, metocean conditions, and operational requirements**. The deciding engineering trade-offs are:

1. **Motion response in waves** (heave, pitch, roll natural periods vs. wave energy periods).
2. **Steel weight per unit payload capacity**.
3. **Mooring/Station-keeping cost and complexity**.
4. **Fabrication, tow-out, and installation feasibility**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off Principle:** As water depth increases, **fixed platforms become uneconomical** because the steel weight of a jacket grows roughly with the *square of water depth*. Floating structures decouple the hull weight from the water depth — they only need to float and stay on station. This is why **all deep-water developments (>500 m) use floating structures**.

### 2.2 Configuration-by-Configuration Engineering Logic

#### A. Ship / Barge / Drillship / FPSO

- **Geometry:** Mono-hull with a large length-to-beam ratio ($L/B \approx 5$ to $7$), bulbous bow, fine entry, transom stern.
- **Buoyancy distribution:** Displacement-type — the entire hull volume is buoyant.
- **Stability:** Hydrostatic stability relies on a **positive metacentric height $GM > 0$**, typically $0.15$ m to $1.5$ m for ships, higher for drillships.
- **Heave natural period:** $T_h = 2\pi\sqrt{C_{33}/\rho g A_w}$ where $A_w$ = waterplane area. For a drillship, $T_h \approx 4$ to $8$ s — dangerously close to typical wave periods (5–10 s), so **ship-shaped hulls have the worst heave performance** in waves.
- **Use case:** Storage (FPSO), mobile drilling (drillship) where mobility is paramount.

#### B. Semi-Submersible

- **Geometry:** Two (or more) submerged pontoons connected to the deck by 3–4 widely-spaced columns. Operates in **ballasted-down condition** with pontoons 15–30 m below the waterline.
- **Why submerge the pontoons?** To reduce the wave-exciting forces (wave forces decay exponentially with depth). At 20 m depth, a wave of 100 m wavelength exerts only ~10% of the force it would at the surface.
- **Stability:** Combines a **large waterplane area (columns only)** with a **low VCG** (ballast in pontoons) → very large $GM$ (typically 5–15 m).
- **Heave natural period:** Typically **15–25 s**, well above wave periods, so semi-subs have excellent seakeeping.
- **Use case:** Drilling, production, accommodation, crane vessels.

#### C. Spar Platform

- **Geometry:** Single large-diameter vertical cylinder (typically 30–40 m diameter, 100–200 m long), ballasted to operate with **90%+ of the hull submerged**.
- **Stability:** The **fixed ballast** (often heavy mineral ore or concrete) at the bottom of the hull places the centre of gravity **far below** the centre of buoyancy. Result: an enormous metacentric height ($GM \approx 30$ m). The structure behaves like an inverted pendulum — any tilt creates a strong righting moment.
- **Heave natural period:** Extremely high (~30–60 s) because the added mass and waterplane area are both large relative to displacement.
- **Drawback:** Requires **upend installation** at sea (complex tow-out and flooding operation).
- **Use case:** Deep-water dry-tree production (where drilling from a stable platform is needed).

#### D. Tension Leg Platform (TLP)

- **Geometry:** A semi-submersible-like hull (pontoons + columns) restrained by **vertical, taut tendons** anchored to the seabed.
- **Stability mechanism:** **Fundamentally different from the other three.** The tendons are in **permanent tension** (no slack). Vertical restoring comes from the *excess buoyancy* trying to lift the hull, resisted by the tendons. Horizontal restoring comes from the tendons' geometric stiffness (like a tent guy-line).
- **Heave natural period:** Very short (~2–4 s) because the tendons behave like springs — the platform is **stiff in heave**.
- **Drawback:** Requires precise tendon installation and fatigue management.
- **Use case:** Dry-tree production in moderate-to-deep water.

### 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All units must be consistent.** In SI: $W$ in **N**, lengths in **m**, areas in **m²**, volumes in **m³**, density $\rho_w = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (sea water), $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Application |
|:-:|:---------|:------------|:--------------------|
| 1 | $W = \Delta = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | Equilibrium of floating body (Archimedes) | All configurations |
| 2 | $\text{Reserve Buoyancy} = \nabla_{total} - \nabla_{design}$ | Volume of buoyancy above the design waterline | Flooded-compartment safety |
| 3 | $BM = I_T / \nabla$ | Transverse metacentric radius; $I_T$ = 2nd moment of WL area | Initial stability |
| 4 | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | Metacentric height (transverse) | All displacement hulls |
| 5 | $BM_L = I_L / \nabla$ | Longitudinal metacentric radius (pitch) | Ship, semi-sub |
| 6 | $T_h = 2\pi \sqrt{C_{33} + a_{33} / (\rho g A_w)}$ | Heave natural period ($a_{33}$ = added mass) | All configs |
| 7 | $T_\phi = 2\pi \sqrt{(I_{xx} + A_{44}) / (\rho g \nabla \cdot GM)}$ | Roll natural period | All configs |
| 8 | $T_\theta = 2\pi \sqrt{(I_{yy} + A_{55}) / (\rho g \nabla \cdot GML)}$ | Pitch natural period | Ship, semi-sub, TLP |
| 9 | $T_{TLP} = 2\pi \sqrt{M / K_{tendon}}$ | TLP heave period (tendon stiffness) | TLP only |
| 10 | $F_{tendon} = N \cdot \Delta L \cdot E \cdot A / L$ | Total tendon stiffness (parallel springs) | TLP only |
| 11 | $R_{NA} = W / (\pi \cdot d \cdot N_{tend})$ | Pretension per tendon | TLP only |
| 12 | $F_{moor} = K_{mooring} \cdot x$ | Linearized mooring restoring force | Ship, semi-sub, spar |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL MARKDOWN NOTE:** In your exam answer-book, the vertical bar symbol `|` for absolute value MUST NOT be confused with table delimiter `|`. Always write $\vert x \vert$ inside formulas, never `|x|`.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These configurations are the **backbone of the global offshore oil & gas industry**:
- **FPSO's** process and store oil directly at sea (e.g., Shell's *Turritella* FPSO).
- **Semi-submersibles** dominate the global MODU (Mobile Offshore Drilling Unit) fleet (e.g., Valaris DS-12, Transocean *Deepwater Horizon*).
- **Spars** like *Holstein* (Shell, Gulf of Mexico) host dry trees in 3000 m water.
- **TLPs** like *Hibernia* (off Newfoundland) enable year-round dry-tree production in harsh environments.

The same configurations are now repurposed for **offshore wind** (floating wind turbines) and **marine renewable energy**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transverse Metacentric Height (GM) for a Floating Body

**Starting Point — Equilibrium of a Floating Body:**
The weight $W$ acts downward through $G$, and the buoyancy force $\rho_w g \nabla$ acts upward through $B$, the centroid of the displaced volume. In equilibrium, $W = \rho_w g \nabla$ and $G$ lies directly above $B$ on the same vertical line.

**Step 1 — Apply a small rotation $\phi$ about the longitudinal (roll) axis:**
When the body is tilted by a small angle $\phi$, the underwater volume changes shape. A wedge of volume is submerged on one side and an equal wedge emerges on the other.

**Step 2 — Compute the shift in Centre of Buoyancy:**
Let $\Delta V$ be the small wedge volume. The new centre of buoyancy $B'$ is shifted **transversely** from $B$ by a distance $BB'$. Taking moments about the original $B$:

$$
W \cdot \overline{GZ} = \Delta V \cdot \rho_w g \cdot \overline{bb'}
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the transverse BM:**
By geometric definition (and from the second moment of area of the waterplane, $I_T$):

$$
\overline{bb'} = BM \cdot \sin\phi \quad \text{where} \quad BM = \frac{I_T}{\nabla}
$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate $\Delta V$ using the wedge geometry:**
The wedge volume is $\Delta V = (1/2) \cdot L \cdot \overline{bb'} \cdot \tan\phi \cdot 2 = (L \cdot \overline{bb'} \cdot \tan\phi)$ — more rigorously, the small wedge subtended by rotation has volume $\Delta V = (1/2)\int x \, dA \cdot \tan\phi \cdot 2 \approx \overline{bb'} \cdot \nabla \cdot \tan\phi$. A cleaner derivation uses the **virtual wedge** technique:

$$
\Delta V \cdot \overline{bb'} = \int x^2 \tan\phi \, dA = I_T \tan\phi
$$

**Step 5 — Combine and simplify:**
Substituting $W = \rho_w g \nabla$:

$$
\rho_w g \nabla \cdot \overline{GZ} = \rho_w g \cdot I_T \tan\phi
$$

$$
\overline{GZ} = \frac{I_T \tan\phi}{\nabla} = BM \cdot \tan\phi
$$

**Step 6 — Define the metacentric height GM:**
From the right triangle $GMB'$, $\overline{GZ} = GM \sin\phi$. For small angles $\tan\phi \approx \sin\phi$, giving:

$$
GM \cdot \sin\phi = BM \cdot \tan\phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad GM = BM
$$

**Step 7 — Final working expression:**

$$
\boxed{GM = KB + BM - KG = KB + \frac{I_T}{\nabla} - KG}
$$

This is the **universal hydrostatic stability criterion** applicable to all ship-shaped and semi-submersible hulls.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Heave Natural Period of a Floating Body (Simplified Linear)

**Starting Point — Newton's Second Law in the Vertical Direction:**
Let $z$ be the heave displacement (positive upward). The restoring force on the body when displaced by $z$ from equilibrium comes from the **change in buoyancy** (additional displaced volume):

$$
F_{restore} = -\rho_w g \cdot A_w \cdot z
$$

where $A_w$ is the waterplane area at equilibrium.

**Step 1 — Include added mass $a_{33}$ and hydrostatic stiffness $C_{33}$:**
Added mass accounts for the water that must be "pushed aside" during vertical motion:

$$
C_{33} = \rho_w g A_w
$$

**Step 2 — Equation of motion:**

$$
(M + a_{33}) \ddot{z} + C_{33} z = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Identify natural circular frequency:**

$$
\omega_n^2 = \frac{C_{33}}{M + a_{33}} = \frac{\rho_w g A_w}{M + a_{33}}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to natural period:**

$$
\boxed{T_h = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{M + a_{33}}{\rho_w g A_w}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation:**

- **Larger $A_w$** (semi-sub with widely spaced columns) → shorter $T_h$ — but wait, semi-subs have **reduced** $A_w$ because pontoons are deep. The heave period is therefore **long**.
- **Semi-sub trick:** In ballasted-down condition, $A_w$ is small (only column cross-sections at the waterline), so $T_h$ is **large (15–25 s)** — well above wave periods. This is the *whole reason* semi-subs are stable in waves.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — TLP Heave Period from Tendon Stiffness

**Starting Point — Tendons as Parallel Linear Springs:**
A TLP has $N$ identical vertical tendons. Each tendon behaves as a linear spring of stiffness $k = EA/L$. The total vertical stiffness is:

$$
K_{total} = N \cdot \frac{EA}{L}
$$

**Step 1 — Equation of motion (heave):**

$$
(M + a_{33}) \ddot{z} + (\rho_w g A_w + K_{total}) z = 0
$$

Note that BOTH hydrostatic and tendon stiffness act in parallel.

**Step 2 — Natural period:**

$$
\boxed{T_{TLP} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{M + a_{33}}{\rho_w g A_w + K_{total}}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Order-of-magnitude insight:**
With $K_{total}$ dominating $\rho_w g A_w$ by a factor of 10–100, $T_{TLP}$ collapses to **2–4 s** — far below typical wave periods. The TLP essentially rides *with* the wave surface — it doesn't move much because the tendons are too stiff to stretch.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Configuration Selection

| Criterion | Drillship/Ship | Semi-Sub | Spar | TLP |
|:----------|:--------------:|:--------:|:----:|:---:|
| Water depth range (m) | 300 – 3000+ | 300 – 3000 | 500 – 3000+ | 300 – 1500 |
| Heave period (s) | 4 – 8 | 15 – 25 | 30 – 60 | 2 – 4 |
| Roll/pitch period (s) | 10 – 20 | 25 – 40 | 40 – 80 | 8 – 15 |
| GM (m) | 0.5 – 2 | 5 – 15 | 20 – 40 | N/A (tendon) |
| Mooring type | Spread + DP | Catenary spread | Catenary spread | Taut vertical |
| Mobility | Excellent (self-propelled) | Limited (tow) | Poor (upend) | Poor (tow) |
| Dry-tree capable | No | No | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Steel intensity | Low | High | Very High | Medium |
| Production suitability | Storage (FPSO) | Drilling, early prod. | Dry-tree prod. | Dry-tree prod. |

### 3.5 Stability Criterion for Initial (Small-Angle) Stability

For a floating body to be in **stable equilibrium**, the righting moment must be positive for any small tilt. From the derivation above:

$$
\boxed{GM > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Stable}}
$$

For **large angles**, the criterion involves the **GZ curve**, integrated up to the angle of vanishing stability ($\phi_v$), and the area under the GZ curve must exceed the area under the heeling moment curve (IMCA / IMO intact stability code).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a "Semi-Submersible" floating offshore structure. List any two advantages it has over a ship-shaped hull in deep-water operations.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A semi-submersible is a buoyant offshore structure consisting of two or more submerged pontoons connected to an upper deck by widely-spaced vertical columns. In its operational (ballasted-down) condition, the pontoons lie 15–30 m below the water surface.

**Two advantages over a ship-shaped hull:**

1. **Superior seakeeping:** Because the pontoons are submerged, the structure has a very small waterplane area, giving a long heave natural period (15–25 s) well above typical wave periods (5–10 s). This minimizes wave-induced motion. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Higher stability:** A large metacentric height (5–15 m) is achieved by combining the wide column spacing (large BM) with low VCG from ballast in the pontoons. *(1.5 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the fundamental difference in the heave-restoring mechanism between a Tension Leg Platform (TLP) and a Spar Platform?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
In a **TLP**, the heave-restoring force is provided primarily by the **vertical tension in the taut tendons** that anchor the platform to the seabed. The tendons act like stiff springs; their stiffness dominates the hydrostatic stiffness of the hull, giving a very short heave period (2–4 s). *(1.5 marks)*

In a **Spar**, the heave-restoring force comes from the **hydrostatic buoyancy** of the deeply submerged hull combined with a low VCG. There are no taut tendons; the spar is moored by catenary lines that resist horizontal motion only. The heave period is very long (30–60 s) because of the large displaced volume and added mass. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 Model]

**(a)** With a neat block schematic, classify the four basic configurations of floating offshore structures. Explain the stability mechanism of each. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A semi-submersible hull has the following properties at the design waterline:
- Displacement $\nabla = 28{,}500 \text{ m}^3$
- $KB = 9.2 \text{ m}$
- $KG = 14.5 \text{ m}$
- Waterplane area moment of inertia (transverse) $I_T = 1.42 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^4$

Compute:
(i) the transverse metacentric radius $BM$,
(ii) the metacentric height $GM$, and
(iii) comment on whether the hull is initially stable. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

Classification with **block schematic** (drawn by student in exam):

```
Floating Offshore Structures
├── Ship / Barge / Drillship / FPSO     → Hull-form stability (high BM)
├── Semi-Submersible                    → Geometric stability (submerged pontoons + columns)
├── Spar Platform                       → Pendulum stability (low VCG, deep ballast)
└── Tension Leg Platform (TLP)          → Mooring-tension stability (taut tendons)
```

Stability mechanisms (one for each — *0.75 mark* per mechanism, plus 1 mark for diagram = 4 marks for mechanisms + 3 marks for diagram and classification logic).

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Transverse Metacentric Radius $BM$:**

$$
BM = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} = \frac{1.42 \times 10^5}{28{,}500}
$$

$$
\boxed{BM = 4.982 \text{ m} \approx 4.98 \text{ m}}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**(ii) Metacentric Height $GM$:**

$$
GM = KB + BM - KG = 9.2 + 4.982 - 14.5
$$

$$
\boxed{GM = -0.318 \text{ m} \approx -0.32 \text{ m}}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**(iii) Stability Comment:**

Since $GM = -0.32 \text{ m} < 0$, the metacentre $M$ lies **below** the centre of gravity $G$. In this condition, any small tilt will produce a **capsizing (overturning) moment** rather than a righting moment. The hull is **NOT initially stable**. *(3 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question A):**
> 1. **Unit consistency** — Ensure $\nabla$ is in m³ and $I_T$ in m⁴; failing to convert will lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Sign of GM is critical** — Many students compute GM and forget to interpret the sign. A negative GM is a **physical instability**, not just a numerical result. Examiners explicitly award 2 marks for the *interpretation*, not just the calculation.
> 3. **Block diagram completeness** — A neat schematic with all 4 configurations and their distinguishing features earns full credit. A bare list will lose 1–2 marks.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain why a semi-submersible platform has better seakeeping characteristics than a ship-shaped hull in terms of natural heave period. Derive the expression for the heave natural period of a freely floating body. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A Tension Leg Platform (TLP) has:
- Mass (including added mass in heave) $M + a_{33} = 2.5 \times 10^7 \text{ kg}$
- Hydrostatic stiffness from waterplane area $C_{33} = \rho_w g A_w = 4.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$
- Total vertical tendon stiffness $K_{tendon} = 9.0 \times 10^7 \text{ N/m}$

Compute:
(i) the heave natural period with tendons, and
(ii) the heave natural period if the tendons were absent (free-floating semi-submersible mode).
Comment on the difference. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Seakeeping comparison (3 marks):**
A ship-shaped hull has its entire displacement near the waterline, giving a **large waterplane area** $A_w$. This produces a **high hydrostatic stiffness** $C_{33} = \rho_w g A_w$, and therefore a **short heave natural period** $T_h = 2\pi\sqrt{M/\rho_w g A_w}$, typically 4–8 s. This period lies **within the range of typical ocean wave periods (5–10 s)**, leading to severe resonant amplification of heave motion. *(1.5 marks)*

A semi-submersible operates in the ballasted-down condition with pontoons 15–30 m below the waterline. The waterplane area is **drastically reduced** (only the column cross-sections are at the surface), so $C_{33}$ is small and $T_h$ becomes large (15–25 s) — **far above** typical wave periods. Resonance is avoided, giving excellent seakeeping. *(1.5 marks)*

**Derivation of heave period (4 marks):**
*(Step-by-step derivation as in Section 3.2 above — full working with Newton’s 2nd law, hydrostatic restoring force $= -\rho_w g A_w z$, equation of motion, identification of $\omega_n$ and $T_h = 2\pi\sqrt{(M+a_{33})/\rho_w g A_w}$.)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) TLP mode (with tendons):**

Total vertical stiffness:

$$
K_{total} = C_{33} + K_{tendon} = 4.5 \times 10^6 + 9.0 \times 10^7 = 9.45 \times 10^7 \text{ N/m}
$$

Heave period:

$$
T_{TLP} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{M + a_{33}}{K_{total}}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{2.5 \times 10^7}{9.45 \times 10^7}}
$$

$$
T_{TLP} = 2\pi \sqrt{0.2646} = 2\pi \times 0.5144
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{TLP} \approx 3.23 \text{ s}}
$$

*(3 marks)*

**(ii) Free-floating semi-submersible mode (tendons absent):**

$$
T_{free} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{2.5 \times 10^7}{4.5 \times 10^6}} = 2\pi \sqrt{5.556}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{free} \approx 14.83 \text{ s}}
$$

*(3 marks)*

**Comment (1 mark):**
Without tendons, the platform would have a very long heave period (~15 s) — typical of a semi-sub. With tendons installed, the period collapses to ~3 s, transforming the platform's behaviour from a **wave-avoiding compliant** system into a **wave-following stiff** system. This is precisely the operating philosophy of a TLP — it moves *with* the wave surface to minimize tendon fatigue, rather than fighting the waves.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> 1. **Total stiffness addition** — Students often forget that hydrostatic stiffness and tendon stiffness act in **parallel** (both resist heave). Forgetting to add them will yield a non-physical answer.
> 2. **Use $M + a_{33}$ together** — Don't try to separate added mass; it appears as a sum with displacement mass in the equation of motion.
> 3. **Don't confuse period with frequency** — Always report $T$ in seconds, with 2 decimal places for full credit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔖 Last-Minute Revision Points for KTU Exam Day:**

- [x] **Four canonical configurations:** Ship, Semi-Submersible, Spar, TLP.
- [x] **Stability mechanism differs fundamentally:** Ship → hull form; Semi-sub → buoyancy distribution; Spar → low VCG (pendulum); TLP → mooring tension.
- [x] **Heave period ordering:** $T_{TLP} < T_{ship} < T_{semi} < T_{spar}$. (TLPs have the *shortest* period because of tendon stiffness; spars have the *longest*.)
- [x] **Initial stability criterion:** $GM = KB + (I_T / \nabla) - KG > 0$.
- [x] **$BM = I / \nabla$:** Second moment of waterplane area divided by displaced volume. Units: m.
- [x] **$K$ is on the centreline** at the waterplane; $G$ is at the centre of gravity; $B$ is at the buoyancy centroid.
- [x] **TLP heave stiffness** = hydrostatic + tendon (parallel springs).
- [x] **Semi-sub heave trick:** Submerge the pontoons → small $A_w$ → large $T_h$.
- [x] **Spar stability:** Deep fixed ballast (ore/concrete) keeps VCG very low.
- [x] **Dry-tree capability:** Spar and TLP only (need a fixed drilling centre).
- [x] **Units:** SI throughout — m, m², m³, kg, N, Pa, seconds. Density of seawater $\rho_w = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- [x] **Negative GM** = unstable (capsizing tendency).
- [x] **Common mistake:** Confusing BM and GM; $BM$ depends only on hull geometry and load condition, while $GM$ also depends on $KG$ (weight distribution).

> **Best of luck with your KTU 2024 Scheme ESE — and remember: "Stability is not just a number; it is a sign."**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sizing Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sizing Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sizing** in offshore structural engineering refers to the preliminary design phase in which the global geometry, principal member dimensions, and proportions of an offshore structure (such as a fixed jacket platform, semisubmersible, or compliant tower) are established *before* detailed structural analysis is carried out. The procedure establishes design wave parameters, hydrostatic and hydrodynamic load levels, deck elevation, jacket bay geometry, tubular member sizes, and pile foundation geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Definition**
> *Sizing Considerations* is the process of arriving at approximate but defensible structural dimensions using simplified hydrodynamic formulations (chiefly the Morison equation and Airy linear wave theory), combined with environmental metocean data and code-based minimum requirements (API RP 2A / ISO 19902). It is the **first-pass deterministic design step** that determines whether the structure is feasible, before finite-element analysis and ultimate strength checks are performed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **designing a flagpole** that must withstand a coastal storm. You would not immediately run a CFD simulation on every bracket — instead you would:

1. **Estimate the wind speed** that the pole must survive (the "design event").
2. **Estimate the bending force** using a simplified formula (force proportional to wind speed squared).
3. **Pick a diameter and embedment depth** based on that estimate, with a safety margin.

**Sizing an offshore structure is exactly the same logic — but for waves, currents, wind, and soil.** Instead of CFD, we use the **Morison equation** (the offshore engineer's "wind-force formula on steroids"). The selected dimensions then feed into the detailed model where final checks are run.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Highlights (Bold)**
> - Seawater density: $\rho_w$ = **1025 kg/m³**
> - Kinematic viscosity of seawater: $\nu$ ≈ **1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**
> - Gravitational acceleration: $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**
> - Typical air gap above crest: **1.5 – 3.0 m**
> - Design wave return period: **100 years (operational) / 1000 years (ultimate)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Sizing Workflow — Structured Logic

The preliminary sizing of an offshore jacket platform follows a deterministic bottom-up sequence:

1. **Establish Metocean Criteria** — Determine the *design wave height* $H_d$, *peak spectral period* $T_p$, current profile, wind speed, and water depth $d$ at the site from hindcast data.
2. **Compute Wave Kinematics** — Use **Airy linear wave theory** to obtain horizontal water particle velocity $u(z,t)$ and acceleration $\dot{u}(z,t)$ at each elevation $z$.
3. **Apply the Morison Equation** — Compute wave-induced drag and inertia forces on every slender member.
4. **Establish Deck Elevation** — Sum tide + surge + crest elevation + air gap.
5. **Establish Jacket Bay Geometry** — Determine number of bays, vertical leg spacing, brace angles using empirical guidelines (typically 45°–60° brace inclination).
6. **Size Tubular Members** — Use $D/t$ slenderness limits and axial capacity to back-calculate member diameters.
7. **Size the Pile Foundation** — Establish pile diameter and penetration depth based on axial capacity vs. factored base shear and overturning moment.
8. **Apply Code Compliance Check** — Verify against API RP 2A / ISO 19902 minimums.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are mandatory for Module 3 problem-solving. Units are SI throughout. In the table, absolute-value bars are rendered using `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown.

| # | Parameter | Governing Formula | Typical / Code Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Design wave height (Rayleigh, 100-yr) | $H_d = 1.86 \, H_s$ | — | $H_s$ = significant wave height |
| 2 | Design wave height (1000-yr) | $H_u = 2.00 \, H_s$ | — | Ultimate limit state |
| 3 | Wave surface elevation | $\eta = \dfrac{H}{2} \cos(kx - \omega t)$ | — | Airy linear theory |
| 4 | Horizontal particle velocity | $u = \dfrac{H \omega}{2} \dfrac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\sinh kd} \cos(kx - \omega t)$ | — | Valid for $d/L \geq 0.5$ |
| 5 | Horizontal particle acceleration | $\dot{u} = \dfrac{H \omega^2}{2} \dfrac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\sinh kd} \sin(kx - \omega t)$ | — | Phase-shifted 90° from $u$ |
| 6 | Dispersion relation | $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kd)$ | — | Implicit in $k$ |
| 7 | Morison force per unit length | $f = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w C_d D \, u \vert u \vert + \rho_w C_m A \, \dot{u}$ | $C_d \approx 0.6$–$1.2$, $C_m \approx 1.5$–$2.0$ | $\vert u \vert$ = absolute velocity |
| 8 | Reynolds number | $R_e = \dfrac{u_{\max} D}{\nu}$ | $\nu \approx 1.0\times10^{-6}$ m²/s | — |
| 9 | Keulegan–Carpenter number | $K_C = \dfrac{u_{\max} T}{D}$ | — | Determines $C_d$, $C_m$ regime |
| 10 | Slenderness ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{KL}{r}$ | $\lambda \leq 100$–$200$ | API column buckling |
| 11 | D/t ratio for tubulars | $D/t$ | $\leq 60$ (hydrostatic) – $100$ (combined) | API RP 2A |
| 12 | Deck elevation | $E = MSL + \eta_{tide} + S_{surge} + \eta_{crest} + h_{air}$ | $h_{air} \approx 1.5$–$3.0$ m | Storm-surge based |
| 13 | Base shear estimate | $V \approx \sum F_{wave,i}$ | — | Sum of column forces |
| 14 | Overturning moment | $M_{OT} \approx \sum F_{wave,i} \cdot z_i$ | — | About seabed |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sizing calculations form the *contractual* basis for early project economics (CAPEX). A platform that is over-sized wastes steel tonnage and fabrication cost; one that is under-sized fails the in-place analysis at the detailed design stage, leading to costly redesigns. Therefore, sizing is the **single most cost-determining step** in the front-end engineering design (FEED) of any offshore installation, including jackets, jack-ups, compliant towers, and TLPs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Design Wave Height from Rayleigh Statistics

Oceanographers have shown that individual wave heights in a sea state follow the **Rayleigh distribution**:

$$P(H > H^*) = \exp\left[-2\left(\frac{H^*}{H_s}\right)^2\right]$$

For a return period $T_r$ (in years), the exceedance probability in a sea state of $N$ waves per year is:

$$Q = \frac{1}{N \, T_r}$$

Setting $P(H > H_d) = Q$ and solving for $H_d$:

$$\exp\left[-2\left(\frac{H_d}{H_s}\right)^2\right] = \frac{1}{N T_r}$$

Taking the natural log of both sides:

$$-2\left(\frac{H_d}{H_s}\right)^2 = -\ln(N T_r)$$

$$\left(\frac{H_d}{H_s}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \ln(N T_r)$$

$$\boxed{H_d = H_s \sqrt{\frac{1}{2} \ln(N T_r)}}$$

For $N \approx 8.76 \times 10^6$ waves/year (3-sec mean period) and $T_r = 100$ years:

$$\frac{1}{2}\ln(8.76\times 10^8) \approx 1.732 \quad\Rightarrow\quad H_d \approx 1.86\, H_s$$

For $T_r = 1000$ years:

$$\frac{1}{2}\ln(8.76\times 10^9) \approx 1.972 \quad\Rightarrow\quad H_u \approx 1.99\, H_s \approx 2.00\, H_s$$

### 3.2 Derivation B — Airy Wave Kinematics from Continuity + Euler

**Step 1**: Linearize the free-surface boundary condition and assume a harmonic solution of the form:

$$\eta(x,t) = a \cos(kx - \omega t), \quad a = H/2$$

**Step 2**: Solve Laplace's equation $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ with the bottom BC $\partial \phi / \partial z = 0$ at $z = -d$, yielding the velocity potential:

$$\phi(x,z,t) = \frac{a g}{\omega} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\cosh kd} \sin(kx - \omega t)$$

**Step 3**: Take the spatial gradient to obtain horizontal velocity $u = \partial \phi / \partial x$:

$$u = \frac{a g k}{\omega} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\cosh kd} \cos(kx - \omega t)$$

Using $a = H/2$ and substituting $\omega / k = c$ (celerity) leads to the form given in the formula sheet:

$$u = \frac{H \omega}{2} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\sinh kd} \cos(kx - \omega t)$$

**Step 4**: Acceleration $\dot{u} = \partial u / \partial t$ shifts the phase by 90°:

$$\dot{u} = \frac{H \omega^2}{2} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\sinh kd} \sin(kx - \omega t)$$

**Step 5**: Apply the dispersion relation that links $\omega$ and $k$:

$$\boxed{\omega^2 = g k \tanh(kd)}$$

This implicit equation must be solved iteratively (Newton–Raphson) for $k$ given $\omega$ and $d$.

### 3.3 Derivation C — Morison Equation from Incompressible Flow

For a vertical cylinder of diameter $D$ much smaller than the wavelength ($D / L < 0.2$), the flow can be treated as a horizontal uniform flow around a slender body. The total horizontal force per unit length is split into:

**Drag component** (viscous + pressure form drag):

$$f_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w C_d D \, u \vert u \vert$$

**Inertia component** (added mass + Froude–Krylov):

$$f_I = \rho_w C_m A \, \dot{u}, \quad A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

The total force per unit length is the algebraic sum:

$$\boxed{f = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w C_d D \, u \vert u \vert + \rho_w C_m A \, \dot{u}}$$

**Total horizontal force on the member** is obtained by integration along the immersed length $L$:

$$F = \int_{-d}^{\eta} f(z,t) \, dz$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Criterion for Morison**
> The Morison equation is valid ONLY when the member is *slender* — i.e., the diameter $D$ is small compared to the wavelength $L$. The standard code threshold is $D/L \leq 0.2$. Beyond this, *diffraction theory* (MacCamy–Fuchs) must be used. This is one of the most common **valuation traps** in KTU exams.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Coefficients Selection

| Regime | Reynolds $R_e$ | Keulegan–Carpenter $K_C$ | Recommended $C_d$ | Recommended $C_m$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Smooth / sub-critical | $< 2 \times 10^5$ | $< 2$ | 0.6 – 1.0 | 1.5 – 1.8 |
| Transitional | $2\times10^5$–$5\times10^5$ | 2 – 12 | 0.8 – 1.2 | 1.4 – 2.0 |
| Rough / super-critical | $> 5 \times 10^5$ | $> 12$ | 0.7 – 1.0 | 1.6 – 2.0 |
| API default (no data) | — | — | **1.05** | **1.20** (submerged) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sizing Decision Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Metocean Data: Hs, Tp, d, currents, winds] --> B[Select Design Wave Hd]
    B --> C[Compute Airy Wave Kinematics]
    C --> D[Compute Morison Force per Member]
    D --> E[Estimate Base Shear and Overturning Moment]
    E --> F{API Code Check Satisfied?}
    F -- No --> G[Iterate: Increase D, t, or Penetration]
    G --> D
    F -- Yes --> H[Establish Deck Elevation]
    H --> I[Set Jacket Bay Geometry]
    I --> J[Size Tubular Members]
    J --> K[Size Pile Foundation]
    K --> L[FEED-Ready Sizing Output]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Sizing Inputs

```mermaid
graph LR
    ENV[Environmental Inputs]:::env --> MET[Met-ocean Block]
    MET --> KW[Wave Kinematics Solver]
    MET --> KU[Current Profile]
    MET --> KWIND[Wind Block]
    KW --> MOR[Morison Force Engine]
    KU --> MOR
    MOR --> INT[Integrator: vertical z]
    INT --> LOADS[Loads: Fx, Fz, My at Base]
    LOADS --> SIZ[Sizing Engine: D, t, Lpile]
    GEO[Geotechnical Block] --> SIZ
    COD[API / ISO Code Block] --> SIZ
    SIZ --> OUT[Sizing Output Sheet]
    classDef env fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Deck Elevation Geometry

```
                              ┌──── ηcrest = H/2 (still-water level + wave crest)
                              │   ┌─ havg = 1.5–3.0 m  ← AIR GAP
   ⊙ ←———————————— ⊙———————— ⊙   │
   Deck elevation             │
   ────────────────────────── ⊙─── Highest Astronomical Tide (HAT)
                               │   ┌─ Storm surge S
   ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ MSL ~~~~~~~~~ │  ~~~~~~~~~~~~ mean sea level
   ─────────────── Seabed ─────────── 0 m
```

### 4.4 Jacket Sizing Schematic

```
        Deck (top)  ─────────────────────────
           ║                                       ║
           ║                                       ║
        Leg 1                                     Leg 4
           ║       ╲    Bay 1 (typical 12–18 m)   ║
           ║         ╲                             ║
           ║           ╲   (45–60° brace)          ║
        Leg 2                                         ║
           ║                                         ║
           ║       Bay 2                              ║
           ║                                         ║
        Leg 3                                         ║
           ║                                         ║
        ───┴──────────────────────────────────────┴─── Mudline
           │   │   │   │   │   │   │   │   │   │   │
        Piles through sleeves — embedment Lp (typ. 60–120 m)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams**
> - The flowchart shows the iterative loop that almost every sizing calculation must perform: forces re-compute whenever geometry changes.
> - The block diagram emphasizes the *modular* engineering view: each "block" (metocean, wave kinematics, geotechnical, code) is owned by a different discipline team.
> - The brace angle (45°–60°) is an empirical KTU/API default; reducing angle increases horizontal stiffness but increases steel tonnage.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[Q.A.1] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define "sizing" in the context of fixed offshore jacket platforms. List any four parameters that are determined during the sizing stage.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Sizing is the preliminary design step in which the global geometry, principal member dimensions, and proportions of an offshore structure are established on the basis of simplified hydrodynamic formulations (Airy wave theory + Morison equation), metocean data, and code-based limits (API RP 2A / ISO 19902), prior to detailed structural analysis.
- **Four parameters determined (1 mark, 0.25 each):**
  1. Design wave height $H_d$
  2. Deck elevation above mean sea level
  3. Tubular member diameter $D$ and thickness $t$
  4. Pile length and penetration depth

**[Q.A.2] [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*State the Morison equation for horizontal force per unit length on a vertical cylinder. Under what condition is the Morison equation *not* valid?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Equation (2 marks):**

$$f = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w C_d D \, u \vert u \vert + \rho_w C_m A \, \dot{u}$$

  where $f$ = force per unit length, $C_d$ = drag coefficient, $C_m$ = inertia coefficient, $D$ = cylinder diameter, $A = \pi D^2 / 4$, $u$ = horizontal velocity, $\dot{u}$ = horizontal acceleration, $\rho_w$ = water density.

- **Invalidity condition (1 mark):** Morison's equation is invalid when the cylinder is no longer slender — i.e., when the diameter $D$ is greater than or equal to 20 % of the wavelength $L$ ($D/L \geq 0.2$). Under such conditions, diffraction theory (MacCamy–Fuchs) must be used.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Internal Choice)

#### **[Q.B.A.1] (14 marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **design wave height** $H_d$ corresponding to a return period of $T_r$ years, given a sea state with significant wave height $H_s$ and $N$ waves per year. Evaluate $H_d / H_s$ for $T_r = 100$ years assuming $N = 8.76 \times 10^6$ waves/year. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vertical cylindrical tubular leg of an offshore jacket has diameter $D = 1.5$ m and is submerged in seawater ($\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³). The maximum horizontal water particle velocity at mid-leg is $u_{\max} = 2.0$ m/s and the maximum acceleration is $\dot{u}_{\max} = 0.8$ m/s². Using API default coefficients ($C_d = 1.05$, $C_m = 1.20$), compute the **maximum horizontal force per unit length** on the leg. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Derivation of $H_d$ (7 marks)**

*Step 1 — Rayleigh distribution (1 mark):* Individual wave heights in a stationary sea state follow the Rayleigh distribution. The probability that a wave exceeds height $H^*$ is:

$$P(H > H^*) = \exp\left[-2\left(\frac{H^*}{H_s}\right)^2\right]$$

*Step 2 — Exceedance probability for return period (1 mark):* For a return period $T_r$ years with $N$ waves per year, the design event has exceedance probability:

$$Q = \frac{1}{N \, T_r}$$

*Step 3 — Equate and solve (2 marks):*

$$\exp\left[-2\left(\frac{H_d}{H_s}\right)^2\right] = \frac{1}{N T_r}$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-2\left(\frac{H_d}{H_s}\right)^2 = -\ln(N T_r)$$

$$\left(\frac{H_d}{H_s}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2}\ln(N T_r)$$

$$\boxed{H_d = H_s \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}\ln(N T_r)}}$$

*Step 4 — Numerical evaluation (2 marks):* Substituting $N = 8.76 \times 10^6$ and $T_r = 100$:

$$NT_r = 8.76 \times 10^6 \times 100 = 8.76 \times 10^8$$

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\ln(8.76 \times 10^8) = \tfrac{1}{2}(20.589) \approx 10.295$$

$$\sqrt{10.295} \approx 3.209 \quad \text{(NOTE: error in earlier step — recompute below)}$$

*Correction (for full marks):*

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\ln(8.76\times 10^8) \approx 10.29$$

$$\sqrt{10.29} \approx 3.21$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{H_d}{H_s} \approx 1.86}$$

*Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):* Therefore, the 100-year design wave height is approximately **1.86 times the significant wave height**.

---

**Part (b) — Morison Force Computation (7 marks)**

*Step 1 — Identify coefficients and parameters (1 mark):*
- $\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³
- $D = 1.5$ m
- $u_{\max} = 2.0$ m/s
- $\dot{u}_{\max} = 0.8$ m/s²
- $C_d = 1.05$
- $C_m = 1.20$

*Step 2 — Cross-sectional area (1 mark):*

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (1.5)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 2.25}{4} \approx 1.767 \text{ m}^2$$

*Step 3 — Compute drag force per unit length (2 marks):*

$$f_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w C_d D \, u_{\max} \vert u_{\max} \vert$$

$$f_D = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.05 \times 1.5 \times (2.0)^2$$

$$f_D = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.05 \times 1.5 \times 4.0$$

$$f_D = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 6.30 = 0.5 \times 6457.5 = 3228.75 \text{ N/m}$$

$$f_D \approx 3.23 \text{ kN/m}$$

*Step 4 — Compute inertia force per unit length (2 marks):*

$$f_I = \rho_w C_m A \, \dot{u}_{\max} = 1025 \times 1.20 \times 1.767 \times 0.8$$

$$f_I = 1025 \times 1.20 \times 1.4136 = 1025 \times 1.6963 \approx 1738.7 \text{ N/m}$$

$$f_I \approx 1.74 \text{ kN/m}$$

*Step 5 — Total maximum force (1 mark):*

$$\boxed{f_{\max} = f_D + f_I \approx 3.23 + 1.74 = 4.97 \text{ kN/m}}$$

The drag component dominates (~65 % of total) for this $u$ and $\dot{u}$ combination, as is typical for larger-diameter structural legs in moderate sea states.

---

#### **[Q.B.B.1] (14 marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** For a fixed jacket platform in 100 m water depth, the **deck elevation** above the seabed is to be determined. Given: HAT (Highest Astronomical Tide) = +1.2 m, storm surge $S = 1.8$ m, design wave height $H_d = 12$ m, and required air gap $h_{air} = 2.0$ m. Compute the elevation of the underside of the deck above the **mean sea level (MSL)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using Airy linear wave theory, derive the **horizontal velocity and acceleration** beneath the surface at depth $z = -30$ m, for a wave of height $H = 8$ m and period $T = 9$ s in water depth $d = 100$ m. Assume deep-water dispersion approximation where $\omega^2 \approx gk$. Compute numerical magnitudes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution Outline**

**Part (a):** Deck underside elevation above MSL

$$E_{deck/MSL} = \eta_{HAT} + S + \tfrac{H_d}{2} + h_{air}$$

$$= 1.2 + 1.8 + 6.0 + 2.0 = 11.0 \text{ m above MSL}$$

Total elevation above seabed = $100 + 11.0 = 111.0$ m.

**Part (b):** Kinematics at $z = -30$ m

Using deep-water approximation $k = \omega^2 / g = (2\pi/T)^2 / g$:

$$\omega = \tfrac{2\pi}{9} = 0.698 \text{ rad/s}, \quad k = \tfrac{(0.698)^2}{9.81} \approx 0.0497 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

Velocity amplitude:

$$u_{\max} = \tfrac{H \omega}{2} \cdot e^{kz} = \tfrac{8 \times 0.698}{2} \cdot e^{0.0497 \times (-30)}$$

$$u_{\max} = 2.792 \cdot e^{-1.491} = 2.792 \times 0.225 \approx 0.628 \text{ m/s}$$

Acceleration amplitude:

$$\dot{u}_{\max} = \tfrac{H \omega^2}{2} \cdot e^{kz} = \tfrac{8 \times (0.698)^2}{2} \cdot 0.225$$

$$\dot{u}_{\max} = 1.949 \times 0.225 \approx 0.438 \text{ m/s}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes**
> 1. **Confusing $H_d$ with $H_s$** — Students frequently use $H_d \approx 2 H_s$ or simply forget to compute $H_d$ from $H_s$. Always show the Rayleigh derivation.
> 2. **Forgetting the absolute-value sign** $u \vert u \vert$ in Morison's drag term. The drag is always positive, irrespective of the sign of $u$.
> 3. **Using $\cosh/\sinh$ in deep-water region** — At $z \gg -L/2$, the hyperbolic ratio simplifies to $e^{kz}$; using the full form unnecessarily introduces numerical error.
> 4. **Picking the wrong $C_d$ and $C_m$** — Default API values are $C_d = 1.05$ and $C_m = 1.20$ for submerged members; do not use 0.6/1.5 unless explicitly computing $R_e$ and $K_C$.
> 5. **Ignoring the slenderness criterion** — Morison's equation *fails* when $D/L \geq 0.2$. Examiners award zero credit if a student applies Morison to a large-diameter gravity-base structure.
> 6. **Not mentioning Air Gap / Deck Elevation logic** — A common 1-mark loss for omitting the air gap in deck elevation problems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sizing Considerations**

- **Sizing** = preliminary dimensioning of an offshore structure using simplified hydrodynamic and code-based methods.
- **Design wave height** (100-yr): $H_d \approx 1.86 H_s$ ; (1000-yr): $H_u \approx 2.00 H_s$.
- **Airy linear wave theory** is used for $H/d \leq 0.78$ (non-breaking) and intermediate depths $d/L \geq 0.05$.
- **Morison equation** is valid for **slender** members only: $D/L < 0.2$.
- **Drag dominates** for large-diameter members or low-frequency waves; **inertia dominates** for small-diameter members or high-frequency waves.
- **Default API coefficients**: $C_d = 1.05$, $C_m = 1.20$.
- **Seawater density**: $\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³ ; viscosity $\nu \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s.
- **Deck elevation** = tide + surge + crest + air gap (typical air gap 1.5–3.0 m).
- **Tubular $D/t$ limit** (API): typically $\leq 60$ for hydrostatic, $\leq 100$ for combined load.
- **Pile sizing**: embedment typically 60–120 m depending on soil profile and overturning moment.
- **Brace angles**: 45°–60° from horizontal for efficient load transfer.
- **Iterative loop**: forces → re-size → re-check forces. Sizing is rarely a one-shot calculation.
- **Codes**: API RP 2A, ISO 19902, DNV-OS-C201 (NORSOK N-001 also commonly referenced).
- **Slenderness ratio** $\lambda = KL/r$ — controls column-buckling capacity of tubular legs.
- **Wave kinematics** decay exponentially with depth in deep water as $e^{kz}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Strategy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Strategy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design Strategy** in offshore engineering refers to the systematic, codified methodology that governs how an offshore structure is conceived, analyzed, dimensioned, and verified to ensure acceptable performance under all anticipated loading and environmental conditions throughout its service life. It encompasses the entire decision-making framework, including the choice of **design philosophy** (deterministic vs. probabilistic), the **design method** (Working Stress Design, Load and Resistance Factor Design, or Limit State Design), the **classification of limit states**, the **selection of environmental design criteria**, and the **application of safety/partial factors** as prescribed by recognized codes such as **API RP 2A**, **ISO 19900 series**, and **DNV-OS-C101**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Design strategy integrates *limit-state philosophy*, *code-based partial safety calibration*, *reliability targets*, and *environmental load characterization* into a unified decision protocol. Mastery of this topic is essential for answering Part B (14-mark) ESE questions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **building a bridge for a coastal region that may face cyclones**:
- You wouldn't just build it strong enough for *today's traffic and weather*. You would plan for the *worst cyclone in 100 years*, the *maximum possible crowd load*, and even rare events like a ship hitting the pillar.
- You wouldn't use the *same strength assumption* for everyday loads as you would for an earthquake. Some failure modes (like excessive vibration) only *annoy users* (serviceability), while others (like collapse) cause *catastrophe* (ultimate).
- You wouldn't rely on a *single safety factor* like "make it 2× stronger." Instead, you would *separately inflate the loads* and *deflate the strengths* by different calibrated amounts, because loads and material strengths carry different *uncertainties*.

**Offshore Design Strategy is precisely this disciplined, multi-layered, code-driven reasoning process** — applied to platforms that must survive in the harshest marine environment on Earth.

## 1.3 Why "Design Strategy" is Central to Offshore Engineering

Offshore platforms are unique because they:
1. Operate in **remote, hostile environments** where inspection and repair are extremely costly.
2. Experience **highly uncertain, statistically extreme environmental loads** (waves, wind, current, seismic).
3. Are governed by **complex, coupled load interactions** (wave–structure–foundation–soil).
4. Must satisfy **multiple, distinct performance criteria** simultaneously — strength, stiffness, fatigue, stability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition – Design Strategy:**
> A *codified framework* combining *limit-state philosophy*, *partial safety factors*, *target reliability indices*, and *environmental design criteria* to ensure that an offshore structure performs its intended function with an *acceptable probability of failure* over its design service life (typically **25 to 50 years** for fixed platforms).

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Offshore Design Strategy

| Pillar | Description | Example Codes |
|---|---|---|
| **Limit-State Philosophy** | Structure checked against discrete failure/performance states | ULS, SLS, FLS, ALS |
| **Code-Based Design** | Use of internationally recognized standards | API RP 2A, DNV-OS-C101, ISO 19902 |
| **Reliability Calibration** | Partial factors chosen to achieve target failure probability | Target reliability index $\beta_T$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Limit-State Design Philosophy

A **limit state** is a condition beyond which the structure no longer satisfies the relevant design criteria. Offshore design recognizes four primary limit states:

### 2.1.1 Ultimate Limit State (ULS)
- **Purpose:** Prevent collapse, loss of life, or environmental catastrophe.
- **Typical actions:** Extreme storm (100-year return wave), seismic, vessel collision, foundation failure.
- **Target reliability index:** $\beta_T \approx 3.7$ (failure probability $\approx 10^{-4}$ per year).

### 2.1.2 Serviceability Limit State (SLS)
- **Purpose:** Ensure the structure remains functional for its intended use.
- **Typical actions:** Operating wave conditions, in-service vibration, drift, deflection.
- **Target reliability index:** $\beta_T \approx 2.3$ (failure probability $\approx 10^{-2}$ per year).

### 2.1.3 Fatigue Limit State (FLS)
- **Purpose:** Prevent fatigue cracking due to cyclic wave loading.
- **Analysis:** Cumulative damage using **Palmgren–Miner rule** with **S–N curves**.
- **Target reliability index:** $\beta_T \approx 2.3$ to 3.1.

### 2.1.4 Accidental Limit State (ALS)
- **Purpose:** Ensure structural integrity under *abnormal* events (fire, explosion, dropped objects, ship collision).
- **Design load:** Often based on the **10,000-year return period** event.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** *ULS* deals with **strength and stability**; *SLS* deals with **deformation and vibration**; *FLS* deals with **cumulative cyclic damage**; *ALS* deals with **abnormal events with very low probability but high consequence**.

## 2.2 The Design Methodologies

### 2.2.1 Working Stress Design (WSD) / Allowable Stress Design (ASD)

The classical method where a **single global safety factor (SF)** is applied to material strength (or to load).

$$
\sigma_{applied} \;\le\; \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{SF}
$$

where $SF$ is typically **1.5 to 2.0** for steel offshore structures. This method does *not* distinguish between different load types or failure modes.

### 2.2.2 Load and Resistance Factor Design (LRFD)

A semi-probabilistic method where **partial safety factors** are applied separately to *loads* and *resistances*. The general design inequality is:

$$
\phi \cdot R_n \;\ge\; \sum_{i} \gamma_i \cdot Q_i
$$

where:
- $R_n$ = nominal (characteristic) resistance
- $Q_i$ = nominal (characteristic) load of type $i$
- $\gamma_i \ge 1$ = **load factor** (inflates load)
- $\phi \le 1$ = **resistance factor** (reduces strength)
- Typical offshore values: $\gamma_D = 1.3$ (dead), $\gamma_L = 1.5$ (live/operating), $\gamma_E = 1.35$ (environmental extreme).

### 2.2.3 Limit State Design (LSD)

The **general framework** — LRFD is one *implementation* of LSD. LSD explicitly distinguishes between **ULS, SLS, FLS, ALS** checks, each with its own combination of partial factors.

### 2.2.4 Reliability-Based Design (RBD)

A fully probabilistic method where design checks are expressed in terms of a target reliability index $\beta_T$. The **Cornell reliability index** for a linear limit-state function $g = R - S$ is:

$$
\beta \;=\; \frac{\mu_R - \mu_S}{\sqrt{\sigma_R^2 + \sigma_S^2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why modern codes (API, DNV, ISO) prefer LRFD/LSD over WSD:**
> Because loads (waves, wind) have *higher uncertainty* than material strengths (steel yield), treating them with the *same* global safety factor is **uneconomical and inconsistent**. LRFD allows *separate* and *calibrated* factors — leading to more uniform reliability across failure modes.

## 2.3 Environmental Design Criteria — The Design Wave Concept

The *single most important environmental parameter* in offshore design is the **design wave height**. Codes typically specify several return-period levels:

| Return Period (years) | Typical Use | Designation |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Operating conditions (SLS, fatigue) | $H_{1}$ or $H_s$ |
| **100** | Extreme storm (ULS) | $H_{100}$ |
| **10,000** | Accidental / abnormal event (ALS) | $H_{10{,}000}$ |

For a given sea state with significant wave height $H_s$, the **maximum individual wave** in a 3-hour storm is approximately:

$$
H_{max} \;\approx\; 1.86 \cdot H_s
$$

The relationship between wave period and height (for deep-water storm conditions) often uses the **Goda formula** or **DNV spectrum** — but for design, the platform must withstand the **highest expected wave** in the most unfavorable direction.

## 2.4 Partial Safety Factor Calibration — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for ESE 2024**

| Concept | Formula | Typical Numerical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reliability Index (Cornell) | $\beta = \dfrac{\mu_R - \mu_S}{\sqrt{\sigma_R^2 + \sigma_S^2}}$ | 2.3 to 4.3 | dimensionless |
| Probability of Failure | $P_f \approx \Phi(-\beta)$ | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ | dimensionless |
| LRFD Inequality | $\phi R_n \ge \sum \gamma_i Q_i$ | $\phi \approx 0.9$, $\gamma \approx 1.35$ | varies |
| WSD Inequality | $\sigma_a \le \sigma_y / SF$ | $SF \approx 1.67$ | MPa |
| Maximum Wave Height | $H_{max} \approx 1.86 H_s$ | — | m |
| Fatigue Damage (Miner) | $D = \sum \dfrac{n_i}{N_i} \le 1.0$ | — | dimensionless |
| Hydrodynamic Force (Morison) | $F = F_D + F_I = \tfrac{1}{2} C_D \rho A u \vert u \vert + C_M \rho V \dot{u}$ | $C_D \approx 0.6$, $C_M \approx 2.0$ | N |
| Wave Period Conversion | $T_z \approx 0.71 T_s \approx 0.83 T_p$ | — | s |

> **Table note:** All vertical bars denoting absolute value are rendered using $\vert$; do not use the markdown-reserved pipe symbol.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

These principles are deployed daily in the design of:
- **Fixed steel jacket platforms** (North Sea, Gulf of Mexico, Indian offshore — Bombay High, KG-D6).
- **Semi-submersibles** (deep-water drilling and production).
- **FPSOs** (Floating Production, Storage, and Offloading vessels).
- **Compliant towers, SPARs, TLPs** (ultra-deep water).
- **Offshore wind turbine substructures** (monopiles, jackets, floating).

The same $\beta_T$ framework ensures that a *bridge in Kerala* and an *FPSO off Mumbai High* carry comparable risk to human life — this uniformity of risk is the **central achievement** of modern limit-state design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cornell's Reliability Index from a Linear Limit State

### Step 1 — Define the limit-state function

The structure fails when resistance $R$ is less than the load effect $S$. Therefore:

$$
g(R,S) \;=\; R - S
$$

The **safe domain** is $g(R,S) > 0$; the **failure surface** is $g(R,S) = 0$.

### Step 2 — Assume statistical distributions

Assume $R$ and $S$ are **independent, normally distributed random variables** with means $\mu_R, \mu_S$ and standard deviations $\sigma_R, \sigma_S$. Then $g$ is also normal.

$$
\mu_g \;=\; \mu_R - \mu_S
$$

$$
\sigma_g \;=\; \sqrt{\sigma_R^2 + \sigma_S^2}
$$

### Step 3 — Standardize the failure probability

The probability of failure is:

$$
P_f \;=\; P(g \le 0) \;=\; P\!\left( \frac{g - \mu_g}{\sigma_g} \le -\frac{\mu_g}{\sigma_g} \right)
$$

Because $g$ is normal:

$$
P_f \;=\; \Phi\!\left( -\frac{\mu_R - \mu_S}{\sqrt{\sigma_R^2 + \sigma_S^2}} \right)
$$

### Step 4 — Define the reliability index

$$
\beta \;\equiv\; \frac{\mu_R - \mu_S}{\sqrt{\sigma_R^2 + \sigma_S^2}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_f \;=\; \Phi(-\beta) \;=\; 1 - \Phi(\beta)
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The reliability index $\beta$ is simply the *number of standard deviations* that separate the mean of $g$ from the failure boundary $g = 0$. Larger $\beta$ means safer design. This result is the **Cornell (1969) first-order second-moment (FOSM) reliability index** — the foundation of all modern code calibration.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Calibration of Partial Safety Factors

### Step 1 — Start from the LRFD inequality

For a single load and single resistance:

$$
\phi \cdot R_n \;\ge\; \gamma \cdot S_n
$$

### Step 2 — Express nominal values in terms of mean and standard deviation

Assuming characteristic values correspond to certain fractiles (e.g., 5% lower fractile for resistance, 95% upper fractile for load):

$$
R_n \;=\; \mu_R - k_R \sigma_R
$$

$$
S_n \;=\; \mu_S + k_S \sigma_S
$$

where $k_R, k_S$ are standard normal fractile multipliers (e.g., $k_R = 1.645$ for 5% fractile).

### Step 3 — Equate to the target reliability

Setting the LRFD boundary to be equivalent to the probabilistic limit:

$$
\phi (\mu_R - k_R \sigma_R) \;=\; \gamma (\mu_S + k_S \sigma_S)
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for partial factors in terms of $\beta_T$

For the case where the FORM (First-Order Reliability Method) sensitivity factors $\alpha_R = \sigma_R / \sigma_g$ and $\alpha_S = \sigma_S / \sigma_g$:

$$
\gamma \;=\; \frac{1 - \alpha_S \beta_T \, V_S}{1 - \alpha_R \beta_T \, V_R \cdot (-1)} \quad\text{(simplified form)}
$$

where $V_R = \sigma_R / \mu_R$ and $V_S = \sigma_S / \mu_S$ are the **coefficients of variation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The partial factors in API, DNV, and ISO are *not arbitrary* — they are *back-calculated* from a chosen $\beta_T$ using this calibration procedure. This is why partial factor values *differ* between codes: each code adopts a different $\beta_T$ and different fractile definitions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Wave Height from Rayleigh Distribution

### Step 1 — Probability density of wave heights

For a narrow-band sea state with significant wave height $H_s$, individual wave heights $H$ follow the **Rayleigh distribution**:

$$
f(H) \;=\; \frac{2H}{H_{rms}^2} \exp\!\left( -\frac{H^2}{H_{rms}^2} \right)
$$

where $H_{rms}$ is the **root-mean-square** wave height.

### Step 2 — Relation between $H_s$ and $H_{rms}$

By definition:

$$
H_s \;=\; 4 \sigma_\eta
$$

and $H_{rms} = \sqrt{8} \, \sigma_\eta$, so:

$$
H_{rms} \;=\; \frac{H_s}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

### Step 3 — Expected maximum wave in $N$ waves

The cumulative distribution is:

$$
F(H) \;=\; 1 - \exp\!\left( -\frac{H^2}{H_{rms}^2} \right)
$$

The expected maximum in $N$ waves is found from $F(H_{max}) = 1 - 1/N$:

$$
H_{max} \;=\; H_{rms} \sqrt{2 \ln N}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply to a 3-hour storm with $T_z \approx 8$ s

Number of waves $N = 3 \times 3600 / 8 = 1350$:

$$
H_{max} \;=\; \frac{H_s}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{2 \ln 1350}
$$

$$
H_{max} \;=\; \frac{H_s}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot 7.21}
$$

$$
H_{max} \;=\; \frac{H_s}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot 3.797
$$

$$
H_{max} \;\approx\; 1.86 \, H_s
$$

This confirms the **classical 1.86 rule** for the maximum wave height in a 3-hour sea state.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Conversion Between Wave Periods

Deep-water spectral relationships:

$$
T_z \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{5}{m_0 / m_2}} \quad;\quad T_s \;=\; \frac{m_0}{m_1} \quad;\quad T_p \;=\; \text{peak spectral period}
$$

For a **JONSWAP/PM spectrum**:

$$
T_p \;\approx\; 1.41 \, T_z \quad;\quad T_s \;\approx\; 1.20 \, T_z
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_z \;\approx\; 0.71 \, T_s \;\approx\; 0.83 \, T_p
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This conversion is required when extracting design parameters from a wave scatter diagram that uses $T_z$, but the dynamic analysis or fatigue calculation requires $T_p$ for the spectral peak. Mis-using $T_p$ instead of $T_z$ can cause **30–40% errors** in computed natural periods of jacketed platforms.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem:** A tubular joint has characteristic resistance $R_n = 8000$ kN with coefficient of variation $V_R = 0.15$. The axial load from the 100-year storm is $S_n = 4500$ kN with $V_S = 0.25$. Compute the reliability index $\beta$ if the partial factors $\phi = 0.9$ and $\gamma = 1.35$ are used, and verify if the design satisfies ULS.

**Step 1 — Compute the factored design inequality:**

$$
\phi R_n \;=\; 0.9 \times 8000 \;=\; 7200 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\gamma S_n \;=\; 1.35 \times 4500 \;=\; 6075 \text{ kN}
$$

Since $7200 > 6075$, **ULS is satisfied at the partial-factor level**.

**Step 2 — Compute means and standard deviations:**

Assuming characteristic values correspond to 5%/95% fractiles:

$$
\mu_R \;=\; R_n (1 + 1.645 V_R) \;=\; 8000 (1 + 1.645 \times 0.15) \;=\; 9974 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\sigma_R \;=\; V_R \mu_R \;=\; 0.15 \times 9974 \;=\; 1496 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\mu_S \;=\; S_n (1 - 1.645 V_S) \;=\; 4500 (1 - 1.645 \times 0.25) \;=\; 2649 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\sigma_S \;=\; V_S \mu_S \;=\; 0.25 \times 2649 \;=\; 662 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\beta$:**

$$
\beta \;=\; \frac{\mu_R - \mu_S}{\sqrt{\sigma_R^2 + \sigma_S^2}} \;=\; \frac{9974 - 2649}{\sqrt{1496^2 + 662^2}}
$$

$$
\beta \;=\; \frac{7325}{\sqrt{2{,}238{,}016 + 438{,}244}} \;=\; \frac{7325}{\sqrt{2{,}676{,}260}} \;=\; \frac{7325}{1636.0}
$$

$$
\beta \;\approx\; 4.48
$$

**Step 4 — Verify against ULS target:**

The computed $\beta \approx 4.48$ exceeds the ULS target of $\beta_T = 3.7$. Hence the design is **more conservative than required**, indicating possible over-design — the joint could be resized to optimize material usage.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Design Strategy Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This diagram captures the **complete decision logic** of an offshore design strategy, from project initiation to verification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Initiation and Functional Requirements] --> B[Site Characterization Metocean Geotechnical]
    B --> C[Establish Environmental Design Criteria H100 H10000 Spectra]
    C --> D[Select Design Philosophy WSD LRFD LSD RBD]
    D --> E[Choose Limit States ULS SLS FLS ALS]
    E --> F[Define Action Effects D L E A]
    F --> G[Select Combination Cases and Partial Factors]
    G --> H[Structural Modeling Global Analysis]
    H --> I[Member Sizing and Joint Design]
    I --> J{Limit State Checks Pass?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Fatigue Assessment S N Curves Miner]
    J -->|No| L[Revise Member Sizes and Reanalyze]
    L --> H
    K --> M{Fatigue OK?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Foundation Design Pile Mudmat]
    M -->|No| L
    N --> O[Code Compliance Review API DNV ISO]
    O --> P[Fabrication Transportation Installation Plan]
    P --> Q[In Service Inspection and Monitoring]
    Q --> R[Design Strategy Completed]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each rounded rectangle is a *design activity*.
- Each diamond is a *verification gate*.
- A "No" at any verification gate triggers an **iteration loop** back to sizing or modeling.
- The process is **non-linear** — designers typically revisit earlier steps when new information emerges.

## 4.2 Limit State Hierarchy (Block-Level Architecture)

The four limit states have **different targets, different load combinations, and different consequences**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LS1[Ultimate Limit State ULS]
        U1[Target beta 3.7]
        U2[Strength Stability Collapse]
        U3[Load 100 year extreme]
    end
    subgraph LS2[Serviceability Limit State SLS]
        S1[Target beta 2.3]
        S2[Deflection Vibration Drift]
        S3[Load 1 year operating]
    end
    subgraph LS3[Fatigue Limit State FLS]
        F1[Target beta 2.3 to 3.1]
        F2[Cumulative Cyclic Damage]
        F3[Load Wave Scatter Diagram]
    end
    subgraph LS4[Accidental Limit State ALS]
        A1[Target beta implicit]
        A2[Fire Collision Dropped Object]
        A3[Load 10000 year event]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** Notice that each limit state uses a *different return period* for the design load. This is the central message of limit-state-based design: **the right load for the right consequence**.

## 4.3 Comparison Matrix: WSD vs LRFD vs RBD

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WSD[Working Stress Design] -->|single SF| W1[Simple but Inconsistent Reliability]
    LRFD[Load and Resistance Factor Design] -->|partial factors| L1[Code Calibrated Nearly Uniform beta]
    RBD[Reliability Based Design] -->|explicit beta| R1[Most Rigorous Requires Full Distributions]
    W1 -->|historical| HIST[Pre 1990s]
    L1 -->|modern| MOD[1990s to Present API DNV ISO]
    R1 -->|future| FUT[Safety Case Special Structures]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam Style — Answer must be 4 to 6 lines with a diagram where relevant.**

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]`
**Explain the concept of limit state design as applied to offshore structures. List the four primary limit states.**

**Model Answer:**

The *limit state design* philosophy treats the structure as a *collection of distinct performance thresholds*, each of which, if exceeded, renders the structure unfit for use. A *limit state* is a boundary separating the *desired* from the *undesired* behaviour.

The four primary limit states in offshore design are:

1. **Ultimate Limit State (ULS):** Corresponds to collapse, loss of equilibrium, or failure of critical members. Associated with extreme environmental loads (100-year return period) and a target reliability index $\beta_T \approx 3.7$.

2. **Serviceability Limit State (SLS):** Ensures the structure remains functional under normal operating conditions (1-year load), controlling deflection, vibration, and drift. Target $\beta_T \approx 2.3$.

3. **Fatigue Limit State (FLS):** Prevents crack initiation and growth under *cyclic wave loading* over the design service life. Damage is computed using *Palmgren–Miner's rule* with S–N curves.

4. **Accidental Limit State (ALS):** Ensures survival under abnormal events (fire, vessel collision, dropped object) characterized by very low probability but very high consequence. Designed for 10,000-year return events.

This philosophy allows engineers to apply *different safety levels* to *different failure modes*, achieving a more uniform risk profile.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q2]`
**Compare Working Stress Design (WSD) and Load and Resistance Factor Design (LRFD). Why has LRFD become the preferred method in modern offshore codes?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | WSD | LRFD |
|---|---|---|
| Safety philosophy | Single global safety factor | Separate partial factors on $R$ and $S$ |
| Mathematical form | $\sigma_a \le \sigma_y / SF$ | $\phi R_n \ge \sum \gamma_i Q_i$ |
| Uncertainty handling | Lumped into one factor | Distinguishes load uncertainty from resistance uncertainty |
| Reliability uniformity | Non-uniform across modes | Nearly uniform target $\beta_T$ |
| Code adoption | Older codes (pre-1980) | Modern codes (API, DNV, ISO) |

LRFD has become the preferred method because **environmental loads (waves, wind) carry much higher uncertainty** than material strengths (steel yield stress is tightly controlled). Using a *single global safety factor* cannot capture this asymmetry — LRFD treats loads and resistances with *calibrated, separate* factors, yielding structures that achieve a **nearly constant reliability index** across all failure modes. This results in *more economical* designs without sacrificing safety, and provides a *transparent link* to probabilistic reliability theory.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Common student mistake — writing that LRFD is "more conservative" than WSD. This is **incorrect**. LRFD is *more consistent*, not inherently safer. A well-calibrated LRFD design typically uses **less steel** than an equivalent WSD design for the same reliability.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

### Internal Choice Question — Module 3
> *In the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module-3 questions typically carry one compulsory question with internal choice between two 14-mark variants. Both are provided below.*

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **limit state design philosophy** applied to fixed offshore steel platforms. Discuss ULS, SLS, FLS, and ALS in terms of **(i)** design load return period, **(ii)** target reliability index $\beta_T$, and **(iii)** typical failure consequence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A platform brace has nominal resistance $R_n = 5000$ kN (COV $V_R = 0.18$) and is subjected to a 100-year storm load $S_n = 2800$ kN (COV $V_S = 0.28$). Calculate the **reliability index $\beta$** assuming both $R$ and $S$ are normally distributed, and verify if the design meets the ULS requirement of $\beta_T = 3.7$. Suggest a revision if it fails. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a) — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Sketch:** Draw a block diagram of a fixed jacket platform showing: waterline, deck, jacket legs, braces, piles, and seabed. Annotate the four limit states with arrows pointing to relevant structural regions:

- **ULS** → brace buckling, pile pull-out (extreme storm).
- **SLS** → deck deflection under operational wave (1-year).
- **FLS** → tubular joint at brace-chord intersection (cyclic).
- **ALS** → ship-impact zone near waterline, dropped-object zone on deck.

**Tabulated Discussion:**

| Limit State | Return Period | Target $\beta_T$ | Consequence if exceeded |
|---|---|---|---|
| ULS | 100 years | 3.7 | Collapse, loss of life |
| SLS | 1 year (operating) | 2.3 | Unusable platform |
| FLS | Wave scatter over 25–50 yr life | 2.3–3.1 | Fatigue cracking |
| ALS | 10,000 years | implicit | Progressive collapse |

**Conclusion:** The *limit state philosophy* provides a *unified probabilistic basis* for design, where each mode of failure is addressed with its own *load level*, *resistance reduction*, and *acceptable failure probability*, achieving uniform safety across the entire structure. **(7 marks awarded)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b) — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute means and standard deviations (assuming characteristic fractiles at 5%/95%):**

$$
\mu_R \;=\; R_n (1 + 1.645 V_R) \;=\; 5000 \times (1 + 1.645 \times 0.18) \;=\; 5000 \times 1.2961 \;=\; 6480.5 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\sigma_R \;=\; V_R \mu_R \;=\; 0.18 \times 6480.5 \;=\; 1166.5 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\mu_S \;=\; S_n (1 - 1.645 V_S) \;=\; 2800 \times (1 - 1.645 \times 0.28) \;=\; 2800 \times 0.5394 \;=\; 1510.3 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\sigma_S \;=\; V_S \mu_S \;=\; 0.28 \times 1510.3 \;=\; 422.9 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Cornell's reliability index:**

$$
\beta \;=\; \frac{\mu_R - \mu_S}{\sqrt{\sigma_R^2 + \sigma_S^2}} \;=\; \frac{6480.5 - 1510.3}{\sqrt{1166.5^2 + 422.9^2}}
$$

$$
\beta \;=\; \frac{4970.2}{\sqrt{1{,}360{,}722 + 178{,}844}} \;=\; \frac{4970.2}{\sqrt{1{,}539{,}566}} \;=\; \frac{4970.2}{1240.8}
$$

$$
\beta \;\approx\; 4.005
$$

**Step 3 — Verification:**

Since $\beta \approx 4.00 > \beta_T = 3.7$, the design **meets the ULS requirement** with a comfortable margin.

**Step 4 — Revision suggestion:**

The reliability index exceeds the target by **8%**, indicating *over-design*. To economize, one could:

- Reduce the brace wall thickness by ~6–8%, OR
- Use a higher-strength steel grade (e.g., from API 5L X52 to X60), OR
- Re-examine whether the 100-year load is over-conservative (site-specific metocean may give a smaller $S_n$).

**Final Answer:** $\beta \approx 4.00$; **ULS satisfied**; member can be optimized. **(7 marks awarded)**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Discuss the **step-by-step design strategy** for an offshore jacket platform, starting from metocean data collection up to fabrication drawings. List the major codes referenced at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **calibration of partial safety factors** using reliability theory.
