## 4.3 Mermaid Flow: LRFD Iterative Design Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Step 1: Preliminary Sizing\nBased on Experience or Code Tables"]
    P2["Step 2: Compute Load Effect S_n\nHydrodynamic + Static"]
    P3["Step 3: Apply Partial Factors\nS_d = gamma_S times S_n"]
    P4["Step 4: Compute Resistance R_n\nMaterial Strength minus Tolerance"]
    P5["Step 5: Apply Resistance Factor\nR_d = R_n divided by gamma_R"]
    P6{"Step 6: R_d >= S_d ?"}
    P7["Step 7: Adjust Member Size\nIncrease OD or Thickness"]
    P8["Step 8: Final Design\nFabrication Drawings"]

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 -->|"Yes"| P8
    P6 -->|"No"| P7
    P7 --> P2

    style P6 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P8 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Diagrams for KTU Exam:** When asked to "explain the design strategy for offshore platforms," always draw or describe a **hierarchical flow** from philosophy → limit states → environmental criteria → PSF calibration → acceptance. Examiners reward structured answers over rambling ones.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the Working Stress Design (WSD) philosophy adopted in offshore structures. Mention its limitation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** WSD is a deterministic design approach where the working (service) loads are increased by a single global Factor of Safety (FoS) and compared against the yield strength of the material:

$$\sigma_{working} = \frac{F_o \cdot S_n}{A} \leq \frac{F_y}{FoS}$$

In API RP 2A-WSD (14th edition), the global FoS = **1.67** for axial tension, **2.0** for bending, and **1.25**–**2.0** for tubular joints.

**Procedure (1 mark):** (i) Compute nominal load $S_n$, (ii) Multiply by FoS, (iii) Check against yield/ultimate strength.

**Limitation (1 mark):** It does not differentiate between load uncertainty and resistance uncertainty. A large wave load (high uncertainty) and a small dead load (low uncertainty) are amplified by the same factor, leading to over- or under-conservative designs.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*Define "Target Reliability Index" and state its typical values for ULS and ALS limit states in offshore platform design.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Target Reliability Index ($\beta_T$) is the minimum acceptable safety level prescribed by a design code, representing the inverse standard normal of the acceptable annual probability of failure:

$$P_f = \Phi(-\beta_T)$$

**Values (1 mark):**

| Limit State | $\beta_T$ | $P_f$ per year |
|---|---|---|
| **ULS** | **3.72** | $\approx 10^{-4}$ |
| **ALS** | **4.26 – 4.75** | $\approx 10^{-5}$ |
| **SLS** | 1.5 – 2.5 | $\approx 10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### Question B (Choice A) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Limit State Design philosophy** for offshore structures. List any **four** limit states and their corresponding target reliability indices. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Cornell Reliability Index** $\beta$ from first principles for two normally distributed random variables (load $S$ and resistance $R$). A jacket leg has $\mu_R = 8000$ kN, $\sigma_R = 600$ kN, $\mu_S = 4500$ kN, $\sigma_S = 900$ kN. Compute the reliability index and the annual probability of failure. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B (Choice A)

#### (a) Limit State Design Philosophy (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Limit State Design (LSD) is a reliability-based philosophy where a structure is designed such that the probability of violating any critical performance boundary (limit state) is below a prescribed target. It uses **partial safety factors** to separately account for load and resistance uncertainties.

**Limit States (4 marks):**

1. **Serviceability Limit State (SLS)** — Ensures deformations, vibrations, and crack widths remain within serviceable limits under frequent loading. $\beta_T \approx 1.5$ to $2.5$.
2. **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)** — Prevents collapse under extreme environmental loading (100-year storm). $\beta_T \approx 3.72$.
3. **Fatigue Limit State (FLS)** — Limits cumulative damage from wave-induced cyclic loading using Palmgren-Miner rule: $D = \sum (n_i / N_i) \leq 1.0$.
4. **Accidental Limit State (ALS)** — Ensures survival after a localized damage event (e.g., ship impact, dropped object). $\beta_T \approx 4.26$ to $4.75$.

**Design Format (1 mark):**

$$R_d = \frac{R_n}{\gamma_R} \geq S_d = \gamma_S \cdot S_n$$

---

#### (b) Derivation and Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Given $R \sim N(\mu_R, \sigma_R^2)$ and $S \sim N(\mu_S, \sigma_S^2)$, define the safety margin:

$$Z = R - S$$

Failure occurs when $Z \leq 0$. Since $R$ and $S$ are independent and normal:

$$\mu_Z = \mu_R - \mu_S$$

$$\sigma_Z^2 = \sigma_R^2 + \sigma_S^2$$

Standardize $Z$ to obtain the standard normal variable $U$:

$$P_f = P(Z \leq 0) = P\!\left( U \leq \frac{0 - \mu_Z}{\sigma_Z} \right) = \Phi(-\beta)$$

The reliability index is therefore:

$$\beta = \frac{\mu_R - \mu_S}{\sqrt{\sigma_R^2 + \sigma_S^2}}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

$$\beta = \frac{8000 - 4500}{\sqrt{600^2 + 900^2}} = \frac{3500}{\sqrt{360000 + 810000}} = \frac{3500}{\sqrt{1170000}}$$

$$\sqrt{1170000} \approx 1081.67$$

$$\boxed{\beta = \frac{3500}{1081.67} \approx 3.24}$$

Probability of failure:

$$P_f = \Phi(-3.24) = 1 - \Phi(3.24) \approx 1 - 0.99940 = 6.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Interpretation:** Since $\beta = 3.24 < \beta_T = 3.72$ (ULS target), the jacket leg as designed is **under-designed** for ULS. Either increase the resistance ($\mu_R$) or reduce the load ($\mu_S$).

---

### Question B (Choice B) — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** Compare **Working Stress Design (WSD)** and **Load and Resistance Factor Design (LRFD)** philosophies for offshore platforms, highlighting **five** key differences. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An offshore structure is to be designed using LRFD. The characteristic dead load $G_k = 5000$ kN, characteristic live load $Q_k = 2000$ kN, and characteristic environmental load $E_k = 8000$ kN. Partial safety factors are: $\gamma_G = 1.35$, $\gamma_Q = 1.30$, $\gamma_E = 1.20$. The characteristic resistance $R_k = 25000$ kN with $\gamma_R = 1.15$. Verify the adequacy of the design. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B (Choice B)

#### (a) Comparison WSD vs LRFD (7 marks)

| Parameter | WSD | LRFD |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Deterministic, empirical | Probabilistic, reliability-based |
| **Safety Format** | Single global FoS | Multiple partial safety factors |
| **Load–Resistance Differentiation** | Not possible | Possible (different $\gamma_S$ and $\gamma_R$) |
| **Calibration** | Based on past experience | Calibrated to target $\beta_T$ |
| **Code Examples** | API RP 2A 14th Ed. (legacy) | API 2A-LRFD, ISO 19902 |
| **Reliability Level** | Non-uniform across members | Approximately uniform |
| **Modern Status** | Phased out in new projects | **Current standard** |

(Any **5** differences = 5 × 1 mark = 5 marks; introductory statement = 1 mark; concluding statement = 1 mark.)

---

#### (b) LRFD Numerical Verification (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute Design Load Effect (2 marks):**

For an offshore platform, the combination rule (per ISO 19902 / API LRFD) for ULS is:

$$S_d = \gamma_G \cdot G_k + \gamma_Q \cdot Q_k + \gamma_E \cdot E_k$$

$$S_d = (1.35 \times 5000) + (1.30 \times 2000) + (1.20 \times 8000)$$

$$S_d = 6750 + 2600 + 9600 = 18{,}950 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Design Resistance (2 marks):**

$$R_d = \frac{R_k}{\gamma_R} = \frac{25000}{1.15} = 21{,}739.13 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Compare Against Design Criterion (2 marks):**

Check the inequality:

$$R_d \geq S_d$$

$$21{,}739.13 \text{ kN} \geq 18{,}950 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Since the design resistance exceeds the design load effect by approximately **14.7%**, the member is **adequate** per LRFD requirements. The reserve margin is appropriate for offshore structures where additional uncertainties (scour, fatigue, corrosion) accumulate over the service life.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\beta$ with $P_f$** — Many students write $P_f = \Phi(\beta)$ instead of $P_f = \Phi(-\beta)$. The negative sign is **critical**.
> 2. **Forgetting the square root in $\sigma_Z$** — Variances add, not standard deviations. Common error: writing $\sigma_R + \sigma_S$.
> 3. **Mixing up WSD and LRFD** — In WSD, FoS *divides* resistance; in LRFD, $\gamma_R$ divides resistance and $\gamma_S$ multiplies load. Reversing these signs costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — Always state kN or MPa explicitly.
> 5. **Ignoring the return-period-to-probability conversion** — Examiners frequently test $T_R = 1/P_f$; a wrong derivation here loses 4 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Design Strategy** = systematic philosophy + limit states + PSF + acceptance criteria.
- ⭐ **WSD** uses a **single global Factor of Safety** (FoS ≈ 1.67 – 2.0); **LRFD** uses **multiple partial safety factors** ($\gamma_S$, $\gamma_R$).
- ⭐ **Four limit states:** SLS, ULS, FLS, ALS — memorize their target $\beta_T$ values.
- ⭐ **Design Wave Return Period = 100 years** (operating/ULS); **Survival = 10,000 years** (ALS).
- ⭐ **Cornell Reliability Index:** $\beta = \dfrac{\mu_R - \mu_S}{\sqrt{\sigma_R^2 + \sigma_S^2}}$
- ⭐ **Probability of Failure:** $P_f = \Phi(-\beta)$
- ⭐ **Lognormal PSF formulas:** $\gamma_R = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.75 V_R \beta}$; $\gamma_S = 1 + 0.75 V_S \beta$
- ⭐ **Return Period Relation:** $T_R = 1/\lambda$; small $P_f \Rightarrow T_R \approx 1/P_f$
- ⭐ **Design Format:** $R_n / \gamma_R \geq \gamma_S \cdot S_n$
- ⭐ **ISO 19902 / API 2A-LRFD** is the **current standard**; WSD is legacy.
- ⭐ For KTU 2024 Scheme: Always conclude a design verification with **residual margin (%)** and **statement of adequacy**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Consideration for Components. Gravity Structures: Basic Configuration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Design Considerations for Offshore Structural Components?

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> **Design Considerations** refer to the systematic engineering framework of guidelines, criteria, and constraints that govern the sizing, material selection, geometry, and detailing of individual load-bearing and load-transferring **components** of an offshore structure to ensure **ultimate limit state (ULS)**, **serviceability limit state (SLS)**, **fatigue limit state (FLS)**, and **progressive collapse limit state (PLS)** requirements are satisfied over the structure's design life.

For the **Gravity-Based Structure (GBS)**, design considerations cover the **deck**, **shafts/columns**, **pontoons**, **base raft/skirt**, and **foundation soil interaction** — each engineered to resist self-weight, hydrostatic pressure, wave/current/buoyancy, impact, seismic, and operational loads.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Underwater Apartment Building"

Imagine a giant **concrete apartment building tipped on its side and sunk to the seabed**. Each "room" is a hollow concrete cell (called a *cell* or *compartment*) that is ballasted with seawater, sand, or heavy minerals to keep it pinned to the ocean floor. The roof of the building pokes above the water, and on top of that we mount an oil platform. The building must:

1. **Not float away** → it must be heavy enough (gravity does the work).
2. **Not tip over** → the base must be wide and the center of gravity kept low.
3. **Not crack** → the concrete and steel reinforcement must handle the crushing pressure of the ocean.
4. **Not be eaten by rust** → the steel must be protected against the corrosive saltwater.

This is the essence of designing a **Gravity Structure**.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Density of seawater** ≈ **1025 kg/m³** (used for buoyancy calculations).
> - **Density of reinforced concrete** ≈ **2500 kg/m³** (typical for GBS construction).
> - **Density of iron ore ballast** ≈ **3000–5000 kg/m³** (used to increase self-weight).
> - **Acceleration due to gravity, g = 9.81 m/s²**.
> - **Design life** of offshore structures = **25 to 50 years** (typical: **30 years** for fixed platforms, **50 years** for GBS).
> - **Partial safety factors** for dead load γ_G = **1.1 to 1.35**, live load γ_Q = **1.3 to 1.5**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Design of offshore structural components must integrate environmental loading (wind, wave, current, ice), accidental loading (ship impact, fire, explosion), and functional loading (drilling, production, storage) under the **LRFD (Load and Resistance Factor Design)** philosophy prescribed in **API RP 2A / ISO 19900 / DNV-OS-C101**.

## 1.2 Basic Configuration of Gravity Structures

### Definition

> A **Gravity Structure** (also called **Gravity-Based Structure, GBS**) is a fixed offshore structure that derives its stability against environmental overturning and sliding forces primarily from its **self-weight and ballast weight**, rather than from piles driven into the seabed. It rests directly on the seabed (often with a skirt foundation) and transfers all loads to the supporting soil through bearing and friction.

### Sub-Types of Gravity Structures

| Sub-Type | Key Feature | Typical Use |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Concrete GBS (Condeep type)** | Cellular concrete base with tapered shafts | North Sea (Statfjord, Gullfaks) |
| **Steel GBS (Caisson type)** | Steel cylindrical or rectangular caisson | Shallow water, storage |
| **Doris-type GBS** | Circular base raft with central shaft | Early GBS designs (Ekoﬁsk) |
| **Andoc-type GBS** | Rectangular cellular base with corner shafts | Concrete gravity platforms |
| **Sea Tank-type GBS** | Cellular base with cylindrical oil storage | Storage and loading platforms |

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown — Design Considerations for Components

### Step A: Identify Functional Requirements
Each component has a **primary function** that drives its design:
- **Deck** → supports topside equipment, drilling, living quarters.
- **Shafts/Columns** → transfer deck loads to the base, house risers.
- **Pontoons** → provide buoyancy during towing, support shafts.
- **Base Raft** → distributes weight onto seabed soil, resists overturning.
- **Skirt** → transfers shear and tension to deeper soil strata.

### Step B: Enumerate All Loads
Design load categories per **API RP 2A / ISO 19902**:

$$\text{Total Design Load} = L_{\text{Dead}} + L_{\text{Live}} + L_{\text{Env}} + L_{\text{Accidental}}$$

- **Dead Load (DL):** Self-weight of structure, equipment, ballast.
- **Live Load (LL):** Personnel, consumables, drilling supplies.
- **Environmental Load (EL):** Wind, wave, current, snow, ice, earthquake.
- **Accidental Load (AL):** Ship impact, dropped objects, fire, explosion.

### Step C: Establish Limit States
- **ULS (Ultimate):** No collapse, no rupture, foundation bearing capacity not exceeded.
- **SLS (Serviceability):** Deflection limits, vibration limits, no permanent deformation.
- **FLS (Fatigue):** Cyclic wave loading over design life — typically 10⁷ to 10⁸ cycles.
- **PLS (Progressive Collapse):** Loss of one component must not cause progressive failure.

### Step D: Apply Partial Safety Factors
Load combinations for **ULS**:

$$S_d = \gamma_g G_k + \gamma_q Q_k + \gamma_e E_k + \gamma_a A_k$$

### Step E: Material Selection
- **Concrete:** High-strength (C40 to C70), durable against chloride ingress, sulfate attack.
- **Reinforcing steel:** High-yield (500 MPa) with adequate cover (≥ 50 mm in splash zone).
- **Prestressing steel:** Tendons (1860 MPa) for shafts and base slab to resist tension from hydrostatic pressure and bending.

### Step F: Geometry Optimization
- **Shaft taper:** Top diameter smaller than base diameter (saves material, reduces wave load).
- **Cellular base:** Honeycomb arrangement of cells provides buoyancy during tow-out, then ballasted for service.
- **Skirt depth:** 2–10 m below seabed to transfer loads to competent soil.

## 2.2 Why the Configuration Matters — The Underlying Physics

### The Stability Triangle
A gravity structure must satisfy three stability criteria:

1. **Overturning stability** → Resisting moment > Overturning moment.
2. **Sliding stability** → Friction + passive resistance > Horizontal force.
3. **Bearing stability** → Maximum contact pressure < Allowable soil bearing capacity.

### Configuration Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Gravity"?** Unlike jacket platforms that use **piled foundations** to anchor them, a GBS uses its **own weight (gravity force = m·g)** to resist uplift and overturning. The wider the base and the lower the center of gravity, the greater the **resisting moment arm** against wave-induced overturning moments.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Units |
|-------|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F_{\text{buoy}} = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{disp}}$ | Buoyancy force (Archimedes' principle) | N |
| 2 | $F_{\text{grav}} = m_{\text{struct}} \cdot g$ | Total gravitational (self-weight + ballast) force | N |
| 3 | $\text{SF}_{\text{OT}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{resisting}}}{M_{\text{overturning}}}$ | Overturning safety factor (must be ≥ 1.5 for ULS) | — |
| 4 | $\text{SF}_{\text{SL}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{friction}} + F_{\text{passive}}}{F_{\text{horizontal}}}$ | Sliding safety factor (must be ≥ 1.2 for ULS) | — |
| 5 | $q_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{V}{A} + \dfrac{M \cdot x}{I}$ | Maximum base contact pressure (combined axial + bending) | kPa |
| 6 | $M_{\text{wave}} = F_{\text{wave}} \cdot h_{\text{action}}$ | Wave overturning moment (force × lever arm) | kN·m |
| 7 | $M_{\text{resist}} = W_{\text{buoy-submerged}} \cdot \dfrac{B}{2}$ | Resisting moment from submerged weight × half-base width | kN·m |
| 8 | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{N}{A} + \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | Combined axial + bending stress (shaft design) | MPa |
| 9 | $\Delta\sigma_{\text{stress}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{max}} - \sigma_{\text{min}}}{2}$ | Stress range for fatigue analysis (S-N curve) | MPa |
| 10 | $t_{\text{wall}} = \dfrac{p \cdot r}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}$ | Wall thickness for hydrostatic pressure (Hoop stress) | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the backbone of **GBS preliminary sizing**. In KTU exams, you will most commonly be asked to compute the **overturning safety factor**, **base contact pressure**, or **ballast requirement** for a given geometry.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Submerged Weight of a GBS

The **submerged weight (W′)** is the difference between the total structural weight and the displaced water weight. This is the *effective* weight that contributes to stability.

### Step 1: Define Total Weight

$$W_{\text{total}} = W_{\text{struct}} + W_{\text{ballast}} + W_{\text{topside}}$$

Here, $W_{\text{struct}}$ = self-weight of concrete and steel; $W_{\text{ballast}}$ = seawater, sand, or iron-ore ballast; $W_{\text{topside}}$ = deck and equipment weight.

### Step 2: Define Buoyancy Force

By Archimedes' principle, the upward buoyancy force equals the weight of displaced water:

$$F_{\text{buoy}} = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{disp}}$$

where $V_{\text{disp}}$ = submerged volume of the structure.

### Step 3: Compute Submerged Weight

The submerged weight (effective downward force) is:

$$\begin{aligned}
W^{\prime} &= W_{\text{total}} - F_{\text{buoy}} \\
&= W_{\text{total}} - \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{disp}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logical transition:** The total downward weight (gravity) is reduced by the upward buoyancy. The net downward force is what holds the structure on the seabed.

### Step 4: Express in Compact Form

$$W^{\prime} = V_{\text{disp}} \cdot g \cdot (\rho_{\text{avg}} - \rho_w)$$

where $\rho_{\text{avg}}$ = average density of the GBS (including ballast) = $W_{\text{total}} / (g \cdot V_{\text{total}})$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Overturning Safety Factor

### Step 1: Identify Moments about the Toe of the Base

Consider the structure rotating about the **downwind edge (toe)** of the base slab.

### Step 2: Resisting Moment

The restoring moment is created by the submerged weight acting through the centroid of the base, at a horizontal lever arm of $\dfrac{B}{2}$ (half the base width):

$$M_{\text{resisting}} = W^{\prime} \cdot \dfrac{B}{2}$$

### Step 3: Overturning Moment

The wave, wind, and current produce horizontal force $F_H$ at elevation $z_F$ above the base. The moment arm is the elevation:

$$M_{\text{overturning}} = F_H \cdot z_F + M_{\text{wave-bending}}$$

### Step 4: Safety Factor

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SF}_{\text{OT}} &= \dfrac{M_{\text{resisting}}}{M_{\text{overturning}}} \\
&= \dfrac{W^{\prime} \cdot (B/2)}{F_H \cdot z_F + M_{\text{wave-bending}}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Code Requirement (API RP 2A / DNV-OS-C101):**

$$\text{SF}_{\text{OT}} \geq 1.5 \quad \text{(for ULS environmental load combination)}$$

**Logical transition:** A higher safety factor means the structure is more resistant to overturning. Designers increase $W^{\prime}$ (add ballast), increase $B$ (wider base), or both.

## 3.3 Derivation: Base Contact Pressure (Eccentric Loading)

When horizontal wave load creates an overturning moment, the contact pressure on the base is no longer uniform.

### Step 1: Equivalent Eccentricity

The applied moment $M$ and vertical load $V$ create an **equivalent eccentricity** $e$:

$$e = \dfrac{M}{V}$$

### Step 2: Kern Limit

For the pressure to remain **compressive everywhere** (no tension/separation at the heel), the eccentricity must satisfy:

$$e \leq \dfrac{B}{6}$$

This is called the **middle-third rule**.

### Step 3: Maximum and Minimum Contact Pressure

For a rectangular base of width $B$ and length $L$:

$$\begin{aligned}
q_{\text{max}} &= \dfrac{V}{B \cdot L} + \dfrac{6M}{B^2 \cdot L} \\
q_{\text{min}} &= \dfrac{V}{B \cdot L} - \dfrac{6M}{B^2 \cdot L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logical transition:** When $e = B/6$ (kern limit), $q_{\text{min}} = 0$ — the entire base is in compression, but the heel is just about to lift. When $e > B/6$, part of the base separates from the soil — a non-physical assumption for soil, so a **reduced effective base** must be used.

### Step 4: Allowable Bearing Check

$$q_{\text{max}} \leq q_{\text{allow}} = \dfrac{q_{\text{ult}}}{FS_{\text{bearing}}}$$

where $q_{\text{ult}}$ is the ultimate bearing capacity from soil mechanics (Terzaghi, Meyerhof, or Skempton) and $FS_{\text{bearing}} \approx 2.0$ to 3.0.

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Typical Problem)

**Problem:** A square GBS base of dimensions $B = 60$ m $\times L = 60$ m, height $H = 25$ m, is submerged in seawater ($\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³). Total structural weight = **500,000 tonnes**. Vertical load from deck and ballast = **200,000 tonnes**. Wave force = **150 MN** acting at **20 m above base**. Compute (a) submerged weight, (b) overturning safety factor, (c) base eccentricity, and (d) maximum contact pressure.

### Solution

**Given:** $B = L = 60$ m, $H = 25$ m, $\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $m_{\text{total}} = 700,000$ t (i.e., 500,000 + 200,000).

**(a) Submerged Weight**

First, displaced volume:
$$V_{\text{disp}} = B \cdot L \cdot H = 60 \times 60 \times 25 = 90{,}000 \text{ m}^3$$

Mass of displaced seawater:
$$m_w = \rho_w \cdot V_{\text{disp}} = 1025 \times 90{,}000 = 92{,}250{,}000 \text{ kg} = 92{,}250 \text{ t}$$

Submerged weight:
$$\begin{aligned}
W^{\prime} &= (m_{\text{total}} - m_w) \cdot g \\
&= (700{,}000 - 92{,}250) \times 9.81 \times 10^3 \text{ kN} \\
&= 607{,}750 \times 9.81 = 5{,}962{,}028 \text{ kN} \approx 5962 \text{ MN}
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) Overturning Safety Factor**

Resisting moment:
$$M_{\text{res}} = W^{\prime} \cdot \dfrac{B}{2} = 5962 \times 30 = 178{,}860 \text{ MN·m}$$

Overturning moment:
$$M_{\text{OT}} = F_H \cdot z_F = 150 \times 20 = 3000 \text{ MN·m}$$

Safety factor:
$$\text{SF}_{\text{OT}} = \dfrac{178{,}860}{3000} = 59.6 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{EXCELLENTLY STABLE}$$

**(c) Eccentricity**

$$e = \dfrac{M_{\text{OT}}}{W^{\prime}} = \dfrac{3000}{5962} = 0.503 \text{ m}$$

Kern limit: $B/6 = 60/6 = 10$ m. Since $0.503 \ll 10$, the structure is **well within the middle third**.

**(d) Maximum Contact Pressure**

$$q_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{W^{\prime}}{B \cdot L} + \dfrac{6 \cdot M_{\text{OT}}}{B^2 \cdot L}$$

$$q_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{5{,}962{,}000}{3600} + \dfrac{6 \times 3{,}000{,}000}{3600 \times 60} = 1656.1 + 83.3 = 1739.4 \text{ kPa}$$

$$q_{\text{min}} = 1656.1 - 83.3 = 1572.8 \text{ kPa}$$

**Interpretation:** The pressure distribution is nearly uniform (1657 ± 84 kPa), confirming very stable behaviour.
