# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: Define a Gravity-Based Structure (GBS) and list any two advantages over a piled jacket platform.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Gravity-Based Structure (GBS)** is a fixed offshore structure that derives its stability primarily from its **self-weight and ballast weight** acting on the seabed, rather than from driven piles. It typically consists of a **concrete (or steel) cellular base raft** with **shafts/columns** supporting the deck.

**Two advantages over jacket platform:**

1. **Suitable for deep water and soft soil** — GBS can be founded on weak seabeds where pile driving is difficult, by distributing loads over a large base area.
2. **No pile driving required** — eliminates underwater noise, reduces offshore installation time, and provides built-in oil storage in cellular base.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: What is the "middle-third rule" in the design of GBS foundations? State its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **middle-third rule** states that for a gravity foundation, the **eccentricity** of the resultant vertical load must lie within the **middle third** of the base width, i.e.,
> $$e = \dfrac{M}{V} \leq \dfrac{B}{6}$$
> **Significance:** If this rule is satisfied, the **contact pressure distribution** under the base remains **compressive everywhere** (no tension or soil separation at the heel). This ensures full base contact with the soil and prevents rocking/tilting failure modes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — **CHOICE 1**

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **basic configuration of a Condeep-type gravity structure** with a neat sketch. List the functions of each major component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A circular GBS has a base diameter of **80 m** and a height of **30 m**. The total weight (structure + ballast + topside) is **800,000 tonnes**. The horizontal wave force is **200 MN** acting at an elevation of **25 m above the base**. Seawater density is **1025 kg/m³**. Compute: (i) Submerged weight, (ii) Overturning safety factor, (iii) Maximum base contact pressure, (iv) Check the middle-third rule. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Condeep-Type GBS Configuration:**

The **Condeep** (Concrete Deep Water) platform consists of:
- **Cellular concrete base raft** (honeycomb of cylindrical or rectangular cells) — provides buoyancy during tow-out and acts as foundation in service.
- **Three or four tapered concrete shafts** — extend from the base to support the deck; house risers, j-tubes, and access.
- **Steel deck (topside)** — supports drilling rig, production facilities, helideck, accommodation.
- **Skirt foundation** — steel extensions below base for load transfer to deeper soil.

**Functions of each component:**

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| Cellular Base | Buoyancy during tow, ballast storage, foundation bearing |
| Shafts | Transfer deck loads to base, house risers |
| Deck | Support operational equipment and personnel |
| Skirt | Transfer shear/tension to soil, prevent scour |

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 80$ m, $H = 30$ m, $m_{\text{total}} = 800,000$ t, $F_H = 200$ MN, $z_F = 25$ m, $\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1: Submerged Weight**

Base area: $A = \pi D^2/4 = \pi \times 80^2/4 = 5026.55$ m²
Volume: $V = A \times H = 5026.55 \times 30 = 150,796$ m³
Mass of displaced water: $m_w = 1025 \times 150,796 = 154,565,900$ kg $= 154,566$ t

$$\begin{aligned}
W^{\prime} &= (m_{\text{total}} - m_w) \cdot g \\
&= (800,000 - 154,566) \times 9.81 \times 10^3 \\
&= 645,434 \times 9.81 = 6,332,706 \text{ kN} \approx 6333 \text{ MN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Overturning Safety Factor**

Resisting moment: $M_{\text{res}} = W^{\prime} \cdot D/2 = 6333 \times 40 = 253,320$ MN·m
Overturning moment: $M_{\text{OT}} = F_H \cdot z_F = 200 \times 25 = 5000$ MN·m

$$\text{SF}_{\text{OT}} = \dfrac{253,320}{5000} = 50.66 \gg 1.5 \quad \text{(SAFE)}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Base Contact Pressure**

$$q_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{W^{\prime}}{A} + \dfrac{M_{\text{OT}} \cdot c}{I}$$

For a circular base: $I = \pi D^4/64 = \pi \times 80^4/64 = 2,010,619$ m⁴; $c = D/2 = 40$ m

$$q_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{6,332,706}{5026.55} + \dfrac{5000 \times 10^3 \times 40}{2,010,619}$$

$$q_{\text{max}} = 1259.9 + 99.5 = 1359.4 \text{ kPa} \approx 1.36 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Middle-Third Check**

$$e = \dfrac{M_{\text{OT}}}{W^{\prime}} = \dfrac{5000 \times 10^3}{6,332,706} = 0.79 \text{ m}$$

Kern limit: $D/6 = 80/6 = 13.33$ m. Since $0.79 \ll 13.33$, the structure is **well within the middle third** ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — **CHOICE 2**

**Q: (a)** Discuss in detail the **design considerations for the major components** of a fixed offshore platform. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A square GBS of base dimension **50 m × 50 m** and height **20 m** is ballasted with iron ore ($\rho_{\text{ore}} = 4500$ kg/m³) in its base cells. The structure itself has a mass of **400,000 tonnes**, and the deck mass is **50,000 tonnes**. Compute the **volume of iron ore ballast** required to achieve an **overturning safety factor of 2.0** against a wave force of **120 MN** at **15 m elevation**. Use $\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Design Considerations for Major Components:**

1. **Deck:** Designed for combined dead + live + environmental + accidental loads; must accommodate drilling loads (vibration, rotary), and provide adequate stiffness to avoid excessive deflection under equipment operation.

2. **Shafts/Columns:** Designed for axial compression from deck, bending from waves (Morison equation), and local buckling. Concrete shafts use prestressing to handle hydrostatic tension and bending. Steel shafts require corrosion protection (cathodic protection, coating).

3. **Pontoons/Cellular Base:** Designed for hydrostatic pressure during tow-out (when submerged), bearing pressure on seabed in service, and buoyancy control. Concrete thickness governed by hoop stress and punching shear.

4. **Base Raft:** Designed for soil bearing pressure, edge bearing under eccentric loading, and bending from differential settlement.

5. **Skirt/Foundation:** Designed for shear transfer under sliding loads, tension under uplift, and penetration resistance during installation.

6. **Connections:** Critical at shaft-base and shaft-deck junctions. Designed for fatigue (welded steel) and crack control (concrete).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $B = L = 50$ m, $H = 20$ m, $m_{\text{struct}} = 400,000$ t, $m_{\text{deck}} = 50,000$ t, $F_H = 120$ MN, $z_F = 15$ m, $\rho_{\text{ore}} = 4500$ kg/m³, $\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³, $\text{SF}_{\text{OT}} = 2.0$.

**Step 1: Set up stability equation**

Required condition:
$$\text{SF}_{\text{OT}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{res}}}{M_{\text{OT}}} = 2.0$$

So required $M_{\text{res}} = 2 \times M_{\text{OT}} = 2 \times 120 \times 15 = 3600$ MN·m

**Step 2: Required submerged weight**

$$M_{\text{res}} = W^{\prime} \cdot \dfrac{B}{2} \Rightarrow W^{\prime} = \dfrac{3600 \times 2}{50} = 144 \text{ MN}$$

**Step 3: Submerged weight expression**

Displaced water mass: $m_w = 1025 \times 50 \times 50 \times 20 = 51,250,000$ kg $= 51,250$ t

Let $m_b$ = mass of iron ore ballast (in tonnes).

$$W^{\prime} = (m_{\text{struct}} + m_{\text{deck}} + m_b - m_w) \cdot g$$

In force units: $W^{\prime} = (400,000 + 50,000 + m_b - 51,250) \times 9.81 \times 10^{-3}$ MN

$$144 = (398,750 + m_b) \times 9.81 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$398,750 + m_b = \dfrac{144}{0.00981} = 14{,}679.9$$

$$m_b = 14{,}679.9 - 398{,}750 = \text{NEGATIVE VALUE (ERROR)}$$

**Interpretation:** The structural + deck weight alone provides *far more* than enough submerged weight. The structure is **massively over-stable**. Therefore, **no additional iron ore ballast is required**; in fact, the SF with zero ballast is:

Without ballast: $W^{\prime} = 398,750 \times 9.81 \times 10^{-3} = 3911.7$ MN
$M_{\text{res}} = 3911.7 \times 25 = 97,792$ MN·m
$\text{SF}_{\text{OT}} = 97,792 / 1800 = 54.3 \gg 2.0$

**Comment:** The structure is severely over-designed. A smaller base footprint (e.g., 30 m × 30 m) or reduced deck weight would be more economical.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting buoyancy** — Many students compute total weight (struct + deck) without subtracting displaced water. Always subtract buoyancy.
> 2. **Wrong lever arm** — Resisting moment uses $B/2$ (half base width), NOT the full base width.
> 3. **Ignoring units** — Convert tonnes to kg, then multiply by $g$ to get force in N. Mixing kN and MN is the most common error.
> 4. **Kern limit check** — Always verify $e \leq B/6$; otherwise the contact pressure formula breaks down.
> 5. **Wave force elevation** — If the wave force is given at the still water level, the lever arm is the height above the base — do not confuse with wave height.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **GBS = Self-weight + Ballast = Stability** (no piles needed).
- ✅ **Components:** Deck → Shafts → Pontoons → Base Raft → Skirt → Seabed.
- ✅ **Submerged weight** $W^{\prime} = (m_{\text{total}} - \rho_w V_{\text{disp}}) \cdot g$.
- ✅ **Overturning SF** $\geq$ **1.5** (API RP 2A / DNV).
- ✅ **Sliding SF** $\geq$ **1.2**.
- ✅ **Middle-third rule** $e \leq B/6$ for full contact.
- ✅ **Kern limit:** $q_{\text{max}} = V/(BL) + 6M/(B^2L)$; $q_{\text{min}} = V/(BL) - 6M/(B^2L)$.
- ✅ **Condeep** = cellular base + tapered shafts (3 or 4); used in North Sea.
- ✅ **Doris** = circular base + central shaft; oldest GBS type.
- ✅ **Andoc** = rectangular base + 4 corner shafts.
- ✅ **Sea Tank** = cylindrical storage cells in base.
- ✅ **Ballast types:** seawater (cheap, low density), sand (medium), iron ore (heaviest).
- ✅ **Concrete grade** for GBS: C40 to C70; cover ≥ 50 mm in splash zone.
- ✅ **Skirt depth** = 2–10 m for soil interaction.
- ✅ **Fatigue** is critical in splash zone; **prestressing** resists hydrostatic tension.
- ✅ **Limit states:** ULS, SLS, FLS, PLS — all must be checked.
- ✅ **LRFD load combination:** $S_d = \gamma_g G_k + \gamma_q Q_k + \gamma_e E_k + \gamma_a A_k$.
- ✅ **Default safety factors (typical):** γ_G = 1.1–1.35, γ_Q = 1.3–1.5.
- ✅ **Always convert units consistently:** tonnes → kg → N (or kN, or MN).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sizing Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sizing Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sizing Considerations** in offshore structural engineering refer to the systematic preliminary determination of the geometric and structural dimensions of an offshore platform — including its substructure (jacket/template), topside (deck), tubular members, bracing, and foundation elements — based on functional requirements, environmental loading, water depth, fabrication/transportation limits, and code-based design guidelines (e.g., API RP 2A-WSD, ISO 19902, AISC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Sizing is the **iterative preliminary design phase** that bridges conceptual design and detailed analysis. It ensures the structure is hydrodynamically stable, fabricationally feasible, and code-compliant *before* rigorous in-place and dynamic analyses are performed.

The sizing process addresses four fundamental engineering questions:

1. **What loads will act on the structure?** (Waves, wind, current, seismic)
2. **What is the minimum elevation of the deck above water?** (Air gap)
3. **What diameter, thickness, and length should each tubular member have?**
4. **What size pile is required to resist overturning and vertical loads?**

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Coastal Lighthouse Builder"**
>
> Imagine you are a lighthouse engineer asked to build a steel tower standing in 100 m of seawater. Before drawing any blueprints, you must make quick decisions: *How thick should the legs be? How high above the waves should the lamp room sit? How deep should the foundations reach into the seabed?*
>
> You don't perform a finite element analysis for every nut and bolt during initial sizing. Instead, you use **empirical rules of thumb, simplified hydrodynamic formulas, and past project data** to lock in a "ballpark" geometry. Once this geometry works on paper (i.e., it survives simple hand calculations), you proceed to detailed computer analysis.
>
> **This entire "ballpark" exercise is what we call Sizing.** It is the engineering equivalent of trying on clothes before tailoring — establishing the right "fit" before committing to a final design.

## 1.3 Key Design Parameters (with Standard Units)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Water depth | $d$ | 30 – 300+ | m |
| Design wave height | $H$ | 5 – 30 | m |
| Wave period | $T$ | 6 – 18 | s |
| Current velocity | $V_c$ | 0.5 – 1.5 | m/s |
| Wind velocity | $V_w$ | 30 – 80 | m/s |
| Marine growth thickness | $t_g$ | 50 – 150 | mm |
| Corrosion allowance | $t_c$ | 3 – 12 | mm |
| Steel density | $\rho_s$ | **7850** | kg/m³ |
| Sea water density | $\rho_w$ | **1025** | kg/m³ |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Elevation:** Most sizing rules use **Mean Sea Level (MSL)** or **Lowest Astronomical Tide (LAT)** as datum. API RP 2A traditionally uses **Mean Lower Low Water (MLLW)** + storm surge as the design water level.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sizing Philosophy — Step-by-Step Logic

The KTU 2024 syllabus frames sizing as a **sequential, iterative procedure**. Each step feeds into the next:

### Step 1 — Establish the Environmental Design Criteria
The metocean data is collected from hindcasts and site-specific measurements. The **100-year return period** values are typically adopted for in-place design.

$$\boxed{H_{1/3} = 4\sigma_\eta \quad ; \quad H_{max} \approx 1.86\,H_s}$$

where $H_s$ is the significant wave height and $\sigma_\eta$ is the standard deviation of the surface elevation.

### Step 2 — Determine the Wave Crest Elevation
The crest elevation above MSL is given by:

$$\eta_c = \frac{H}{2} \cdot \frac{\cosh\left[k(d+z)\right]}{\sinh(kd)}$$

For deep water ($d > 0.5L$), this simplifies to $\eta_c \approx 0.5\,H$.

### Step 3 — Determine the Minimum Deck Elevation
The deck underside must clear the wave crest by a safety margin:

$$\boxed{E_{deck} = \eta_c + \Delta_{surge} + \Delta_{run-up} + S_{air-gap}}$$

where $S_{air-gap}$ is typically **1.5 m** per API RP 2A for non-evacuated platforms.

### Step 4 — Size the Tubular Members
Tubular members are sized for combined **axial + bending** using simplified column-buckling curves. The diameter-to-thickness ratio is constrained by:

$$\boxed{\frac{D}{t} \leq 60 \text{ (for } H \text{- or } J \text{-pile)} \quad ; \quad \frac{D}{t} \leq 300 \text{ (for braces)}}$$

### Step 5 — Estimate Wave Forces (Morison's Equation)
For slender members (where $D/L < 0.2$), the inline force is:

$$\boxed{F_w = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_w\,C_D\,D\,u\mid u\mid + \rho_w\,C_M\,\tfrac{\pi D^2}{4}\,\dot{u}}$$

where $C_D$ and $C_M$ are the drag and inertia coefficients respectively.

### Step 6 — Size the Jacket Legs and Piles
Pile diameter is sized using the overturning moment and base shear requirements. Initial pile length:

$$L_{pile} \approx \frac{P_{axial}}{f_s \cdot \pi D_{pile}} + \text{embedment for lateral fixity}$$

## 2.2 Hydrodynamic Coefficient Selection

| Member Orientation | $C_D$ | $C_M$ |
|---|---|---|
| Smooth cylinder, smooth flow | **0.65** | **1.6 – 2.0** |
| Rough cylinder (marine growth) | **0.65 – 1.05** | **1.2 – 1.6** |
| Appurtenances / conductors | **1.05** | **1.2 – 2.0** |

> [!NOTE]
> **API RP 2A Default Values:** $C_D = 0.65$ for smooth cylinders, $C_M = 1.6$ for smooth, **2.0** for rough. KTU examiners expect these standard values unless problem data specifies otherwise.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L = \frac{gT^2}{2\pi}\tanh(kd)$ | Wave length (dispersion) | m |
| 2 | $\eta_c = \frac{H}{2}\cdot\frac{\cosh(k(d+z))}{\sinh(kd)}$ | Wave crest at depth $z$ | m |
| 3 | $u_{max} = \frac{H\,g}{2}\cdot\frac{\cosh(k(d+z))}{\omega\cosh(kd)}$ | Horizontal water particle velocity | m/s |
| 4 | $\dot{u}_{max} = \frac{H\pi g}{T L}\cdot\frac{\cosh(k(d+z))}{\cosh(kd)}$ | Horizontal water particle acceleration | m/s² |
| 5 | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_w C_D D u\vert u\vert$ | Drag force per unit length | N/m |
| 6 | $F_I = \rho_w C_M (\pi D^2/4)\dot{u}$ | Inertia force per unit length | N/m |
| 7 | $E_{deck} = \eta_c + S + \Delta_{air}$ | Minimum deck elevation | m |
| 8 | $D/t \leq 60$ (leg/pile), $\leq 300$ (braces) | Member slenderness | — |
| 9 | $\sigma_c = \pi^2 E/(K L/r)^2$ | Euler buckling stress | Pa |
| 10 | $A_{pile} = P_{axial}/f_y$ | Initial pile cross-section | m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on pipe symbols:** All "absolute value" or "magnitude" operations in formulas use the mathematical symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ to avoid conflict with markdown table delimiters.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sizing considerations are not academic exercises — they directly influence:
- **Fabrication yard capacity** (maximum lift weight, maximum jacket width for transportation)
- **Vessel selection** (barge / launch barge dimensions)
- **Schedule** (welding tonnage estimates)
- **Cost** (steel weight is the dominant CAPEX driver for fixed platforms)
- **Installation** (pile driving feasibility, hammer selection)

In the KTU 2024 outcome-based framework, sizing maps to **CO3** — *"Apply preliminary sizing techniques to offshore structural systems under environmental loading."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Wave Crest Elevation using Linear (Airy) Wave Theory

We begin with the Airy velocity potential $\phi$ and surface displacement $\eta$.

**Step 1 — Surface displacement:**
$$\eta(x,t) = \frac{H}{2}\cos(kx - \omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Maximum crest at $x=0$, $t=0$:**
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{H}{2}$$

**Step 3 — To find the velocity potential at a depth $z$ below SWL, apply linear theory:**
$$\phi(x,z,t) = \frac{H g}{2\omega}\cdot\frac{\cosh(k(d+z))}{\cosh(kd)}\sin(kx-\omega t)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the dynamic free-surface boundary condition (linearized):**
$$\eta = -\frac{1}{g}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}\bigg|_{z=0}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting and simplifying, the velocity potential at $z=0$ yields the dispersion relation:**
$$\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kd)$$

**Step 6 — Substituting $\phi$ at any elevation $z$, the crest elevation profile becomes:**
$$\eta_c(z) = \frac{H}{2}\cdot\frac{\cosh\!\bigl(k(d+z)\bigr)}{\cosh(kd)}$$

This is the **complete Airy-wave crest profile** used in KTU sizing calculations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Horizontal Water Particle Velocity and Acceleration

Starting from the velocity potential, the horizontal velocity is:

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{H\pi}{T}\cdot\frac{\cosh\!\bigl(k(d+z)\bigr)}{\sinh(kd)}\cos(kx - \omega t)$$

The maximum horizontal velocity occurs at the wave crest ($kx - \omega t = 0$):

$$u_{max}(z) = \frac{H\,g\,k}{\omega}\cdot\frac{\cosh\!\bigl(k(d+z)\bigr)}{\omega\cosh(kd)} = \frac{H\pi}{T}\cdot\frac{\cosh\!\bigl(k(d+z)\bigr)}{\sinh(kd)}$$

Similarly, the horizontal acceleration:

$$\dot{u}_{max}(z) = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}\bigg|_{max} = \frac{H\pi^2}{T^2}\cdot\frac{\cosh\!\bigl(k(d+z)\bigr)}{\sinh(kd)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For deep water ($d > 0.5L$), these simplify to $u_{max} \approx \pi H / T \cdot e^{kz}$ and $\dot{u}_{max} \approx (2\pi^2 H/T^2)\cdot e^{kz}$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Problem — Typical KTU 2024 Style

> **Problem:** A fixed offshore platform is to be installed in $d = 80$ m water depth. The design wave has $H = 12$ m and $T = 10$ s. The jacket has a vertical bracing member of diameter $D = 0.8$ m located at mid-depth (z = −40 m from SWL). Using API RP 2A recommended coefficients ($C_D = 0.65$, $C_M = 2.0$), determine:
>
> **(a)** the wave length and maximum crest elevation,
> **(b)** the maximum drag and inertia forces per unit length on the member.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Wave Length**

We solve the dispersion relation iteratively:

$$\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kd)$$

with $\omega = 2\pi/T = 0.628$ rad/s, so $\omega^2 = 0.395$ rad²/s².

**Iteration 1 — Assume deep water:** $L_0 = gT^2/(2\pi) = 153.1$ m; $k_0 = 2\pi/L_0 = 0.0410$ rad/m.

**Iteration 2:** $\tanh(k_0 d) = \tanh(3.28) \approx 0.997 \approx 1$.

So deep-water assumption is acceptable: $L \approx 153$ m.

$$\boxed{L \approx 153 \text{ m} \quad ; \quad k = 0.0410 \text{ rad/m}}$$

Maximum crest at SWL:

$$\eta_c = \frac{H}{2} = 6.0 \text{ m}$$

**Part (b) — Wave Kinematics at z = −40 m**

Using the full linear expressions:

$$u_{max} = \frac{H\pi}{T}\cdot\frac{\cosh\!\bigl(k(d+z)\bigr)}{\sinh(kd)}$$

Compute arguments: $k(d+z) = 0.041 \times 40 = 1.640$; $kd = 0.041 \times 80 = 3.280$.

Numerical values:
- $\cosh(1.640) \approx 2.674$
- $\sinh(3.280) \approx 13.49$

Therefore:

$$u_{max} = \frac{12 \times 3.1416}{10}\cdot\frac{2.674}{13.49} = 3.770 \times 0.1982 = 0.747 \text{ m/s}$$

For acceleration:

$$\dot{u}_{max} = \frac{12 \times 9.870}{100}\cdot\frac{2.674}{13.49} = 1.184 \times 0.1982 = 0.235 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Drag force per unit length:**

$$F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_w\,C_D\,D\,u_{max}^2$$

$$F_D = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.65 \times 0.8 \times (0.747)^2$$

$$F_D = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.65 \times 0.8 \times 0.558 = 148.7 \text{ N/m}$$

**Inertia force per unit length:**

$$F_I = \rho_w\,C_M\,(\pi D^2/4)\,\dot{u}_{max}$$

$$F_I = 1025 \times 2.0 \times \left(\frac{\pi \times 0.64}{4}\right) \times 0.235$$

$$F_I = 1025 \times 2.0 \times 0.5027 \times 0.235 = 242.2 \text{ N/m}$$

**Total Maximum Force (peak not simultaneous — but for sizing, vector sum):**

$$F_{total} = F_D + F_I \approx 148.7 + 242.2 = 390.9 \text{ N/m}$$

$$\boxed{F_{total} \approx 391 \text{ N/m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask: *Why isn't $F_{total}$ simply $\sqrt{F_D^2 + F_I^2}$?* Answer: For conservative **sizing** (where exact phase is uncertain), the linear sum is used. For **fatigue** and **ultimate strength**, the square-root-of-sum-of-squares (SRSS) is more appropriate.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Member Selection by Loading Type

| Loading Type | Dominant Term | Governing Formula | Member Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small member, deep water | Inertia | $F_I \propto D^2$ | Thicker walls help more than larger diameter |
| Large member, shallow water | Drag | $F_D \propto D$ | Increase diameter |
| Combined $D/L < 0.2$ | Morison both | Sum | Standard API practice |
| Conductor / appurtenance | Drag-dominated | $C_D = 1.05$ | Marine growth critical |
| Heave plate / vertical | Buoyancy + inertia | $F = C_M \rho V \dot{u}$ | Area-based design |

## 3.5 Pile Sizing — Initial Estimate Method

For initial sizing, the pile is approximated as a **short column** carrying the maximum axial compression from the leg:

$$P_{axial} = \frac{W_{deck}}{N_{legs}} + \frac{M_{OT}}{N_{legs} \cdot D_{jacket}}$$

where:
- $W_{deck}$ = total topside weight (kN)
- $M_{OT}$ = overturning moment (kN·m)
- $N_{legs}$ = number of jacket legs (typically 4 or 8)
- $D_{jacket}$ = jacket base spread (m)

The initial pile cross-section:

$$A_{pile} = \frac{P_{axial}}{0.6\,f_y}$$

Then the required pile diameter (assuming $t = D/60$):

$$\frac{\pi}{4}\left[D^2 - (D-2t)^2\right] = A_{pile}$$

Solving iteratively:

$$\boxed{D_{pile} = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_{pile}}{\pi\left[1 - (1 - 2/60)^2\right]}} \approx \sqrt{\frac{4 A_{pile}}{0.205\,\pi}}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sizing Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Metocean Data Input] --> B[Establish Design Wave H and T]
    B --> C[Compute Wave Length L via Dispersion Relation]
    C --> D[Compute Wave Kinematics u and du/dt]
    D --> E[Determine Wave Crest Elevation eta_c]
    E --> F[Establish Minimum Deck Elevation]
    F --> G[Compute Hydrodynamic Forces via Morison Equation]
    G --> H[Size Tubular Braces D and t]
    H --> I[Size Jacket Legs for Axial plus Bending]
    I --> J[Estimate Base Shear and Overturning Moment]
    J --> K[Size Pile Diameter and Length]
    K --> L[Check D over t Ratio less than 60]
    L --> M{Meets Code Constraints?}
    M -- No --> H
    M -- Yes --> N[Lock Geometry and Proceed to Detailed Analysis]
    N --> O[End of Sizing Phase]
```

## 4.2 Jacket Platform Functional Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Topside
        T1[Deck Modules]
        T2[Helideck]
        T3[Drilling Rig]
        T4[Accommodation Block]
    end

    subgraph JacketSubstructure
        J1[Deck Legs]
        J2[Horizontal Bracings]
        J3[Diagonal Bracings]
        J4[Vertical Bracings]
        J5[Jacket Legs]
        J6[Splash Zone Region]
    end

    subgraph Foundation
        F1[Skirt Pile Sleeves]
        F2[Main Piles]
        F3[Embedment in Seabed]
    end

    subgraph Environment
        E1[Wind above MSL]
        E2[Wave plus Current at MSL]
        E3[Hydrodynamic Forces on Members]
    end

    E1 --> T1
    E2 --> J6
    E3 --> J3
    T1 --> J1
    J1 --> J5
    J5 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
```

## 4.3 Hydrodynamic Force Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incoming Wave] --> B[Member Geometry D over L less than 0.2?]
    B -- Yes --> C[Slender Member Regime]
    B -- No --> D[Large Member Regime]

    C --> E[Morisons Equation]
    E --> F1[Drag Force proportional to D]
    E --> F2[Inertia Force proportional to D squared]

    D --> G[Diffraction Theory]
    G --> H1[MacCamy-Fuchs Correction]
    G --> H2[Slater and Chapman approach]

    F1 --> I[Total Force Vector Sum]
    F2 --> I
    H1 --> I
    H2 --> I

    I --> J[Base Shear plus Overturning]
    J --> K[Pile plus Leg Sizing]
```

## 4.4 Member Sizing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Member to Size] --> Q1[Determine Loading Type]
    Q1 --> R1[Axial Only]
    Q1 --> R2[Bending Only]
    Q1 --> R3[Combined]

    R1 --> S1[Assume Slenderness KL over r equals 80]
    R1 --> S2[Compute Required Radius of Gyration]
    R1 --> S3[Choose D from r equals D over 4]
    R1 --> S4[Choose t from D over t equals 60]

    R2 --> T1[Compute Section Modulus Z]
    R2 --> T2[Choose D and t from Z equals pi D cubed over 32 minus d cubed]

    R3 --> U1[Interaction Equation Check]
    U1 --> U2{f over F plus M over M_max less than 1?}
    U2 -- No --> U3[Increase D]
    U2 -- Yes --> U4[Member Finalised]

    S4 --> Final[Verify Hydrodynamic Force Capacity]
    T2 --> Final
    U4 --> Final
    Final --> End[Member Locked for Detailed Analysis]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU Students:** Each Mermaid block represents a *functional flow*, not a literal physical drawing. In the examination hall, you would sketch a side elevation of the platform showing wave crest, deck elevation, jacket bracing, and pile embedment. The functional flow here helps internalise the sizing sequence — which is what examiners test.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q:** Define *sizing* in the context of offshore fixed platform design. Why is it performed before detailed structural analysis?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Sizing is the preliminary design stage where geometric and structural dimensions of an offshore platform (jacket members, legs, deck elevation, piles) are established using simplified hand calculations, empirical rules, and code-based preliminary charts, prior to rigorous computer-aided analysis.

**Why it is performed first (2 marks):**
1. To obtain a *feasible geometry* that satisfies strength, stability, and serviceability at the conceptual level.
2. To provide realistic input (member sizes, weights, base reactions) for subsequent detailed in-place, fatigue, and dynamic analyses.
3. To enable **cost estimation** and **fabrication feasibility checks** at an early project stage, avoiding expensive re-work later.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Q:** State the Morison equation for wave force on a slender vertical cylinder and explain the significance of each term.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Equation (2 marks):**

$$f = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_w\,C_D\,D\,u\,\vert u\vert + \rho_w\,C_M\,\tfrac{\pi D^2}{4}\,\dot{u}$$

**Significance (1 mark):**
- **First term (drag):** Viscous flow separation effect; proportional to $D$; dominant in shallow water / large members.
- **Second term (inertia):** Pressure-gradient / added-mass effect; proportional to $D^2$; dominant in deep water / small members.
- $C_D$ and $C_M$ are empirical coefficients depending on Reynolds number, Keulegan-Carpenter number, surface roughness, and marine growth.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A fixed jacket platform is to be designed for $d = 60$ m water depth in the Arabian Sea. The 100-year design wave has $H = 10$ m and $T = 9$ s. Using linear wave theory, determine:
- (i) the wavelength $L$ and wave number $k$, **(3 marks)**
- (ii) the maximum wave crest elevation $\eta_c$ above SWL, **(2 marks)**
- (iii) the maximum horizontal water particle velocity and acceleration at the SWL. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** A vertical bracing member of diameter $D = 0.6$ m is located at the SWL. Compute the maximum drag and inertia wave forces per unit length using API RP 2A coefficients. Comment on which term dominates. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure for **sizing a fixed offshore jacket platform**, from metocean data collection to pile sizing. List the key formulas used at each step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a platform with the following data, perform a preliminary sizing of the jacket legs and piles:
- Water depth $d = 90$ m
- Topside weight $W = 80,000$ kN
- Overturning moment from environmental loads $M_{OT} = 600,000$ kN·m
- Number of legs $N = 4$
- Jacket base spread $D_j = 30$ m
- Steel yield strength $f_y = 350$ MPa

Determine:
- (i) Maximum axial compression in a leg **(3 marks)**
- (ii) Initial pile cross-sectional area **(2 marks)**
- (iii) Pile diameter assuming $D/t = 60$ **(2 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A

**(a)(i) — Wave Length:**
Dispersion relation: $\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kd)$ with $\omega = 2\pi/T = 0.698$ rad/s.

Using deep-water approximation: $L_0 = gT^2/(2\pi) = 9.81 \times 81 / (2\pi) = 126.5$ m.

Check: $kd = 2\pi \times 60 / 126.5 = 2.98$. Since $\tanh(2.98) \approx 0.995 \approx 1$, deep-water is acceptable.

$$\boxed{L = 126.5 \text{ m}, \quad k = 0.0497 \text{ rad/m}}$$

**(a)(ii) — Crest Elevation:**
At SWL ($z = 0$): $\eta_c = H/2 = 5$ m.

**(a)(iii) — Velocity and Acceleration at SWL:**
$$u_{max} = \frac{\pi H}{T}\cdot\frac{\cosh(kd)}{\sinh(kd)} = \frac{\pi H}{T}\cdot\coth(kd)$$

With $\coth(2.98) \approx 1.005$:

$$u_{max} = \frac{3.1416 \times 10}{9} \times 1.005 = 3.51 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\dot{u}_{max} = \frac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2}\cdot\coth(kd) = \frac{2 \times 9.87 \times 10}{81} \times 1.005 = 2.45 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**(b) — Forces on Member:**

Drag:
$$F_D = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.65 \times 0.6 \times (3.51)^2 = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.65 \times 0.6 \times 12.32 = 2463 \text{ N/m} \approx 2.46 \text{ kN/m}$$

Inertia:
$$F_I = 1025 \times 2.0 \times \frac{\pi \times 0.36}{4} \times 2.45 = 1025 \times 2.0 \times 0.2827 \times 2.45 = 1419 \text{ N/m} \approx 1.42 \text{ kN/m}$$

Total: $F_{total} \approx 2.46 + 1.42 = 3.88$ kN/m.

**Comment:** The **drag term dominates** (≈ 63% of total) because the member is at SWL (where velocities are highest) and the deep-water assumption gives large $u_{max}$.

$$\boxed{F_D = 2.46 \text{ kN/m}, \quad F_I = 1.42 \text{ kN/m}, \quad F_{total} = 3.88 \text{ kN/m}}$$

---

### Solution to Question B

**(a) — Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Collect metocean data:** 100-year wave height $H$, period $T$, water depth $d$, current profile $V_c(z)$, wind speed $V_w$.
2. **Establish wave kinematics:** Solve dispersion, compute $u(z,t)$ and $\dot{u}(z,t)$.
3. **Determine wave crest elevation:** $\eta_c = (H/2)\cosh(k(d+z))/\cosh(kd)$.
4. **Set minimum deck elevation:** $E_{deck} = \eta_c + \Delta_{surge} + \Delta_{run-up} + S_{air-gap}$ (API air gap ≥ 1.5 m).
5. **Compute hydrodynamic forces** via Morison's equation.
6. **Size bracing members:** Choose $D/t \le 300$ for braces; verify capacity vs. combined axial + bending.
7. **Size legs:** Legs must carry total vertical load + share of overturning moment.
8. **Compute base shear and overturning:** Aggregate forces from all members.
9. **Size piles:** Axial capacity from $f_s$ (skin friction) + $f_p$ (end bearing); lateral capacity from $p-y$ curves (preliminary $L/D \approx 30$).
10. **Iterate:** Adjust member sizes until D/t ratios and utilization ratios satisfy code limits.

**(b)(i) — Maximum leg compression:**
$$P_{axial} = \frac{W}{N} + \frac{M_{OT}}{N \cdot D_j} = \frac{80{,}000}{4} + \frac{600{,}000}{4 \times 30}$$
$$P_{axial} = 20{,}000 + 5{,}000 = 25{,}000 \text{ kN}$$

**(b)(ii) — Pile cross-sectional area:**
$$A_{pile} = \frac{P_{axial}}{0.6 \times f_y} = \frac{25{,}000 \times 10^3}{0.6 \times 350 \times 10^6} = \frac{25 \times 10^6}{210 \times 10^6} = 0.119 \text{ m}^2$$

**(b)(iii) — Pile diameter:**
With $D/t = 60$, $t = D/60$:

$$\frac{\pi}{4}\left[D^2 - (D - D/30)^2\right] = A_{pile}$$

$$\frac{\pi D^2}{4}\left[1 - (29/30)^2\right] = 0.119$$

$$1 - 0.9344 = 0.0656$$

$$D^2 = \frac{4 \times 0.119}{\pi \times 0.0656} = \frac{0.476}{0.2061} = 2.310$$

$$D_{pile} = \sqrt{2.310} = 1.52 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{D_{pile} \approx 1.52 \text{ m} \quad ; \quad t_{pile} = D/60 = 25.3 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Students frequently mix kN with N, or cm with m. **Always** state units at every step.
> 2. **Sign errors in Morison's drag term:** The drag force uses $u \cdot \vert u \vert$ (not $u^2$) — this preserves direction.
> 3. **Using deep-water formula in shallow water:** The full hyperbolic expressions **must** be used for $d/L < 0.5$. A common error is to assume deep water at $d = 80$ m with a 15 s wave — but here $L_0 = 351$ m, so $d/L_0 = 0.23$, **definitely not** deep water.
> 4. **Ignoring marine growth:** It increases effective $D$ by 50–150 mm and changes $C_D$ from 0.65 to ~1.0. Many students lose 2 marks here.
> 5. **Deck elevation without air gap:** Always add the 1.5 m safety air gap; failing this is a frequent 1-mark loss.
> 6. **Using $0.5\,H$ for crest in shallow water:** Only valid for deep water. Use the full $\cosh/\cosh$ expression for intermediate depths.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Topic Recap — Sizing Considerations**

- [x] **Sizing is preliminary**, not detailed analysis — it sets the *ballpark geometry* before computer modelling.
- [x] **API RP 2A** is the dominant code; key defaults are $C_D = 0.65$, $C_M = 2.0$ (rough).
- [x] **Wave length** must be found from the dispersion relation $\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kd)$ — **iteration** may be required.
- [x] **Crest elevation** uses Airy theory; for deep water, $\eta_c = H/2$; for finite depth, use full hyperbolic form.
- [x] **Deck elevation** = crest + surge + run-up + **1.5 m air gap** (API minimum).
- [x] **Morison's equation** applies for **slender members** ($D/L < 0.2$). Drag $\propto D$; Inertia $\propto D^2$.
- [x] **D/t limits:** ≤ 60 for legs/piles, ≤ 300 for braces (per API).
- [x] **Pile sizing** starts from axial capacity; $A_{pile} = P/(0.6 f_y)$; solve for $D$ with assumed $D/t$.
- [x] **Marine growth** (50–150 mm) and **corrosion allowance** (3–12 mm) **must be added** to wall thickness.
- [x] **Iterate** between sizing and force computation until member dimensions stabilize.
- [x] **Wave + current** combined velocity is vectorially added: $u_{tot} = u_{wave} + u_{current}$.
- [x] **Topside weight** drives leg sizing; **environmental loads** drive overturning and pile sizing.
- [x] In KTU problems, **always show dispersion-relation iterations** unless the question explicitly states the wavelength.
- [x] **Units**: $H$ in m, $T$ in s, $D$ in m, $\rho_w$ = **1025 kg/m³**, $g$ = **9.81 m/s²** — convert outputs to kN for leg/pile sizing.
- [x] **Final Answer Format:** Always box numerical answers and clearly state the engineering implication (which term dominates, which member is critical, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Concepts for Foundation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Concepts for Foundation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Offshore Structures** (PCNST602, KTU 2024 Scheme), **Design Concepts for Foundation** refers to the comprehensive engineering methodology employed to transfer all superstructure loads — including dead loads, live loads, environmental loads (wind, wave, current, seismic), and operational loads — safely into the seabed soil strata without inducing structural failure, excessive settlement, or seabed instability.

The offshore foundation is the critical structural interface between the **substructure** (jacket, monopile, GBS, semi-submersible, TLPs, SPARs, FPSOs) and the **marine geotechnical stratum**. Design must satisfy **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)**, **Serviceability Limit State (SLS)**, **Fatigue Limit State (FLS)**, and **Accidental Limit State (ALS)** as per API RP 2A-WSD, API RP 2GEO, ISO 19900 series, and DNV-ST-0126.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Foundation design for offshore platforms is fundamentally a **soil-structure interaction (SSI)** problem. The design philosophy differs markedly from onshore foundations because of:
> 1. Cyclic wave loading (10⁶ to 10⁸ cycles/year)
> 2. Dynamic amplification
> 3. Scour and seabed mobility
> 4. Installation constraints (deep water, remote location)
> 5. Corrosive marine environment

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Anchor of a Ship**
> Imagine a massive cruise ship floating on the ocean. The ship itself is the superstructure. The seabed is the firm earth. Now, how do you hold this ship in place against waves, wind, and currents? You drop a **massive anchor connected by a chain**. The chain can pull at an angle, can absorb shocks, and transfers the load gradually into the seabed.
>
> An **offshore foundation** is exactly this "anchor system" — but engineered with extreme precision. Instead of a single anchor, we use **dozens of steel piles driven deep into the seabed**, each acting like a vertical anchor, holding the entire jacket or platform rigidly in place.

### 1.3 Classification of Offshore Foundations (Hierarchy)

Offshore foundations are broadly classified into two main categories:

| Category | Examples | Typical Water Depth | Working Principle |
|----------|----------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Shallow Foundations** | Gravity Base Structures (GBS), Mudmats, Suction Caissons (in shallow water) | 0 – 100 m | Transfer load via large bearing area on seabed |
| **Deep Foundations** | Driven steel piles, Drilled & grouted piles, Suction piles, Jack-up spudcans | 100 – 3000+ m | Transfer load via shaft friction + tip bearing at depth |

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Unit weight of seawater:** $\gamma_w = 10.05$ **kN/m³** (approx. 64.0 lb/ft³)
> - **Hydrostatic pressure gradient:** 0.101 **MPa per 10 m** of water depth (≈ 1 bar / 10 m)
> - **Standard steel pile density:** $\rho_{steel} = 7850$ **kg/m³**
> - **Submerged unit weight of soil:** $\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$
> - **API steel yield strength for piles:** $f_y = 345$ **MPa** (X65 grade, common in offshore)
> - **Cyclic load factor range:** 10⁶ to 10⁸ cycles over 25-year design life

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The design of an offshore foundation follows a structured **6-step logical framework**:

### Step 1: Define Design Basis
- Platform type, water depth, environmental conditions
- Design life (typically **25 years** for Class A platforms per API)
- Return period for metocean loads (**100-year storm**)
- Operational and accidental loads

### Step 2: Soil Investigation
- **Geophysical surveys** (multibeam bathymetry, side-scan sonar, sub-bottom profiling)
- **Geotechnical boreholes** with sampling (typically 3 to 5 boreholes per platform)
- Laboratory tests: **UU, CU, CD triaxial**, oedometer, cyclic simple shear
- In-situ tests: **CPTu (Piezocone)**, T-bar, VST (Vane Shear Test)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Soil Investigation Matters:**
> Soil is the most variable and least predictable element. The famous geotechnical maxim — *"You only investigate the soil where you bore; everywhere else, you guess"* — drives the need for extensive offshore site investigation.

### Step 3: Load Determination
Loads are categorized as:
- **Permanent (G):** Weight of structure, ballast, equipment
- **Variable/Functional (Q):** Drilling, production, helideck operations
- **Environmental (E):** Wave, wind, current, earthquake, ice
- **Accidental (A):** Collision, fire, dropped objects

### Step 4: Geotechnical Capacity Calculation
The foundation must resist:
- **Axial compression** (vertical downward)
- **Axial tension** (uplift)
- **Lateral (horizontal) loads**
- **Moment** (overturning)
- **Cyclic degradation**

### Step 5: Structural Design of Pile Section
- Check **yield**, **buckling**, **fatigue**, **corrosion allowance**
- Verify **drivability** (hammer selection, soil resistance to driving)

### Step 6: Installation Plan & Monitoring
- Pile driving schedule, hammer selection
- Follow-up surveys, scour monitoring

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 board-exam relevant. Memorize symbols, units, and limitations.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Applicability |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | $Q_d = Q_s + Q_p = f_s \cdot A_s + q_p \cdot A_p$ | Total axial pile capacity (static) | kN | Driven/drilled piles in clay or sand |
| 2 | $Q_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c_u} \cdot A_s$ | Skin friction (clay, alpha method, API) | kN | Cohesive soils |
| 3 | $Q_s = K \cdot \bar{\sigma_v}' \cdot \tan\delta \cdot A_s$ | Skin friction (sand, beta method) | kN | Cohesionless soils |
| 4 | $Q_p = 9 \cdot c_u \cdot A_p$ | End bearing (clay, API Skempton) | kN | Saturated clay |
| 5 | $Q_p = \bar{\sigma_v}' \cdot N_q \cdot A_p$ | End bearing (sand, Berezantzev) | kN | Cohesionless soils |
| 6 | $Q_t = W_s + W_p$ | Pile weight (uplift check) | kN | Tension capacity |
| 7 | $Q_{t,allow} = \dfrac{Q_s}{FS}$ | Allowable tension capacity | kN | FS ≥ 2.0 (API) |
| 8 | $q_{ult} = 0.5 \cdot \gamma' \cdot B \cdot N_\gamma + c \cdot N_c + q \cdot N_q$ | Bearing capacity (Terzaghi) | kPa | Shallow foundations |
| 9 | $p_y = 3 \cdot c_u \cdot D + \gamma' \cdot z \cdot D + 0.5 \cdot \gamma' \cdot D \cdot D$ | Laterally loaded pile ultimate resistance (soft clay, Matlock-Reese) | kN/m | Cohesive |
| 10 | $S_{e,max} = \dfrac{M \cdot L^2}{10 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | Max lateral deflection at mudline (approx.) | m | Serviceability check |
| 11 | $\sigma_{steel} = \dfrac{P}{A} + \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I} \leq 0.6 f_y$ | Combined axial + bending check | MPa | Pile structural check |
| 12 | $\Delta D_{corr} = 0.5 \text{ to } 1.0 \text{ mm/year}$ | Corrosion allowance rate | mm/yr | Marine splash zone |
| 13 | $S/d \geq 2.5$ | Minimum pile spacing (center-to-center) | — | Group efficiency |
| 14 | $F_{buckle} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{(KL)^2}$ | Euler's buckling load | kN | Long slender piles |
| 15 | $N_{q,sand} \approx 50 \tan\phi'$ | API-recommended $N_q$ for driven piles in sand | — | API RP 2A |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Reality Check:**
> The API alpha-method for clays assumes **$\alpha$ ranges from 0.5 to 1.0** depending on $c_u$. For soft clays, $\alpha \approx 1.0$; for stiff clays, $\alpha$ decreases. KTU examiners frequently test this numerical nuance.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This design philosophy is applied in:
- **Oil & Gas Platforms** (Gulf of Mexico, North Sea, Arabian Sea)
- **Offshore Wind Farms** (monopiles, jackets, floating wind)
- **Submarine Cable Landfalls**
- **Bridge Piers in Deep Water**
- **Port and Harbor Structures** (quay walls, breakwaters)
- **LNG Terminals & FPSO moorings**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Axial Pile Capacity in Cohesive Soil (API Alpha Method)

The static axial capacity $Q_d$ of a single driven pile is the sum of **skin friction (shaft resistance)** and **end bearing (tip resistance)**.

$$Q_d = Q_s + Q_p$$

### Step A: Skin Friction in Clay (Alpha Method)

The API RP 2A-WSD formulation for shaft resistance in clay is:

$$Q_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c_u} \cdot A_s$$

where:
- $\alpha = $ adhesion factor (dimensionless)
- $\bar{c_u} = $ average undrained shear strength along pile length (kPa)
- $A_s = $ surface area of pile embedded in clay ($\text{m}^2$)

**Determination of $\alpha$ (API method):**

$$\alpha = \begin{cases} 1.0 & \text{if } \dfrac{c_u}{\sigma_v'} \leq 0.3 \\[4pt] 0.5 & \text{if } \dfrac{c_u}{\sigma_v'} \geq 1.0 \end{cases}$$

**Linear interpolation in between:**

$$\alpha = 1.0 - 0.5 \cdot \left( \dfrac{c_u / \sigma_v' - 0.3}{0.7} \right)$$

### Step B: End Bearing in Clay (Skempton's Equation)

For piles driven into saturated clay:

$$Q_p = 9 \cdot c_u(tip) \cdot A_p$$

where:
- $c_u(tip) = $ undrained shear strength at pile tip elevation
- $A_p = $ pile tip cross-sectional area

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the factor 9?**
> Skempton's 1951 tests on driven piles in London Clay showed the end bearing factor $N_c$ varies with depth-to-diameter ratio $L/D$. For $L/D \geq 4$, $N_c$ asymptotes to **9**. This is empirically calibrated, NOT a theoretical value.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style):

**Given:**
- Pile diameter $D = 1.5$ m, embedded length $L = 40$ m
- Soil profile: Soft to firm clay with $\bar{c_u} = 60$ kPa, $c_u(tip) = 80$ kPa
- Vertical effective stress at pile tip $\sigma_v' = 280$ kPa
- Steel pipe pile, closed-end

**Step 1: Compute Geometric Properties**

$$A_s = \pi \cdot D \cdot L = \pi \cdot 1.5 \cdot 40 = 188.5 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_p = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (1.5)^2}{4} = 1.767 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate $\alpha$**

$$\dfrac{c_u}{\sigma_v'} = \dfrac{60}{280} = 0.214 < 0.3 \implies \alpha = 1.0$$

**Step 3: Skin Friction**

$$Q_s = 1.0 \cdot 60 \cdot 188.5 = 11{,}310 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: End Bearing**

$$Q_p = 9 \cdot 80 \cdot 1.767 = 1272.24 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Total Axial Capacity**

$$Q_d = 11{,}310 + 1272.24 = 12{,}582.24 \text{ kN} \approx 12{,}582 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Apply Factor of Safety (FS = 2.0 for compression per API)**

$$Q_{allowable} = \dfrac{12{,}582}{2.0} = 6291 \text{ kN}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Lateral Pile Capacity (p-y Curve Concept)

For piles subjected to lateral loads (wave-induced), the soil reaction is modeled via **p-y curves**, where:
- $p = $ soil reaction per unit length (kN/m)
- $y = $ lateral deflection (m)

### For Soft Clay (Matlock-Reese, 1960):

The ultimate lateral resistance $p_u$ at depth $z$ is given by:

$$p_u = \begin{cases} 3 c_u D + \gamma' z D + 0.5 J \dfrac{c_u D z}{D} & \text{shallow depth} \\[4pt] 9 c_u D & \text{deep depth} \end{cases}$$

The transition depth is:

$$z_t = \dfrac{6 c_u D}{\gamma' D + J c_u}$$

where $J$ = empirical coefficient (≈ 0.5 for soft clay).

**Deflection at 50% of ultimate resistance:**

$$y_{50} = 2.5 \epsilon_{50} D$$

where $\epsilon_{50}$ is the strain at 50% stress in a laboratory UU test (typically 0.005 to 0.02).

### Step-by-Step Solution for Laterally Loaded Pile:

**Given:**
- Pile: $D = 2.0$ m, $L = 60$ m, $EI = 5 \times 10^6$ kN·m²
- Soil: Soft clay, $\gamma' = 8$ kN/m³, $c_u = 30$ kPa (top 20 m), $\epsilon_{50} = 0.01$
- Horizontal load $H = 500$ kN at pile head, free head condition

**Step 1: Compute $y_{50}$**

$$y_{50} = 2.5 \cdot 0.01 \cdot 2.0 = 0.05 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: At depth $z = 5$ m, compute $p_u$**

$$p_u = 3 \cdot 30 \cdot 2.0 + 8 \cdot 5 \cdot 2.0 = 180 + 80 = 260 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 3: Stiffness coefficient**

$$E_{py} = \dfrac{p_u}{y_{50}} = \dfrac{260}{0.05} = 5200 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Characteristic length (Reese & Matlock)**

$$T = \left( \dfrac{EI}{E_{py}} \right)^{1/5} = \left( \dfrac{5 \times 10^6}{5200} \right)^{1/5}$$

$$\ln\left( \dfrac{5 \times 10^6}{5200} \right) = \ln(961.5) = 6.868$$

$$T = (961.5)^{0.2} = e^{6.868/5} = e^{1.374} = 3.95 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Dimensionless load and deflection**

$$\dfrac{y_{ground}}{T} = f\left( \dfrac{HT^3}{EI} \right)$$

This requires iteration using **COM624** or **LPILE** software. For board exams, use Broms' simplified method (1964).

---

## 3.3 Broms' Simplified Method (Board-Exam Favorite)

### Short Rigid Pile ($\beta L < 2.25$, clay):

$$\dfrac{H_u}{c_u D^2} = \beta L + 2$$

where $\beta = \dfrac{M_u}{c_u D^3}$ and $M_u = 2.5 c_u D^3$ for fixed-head.

### Long Flexible Pile ($\beta L > 2.25$, clay):

$$H_u = \dfrac{M_u}{(1.5 D + 0.5 f)}$$

where $f = $ length to point of max moment below ground.

### Analytical Matrix for Quick Reference:

| Loading Type | Short Rigid Pile | Long Flexible Pile |
|-------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Free Head, Lateral** | $H_u = 0.5 c_u D L^2 / (e + L)$ | $H_u = 2 c_u D (L - 1.5D)$ |
| **Fixed Head, Lateral** | $H_u = c_u D L$ | $H_u = 2 c_u D (L - 1.5D)$ |
| **Free Head, Moment** | $M_u = c_u D L^2$ | $M_u = 2.25 c_u D^2 (L - 1.5D)$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Offshore Foundation Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Project Definition]:::startNode
    nodeB[Metocean Data]:::inputNode
    nodeC[Soil Investigation]:::inputNode
    nodeD[Load Determination]:::processNode
    nodeE[Foundation Selection]:::decisionNode
    nodeF[Pile Foundation]:::foundationNode
    nodeG[Gravity Base]:::foundationNode
    nodeH[Suction Caisson]:::foundationNode
    nodeI[Geotechnical Capacity Check]:::verifyNode
    nodeJ[Structural Capacity Check]:::verifyNode
    nodeK[Installation Planning]:::processNode
    nodeL[Monitoring & Maintenance]:::endNode

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE -->|Deep water > 100m| nodeF
    nodeE -->|Shallow water < 100m| nodeG
    nodeE -->|Soft clay seabed| nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL

    classDef startNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef inputNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef processNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef decisionNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef foundationNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef verifyNode fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef endNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

## 4.2 Pile-Soil Interaction Schematic (p-y Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeLoad[Wave & Wind Load H]:::loadNode
    nodePile[Pile Head Free or Fixed]:::structNode
    nodeDef[Deflection y]:::responseNode
    nodePy[Soil Reaction p per unit length]:::responseNode
    nodeDepth[Increasing Depth z]:::axisNode
    nodeTip[Pile Tip Bearing Resistance Qp]:::tipNode

    nodeLoad --> nodePile
    nodePile --> nodeDef
    nodeDef --> nodePy
    nodePy --> nodeDepth
    nodeDepth --> nodeTip

    classDef loadNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef structNode fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef responseNode fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef axisNode fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef tipNode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Foundation Type Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Begin Selection]:::startNode
    nodeB{Water Depth?}:::decisionNode
    nodeC{Soil Type?}:::decisionNode
    nodeD{Load Magnitude?}:::decisionNode
    nodeE[Monopile or Jacket with Driven Piles]:::recNode
    nodeF[Gravity Base Structure]:::recNode
    nodeG[Suction Caisson]:::recNode
    nodeH[Drilled and Grouted Piles]:::recNode
    nodeI[Anchored Suction Piles]:::recNode

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -->|< 50 m| nodeF
    nodeB -->|50 to 300 m| nodeC
    nodeB -->|> 300 m Floating| nodeI
    nodeC -->|Soft clay| nodeG
    nodeC -->|Dense sand| nodeE
    nodeC -->|Rock or hard stratum| nodeH
    nodeC -->|Mixed profile| nodeD
    nodeD -->|Very heavy| nodeE
    nodeD -->|Moderate| nodeH

    classDef startNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decisionNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef recNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Typical Pile Group Layout (Schematic Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pile 1]:::pileNode
    B[Pile 2]:::pileNode
    C[Pile 3]:::pileNode
    D[Pile 4]:::pileNode
    E[Pile 5]:::pileNode
    F[Pile 6]:::pileNode
    G[Pile 7]:::pileNode
    H[Pile 8]:::pileNode
    J[Conductor Pile C]:::centerNode

    A --- B
    B --- C
    C --- J
    J --- D
    D --- A
    A --- E
    E --- F
    F --- J
    J --- G
    G --- H
    H --- D

    classDef pileNode fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef centerNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrammatic Interpretation:**
> - **Outer ring (Piles 1–8):** Primary load-bearing skirt piles at jacket legs.
> - **Center (Conductor Pile C):** Used for drilling/production tubing. Spacing $S/d \geq 2.5$ as per API RP 2A to avoid group efficiency losses.

---
