# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** 
Differentiate between a **shallow foundation** and a **deep foundation** for offshore structures. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Shallow Foundation | Deep Foundation |
|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Embedment depth | < 5× width | > 5× width |
| Load transfer | Bearing on seabed | Skin friction + tip bearing |
| Typical type | Gravity Base Structure (GBS) | Driven steel pile |
| Water depth | 0 – 100 m | 100 – 3000+ m |
| Example | Ekofisk Tank (North Sea) | Bullwinkle Platform (GoM) |

**(1 mark each for definition, water depth, and example)** ✓

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
What is the **API alpha method** for computing pile skin friction in clay?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The API alpha method is an empirical technique to compute the **unit skin friction** ($f_s$) on a driven pile in cohesive soil using the equation:

$$f_s = \alpha \cdot c_u$$

where $\alpha$ is the **adhesion factor** depending on the ratio $c_u / \sigma_v'$:
- $\alpha = 1.0$ for $c_u / \sigma_v' \leq 0.3$
- $\alpha = 0.5$ for $c_u / \sigma_v' \geq 1.0$
- Linear interpolation between these limits

**(1 mark for equation, 1 mark for alpha range, 1 mark for rationale)** ✓

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**A.** With a neat sketch, explain the **different types of offshore foundations** used for fixed jacket platforms. Discuss the criteria for selecting a suitable foundation type. **(7 marks)**

**B.** A driven steel pipe pile of diameter **1.2 m** is embedded **35 m** into a soft clay layer. The average undrained shear strength along the shaft is $\bar{c_u} = 50$ kPa, and at the pile tip $c_u(tip) = 90$ kPa. The effective vertical stress at pile tip is 250 kPa. Compute:
- (i) The **alpha adhesion factor** using API method
- (ii) The **skin friction capacity** $Q_s$
- (iii) The **end bearing capacity** $Q_p$
- (iv) The **total allowable axial capacity** with Factor of Safety = 2.5 **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

**A. Types of Offshore Foundations for Fixed Jacket Platforms:**

1. **Driven Steel Piles** — most common. Hollow steel pipes, 1–3 m diameter, driven into seabed using hydraulic hammers. Typical length 30–120 m. Used at jacket leg corners.

2. **Drilled and Grouted Piles** — drilled into rock or hard strata where driving is impossible. Steel casing inserted, annulus grouted.

3. **Suction Caissons (Skirted Foundations)** — large-diameter hollow cylinders (5–30 m) open at bottom. Suction applied inside to draw them into seabed. Excellent for soft clay.

4. **Gravity Base Structures (GBS)** — massive concrete or steel tanks resting on seabed. Weight provides stability against overturning. Limited to shallow water.

5. **Belled Piles / Bored Piles** — used in calcareous soils where shaft friction is unreliable.

**Selection Criteria:**

| Criterion | Influence |
|-----------|-----------|
| **Water depth** | < 100 m → GBS feasible; > 100 m → piles |
| **Soil profile** | Rock → drilled; clay → driven or suction |
| **Load magnitude** | Heavy → multiple driven piles |
| **Scour potential** | Requires deeper embedment or scour protection |
| **Installation equipment** | Hammer availability, drill rig |
| **Environmental loads** | Cyclic → fatigue design becomes critical |
| **Cost and schedule** | Piles usually preferred for deep water |

**(2 marks diagram, 2 marks types, 2 marks criteria, 1 mark conclusion)** = 7 marks ✓

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

**Given:**
- $D = 1.2$ m, $L = 35$ m, $\bar{c_u} = 50$ kPa, $c_u(tip) = 90$ kPa, $\sigma_v' = 250$ kPa

**Step (i): Alpha Factor**

$$\dfrac{c_u}{\sigma_v'} = \dfrac{50}{250} = 0.20 < 0.3$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = 1.0}$$ **(1.5 marks)**

**Step (ii): Skin Friction**

$$A_s = \pi \cdot D \cdot L = \pi \cdot 1.2 \cdot 35 = 131.95 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c_u} \cdot A_s = 1.0 \cdot 50 \cdot 131.95$$

$$\boxed{Q_s = 6597.3 \text{ kN} \approx 6597 \text{ kN}}$$ **(1.5 marks)**

**Step (iii): End Bearing**

$$A_p = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (1.2)^2}{4} = 1.131 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_p = 9 \cdot c_u(tip) \cdot A_p = 9 \cdot 90 \cdot 1.131$$

$$\boxed{Q_p = 916.1 \text{ kN}}$$ **(1.5 marks)**

**Step (iv): Allowable Capacity**

$$Q_d = Q_s + Q_p = 6597.3 + 916.1 = 7513.4 \text{ kN}$$

$$Q_{allowable} = \dfrac{Q_d}{FS} = \dfrac{7513.4}{2.5}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{allowable} = 3005.4 \text{ kN}}$$ **(2.5 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**A.** Explain the **p-y curve method** for analyzing laterally loaded piles in soft clay. Derive the expressions for ultimate soil resistance $p_u$ and characteristic length $T$. **(7 marks)**

**B.** A monopile of diameter $D = 6$ m and embedded length $L = 30$ m is subjected to a horizontal load of $H = 5$ MN at seabed level in soft clay with $c_u = 40$ kPa and $\gamma' = 8$ kN/m³. Using **Broms' method**, check if the pile is **short rigid or long flexible**. Assume free-head condition. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B (Alternative):**

**A. p-y Curve Method:**

The p-y curve method models lateral pile-soil interaction as a **series of non-linear springs** along the pile length, where:
- $p$ = soil reaction force per unit length (kN/m)
- $y$ = local lateral deflection (m)

These curves are generated at discrete depths using empirically derived equations (Matlock-Reese for clay, Reese et al. for sand).

**For Soft Clay (Matlock, 1970):**

$$p_u = 3 c_u D + \gamma' z D + J c_u z$$

Transition depth: $z_t = \dfrac{6 c_u D}{\gamma' D + J c_u}$, beyond which $p_u = 9 c_u D$.

Characteristic length: $T = \left( \dfrac{EI}{E_{py}} \right)^{1/5}$, where $E_{py} = p_u / y_{50}$.

**(3 marks for concept, 2 marks for $p_u$, 2 marks for $T$)** = 7 marks ✓

---

**B. Broms' Method Check:**

Given: $D = 6$ m, $L = 30$ m, $H = 5$ MN, $c_u = 40$ kPa, $\gamma' = 8$ kN/m³, free-head.

**Step 1: Compute $\beta$ (Broms' coefficient)**

$$\beta = \left( \dfrac{k_h D}{4 EI} \right)^{1/5} \approx \left( \dfrac{67 c_u}{EI} \right)^{1/5}$$

Assume $EI = 2 \times 10^8$ kN·m² (typical for large monopile):

$$\beta L = \left( \dfrac{67 \cdot 40}{2 \times 10^8} \right)^{0.2} \cdot 30$$

$$\beta = \left( 1.34 \times 10^{-5} \right)^{0.2} = 0.1654 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

$$\beta L = 0.1654 \cdot 30 = 4.96$$

**Step 2: Check Rigid vs Flexible**

Since $\beta L = 4.96 > 2.25$, the pile is **LONG FLEXIBLE**. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3: Ultimate Lateral Capacity (Long Flexible, Free Head)**

$$H_u = 2 c_u D (L - 1.5 D) = 2 \cdot 40 \cdot 6 \cdot (30 - 1.5 \cdot 6)$$

$$H_u = 2 \cdot 40 \cdot 6 \cdot 21 = 10{,}080 \text{ kN} = 10.08 \text{ MN}$$

**(3 marks)**

**Step 4: Factor of Safety**

$$FS = \dfrac{H_u}{H} = \dfrac{10.08}{5.0} = 2.016$$

Since FS > 2.0 (API requirement for operating conditions), the **pile design is SAFE**. **(2 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the submerged unit weight** when computing effective stress at pile tip. ($\gamma'$ = $\gamma_{sat}$ − $\gamma_w$). **−1 mark**
> 2. **Using total stress instead of effective stress** in sand skin friction calculations. **−1 mark**
> 3. **Mixing up API WSD and LRFD** in notation. Stick to ONE system in a single answer. **−1 mark**
> 4. **Forgetting to compute $A_s = \pi D L$ correctly** — many students forget it is the *embedded* length, not total pile length. **−1 mark**
> 5. **Skipping the alpha-method interpolation step** when $0.3 < c_u/\sigma_v' < 1.0$. Examiner WILL deduct. **−1.5 marks**
> 6. **Confusing Broms' "short rigid" vs "long flexible" criteria** — these are based on $\beta L$, NOT $L/D$. **−1.5 marks**
> 7. **Writing units inconsistently** — kN vs MN vs N. Always use **kN for forces, m for lengths, MPa for stresses**. **−0.5 marks**

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Design Concepts for Foundation**

- ☐ Offshore foundation design is governed by **soil-structure interaction** under **cyclic loading**.
- ☐ Two main categories: **Shallow** (GBS, mudmats) and **Deep** (piles, suction caissons).
- ☐ **Axial capacity** = Shaft friction $Q_s$ + End bearing $Q_p$.
- ☐ **API alpha method** for clays: $\alpha = 1.0$ at low $c_u/\sigma_v'$, decreasing to 0.5.
- ☐ **Skempton's end bearing factor** for driven piles in clay is **9** (for $L/D \geq 4$).
- ☐ **Beta method** for sands: $Q_s = K \sigma_v' \tan\delta \cdot A_s$.
- ☐ **p-y curves** are the standard for lateral pile analysis (Matlock-Reese for clay).
- ☐ **Broms' method** is the simplified board-exam version (use when software unavailable).
- ☐ **Pile spacing** $S/d \geq 2.5$ to avoid group efficiency reduction.
- ☐ **Corrosion allowance**: 0.5–1.0 mm/year in splash zone.
- ☐ **Pile structural checks** include axial + bending interaction: $\sigma \leq 0.6 f_y$.
- ☐ **Factor of Safety**: FS = 2.0 (axial compression), FS = 3.0 (axial tension), FS = 1.5 (lateral, operating).
- ☐ **Codes**: API RP 2A-WSD, API RP 2GEO, ISO 19900, DNV-ST-0126.
- ☐ **Limit States**: ULS (collapse), SLS (deflection), FLS (fatigue), ALS (accident).
- ☐ **Cyclic degradation** of skin friction must be checked for storm/fatigue loading.
- ☐ **Buckling** of slender piles must be checked — Euler's formula applies with effective length factor $K \geq 0.7$.
- ☐ **Scour protection** with rock dumping or mattresses is mandatory in high-current zones.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Considerations for Offshore Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Material Considerations for Offshore Structures # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Material Considerations for Offshore Structures** refer to the systematic engineering evaluation of the mechanical, chemical, and physical properties of construction materials required to withstand the harsh, combined loading conditions of the marine environment. These considerations include strength (yield and ultimate), toughness, weldability, fatigue resistance, corrosion resistance, and fracture mechanics behavior, all governed by international standards such as **API (American Petroleum Institute)**, **DNV (Det Norske Veritas)**, **ISO 19902**, and **EN 10025**. In offshore engineering, the platform, subsea pipelines, risers, and topside modules operate in an aggressive environment characterized by **saltwater immersion**, **wave action**, **wind loading**, **low temperatures**, and **cyclic dynamic stresses**. The material must simultaneously resist: - Static loads (dead weight, hydrostatic pressure) - Dynamic loads (waves, currents, seismic activity) - Environmental degradation (corrosion, biofouling, marine growth) - Temperature extremes (Arctic cold to tropical heat) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of an offshore structure's material selection like choosing the right material for a deep-sea diver's wetsuit and oxygen tank combined. The wetsuit (steel shell) must be strong enough to resist water pressure yet flexible enough to bend with currents without cracking, while the coating (cathodic protection) must continuously fight off the salt "attacks" (corrosion) — just as the diver's body must survive both pressure and cold. Imagine a **massive steel jacket platform** standing in the **North Sea** with waves 30 meters high. The steel used is not the same as the steel used to build a bridge in Kerala. The offshore steel must be: - **Stronger** (higher yield strength to reduce weight) - **Tougher** (resist brittle fracture at low temperatures ~ **-10°C to -40°C**) - **More weldable** (since platforms are fabricated by welding huge tubular sections) - **More corrosion-resistant** (or protected by coatings/cathodic systems) ## 1.3 Primary Materials Used in Offshore Structures | Material Type | Application | Key Property | |---------------|-------------|--------------| | **Structural Steel** | Jackets, decks, modules | High strength-to-weight ratio | | **High-Strength Steel** | Tension legs, moorings | Yield strength **≥ 414 MPa** | | **Marine Concrete** | Gravity-based structures (GBS) | Durability in seawater | | **Stainless Steel (Duplex)** | Piping, risers, process systems | Corrosion resistance | | **Fiber-Reinforced Polymers (FRP)** | Walkways, secondary structures | Corrosion immunity | | **Aluminum Alloys** | Helidecks, living quarters | Lightweight, corrosion resistant | | **Titanium Alloys** | Critical subsea components | High strength, seawater resistant | ## 1.4 Standard Steel Grades for Offshore Use > [!NOTE] > **Most Common Offshore Steel Grades (per API RP 2A / DNV-OS-C101):** > - **ASTM A36** — Mild structural steel (Yield = **250 MPa**) > - **ASTM A572 Gr. 50** — High-strength low-alloy (Yield = **345 MPa**) > - **API 2H Gr. 50** — Marine grade (Yield = **345 MPa**, with improved toughness) > - **API 2W Gr. 50 / 60** — Weathering offshore steel (Yield = **345–414 MPa**) > - **API 2Y Gr. 50 / 60** — Through-thickness improved steel (Yield = **345–414 MPa**) > - **EN 10025 S355 / S420 / S460** — European offshore grades # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Key Mechanical Properties Required The selection of materials for offshore applications is governed by a hierarchy of properties: ### 2.1.1 Strength Properties - **Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$)**: The stress at which the material begins to deform plastically. For offshore steel, minimum yield strength typically ranges from **250 MPa to 460 MPa**. - **Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_u$)**: The maximum stress the material can withstand. Offshore steels have $\sigma_u / \sigma_y$ ratios between **1.4 and 1.6**. - **Charpy V-Notch (CVN) Impact Energy**: Measures toughness. For North Sea applications, minimum **27 J at -40°C** is required (per API RP 2A). ### 2.1.2 Toughness and Fracture Mechanics - **Brittle Fracture Resistance**: Critical in cold environments. Governed by the **Fracture Toughness ($K_{IC}$)** parameter. - **CTOD (Crack Tip Opening Displacement)**: Used to assess weld defects. Typical acceptance: **CTOD ≥ 0.25 mm** at design temperature. ### 2.1.3 Fatigue Properties - **S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve)**: Plots stress amplitude ($S$) versus number of cycles to failure ($N$). - **Endurance Limit**: For steels, typically **$10^6$ to $10^7$ cycles** at stress amplitude = **0.4 to 0.5 × $\sigma_u$**. ### 2.1.4 Corrosion Properties - **Corrosion Rate in Seawater**: Unprotected carbon steel corrodes at **0.1 to 0.5 mm/year** in submerged zones and **0.3 to 1.0 mm/year** in splash zone. ## 2.2 Corrosion Considerations > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Three Corrosion Zones in Offshore Structures:** > 1. **Atmospheric Zone** (above splash): Protected by coatings only > 2. **Splash Zone** (most aggressive): Alternate wet/dry, requires special protection (monel sheathing, vulcanized rubber) > 3. **Submerged Zone**: Protected by coatings + cathodic protection (CP) > 4. **Mud Zone** (below seabed): Low oxygen, typically less corrosive ### Corrosion Protection Methods: - **Protective Coatings**: Epoxy, polyurethane (typical life: **15–25 years**) - **Cathodic Protection**: - *Galvanic (Sacrificial Anode)*: Zinc or aluminum anodes - *Impressed Current*: External DC power source - **Corrosion Allowance**: Extra steel thickness (typically **3–6 mm** on each face) ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All formulas below are critical for KTU university examinations. Memorize the structure and units.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Axial stress (Force / Area) | **MPa** | | 2 | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{F_y}{A}$ | Yield stress | **MPa** | | 3 | $S-N$: $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N)^b$ | Basquin's fatigue equation | **MPa** | | 4 | $\log(\sigma_a) = \log(\sigma'_f) + b \log(2N)$ | Log form of S-N curve | — | | 5 | $K_{IC} = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Stress intensity factor | **MPa·m$^{0.5}$** | | 6 | $CTOD = \dfrac{K_{IC}^2}{E \sigma_y}$ | Approximate CTOD relation | **mm** | | 7 | $CR = \dfrac{\Delta w}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Corrosion rate (mass loss) | **mm/year** | | 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{I}$ | Shear stress | **MPa** | | 9 | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$ | Von Mises equivalent stress | **MPa** | | 10 | $f_y$ design = $\dfrac{\sigma_y}{\gamma_{M0} \text{ or } \gamma_{M2}}$ | Design strength (partial safety) | **MPa** | | 11 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}$ | Elastic strain (Hooke's law) | — | | 12 | $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ MPa | Young's modulus for steel | **MPa** | | 13 | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$, $\nu = 0.3$ | Shear modulus for steel | **MPa** | | 14 | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{Mc}{I}$ | Bending stress | **MPa** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Universal Constants for Steel:** > - Young's Modulus $E$ = **$2.0 \times 10^5$ MPa** (= 200 GPa) > - Poisson's Ratio $\nu$ = **0.3** > - Density $\rho$ = **7850 kg/m$^3$** > - Shear Modulus $G$ = **76.9 GPa** ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Material selection directly determines: 1. **Platform weight** → affects transportation, installation cost 2. **Service life** → typically **20–30 years** for offshore structures 3. **Inspection intervals** → every **2–5 years** under-water inspection 4. **Failure consequence** → offshore failure can cause loss of life, environmental disaster, and economic loss of **billions of rupees** 5. **Repair feasibility** → offshore repair costs are 5–10× onshore equivalents # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Fatigue Life Using Basquin's Equation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Context:** Offshore structures experience millions of wave cycles during their design life. The Basquin equation allows us to predict the number of cycles to failure at a given stress amplitude. **Starting Point**: Experimental observation that, in the high-cycle fatigue regime ($N > 10^3$ cycles), the relationship between stress amplitude and fatigue life is approximately linear on a log-log scale. ### Step 1: Express the linear log-log relationship $$\log(\sigma_a) = \log(A) + B \log(N)$$ **Explanation:** Here $\sigma_a$ is the stress amplitude, $N$ is the number of cycles to failure, and $A$ and $B$ are material constants determined experimentally. ### Step 2: Convert from logarithmic form to exponential form Taking the antilog of both sides (10-base log): $$\sigma_a = A \cdot N^B$$ **Explanation:** This is the power-law form showing that stress amplitude decays as a power function of the number of cycles. ### Step 3: Re-express in standard Basquin form The convention in fatigue engineering is to express the equation using the **fatigue strength coefficient** ($\sigma'_f$) and the **fatigue strength exponent** ($b$): $$\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N)^b$$ **Explanation:** The factor of 2 is included because loading is fully reversed (R = -1), so stress range $\Delta\sigma = 2\sigma_a$. The exponent $b$ is typically negative (between -0.05 and -0.12 for steels), and $\sigma'_f$ is approximately equal to the true fracture strength. ### Step 4: Solve for the number of cycles to failure ($N$) Rearranging to isolate $N$: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_a &= \sigma'_f (2N)^b \\ \left(\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma'_f}\right) &= (2N)^b \\ \left(\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma'_f}\right)^{1/b} &= 2N \\ N &= \dfrac{1}{2} \left(\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma'_f}\right)^{1/b} \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation:** This final form allows direct calculation of fatigue life given the applied stress amplitude and the material's fatigue properties. For typical offshore steel: $\sigma'_f \approx 1000$ MPa, $b \approx -0.10$. ### Step 5: Verification with a numerical example For $\sigma_a = 150$ MPa, $\sigma'_f = 1000$ MPa, $b = -0.10$: $$ \begin{aligned} N &= \dfrac{1}{2} \left(\dfrac{150}{1000}\right)^{1/(-0.10)} \\ N &= \dfrac{1}{2} (0.15)^{-10} \\ N &= \dfrac{1}{2} (1.77 \times 10^8) \\ N &= 8.83 \times 10^7 \text{ cycles} \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation:** At a stress amplitude of 150 MPa, this offshore steel can withstand approximately **88 million cycles** before failure — confirming the very high fatigue resistance of structural steel. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Corrosion Rate from Mass Loss > [!IMPORTANT] > **Context:** Engineers must estimate how quickly steel corrodes in seawater to determine the **corrosion allowance** to be added to member thickness. ### Step 1: Define corrosion rate from mass loss The mass loss due to corrosion is given by Faraday's electrochemical principle, simplified to a practical engineering formula: $$CR = \dfrac{\Delta m}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t}$$ **Explanation:** $CR$ is corrosion rate (mm/year), $\Delta m$ is mass lost (mg), $\rho$ is density of steel (7.85 × 10$^6$ mg/m³ for unit conversion), $A$ is exposed surface area (m²), and $t$ is exposure time (year). ### Step 2: Rearrange to calculate corrosion allowance For a design life of $T$ years, the corrosion allowance $t_c$ is: $$t_c = CR \times T$$ **Explanation:** For unprotected carbon steel in seawater with $CR = 0.3$ mm/year and design life $T = 25$ years: $$t_c = 0.3 \times 25 = 7.5 \text{ mm}$$ So an additional **7.5 mm** thickness is added to each face of the structural member. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hot-Spot Stress for Tubular Joints > [!IMPORTANT] > **Context:** Tubular joints are the most fatigue-critical locations in offshore jackets. The **hot-spot stress** approach is the industry standard. ### Step 1: Define geometric stress concentration factor (SCF) For a tubular T-joint, the SCF at the chord brace intersection is: $$SCF = \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{nom}}$$ **Explanation:** $\sigma_{max}$ is the maximum hot-spot stress at the weld toe, and $\sigma_{nom}$ is the nominal stress in the brace member. ### Step 2: Apply effective notch stress (for fatigue assessment) The hot-spot stress range used in fatigue design is: $$\Delta\sigma_{HS} = SCF \times \Delta\sigma_{nom}$$ **Explanation:** For a typical T-joint with $\beta = d_b / d_c = 0.6$, $\gamma = r / t_c = 12$, and $\tau = t_b / t_c = 0.7$ (where $d_b, d_c$ are brace/chord diameters, $r$ is chord wall radius, $t_b, t_c$ are wall thicknesses), the SCF for axial loading can reach **8 to 12** — explaining why tubular joints are fatigue-critical. ### Step 3: Calculate fatigue life from hot-spot stress range Using the S-N curve in seawater (T curve from DNV-RP-C203): $$\log(N) = \log(K) - m \log(\Delta\sigma_{HS})$$ **Explanation:** For the T curve (in seawater with cathodic protection): $m = 3$ (slope), $K = 10^{\log(a)}$ where $\log(a) = 11.7$ for thickness $t_{ref} = 25$ mm. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Material Selection > [!NOTE] > **Use this matrix to justify material choice in exam answers:** | Design Criterion | Carbon Steel | High-Strength Steel | Duplex Stainless | Concrete | |------------------|--------------|---------------------|------------------|----------| | Initial Cost | **Low** | Medium | High | Low | | Weight | Medium | **Low** | Medium | **High** | | Corrosion Resistance | Poor (needs CP) | Poor (needs CP) | **Excellent** | Good | | Fatigue Resistance | Good | Good | Good | Poor | | Weldability | **Excellent** | Good | Difficult | N/A | | Toughness at Low T | Good (with spec) | Good | Excellent | Brittle | | Typical Use | Jackets, deck | TLP tendons | Risers, piping | GBS | --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Combined Stress Check > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam-style problem preparation:** A tubular brace in an offshore jacket carries axial force $F = 1500$ kN and bending moment $M = 250$ kN·m. The tubular section has outer diameter $D = 500$ mm and wall thickness $t = 25$ mm. Steel grade: API 5L X65 with $\sigma_y = 450$ MPa. Check the adequacy of the section. **Step 1: Section Properties** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left[ D^2 - (D-2t)^2 \right] \\ A &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left[ 500^2 - 450^2 \right] \\ A &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left[ 250000 - 202500 \right] \\ A &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 47500 \\ A &= 37306 \text{ mm}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Section Modulus** $$ \begin{aligned} I &= \dfrac{\pi}{64} \left[ D^4 - (D-2t)^4 \right] \\ I &= \dfrac{\pi}{64} \left[ 500^4 - 450^4 \right] \\ I &= \dfrac{\pi}{64} \left[ 6.25 \times 10^{10} - 4.10 \times 10^{10} \right] \\ I &= \dfrac{\pi}{64} \times 2.15 \times 10^{10} \\ I &= 1.055 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} Z &= \dfrac{I}{D/2} = \dfrac{1.055 \times 10^9}{250} = 4.22 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Axial and Bending Stress** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_a &= \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{1500 \times 10^3}{37306} = 40.2 \text{ MPa} \\ \sigma_b &= \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{250 \times 10^6}{4.22 \times 10^6} = 59.2 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Maximum Combined Stress (using simple additive for conservative check)** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{max} &= \sigma_a + \sigma_b = 40.2 + 59.2 = 99.4 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Safety Check** $$ \begin{aligned} \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_y} &= \dfrac{99.4}{450} = 0.221 = 22.1\% \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation:** The section utilization is only **22.1%** of yield strength — well within safe limits (typically allowable utilization is 60–80% when partial safety factors are applied). The factor of safety $FS = 1/0.221 = 4.52$ — conservative for offshore applications. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Material Selection Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Start: Define Service Environment] --> nodeB{Is it a
fixed jacket
platform?} nodeB -->|Yes| nodeC[Use API 2H or 2Y
Yield 345-414 MPa
CVN 27J at -40C] nodeB -->|No| nodeD{Is it a
floating
structure?} nodeD -->|Yes| nodeE[Use API 2W or
high-strength steel
Yield up to 460 MPa] nodeD -->|No| nodeF{Is it
subsea
equipment?} nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG[Use Duplex
Stainless Steel
or Inconel] nodeF -->|No| nodeH[Use standard
structural steel
per project spec] nodeC --> nodeI[Apply Cathodic Protection
and Coatings] nodeE --> nodeI nodeG --> nodeJ[Use Inherent Corrosion
Resistance] nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeK[Perform Fatigue Analysis
using S-N Curves] nodeJ --> nodeK nodeK --> nodeL[Verify Brittle Fracture
CTOD Criterion] nodeL --> nodeM[Final Material
Approval] ``` ## 4.2 Corrosion Zones and Protection Strategy ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Offshore Platform Member] --> nodeB[Atmospheric Zone
Highest above water] nodeA --> nodeC[Splash Zone
Most Aggressive] nodeA --> nodeD[Submerged Zone
Constant Wetting] nodeA --> nodeE[Mud Zone
Below Seabed] nodeB --> nodeF[Epoxy + Polyurethane
Coating System] nodeC --> nodeG[Monel Sheathing or
Rubber Cladding plus
Extra Thickness] nodeD --> nodeH[Coating plus
Cathodic Protection
Sacrificial Anodes] nodeE --> nodeI[Minimal Protection
Low Oxygen Zone] ``` ## 4.3 Fatigue Life Assessment Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Wave Loading Data] --> nodeB[Stress Spectrum
Transfer Function] nodeC[Structural Model] --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeD[Hot-Spot Stress
Calculation] nodeD --> nodeE[Rainflow Counting] nodeE --> nodeF[Damage Accumulation
Miners Rule] nodeG[S-N Curve
Seawater with CP] --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeH{Damage
Index D
less than 1.0} nodeH -->|Yes| nodeI[Design Acceptable] nodeH -->|No| nodeJ[Redesign
or Strengthen] ``` ## 4.4 Tubular Joint Hot-Spot Location Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Tubular T-Joint Cross Section] --> nodeB[Brace Member
Outer Surface] nodeA --> nodeC[Chord Member
Outer Surface] nodeA --> nodeD[Weld Toe
Hot-Spot Location] nodeB --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE[Critical Fatigue Point
Highest SCF] nodeE --> nodeF[Apply S-N Curve
Class T or T'] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** ### Question 1: [3 Marks] **"List the key mechanical properties required for steel used in offshore structures. Justify why each property is critical."** **Model Answer:** The key mechanical properties required for offshore structural steel are: 1. **High Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$ ≥ 345 MPa):** Required to reduce self-weight of the platform and resist combined wave, wind, and operational loads. A jacket platform can weigh 15,000–30,000 tonnes, so higher strength directly reduces material quantity and installation cost. 2. **Adequate Toughness (CVN ≥ 27 J at design temperature):** Essential to prevent **brittle fracture** in cold marine environments. The North Sea design temperature is **-10°C to -40°C**, where standard mild steel becomes brittle. Toughness ensures the steel absorbs energy before fracturing. 3. **Good Weldability:** Offshore platforms are constructed by welding tubular sections together, sometimes at site. Good weldability (low carbon equivalent, **CE ≤ 0.45**) prevents **hydrogen-induced cracking** and **lamellar tearing** in thick plates. 4. **Excellent Fatigue Resistance:** Offshore structures experience **$10^7$ to $10^8$ wave cycles** during their design life. Fatigue resistance, measured by S-N curves, is critical at weld details and tubular joints where stress concentrations occur. 5. **Corrosion Resistance (or Compatibility with Protection Systems):** Steel must be compatible with **cathodic protection** and coating systems, as unprotected carbon steel corrodes at 0.3–0.5 mm/year in seawater. --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** ### Question 2: [3 Marks] **"Explain the concept of cathodic protection. Differentiate between sacrificial anode and impressed current cathodic protection systems."** **Model Answer:** **Cathodic Protection (CP)** is an electrochemical method of corrosion control in which the metal to be protected is made the **cathode** of an electrochemical cell. By supplying electrons to the structure, the anodic dissolution of iron is suppressed: **Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻** is prevented. There are two principal methods: | Feature | Sacrificial Anode (Galvanic) CP | Impressed Current CP | |---------|-------------------------------|----------------------| | **Power Source** | None (uses galvanic series) | External DC rectifier | | **Anode Material** | Zinc, Aluminum, Magnesium | Inert anodes (Ti, graphite, mixed metal oxide) | | **Driving Force** | Natural potential difference | Impressed DC current (typically 10–50 A) | | **Anode Life** | 10–20 years (consumable) | 20–40+ years (non-consumable) | | **Current Output** | Limited, self-regulating | Adjustable, high current | | **Best Application** | Smaller structures, initial installation | Large platforms, retrofits | | **Cost** | Lower initial | Higher initial, lower anode replacement | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write "steel is strong" without specifying **yield strength in MPa** with a numerical value — this loses 1 mark. > - **Common Mistake #2:** Failing to mention **design temperature** when discussing toughness — examiner specifically checks for this. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Writing only the formula without **defining each variable with units** in derivation problems — partial credit lost. > - **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing **CVN (Charpy V-Notch) energy** with **fracture toughness $K_{IC}$** — they are related but distinct. > - **Common Mistake #5:** Not drawing the **S-N curve** (sketch expected in long answers). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]** **"Discuss in detail the material considerations for offshore steel structures. Cover the following aspects:** **(a) [7 Marks] Steel selection criteria and common grades used in offshore platforms with their mechanical properties.** **(b) [7 Marks] Corrosion mechanisms in marine environment and methods of corrosion protection, including the design of cathodic protection."** #### **Solution to (a) — Steel Selection Criteria and Common Grades [7 Marks]** The selection of steel for offshore structures is a multi-criteria decision process. The following are the major considerations: **1. Strength Requirements:** Offshore platforms are subjected to combined loading from waves, wind, current, and operational loads. The design wave height in the **North Sea** can exceed **30 m**, generating hydrodynamic forces that produce stresses of **100–250 MPa** in primary members. Therefore, the yield strength of steel must be at least **345 MPa** (API 2H Gr. 50) and up to **460 MPa** for high-stress applications. **2. Toughness Requirements:** Brittle fracture is a major concern in cold marine environments. The **Charpy V-Notch (CVN) impact energy** requirement is: - **27 J at -40°C** for primary structure (API RP 2A requirement) - **40 J at -30°C** for critical welded joints - **60 J at -20°C** for Arctic applications **3. Weldability:** The **carbon equivalent (CE)** is calculated using the **IIW formula**: $$CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$$ For offshore steels, **CE ≤ 0.45** is mandatory. Modern offshore grades use **TMCP (Thermo-Mechanically Controlled Processing)** to achieve high strength with low CE, ensuring weldability. **4. Common Offshore Steel Grades:** | Grade | Yield (MPa) | UTS (MPa) | CVN (J @ °C) | Application | |-------|-------------|-----------|--------------|-------------| | ASTM A36 | 250 | 400–550 | — | Mild environments | | API 2H Gr. 50 | 345 | 450–620 | 27 @ -40 | Jackets, decks | | API 2W Gr. 60 | 414 | 520–690 | 34 @ -40 | Critical joints | | API 2Y Gr. 60 | 414 | 520–690 | 34 @ -40 | Through-thickness improved | | EN 10025 S460 | 460 | 540–720 | 27 @ -40 | European standards | **5. Through-Thickness Properties:** For thick plates (>25 mm), the steel must have adequate **through-thickness ductility** (Z-direction) to prevent **lamellar tearing** at welded connections. **API 2Y** grades guarantee Z-direction reduction of area ≥ **20%**. **6. Corrosion Compatibility:** Steel must be compatible with cathodic protection (CP) and protective coating systems. The chemical composition (low sulfur ≤ 0.01%, low phosphorus ≤ 0.03%) improves corrosion resistance. --- #### **Solution to (b) — Corrosion Mechanisms and Protection Methods [7 Marks]** **1. Marine Corrosion Mechanism:** Corrosion in seawater is an **electrochemical process** requiring four elements: **anode**, **cathode**, **electrolyte (seawater)**, and **metallic path**. The reactions are: **At the anode (oxidation):** $$\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$$ **At the cathode (reduction):** $$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$$ **Overall:** $$2\text{Fe} + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{Fe(OH)}_2$$ **2. Corrosion Zones and Rates:** | Zone | Corrosion Rate (mm/year) | Most Aggressive Factor | |------|--------------------------|------------------------| | Atmospheric | 0.05–0.1 | Salt spray, UV | | Splash | 0.5–1.0 | Wet-dry cycles, oxygen | | Submerged | 0.1–0.4 | Dissolved oxygen | | Mud | 0.05–0.15 | Anaerobic bacteria | **3. Protection Methods:** **(a) Protective Coatings:** - Epoxy primer (75–125 μm) + polyurethane topcoat (50–75 μm) - Total system: 250–500 μm - Service life: 15–25 years - Inspection: every 2–5 years **(b) Cathodic Protection — Sacrificial Anode System:** For a jacket platform with submerged surface area of **A m²** and design current density of **i mA/m²**, the required anode mass is: $$M = \dfrac{I \times T \times 8760}{C \times u}$$ Where: - $I = i \times A$ = total current (A) - $T$ = design life (years) - $C$ = electrochemical capacity of anode (**Al: 2700 Ah/kg**; **Zn: 780 Ah/kg**) - $u$ = utilization factor (**0.8 for Al, 0.9 for Zn**) **Example:** For $A = 10,000$ m², $i = 110$ mA/m² (seawater with coating), $T = 25$ years, using aluminum anodes: $$ \begin{aligned} I &= 0.110 \times 10000 = 1100 \text{ A} \\ M &= \dfrac{1100 \times 25 \times 8760}{2700 \times 0.8} \\ M &= \dfrac{2.409 \times 10^8}{2160} \\ M &= 111,528 \text{ kg} \approx 111.5 \text{ tonnes of aluminum anodes} \end{aligned} $$ **(c) Cathodic Protection — Impressed Current System:** Uses inert anodes connected to a DC power source. Typical current: 10–50 A per anode. Used for large platforms where sacrificial anode mass becomes impractical. **(d) Corrosion Allowance:** Extra steel thickness (3–6 mm per face) is added to compensate for long-term metal loss. This is the simplest "passive" protection. **(e) Material Upgrade:** Use of **weathering steel (API 2W)**, **stainless steel cladding**, or **monel sheathing** in splash zone. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]** **"Answer the following:** **(a) [7 Marks] Explain fatigue considerations in offshore structures. Describe the S-N curve approach and Miner's rule for cumulative damage calculation.** **(b) [7 Marks] A steel tubular joint has a hot-spot stress range of 180 MPa. Using the DNV T curve (in seawater with cathodic protection), calculate the allowable number of cycles. Take the thickness exponent correction into account for a wall thickness of 40 mm."** **Given DNV T-Curve Parameters:** - Reference thickness: $t_{ref} = 25$ mm - $\log(a) = 11.7$ for the T curve - Slope exponent: $m = 3$ - Thickness correction factor: $k = 0.25$ for thickness exponent --- #### **Solution to (a) — Fatigue in Offshore Structures [7 Marks]** **1. Introduction to Offshore Fatigue:** Offshore structures experience **cyclic loading** from waves, wind, and operational activities. A typical jacket platform in the North Sea experiences **$10^7$ to $10^8$ stress cycles** during its 25–30 year design life. Fatigue cracking is therefore a governing design consideration, particularly at welded tubular joints where **stress concentration factors (SCF)** can reach 8–15. **2. S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve):** The S-N curve plots **stress amplitude** ($S$ or $\sigma_a$) on the y-axis (log scale) versus **number of cycles to failure** ($N$) on the x-axis (log scale). For steels, the relationship in the high-cycle regime is linear on a log-log plot: $$\log(\sigma_a) = \log(\sigma'_f) + b \log(2N)$$ Or equivalently: $$\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N)^b$$ Where: - $\sigma'_f$ = fatigue strength coefficient (≈ true fracture strength) - $b$ = fatigue strength exponent (typically **-0.05 to -0.12** for steels) **3. Standard S-N Curves for Offshore Use (DNV-RP-C203):** DNV provides standardized S-N curves for different joint classes: - **B curve** (welded plates, no coating) - **C curve** (typical welded joints) - **D curve** (welded tubular joints in air) - **E curve** (tubular joints in air, as-welded) - **F curve** (tubular joints in seawater, no CP) - **T curve** (tubular joints in seawater, with CP) - **T' curve** (improved weld profile, in seawater with CP) **4. Miner's Rule for Cumulative Damage:** The sea state produces a **stress spectrum** with varying amplitudes. The cumulative damage is calculated by summing the damage from each stress range bin: $$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{n_i}{N_i}$$ Where: - $n_i$ = number of cycles at stress range $\Delta\sigma_i$ (from the stress spectrum) - $N_i$ = number of cycles to failure at $\Delta\sigma_i$ (from the S-N curve) - $k$ = total number of stress range bins **Failure Criterion:** The structure is considered to have reached end of fatigue life when **D ≥ 1.0**. A design **fatigue design factor (FDF)** of 3 to 10 is typically applied on life for critical joints. **5. Hot-Spot Stress Method:** For tubular joints, the **effective hot-spot stress** at the weld toe is calculated using: - **Geometric SCF** × **nominal stress** in the brace - Stress is **extrapolated** to the weld toe from strain gauge measurements at 0.4t and 1.0t distances from the weld. **6. Fatigue Improvement Methods:** - Weld profiling (grinding toes to smooth profile) - Post-weld heat treatment (PWHT) - Weld toe peening - Use of **T' curve** with improved welds --- #### **Solution to (b) — Numerical Problem on Allowable Cycles [7 Marks]** **Given:** - Hot-spot stress range: $\Delta\sigma_{HS} = 180$ MPa - DNV T-curve parameters: $\log(a) = 11.7$, $m = 3$, $t_{ref} = 25$ mm - Actual wall thickness: $t = 40$ mm - Thickness exponent: $k = 0.25$ **Step 1: Apply the S-N curve equation (DNV form):** $$\log(N) = \log(a) - m \log(\Delta\sigma) = 11.7 - 3 \log(180)$$ **Step 2: Calculate $\log(180)$:** $$\log(180) = \log(1.8 \times 10^2) = 0.2553 + 2 = 2.2553$$ **Step 3: Calculate allowable cycles at reference thickness:** $$ \begin{aligned} \log(N_{ref}) &= 11.7 - 3 \times 2.2553 \\ \log(N_{ref}) &= 11.7 - 6.7659 \\ \log(N_{ref}) &= 4.9341 \\ N_{ref} &= 10^{4.9341} = 85,925 \text{ cycles} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Apply thickness correction factor:** For wall thickness greater than the reference, the allowable cycles are reduced. The thickness correction is: $$\log(N) = \log(a) - m \log(\Delta\sigma) - k \log\left(\dfrac{t}{t_{ref}}\right)$$ **Step 5: Calculate thickness ratio:** $$\dfrac{t}{t_{ref}} = \dfrac{40}{25} = 1.6$$ $$\log(1.6) = 0.2041$$ **Step 6: Apply the correction:** $$ \begin{aligned} \log(N) &= 4.9341 - 0.25 \times 0.2041 \\ \log(N) &= 4.9341 - 0.0510 \\ \log(N) &= 4.8831 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7: Calculate the corrected number of cycles:** $$ \begin{aligned} N &= 10^{4.8831} \\ N &= 76,470 \text{ cycles} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 8: Verification by direct calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} N &= \dfrac{10^{4.9341}}{1.6^{0.25}} \\ N &= \dfrac{85,925}{1.1247} \\ N &= 76,393 \text{ cycles} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 9: Final Result and Interpretation:** The allowable number of cycles for the tubular joint is **N ≈ 76,400 cycles**. **Interpretation:** If the wave-induced stress spectrum produces an annual number of 3000 cycles at 180 MPa stress range, the fatigue life is: $$\text{Fatigue Life} = \dfrac{76,400}{3000} = 25.5 \text{ years}$$ This is just at the design limit of 25 years. The joint requires either: - Reduced stress range (e.g., by increasing wall thickness) - Improved weld profile (move to T' curve with $\log(a) = 12.0$) - Or a fatigue design factor (FDF) of 3 should be applied: $N_{design} = 76,400 / 3 = 25,467$ cycles --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exams** - ✅ **Primary material** for offshore fixed platforms is **structural steel** (API 2H, 2W, 2Y grades). - ✅ **Yield strength** ranges from **250 MPa (A36)** to **460 MPa (S460)**. - ✅ **Charpy V-Notch (CVN) toughness** requirement: minimum **27 J at -40°C** for North Sea service. - ✅ **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** must be **≤ 0.45** for weldability. - ✅ **Young's Modulus** $E$ = **2.0 × 10⁵ MPa**; Poisson's ratio $\nu$ = **0.3**; density $\rho$ = **7850 kg/m³**. - ✅ **Corrosion rates in seawater**: atmospheric (0.05–0.1), **splash (0.5–1.0)**, submerged (0.1–0.4), mud (0.05–0.15) mm/year. - ✅ **Cathodic protection**: Sacrificial anodes (Zn, Al) or **Impressed Current** (inert anodes + DC source). - ✅ **Aluminum anode capacity** = **2700 Ah/kg**; **Zinc** = **780 Ah/kg**. - ✅ **S-N Curve** for fatigue: $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N)^b$ (Basquin's equation). - ✅ **Minor's rule**: $D = \sum (n_i / N_i)$; failure when **D ≥ 1.0**. - ✅ **Hot-spot stress** = SCF × nominal stress; critical at **tubular T/Y-joints**. - ✅ **DNV T-curve** for tubular joints in seawater with CP: $m = 3$, $\log(a) = 11.7$. - ✅ **Thickness correction**: $\log(N)$ reduced by $k \log(t/t_{ref})$ where $k = 0.25$. - ✅ **CTOD** criterion for weld defects: minimum **0.25 mm** at design temperature. - ✅ **Corrosion allowance**: typically **3–6 mm** extra thickness on each face. - ✅ **Design life** of offshore structures: typically **25–30 years**. - ✅ **Through-thickness ductility** (Z-quality) required for plates > **25 mm** to prevent lamellar tearing. - ✅ **Marine growth** adds weight (typically 30–50 mm thickness, density 1300 kg/m³). - ✅ **Fatigue Design Factor (FDF)**: 3 to 10 applied on fatigue life for design safety. - ✅ **Stainless steel grades** (Duplex, Super-duplex) used for **risers, piping, and process systems** in corrosive service. - ✅ **Concrete GBS (Gravity-Based Structures)** used in **North Sea** for very large oil field developments. - ✅ **Design temperature** for material selection is the **lowest anticipated air or water temperature** during service. - ✅ **END OF TOPIC — Material Considerations for Offshore Structures**

topics.title: Introduction to Construction of Various Types of Offshore Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST602 - OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Construction of Various Types of Offshore Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Offshore Structures** are engineered facilities constructed in marine environments (typically in water depths ranging from shallow coastal zones to **ultra-deepwater regions exceeding 3,000 meters**) for the purpose of exploration, drilling, production, storage, and transportation of hydrocarbons (oil and natural gas). The construction of these structures encompasses a complete lifecycle — from **design, fabrication, load-out, transportation, and installation** at the offshore site, to eventual **decommissioning**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Offshore structures are large, specialized engineering systems built to withstand hostile marine environmental loads (waves, currents, wind, ice, and seabed conditions) while supporting hydrocarbon recovery operations. Their construction methodology is dictated by water depth, seabed geology, functional requirement, and economic feasibility."*

### 1.2 Classification of Offshore Structures (Universal KTU Framework)

Offshore structures are broadly classified into two fundamental categories based on their **static equilibrium mechanism**:

| Category | Sub-Type | Typical Water Depth |
|----------|----------|---------------------|
| **Bottom-Supported (Fixed)** | Steel Jacket Platforms | 10 m – 500 m |
| **Bottom-Supported (Fixed)** | Gravity-Based Structures (GBS) | 10 m – 300 m |
| **Bottom-Supported (Fixed)** | Compliant Towers (CT) | 300 m – 1,000 m |
| **Floating (Moored)** | Semi-Submersibles | 100 m – 3,000 m |
| **Floating (Moored)** | Ship-shaped FPSO / FSO | 100 m – 3,000 m |
| **Floating (Moored)** | Tension Leg Platforms (TLP) | 300 m – 2,000 m |
| **Floating (Moored)** | Spars (Classic & Truss) | 500 m – 3,000 m |
| **Subsea Systems** | Subsea Manifolds, Wellheads, PLEM | All depths |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to build a **bus stop in the middle of a lake**. You have two options:
1. **Drive long poles (stilts) into the lake-bed** and build a platform on top — this is like a **Fixed Jacket Platform**. The ground holds it up rigidly.
2. **Build a floating pontoon** and anchor it with heavy chains so it doesn't drift away — this is like a **Floating Semi-Submersible**. The buoyancy holds it up; the chains only keep it in place.

Now imagine the lake is 2 kilometers deep. Driving poles becomes impossible. So instead, you build an **enormous hollow cylinder (a Spar)**, tow it horizontally to the site, then tip it upright and fill the bottom with heavy ballast. It floats vertically, stabilized like a **bobber on a fishing line**. That is the engineering leap from fixed to floating offshore architecture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Golden Rule:**
> *As water depth increases beyond ~500 m, the mass of steel required for a fixed jacket rises exponentially, while floating systems scale linearly. This economic crossover dictates the choice of structural type.*

### 1.4 Key Engineering Drivers for Construction

The construction methodology is governed by the interaction of four primary engineering variables:

- **Water Depth (d):** Determines whether fixed or floating systems are feasible.
- **Metocean Environment:** Wave height (H), wave period (T), wind speed (V), current speed (U).
- **Functional Requirement:** Drilling, production, storage, processing, or export.
- **Geotechnical Seabed Conditions:** Soil bearing capacity, scour potential, slope stability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industry Metrics to Remember:**
> - **1 meter (m) = 3.281 feet (ft)**
> - **Sea Water Density:** $\rho_w = 1,025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (≈ 64.0 lb/ft³)
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - **API 2A-WSD** is the governing design code for fixed offshore steel structures.
> - **API RP 2FPS** governs floating production systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fixed Offshore Structures (Bottom-Founded)

#### 2.1.1 Steel Jacket Platforms
A **steel jacket** is a space-frame truss structure fabricated from large-diameter tubular steel members, typically launched from a fabrication yard, towed horizontally on a barge, upended at the offshore site, and pinned to the seabed via **driven or drilled piles**.

**Construction Sequence:**
1. **Yard Fabrication** — Welded assembly of tubular chords, braces, and joints in horizontal position.
2. **Load-Out** — Skidding or lifting the jacket onto a heavy-lift transportation barge.
3. **Sea-Towing** — Horizontal tow to the offshore installation site.
4. **Upending** — Controlled ballasting/flooding to rotate the jacket to vertical.
5. **Pile Installation** — Driving hollow steel piles through the jacket legs into the seabed.
6. **Deck (Topside) Installation** — Lifting the prefabricated deck module onto the jacket via crane vessel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Load Hierarchy (API 2A-WSD):**
> $L = D + L_r + (W \text{ or } E)$
> Where: $L$ = Total Load, $D$ = Dead Load, $L_r$ = Live Load, $W$ = Wind, $E$ = Earthquake.

#### 2.1.2 Gravity-Based Structures (GBS)
A **GBS** relies on its **own weight** (often filled with ballast) to resist overturning and sliding. Constructed in **dry docks** or **caissons**, they are floated to site and sunk onto a prepared seabed.

#### 2.1.3 Compliant Towers (CT)
A **compliant tower** is a slender, flexible steel tower fixed to the seabed, designed to *intentionally sway* with lateral wave loads — reducing net base shear by allowing controlled dynamic deflection.

### 2.2 Floating Offshore Structures

#### 2.2.1 Semi-Submersibles
A **semi-submersible** consists of submerged pontoons and vertical columns supporting a deck above the waterline. They achieve stability through **large waterplane area and deep draft** (≈ 20–30 m). They are either **moored (stationary)** or **propelled (mobile drilling units)**.

#### 2.2.2 FPSO (Floating Production, Storage, and Offloading)
An **FPSO** is a ship-shaped or circular hull equipped with processing equipment and a storage tank. Produced oil is stored in the hull and offloaded to shuttle tankers. Construction is similar to commercial tanker shipbuilding.

#### 2.2.3 Tension Leg Platforms (TLP)
A **TLP** uses **vertical tendons in tension** (rather than catenary chains) to moor the hull. Excess buoyancy keeps the tendons taut. TLPs have **minimal vertical motion** but allow horizontal sway.

#### 2.2.4 Spars
A **Spar** is a large vertical cylinder (Classic Spar) or a multi-section structure (Truss Spar). It is stabilized by a **deep draft (~200 m) and low center of gravity** from heavy ballast. Spars are among the deepest-water production systems ever deployed.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are HIGH-YIELD for KTU Board Examinations.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|--------------------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $F_B = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$ | Archimedes' Buoyancy Force | Newtons (N) |
| 2 | $\Delta W = W_{struct} - F_B$ | Net Vertical Force (sign indicates sinking/floating) | N |
| 3 | $T_{tendon} = F_B - W_{hull}$ | TLP Tendon Tension (must remain positive) | N |
| 4 | $\gamma_w = \rho_w \cdot g$ | Specific Weight of Sea Water | N/m³ |
| 5 | $H_s = 4 \sigma_\eta$ | Significant Wave Height (from spectral std. dev.) | m |
| 6 | $\lambda = \frac{g T^2}{2\pi}$ | Deep-water wavelength (linear Airy theory) | m |
| 7 | $p_{dyn} = \rho_w g H_s$ | Approximate Dynamic Wave Pressure on face | Pa |
| 8 | $V_{stability} = \int_{A_{wp}} dA$ | Waterplane Area → gives Initial Transverse Stability | m² |
| 9 | $\overline{GM} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG}$ | Metacentric Height (transverse stability criterion) | m |
| 10 | $F = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Froude Number (used for FPSO/floating hull scaling) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $R = \frac{H_s}{\lambda}$ | Wave Steepness (must be < 1/7 to avoid breaking) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $S_{deck} = \frac{W_{deck}}{A_{footprint}}$ | Deck Bearing Pressure (typically 5–15 kPa) | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Stability Criterion (Floating Bodies):**
> For a floating offshore unit, the **metacentric height $\overline{GM}$ must be positive**:
> $\overline{GM} > 0 \implies \text{Stable Equilibrium}$
> $\overline{GM} = 0 \implies \text{Neutral Equilibrium}$
> $\overline{GM} < 0 \implies \text{Unstable (Capsize Risk)}$

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Oil & Gas Industry:** ~70% of new offshore production comes from deepwater floating systems (FPSOs dominate West Africa & Brazil).
- **Renewable Energy:** Construction principles adapted for **offshore wind turbine jackets** and **floating wind platforms**.
- **Marine Logistics:** FPSO storage method is now used for LNG via **FSRUs (Floating Storage Regasification Units)**.
- **Subsea Engineering:** Construction of **PLETs, PLEMs, manifolds** requires ROVs (Remotely Operated Vehicles) — directly linked to offshore construction methodology.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Buoyancy and Submerged Stability (Floating Unit)

We derive the **net vertical restoring force** experienced by a floating offshore hull, which dictates its submerged equilibrium.

**Starting Physical Statement:**
A floating hull in equilibrium must satisfy Newton's First Law:
$$\sum F_{vertical} = 0$$

**Step 1 — Identify all vertical forces acting on the hull:**
- Weight of the structure $W$ acting downward (through the center of gravity $G$).
- Buoyancy force $F_B$ acting upward (through the center of buoyancy $B$).

**Step 2 — Apply Archimedes' Principle to determine $F_B$:**
The hydrostatic pressure integrated over the submerged hull surface gives:
$$F_B = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$$

Where:
- $\rho_w$ = density of sea water (1,025 kg/m³)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)
- $V_{disp}$ = displaced volume of sea water (m³)

**Step 3 — Write the equilibrium equation:**
For the hull to float in static equilibrium:
$$W = F_B$$
$$W = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the displaced volume (design equation):**
$$\boxed{V_{disp} = \frac{W}{\rho_w \cdot g}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example:**
Given:
- Hull weight $W = 50,000$ tonnes = $50 \times 10^6$ kg
- $\rho_w = 1,025$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

$$V_{disp} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{1,025 \times 9.81} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{10,055.25} \approx 4,973 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
A 50,000-tonne hull must displace exactly **4,973 m³** of sea water. Any excess weight beyond this causes sinking.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: TLP Tendon Tension Equation

**Starting Physical Statement:**
A TLP is held down by tendons that remain taut due to the **excess buoyancy** of the hull. The tendons act as vertical springs.

**Step 1 — Define tendon tension equilibrium:**
At the hull level, the vertical forces are:
- Hull weight $W_h$ (downward)
- Buoyancy $F_B$ (upward)
- Net tendon pull $T_{net}$ (downward, since tendons anchor the hull to seabed)

For static vertical equilibrium:
$$F_B = W_h + T_{net}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for tendon tension:**
$$\boxed{T_{net} = F_B - W_h = \rho_w g V_{disp} - W_h}$$

**Step 3 — Verify sign convention:**
- If $T_{net} > 0$ → Tendons remain in **tension** (correct TLP operating condition).
- If $T_{net} \leq 0$ → Tendons go slack → **Loss of station-keeping → Critical failure**.

**Step 4 — Numerical Example:**
- Hull displacement weight $W_h = 35,000$ tonnes = $35 \times 10^6$ kg
- Submerged buoyancy volume $V_{disp} = 38,000$ m³

$$T_{net} = (1,025)(9.81)(38,000) - 35 \times 10^6$$
$$T_{net} = 382,402,950 - 350,000,000$$
$$\boxed{T_{net} \approx 32.4 \text{ MN}}$$

This tension is distributed among typically **4 to 16 tendons**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Deep-Water Wavelength (Linear Airy Wave Theory)

**Step 1 — Wave dispersion relation (deep water):**
$$\omega^2 = g k$$
Where $\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$ (angular frequency) and $k = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ (wave number).

**Step 2 — Substituting:**
$$\left(\frac{2\pi}{T}\right)^2 = g \left(\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\right)$$
$$\frac{4\pi^2}{T^2} = \frac{2\pi g}{\lambda}$$
$$\lambda = \frac{g T^2}{2\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Verify units:**
$$[\lambda] = \frac{(\text{m/s}^2)(\text{s}^2)}{1} = \text{m} \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example:**
Given wave period $T = 12$ s:
$$\lambda = \frac{(9.81)(144)}{2\pi} = \frac{1412.64}{6.2832}$$
$$\boxed{\lambda \approx 224.8 \text{ m}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Construction Perspective)

| Feature | Jacket Platform | GBS | Semi-Submersible | FPSO | TLP | Spar |
|---------|-----------------|-----|-------------------|------|-----|------|
| **Water Depth (m)** | 10–500 | 10–300 | 100–3,000 | 100–3,000 | 300–2,000 | 500–3,000 |
| **Stability Mechanism** | Piles | Weight | Waterplane + Ballast | Hull Form | Tendon Tension | Deep Ballast |
| **On-site Operation** | Welding | Grouting | Hook-up | Hook-up | Hook-up | Hook-up |
| **Yard Fabrication** | Horizontal | Vertical (dry-dock) | Modular | Shipyard | Modular | Sectional |
| **Marine Spread Required** | Heavy-lift Crane Barge | Crane Barge | Tow + Crane | Tow + Shuttle Tanker | Tow + Crane | Up-end + Tow |
| **Decommissioning Ease** | Difficult | Very Difficult | Moderate | Moderate | Difficult | Difficult |
| **Capital Cost** | Moderate | High (for deep) | High | High | Very High | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Offshore Structure Construction Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FEED Engineering Phase] --> B[Detailed Design]
    B --> C{Yard Selection}
    C --> D[Fabrication Yard]
    D --> E[Load-Out to Barge]
    E --> F[Sea-Tow to Offshore Site]
    F --> G{Structure Type}
    G --> H[Upending for Jacket]
    G --> I[Ballast-Fill for Spar]
    G --> J[Wet-Tow for Semi-Sub]
    H --> K[Pile Installation]
    I --> L[Tendon Hook-Up]
    J --> M[Mooring Spread Lay]
    K --> N[Topside Lift-On]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Commissioning and Hook-Up]
    O --> P[Production Operation]
    P --> Q{End of Field Life}
    Q --> R[Plug and Abandon Wells]
    Q --> S[Partial Removal]
    Q --> T[Full Removal Rigs to Reef]
    R --> U[Site Clearance]
    S --> U
    T --> U
    U --> V[Decommissioning Complete]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Classification Hierarchy of Offshore Structures

```mermaid
graph TD
    OFF[Offshore Structures] --> FIX[Fixed Bottom Supported]
    OFF --> FLOAT[Floating Moored]
    OFF --> SUB[Subsea Systems]
    FIX --> JCK[Steel Jacket]
    FIX --> GBS[Gravity Based Structure]
    FIX --> CT[Compliant Tower]
    FLOAT --> SEMI[Semi-Submersible]
    FLOAT --> FPSO[FPSO FSO]
    FLOAT --> TLP[Tension Leg Platform]
    FLOAT --> SPAR[Spar Platform]
    SUB --> MAN[Subsea Manifold]
    SUB --> WH[Subsea Wellhead]
    SUB --> PLET[PLET and PLEM]
    SUB --> UMB[Umbilicals and Risers]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: TLP Construction Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hull Fabrication in Yard] --> B[Load-Out onto Heavy-Lift Barge]
    B --> C[Sea-Tow to Offshore Site]
    C --> D[Position over Pre-Installed Templates]
    D --> E[Ballast Down Hull to Operating Draft]
    E --> F[Connect Tendons to Hull Porches]
    F --> G[Tension Tendons via Hydraulic Pull-Down]
    G --> H[Topside Lift-On via Crane Vessel]
    H --> I[Riser and Umbilical Hook-Up]
    I --> J[Pre-Production Commissioning]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Load Transfer Path in a Jacket Platform

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DECK[Deck Topside Live and Dead Load] --> PILE[Piles Driven into Seabed]
    DECK --> LEGS[Jacket Legs Compression]
    DECK --> BRACE[Bracing Members Lateral Load]
    BRACE --> LEGS
    LEGS --> PILE
    PILE --> SOIL[Seabed Soil Resistance]
    FORCE[Wave Wind Current Loads] --> DECK
    FORCE --> BRACE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation of Diagram:**
> The jacket acts as a **load-path bridge** between the superstructure (deck) and the geotechnical foundation (piles). Every brace, chord, and joint must transfer forces without exceeding allowable stress limits per **API 2A-WSD Working Stress Design** methodology.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
*Define a "Jacket Platform" in the context of offshore oil and gas production. State its typical water depth range and the role of piles.*

**Model Answer:**
A jacket platform is a **steel space-frame truss structure** constructed from large-diameter tubular members, used for hydrocarbon exploration and production in water depths typically ranging from **10 m to 500 m**. The jacket itself is supported on the seabed by **steel piles driven through its legs** into the foundation soil. These piles transfer all vertical, lateral, and overturning loads from the structure into the seabed, providing stability against wave, wind, and current forces.

*(Examiner's Note: Marks awarded for: correct definition = 1 mark, water depth = 1 mark, role of piles = 1 mark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*Distinguish between a "Semi-Submersible" and an "FPSO" in terms of hull shape, motion behavior, and oil storage capability.*

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Semi-Submersible | FPSO |
|-----------|------------------|------|
| **Hull Shape** | Rectangular pontoons + columns | Cylindrical / Ship-shaped |
| **Motion Behavior** | 3 DOF heave/pitch/roll well-damped | Significant roll/pitch in beam seas |
| **Oil Storage** | Generally none | Large storage tanks in hull |
| **Offloading** | Via shuttle tanker or pipeline | Direct to shuttle tanker via STL buoy |

*(Examiner's Note: A clear table or 3 distinct points each → 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU ESE Module 4 - Common Choice]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **construction sequence of a Steel Jacket Platform** from fabrication yard load-out to pile installation. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Fabrication in Yard (2 marks):**
The jacket is fabricated in a horizontal position in modules using tubular steel chords (typically 1–3 m diameter) and braces. Welded joints are inspected via NDT (radiography, ultrasonic). The structure is assembled into 2D or 3D sub-assemblies and finally integrated into a single jacket.

2. **Load-Out (1 mark):**
The completed jacket is transferred onto a heavy-lift transportation barge using **skid beams, hydraulic trailers, or the Strand Jack method**. The center of gravity must be carefully controlled.

3. **Sea-Towing (1 mark):**
The jacket is towed horizontally to the offshore site using tugboats. Tow speed is typically 3–6 knots, dependent on weather windows.

4. **Upending (2 marks):**
On arrival, the jacket is ballasted to float horizontally. A controlled **flooding of internal compartments** rotates it to vertical, assisted by the upending hinge/cradle on the launch barge.

5. **Pile Installation (1 mark):**
Hollow steel piles are inserted through the jacket legs and driven into the seabed using **hydraulic or steam hammers**, then grouted to the jacket legs.

**(b)** A steel jacket has a total weight (excluding buoyancy) of **8,000 tonnes**. Each of its 4 legs has an internal diameter of **2.5 m** and wall thickness of **60 mm**. During upending at the offshore site, the legs are flooded with sea water. Compute the additional weight due to flooding and the effective down-force on the seabed during pile driving. *(7 marks)*

**Given:**
- $W_{jacket} = 8,000$ tonnes = $8 \times 10^6$ kg
- Number of legs $n = 4$
- Leg outer diameter $D_o = 2.5$ m, wall thickness $t = 0.06$ m
- Inner diameter $D_i = 2.5 - 2(0.06) = 2.38$ m
- Length of flooded leg (assumed full depth to seabed) $L = 80$ m
- $\rho_w = 1,025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Internal cross-sectional area of one leg:**
$$A_{leg} = \frac{\pi}{4} D_i^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (2.38)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(5.6644) = 4.4487 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Volume of water entering each leg:**
$$V_{leg} = A_{leg} \times L = 4.4487 \times 80 = 355.9 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Mass of water in one leg:**
$$m_{leg} = \rho_w \times V_{leg} = 1,025 \times 355.9 = 364,797.5 \text{ kg} \approx 364.8 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 4 — Total flooded weight (4 legs):**
$$W_{flood} = 4 \times 364.8 = 1,459.2 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 5 — Effective down-force during pile driving:**
$$W_{effective} = W_{jacket} + W_{flood} = 8,000 + 1,459.2 = 9,459.2 \text{ tonnes}$$

$$\boxed{W_{effective} \approx 9,459.2 \text{ tonnes } (\approx 92.79 \text{ MN})}$$

This massive down-force ensures pile penetration resistance is overcome during driving.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — **Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **principle, components, and construction methodology of a Tension Leg Platform (TLP)**. Why are tendons preferred over catenary mooring chains for TLPs? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (2 marks):**
A TLP maintains its station through **vertical tendons in tension**, exploiting the **excess buoyancy** of the hull. The hull has more buoyancy than its own weight — so it would naturally float upward; tendons restrain this upward motion, creating a taut mooring system that limits vertical heave to near-zero.

**Components (2 marks):**
- **Hull:** Semi-submersible type with large waterplane area.
- **Tendons:** Vertical or near-vertical pre-tensioned steel tubes.
- **Topside Facilities:** Production and drilling equipment.
- **Foundation Templates / Suction Piles:** Anchor points on the seabed.

**Construction Methodology (2 marks):**
1. Hull fabricated in modules → integrated and load-out at yard.
2. Pre-install foundation templates (suction piles) on seabed.
3. Tow hull to site using wet-tow method.
4. Position over templates → ballast to operating draft.
5. Hook-up tendons using hydraulic pull-down winches.
6. Lift and install topside modules.
7. Hook-up risers and umbilicals.

**Why Tendons Over Catenary Chains? (1 mark):**
Tendons provide **minimal vertical motion** (heave), making TLPs ideal for dry-tree wellheads and direct riser access. Catenary chains allow excessive heave, incompatible with rigid production risers.

**(b)** A TLP hull has the following parameters:
- Total submerged displacement $V_{disp} = 45,000$ m³
- Hull steel weight $W_h = 28,000$ tonnes
- 12 tendons, equally loaded

Calculate:
(i) Net tendon tension.
(ii) Tension per tendon.

*(7 marks)*

**Given:**
- $V_{disp} = 45,000$ m³, $\rho_w = 1,025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $W_h = 28,000$ tonnes = $28 \times 10^6$ kg
- Number of tendons $n = 12$

**Step 1 — Compute buoyancy force:**
$$F_B = \rho_w g V_{disp} = (1,025)(9.81)(45,000)$$
$$F_B = 452,646,375 \text{ N} = 452.65 \text{ MN}$$

**Step 2 — Convert hull weight to Newtons:**
$$W_h = 28 \times 10^6 \times 9.81 = 274.68 \text{ MN}$$

**Step 3 — Net tendon tension:**
$$T_{net} = F_B - W_h = 452.65 - 274.68$$
$$\boxed{T_{net} = 177.97 \text{ MN}}$$

**Step 4 — Tension per tendon:**
$$T_{one} = \frac{T_{net}}{n} = \frac{177.97}{12}$$
$$\boxed{T_{one} \approx 14.83 \text{ MN per tendon}}$$

**Engineering Check:**
Each tendon experiences ~14.83 MN tension. For a 36-inch (≈0.91 m) diameter steel tubular tendon with 50 mm wall thickness, this is well within allowable limits (typical tendon capacity ≈ 50–80 MN).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mistakes:** Forgetting to convert tonnes to kg (×1000) and kg to N (×9.81) loses 1–2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Sign Convention Errors:** In TLP tendon problems, students often write $W_h - F_B$, giving a *negative* tension — this is conceptually wrong; the tendons must always be in *positive* tension.
> 3. **Confusing Buoyancy with Weight:** Buoyancy is in Newtons (force), not in tonnes. Always write $F_B = \rho_w g V$.
> 4. **Skipping Sketch in (a) part:** A *neat sketch* is mandatory for full marks in descriptive questions.
> 5. **Forgetting "g = 9.81":** Many students use only $\rho_w \cdot V$ and miss the gravitational factor.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Introduction to Construction of Offshore Structures**

- **Fixed Structures** (Jacket, GBS, Compliant Tower) suit **shallow to mid-depth** waters (≤ 500 m).
- **Floating Structures** (Semi-Sub, FPSO, TLP, Spar) are essential for **deepwater (>500 m)**.
- **Buoyancy formula:** $F_B = \rho_w g V_{disp}$ — *Archimedes' principle is foundational.*
- **TLP Tendon Tension:** $T_{net} = F_B - W_h$, *must always remain positive.*
- **Spar Stability:** Deep draft + low CoG via ballast → pendulum-like restoring force.
- **FPSO:** Combines production + storage + offloading in a single hull.
- **Jacket Construction Sequence:** Fabrication → Load-out → Tow → Upending → Piling → Topside lift.
- **Design Codes:** API 2A-WSD (Fixed), API RP 2FPS (Floating), API RP 2SK (Mooring).
- **Wave Loads:** Governed by wave height $H_s$, period $T$, and wavelength $\lambda = gT^2 / 2\pi$.
- **Stability Criterion:** Metacentric height $\overline{GM} > 0$ for floating units.
- **Standard Density of Sea Water:** $\rho_w = 1,025$ kg/m³.
- **Decommissioning:** Increasingly important; governs material selection for recyclable steel.
- **Skid-Beam / Strand-Jack:** Standard load-out techniques.
- **Suction Piles:** Modern alternative to driven piles for deepwater foundations.
- **Subsea Systems:** PLEMs, PLETs, manifolds, umbilicals — essential production enablers.

> **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — PCNST602 Offshore Structures (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
