<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Loadout"?

In offshore structural engineering, **Loadout** is the critical marine operation in which a fully fabricated offshore structure (jacket, topsides module, deck, gravity-based structure, or FPSO hull) is transferred from the **fabrication yard (onshore)** onto a **transportation barge or cargo vessel** for subsequent tow-out and offshore installation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> Loadout is defined as the engineered process of safely lifting, sliding, rolling, or floating a completed offshore structure from its fabrication site onto a marine transport vessel, while maintaining the structural integrity of the asset and the stability of the vessel throughout the operation.

## 1.2 Why is Loadout a Critical Engineering Challenge?

Loadout sits at the boundary between **land-based fabrication** and **marine transportation**. It is a "one-shot" operation with extremely low tolerance for error because:

- The structure may weigh anywhere from **2,000 tonnes** (small jackets) to over **60,000 tonnes** (mega topsides).
- The center of gravity shifts continuously during transfer.
- The vessel is partially supported by water (buoyancy) and partially resting on the quayside — a condition that demands **real-time stability monitoring**.
- Operations are governed by **tidal windows**, **weather limits**, and **barge strength criteria**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Moving House onto a Truck" Analogy

Imagine moving a fully built, two-storey prefabricated house from a workshop onto a flatbed truck for highway transport:

- The **house** = the offshore structure (topsides/jacket).
- The **workshop floor** = the fabrication yard.
- The **flatbed truck** = the transportation barge.
- The **rollers, cranes, or hydraulic jacks** = the loadout equipment.
- The **truck's suspension** must not break, and the **house must not topple** during the move.

Just as you would choose between rollers, a forklift, or a crane based on the house's weight and fragility, the offshore engineer selects a specific **loadout method** based on the structure's weight, geometry, yard layout, and available marine assets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Loadout is essentially a carefully choreographed "weight handoff" between two support systems — land and water — using specialized marine engineering techniques.

## 1.4 Classification of Loadout Methods (High-Level Overview)

| S.No | Loadout Method | Typical Weight Range | Best Suited For |
|------|----------------|----------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Skidding (Sliding)** | Up to 30,000 t | Jackets, modules on rails |
| 2 | **SPMT (Self-Propelled Modular Transporters)** | Up to 20,000 t | Topside modules, hulls |
| 3 | **Heavy Lift (Crane Lift)** | Up to 20,000 t | Smaller jackets, modules |
| 4 | **Float-On / Float-Off (Flo-Flo)** | Up to 60,000+ t | GBS, FPSO hulls, mega topsides |
| 5 | **Roll-On / Roll-Off (Ro-Ro)** | Up to 15,000 t | Modules with prepared quay |
| 6 | **Strand Jack / Gantry Lift** | Up to 30,000 t | Integrated decks, bridges |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Each Loadout Method

### 2.1.1 Skidding Method (Sliding on Rails)

**Concept:** The structure rests on **skid shoes** (hardened steel blocks or Teflon-coated pads) and is pulled along **skid beams / rails** using **hydraulic pull cylinders or winches**.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
- Skid beams are aligned between the quayside and the barge deck.
- The structure is jacked up slightly to allow skid shoes to be inserted.
- Pulling force is applied via hydraulic cylinders mounted on the quay.
- Friction force is overcome by horizontal thrust.
- The structure slides across the barge deck until positioned for sea-fastening.

**Why Skidding?**
- Most economical for **heavy, large-footprint structures**.
- Continuous, controlled motion (typically 1–3 m/min).
- No need for heavy-lift crane vessels.

**How It Works (Engineering Mechanics):**
The horizontal pulling force must overcome static and kinetic friction:

$$F_{pull} \geq \mu_{sk} \cdot W_{structure} + F_{inertia}$$

Where:
- $\mu_{sk}$ = skidding friction coefficient (**0.05 to 0.15** for greased PTFE-on-steel).
- $W_{structure}$ = vertical weight of the structure (kN).
- $F_{inertia}$ = inertial force during acceleration ($m \cdot a$).

---

### 2.1.2 SPMT Method (Self-Propelled Modular Transporters)

**Concept:** A grid of **computer-controlled, multi-axle hydraulic trailers** lifts the structure and drives it onto the barge.

**Why SPMT?**
- **Precision:** Each axle is individually controlled (height, steering, load share).
- **No rails required** — works on any paved surface.
- Ideal for **modular construction** — entire pre-assembled decks can be moved.
- Can carry distributed loads up to **40 tonnes/axle line**.

**How It Works:**
- SPMTs position themselves under the structure.
- Hydraulic suspension lifts the structure.
- The SPMT "convoy" drives slowly (0.1–1 km/h) onto the barge via a **temporary ramp**.
- Load is gradually transferred from SPMT to barge as SPMTs retract.

---

### 2.1.3 Float-On / Float-Off (Flo-Flo) Method

**Concept:** The transport vessel is **ballasted (submerged)** at the quayside. The structure is floated over the submerged deck using **winch lines**. The vessel is then **de-ballasted**, lifting the structure out of the water onto the deck.

**Why Flo-Flo?**
- **Only practical method for ultra-heavy structures** (>30,000 t).
- Eliminates frictional loads — water buoyancy carries the structure.
- Used for **GBS (Gravity-Based Structures), FPSO hulls, and integrated topsides**.

**Critical Engineering Parameters:**
1. **Ballast water quantity** — must be precisely calculated.
2. **Tidal alignment** — the structure must float at the right tidal elevation.
3. **Vessel stability** — GM must remain positive throughout.
4. **Mooring line tensions** — must be controlled to avoid collisions.

**Buoyancy Condition (Floating Position):**

$$F_{buoyancy} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{displaced} \geq W_{structure}$$

For a rectangular structure floating:

$$T_{draft} = \frac{W_{structure}}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot L \cdot B}$$

Where:
- $T_{draft}$ = required draft during float-over (m).
- $L, B$ = length and breadth of the structure base (m).
- $\rho_{sw}$ = density of seawater (**1,025 kg/m³**).

---

### 2.1.4 Heavy Lift Crane Method

**Concept:** A heavy-lift vessel or shore-based crane lifts the structure and places it on the barge.

**Limitations:**
- Crane capacity caps weight (~20,000 t for largest marine cranes).
- Requires careful **sling / spreader beam** design.
- Wind and sea-state restrictions are very tight.

---

### 2.1.5 Strand Jack / Gantry Lift

**Concept:** Hydraulic strand jacks anchored to a gantry frame lift the structure vertically, then transfer it laterally onto the barge using skid beams or bogies.

**Application:** Used where vertical clearance is available but no crane is large enough (e.g., dry-dock loadouts of offshore hulls).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Skidding pull force | $F_{pull} = \mu_{sk} \cdot W + m \cdot a$ | kN | Skid loadout design |
| 2 | Skid friction coeff. | $\mu_{sk} \approx 0.05 - 0.15$ | — | PTFE-steel skid |
| 3 | Float-on required draft | $T = \dfrac{W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot L \cdot B}$ | m | Flo-Flo method |
| 4 | Barge ballast weight | $W_b = W_s + W_{cr} - \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{sub}$ | kN | Ballast plan |
| 5 | Buoyancy force | $F_b = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V$ | kN | All marine ops |
| 6 | Metacentric height | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | m | Stability check |
| 7 | $BM$ (for rectangular base) | $BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{disp}} = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12 \cdot V_{disp}}$ | m | Stability |
| 8 | SPMT axle load | $q_{axle} = \dfrac{W_{struct}}{n_{axles} \cdot n_{mod}}$ | t/axle | SPMT selection |
| 9 | Tidal window | $t_w = \dfrac{H_{req} - H_{c}}{2 \cdot R_t}$ | hours | Tide scheduling |
| 10 | Wind overturning moment | $M_w = F_w \cdot h_{cg}$ | kN·m | Crane/Skid safety |
| 11 | Hydrodynamic added mass | $m_a = C_a \cdot \rho_w \cdot V$ | kg | Flo-Flo dynamics |
| 12 | Mooring line pretension | $T_0 = k \cdot \Delta x$ | kN | Mooring design |
| 13 | Crane lift clearance | $h_{req} = h_{struct} + h_{sling} + h_{safety}$ | m | Crane feasibility |
| 14 | Loadout rate (skid) | $v_{sk} = 1 - 3 \;\text{m/min}$ | m/min | Operational planning |
| 15 | Sea-fastening force | $F_{sf} = m \cdot a_{max} \cdot SF$ | kN | Tie-down design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Loadout calculations directly feed into the **transportation engineering phase** of any offshore project. Errors in loadout design have historically caused major incidents, including the 1988 *Ocean Odyssey* loss where incorrect ballast calculations led to the barge sinking. Every loadout must be supported by a **rigorous engineering procedure** reviewed by a **Marine Warranty Surveyor (MWS)**.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Pulling Force for Skidding Loadout

**Problem Statement:**  
A jacket weighing **W = 18,000 tonnes** is to be skidded onto a barge. The skid coefficient is **μ = 0.10**. If the structure must be accelerated from rest to a velocity of 0.5 m/min over 30 seconds, calculate the peak pulling force required.

**Given:**
- $W = 18{,}000 \text{ t} = 18{,}000 \times 1000 = 1.8 \times 10^7 \text{ kg}$
- $\mu_{sk} = 0.10$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Final velocity $v_f = 0.5 \text{ m/min} = \dfrac{0.5}{60} = 8.33 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$
- Acceleration time $t_a = 30 \text{ s}$

**Step 1 — Calculate the weight in Newtons:**

$$W = m \cdot g = (1.8 \times 10^7) \times 9.81$$

$$W = 1.7658 \times 10^8 \text{ N} = 176{,}580 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the acceleration:**

$$a = \frac{v_f - v_i}{t_a} = \frac{8.33 \times 10^{-3} - 0}{30}$$

$$a = 2.778 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the inertial force:**

$$F_{inertia} = m \cdot a = (1.8 \times 10^7) \times (2.778 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$F_{inertia} = 5{,}000 \text{ N} = 5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the friction force:**

$$F_{friction} = \mu_{sk} \cdot W = 0.10 \times 176{,}580$$

$$F_{friction} = 17{,}658 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Total peak pulling force:**

$$F_{pull} = F_{friction} + F_{inertia} = 17{,}658 + 5$$

$$\boxed{F_{pull} = 17{,}663 \text{ kN} \approx 17.66 \text{ MN}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The inertial force is **negligible (<0.03%)** compared to friction. Therefore, for static or quasi-static skidding, $F_{pull} \approx \mu_{sk} \cdot W$. This simplification is widely used in engineering practice.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Float-On Ballast Calculation

**Problem Statement:**  
A rectangular topside of dimensions **L = 60 m, B = 40 m, H = 25 m** and weight **W = 32,000 tonnes** must be floated onto a submerged barge during a loadout. The barge deck area is **L_b = 70 m, B_b = 50 m**. Determine:
1. The draft of the topside during float-over.
2. The submerged depth of the barge deck at float-over.

**Given:**
- $W = 32{,}000 \text{ t}$
- $L = 60 \text{ m}, \; B = 40 \text{ m}$
- $\rho_{sw} = 1{,}025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Convert weight to Newtons:**

$$W = 32{,}000 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 3.1392 \times 10^8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Archimedes' Principle (Buoyancy = Weight):**

$$\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T_{struct} = W$$

**Step 3 — Solve for structural draft:**

$$T_{struct} = \frac{W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot L \cdot B}$$

$$T_{struct} = \frac{3.1392 \times 10^8}{1025 \times 9.81 \times 60 \times 40}$$

$$T_{struct} = \frac{3.1392 \times 10^8}{2.4133 \times 10^7}$$

$$\boxed{T_{struct} = 13.01 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 4 — Barge submerged depth (must provide clearance below keel):**

The barge deck must be submerged by at least **T_struct + safety clearance (0.5–1.0 m)**.

$$T_{barge} = T_{struct} + h_{clearance} = 13.01 + 1.0$$

$$\boxed{T_{barge} = 14.01 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5 — Volume of barge submerged:**

$$V_{sub} = L_b \cdot B_b \cdot T_{barge} = 70 \times 50 \times 14.01$$

$$V_{sub} = 49{,}035 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 6 — Mass of seawater displaced by the barge alone (buoyancy on barge):**

$$m_{disp,b} = \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{sub} = 1025 \times 49{,}035 = 5.0261 \times 10^7 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 7 — Buoyant force on the barge:**

$$F_b = m_{disp,b} \cdot g = 5.0261 \times 10^7 \times 9.81 = 4.931 \times 10^8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 8 — Net ballast required to keep barge submerged:**

$$W_{ballast} = F_b - W_{barge,empty} - W_{struct,buoyed}$$

For a standard barge with lightship weight $W_L \approx 8{,}000 \text{ t} = 7.848 \times 10^7 \text{ N}$:

$$W_{ballast} = 4.931 \times 10^8 - 7.848 \times 10^7 - 3.1392 \times 10^8$$

$$W_{ballast} = 9.07 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx 924 \text{ tonnes}$$

$$\boxed{W_{ballast} \approx 924 \text{ tonnes of seawater}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Barge Stability Check (Metacentric Height)

**Problem Statement:**  
Verify the stability of the barge from Derivation 2 after float-on, but before de-ballasting. Barge waterplane dimensions: **L_b = 70 m, B_b = 50 m**. Lightship KG = **6.0 m**. Lightship displacement volume **V_d = 45,000 m³**.

**Step 1 — Calculate KB (center of buoyancy above keel):**

For a rectangular barge at draft $T = 14.01$ m:

$$KB = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{14.01}{2} = 7.005 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate BM (metacentric radius):**

$$BM = \frac{I_T}{V_{disp}}$$

For a rectangular waterplane area:

$$I_T = \frac{L_b \cdot B_b^3}{12} = \frac{70 \cdot 50^3}{12} = \frac{70 \cdot 125{,}000}{12}$$

$$I_T = 7.2917 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^4$$

$$BM = \frac{7.2917 \times 10^5}{45{,}000} = 16.20 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate KG:**

Assume topside center of gravity is at **h_cg = 22 m above barge deck**. Barge deck is 1.5 m above keel. So topside CG above keel = 1.5 + 22 = **23.5 m**.

Weights:
- Barge lightship: 8,000 t at KG_b = 6.0 m
- Topside (still water-borne): 32,000 t at KG_s = 23.5 m (effective)

$$KG = \frac{W_b \cdot KG_b + W_s \cdot KG_s}{W_b + W_s}$$

$$KG = \frac{(8000 \times 6.0) + (32000 \times 23.5)}{8000 + 32000}$$

$$KG = \frac{48{,}000 + 752{,}000}{40{,}000} = \frac{800{,}000}{40{,}000}$$

$$KG = 20.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate GM:**

$$GM = KB + BM - KG = 7.005 + 16.20 - 20.0$$

$$\boxed{GM = 3.205 \text{ m} > 0 \;\; \Rightarrow \text{Barge is STABLE}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** A positive GM > **0.3 m** is mandatory during all loadout phases. Typical offshore practice requires GM > **1.0 m** for safe operations.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Tidal Window Calculation

**Problem Statement:**  
A float-on loadout requires the structure to be at a draft of **T_struct = 13.01 m**. The local water depth at high tide is **20 m** and at low tide is **16 m**. The tidal rate is **1.2 m/hr**. Determine the working tidal window.

**Step 1 — Available water depth above structure keel:**

Required clearance below structure = $h_{clear} = 0.5 \text{ m}$.

$$H_{req,keel} = T_{struct} + h_{clear} = 13.01 + 0.5 = 13.51 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Tidal range:**

$$\Delta H = 20 - 16 = 4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Time for water to rise from low tide to required depth:**

Water depth required = $H_{req} = 13.51 + (Barge\ keel\ elevation\ above\ seabed)$...  
For simplicity, assume barge keel clearance needs total local depth = **17 m**.

$$t_1 = \frac{17 - 16}{1.2} = 0.833 \text{ hr (from low tide to safe depth)}$$

$$t_2 = \frac{20 - 17}{1.2} = 2.5 \text{ hr (from safe depth to high tide)}$$

**Step 4 — Working window:**

$$\boxed{\Delta t_{window} = 2.5 - 0.833 = 1.667 \text{ hr} \approx 1 \text{ hr } 40 \text{ min}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Float-on operations are extremely tide-dependent. The entire operation must complete within this narrow window — hence the need for meticulous scheduling.
