## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define "Wet Tow" in the context of offshore structure installation. List the two main stability criteria that must be checked during wet tow of a jacket or GBS.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Wet Tow** is the marine operation of transporting a buoyant offshore structure (jacket, GBS, semi-submersible hull, SPAR, or FPSO) from the fabrication yard to the offshore site, with the structure floating in the sea rather than being carried on a vessel.

The two main stability criteria checked during wet tow are:
1. **Intact Stability (GM > 0):** The metacentric height must be positive, with sufficient righting moment against heeling moments from wind, waves, and offset loads. Typical minimum requirement: **GM ≥ 0.5 m**, with righting arm GZ ≥ 0.2 m at 20° heel.
2. **Reserve Buoyancy:** The buoyancy margin must be adequate to handle any compartment flooding, free surface effects, and dynamic loads. Typically ≥ 30% reserve buoyancy is required for ocean tows.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between "Upending" and "Launching" as used in jacket installation. What marine equipment is typically used for each?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Launching** is the operation of sliding the horizontally-fabricated jacket off the launch barge into the sea. The jacket enters the water in the same horizontal orientation in which it was fabricated. Equipment used: **Launch barge** (e.g., with rocker arms or skid beams), and **winches** to control slide-off rate.

- **Upending** is the subsequent operation of rotating the floating jacket from its horizontal (just-launched) orientation to a vertical orientation, ready for pile driving. Equipment used: **Buoyancy tanks** (pre-installed on the jacket), **ballast pumps** for controlled deballasting, **upending frame/crane assist** for the critical-angle phase, and **derrick barge** for the final vertical hold.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Describe in detail, with a labeled flow diagram, the **complete installation sequence of a pile-supported steel jacket platform** in 100 m water depth. Include the marine spread, equipment, weather windows, and key engineering checks at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A jacket is being upended. Buoyancy tanks of total volume **V = 3,800 m³** provide uplift. The jacket's CG is **22 m** from the pivot, and the CB of the tanks is **28 m** from the pivot. Jacket weight in air is **7,500 tonnes**. Seawater density is **1.025 t/m³**. Calculate:
   (i) The buoyant force provided by the tanks.
   (ii) The net moment about the pivot when the jacket is at **45°** from vertical (assume the moment arms scale as cos θ).
   (iii) Comment on whether the jacket will self-upend from this position. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Jacket Installation Sequence (7 marks):**

| Step | Operation | Equipment | Weather Window (Hs) | Engineering Check |
|------|-----------|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Fabrication** in yard (horizontal) | Welding yards, gantry cranes | N/A (onshore) | Weld QC, dimensional, weight control |
| 2 | **Load-out** onto launch barge | SPMTs or strand jacks | < 1.0 m | Skid track alignment, COG, sea-fastenings |
| 3 | **Wet tow** to site | Tugboats (2–3) | < 2.0 m | GM > 0, tow speed, route planning |
| 4 | **Positioning** at site | Anchor handling | < 1.5 m | Site survey, anchor pattern |
| 5 | **Launching** (slide-off) | Launch barge deballasting | < 1.5 m | Slide-off rate, jacket trim |
| 6 | **Upending** | Buoyancy tanks + crane assist | < 1.5 m | Buoyancy check, upending moment, crane capacity |
| 7 | **Pile driving** | Hydraulic hammer on pile barge | < 1.8 m | Hammer energy, soil capacity, blow count |
| 8 | **Grouting** piles | Grout pumps, ROV | < 2.0 m | Grout strength, void filling |
| 9 | **Topside installation** | Heavy-lift crane vessel (HLV) | < 2.0 m | Lift weight vs SWL, DAF, sea states |
| 10 | **Hook-up & commissioning** | CSV, ROV | < 2.5 m | Riser pull-in, electrical, process |

(1 mark for each major step explained; 1 mark for diagram/flow; 1 mark for engineering checks summary)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** V = 3,800 m³, d_CG = 22 m, d_CB = 28 m, W = 7,500 t, ρ_w = 1.025 t/m³, θ = 45°

**(i) Buoyant Force:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_b &= \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V \\
F_b &= 1{,}025 \ \text{kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2 \times 3{,}800 \ \text{m}^3 \\
F_b &= 38{,}217{,}450 \ \text{N} \approx 38{,}217 \ \text{kN} \\
F_b \text{ in tonnes-force} &\approx 3{,}895 \ \text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Marks: 2 — 1 for formula, 1 for correct evaluation**

**(ii) Net Moment at 45° from Vertical:**

The jacket is at 45° from vertical, meaning the moment arms are reduced by cos(45°) = 0.7071 (since the horizontal projection is the original arm × sin θ from horizontal, or × cos θ from vertical — depending on convention). Using the convention that the effective moment arm reduces by cos θ from the maximum (vertical-aligned):

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{CG,\ eff} &= 22 \times \cos 45° = 22 \times 0.7071 = 15.56 \ \text{m} \\
d_{CB,\ eff} &= 28 \times \cos 45° = 28 \times 0.7071 = 19.80 \ \text{m} \\
W_{kN} &= 7{,}500 \times 9.81 = 73{,}575 \ \text{kN} \\
M_{gravity} &= W \times d_{CG,\ eff} = 73{,}575 \times 15.56 = 1{,}144{,}827 \ \text{kN·m} \\
M_{buoyancy} &= F_b \times d_{CB,\ eff} = 38{,}217 \times 19.80 = 756{,}696 \ \text{kN·m} \\
M_{net} &= M_{buoyancy} - M_{gravity} \\
M_{net} &= 756{,}696 - 1{,}144{,}827 = -388{,}131 \ \text{kN·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Marks: 3 — 1 for cos reduction, 1 for moment calculation, 1 for net result**

**(iii) Comment (2 marks):**

The **net moment is negative** (-388,131 kN·m), which means the **gravity moment dominates** the buoyancy moment at 45° from vertical. The jacket will **NOT self-upend** from this position — it will tend to fall back toward horizontal. Upending assistance is required — typically from a **derrick barge crane** providing a downward pull on the top of the jacket, or from **selective deballasting** of the buoyancy tanks to increase their effective uplift. The critical angle where the jacket becomes self-upending typically occurs between **15° and 30° from vertical** for most designs.

**Marks: 2 — 1 for negative moment interpretation, 1 for engineering mitigation**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative]**

**(a)** Compare the **installation methods of a Tension Leg Platform (TLP)** and a **SPAR platform** in deep water (≥ 1,500 m). Discuss the marine spread, sequence, and the unique engineering challenges of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A TLP has displacement **Δ = 95,000 tonnes**, supported by **n = 6 tendons**. The pre-tension is **10% of displacement**. During a severe storm, the maximum heave force on the hull is **F_heave = 18,000 kN** (downward). Calculate:
   (i) The static pre-tension per tendon.
   (ii) The maximum and minimum top tension per tendon during the storm.
   (iii) The factor of safety on tendon capacity if the ultimate tendon strength is **40,000 kN** per tendon. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) TLP vs SPAR Installation Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | TLP Installation | SPAR Installation |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Hull Orientation at Tow** | Vertical or horizontal (depending on size) | Horizontal (upside-down) |
| **Marine Spread** | Tugs + tendon-lay vessel + HLV + ROVs | Ocean tugs + HLV + mooring lay vessel |
| **Critical Operation** | Tendon hook-up & pull-down | Upending (rotation) |
| **Anchoring System** | Vertical tendons to suction/driven piles | Spread mooring (taut or semi-taut chains) |
| **Topside Installation** | Heavy-lift (after tendons locked) | Float-over (after upending & ballasting) |
| **Weather Sensitivity** | Moderate (tendons can be installed in moderate seas) | High (upending requires calm seas, Hs < 1.5 m) |
| **Time on Site** | 4–8 months | 6–10 months |
| **Key Engineering Challenge** | Tendon fatigue from dynamic loads; tendon-tower alignment | Maintaining stability during upending; controlling ballast rates |

(1 mark per significant comparison point × 5 key points; 2 marks for engineering challenges discussion)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** Δ = 95,000 t, n = 6, pre-tension = 10% of Δ, F_heave = 18,000 kN, UTS_tendon = 40,000 kN

**(i) Static Pre-Tension Per Tendon:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{pre,\ total} &= 0.10 \times \Delta \times g = 0.10 \times 95{,}000 \times 9.81 = 93{,}195 \ \text{kN} \\
T_{pre,\ per\ tendon} &= \frac{93{,}195}{6} = 15{,}532.5 \ \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Marks: 2**

**(ii) Maximum and Minimum Top Tension:**

When heave force is **downward**, the tendons carry more load (the hull pulls up while the wave pushes down — but in a TLP, heave-down increases tendon tension).

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{max,\ per\ tendon} &= T_{pre,\ per\ tendon} + \frac{F_{heave}}{n} \\
T_{max,\ per\ tendon} &= 15{,}532.5 + \frac{18{,}000}{6} = 15{,}532.5 + 3{,}000 = 18{,}532.5 \ \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

For minimum (heave upward — the hull lifts, relieving tendons):

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{min,\ per\ tendon} &= T_{pre,\ per\ tendon} - \frac{F_{heave}}{n} = 15{,}532.5 - 3{,}000 = 12{,}532.5 \ \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Students often assume heave force is only downward. In a TLP, **heave-UP (set-down relief)** can reduce tendon tension toward zero, which is a **stability concern** — if tendons go slack, the platform can lose its "fixed" behavior. Design must ensure **T_min > 0** at all times. Here T_min = 12,532.5 kN > 0, so it's safe.

**Marks: 3 — 1 for max formula, 1 for min formula, 1 for correct evaluation**

**(iii) Factor of Safety:**

$$\begin{aligned}
FoS &= \frac{UTS_{tendon}}{T_{max,\ per\ tendon}} = \frac{40{,}000}{18{,}532.5} = 2.16
\end{aligned}$$

**Marks: 2 — 1 for formula, 1 for evaluation**

**Comment (1 mark):** A FoS of 2.16 is acceptable for tendon design (typical required FoS for ultimate strength is 2.0–2.5). However, **fatigue** rather than ultimate strength usually governs tendon design life in TLP applications.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Installation Methods Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Confusing "wet tow" and "launch":** Wet tow = marine transportation; launch = sliding off the barge. Don't mix these up.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of buoyancy tanks:** A jacket will not self-upend without buoyancy tanks. The tanks do NOT provide net positive reserve buoyancy; they provide **moment imbalance** for rotation. Students who say "buoyancy tanks float the jacket at vertical" are wrong — they only help in the upending phase.
> 3. **Ignoring weather windows:** Always state the **Hs limit** for each operation. KTU examiners award marks for awareness of operational sea-state limits.
> 4. **Forgetting the marine spread:** A "complete" answer must name the **tugboats, crane vessel, pile-driving barge, ROV, AHV**, etc. — not just the operations.
> 5. **Mixing up TLP and SPAR mechanics:** TLP = tendons (vertical, pre-tensioned). SPAR = spread mooring (chain/wire, catenary). They look similar in pictures but are installed VERY differently.
> 6. **Using sign convention errors in moments:** Negative net moment ≠ failure; it means **assistance required**. State this clearly.
> 7. **Skipping the formula box:** In 14-mark problems, ALWAYS show the formula, substitute, and evaluate. Even if the answer is wrong, you get method marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Installation Methods for Offshore Structures**

- [ ] **Master the 4 universal installation phases:** Fabrication & Load-out → Transportation → Installation/Lowering → Hook-up/Commissioning.

- [ ] **Jacket Platforms:**
  - Method: launch → upend → drive piles → install topside.
  - Key check: reserve buoyancy (often negative at vertical — held by derrick barge).
  - Pile hammers: 200–6,000 kJ energy.

- [ ] **Gravity-Based Structures (GBS):**
  - Method: dry-dock build → flood & float → wet-tow (submerged) → site positioning → controlled ballasting.
  - No pile driving; relies on self-weight + skirts.

- [ ] **Semi-Submersibles:**
  - Method: fabricate → wet-tow → pre-lay mooring → hook-up → ballast to operating draft.
  - Operating draft typically 20–30 m (low waterplane area = high stability).

- [ ] **FPSOs:**
  - Method: convert/build → long-distance tow → pre-lay mooring → hook to turret/SPM.
  - Weathervaning capability — single-point mooring allows 360° rotation.

- [ ] **TLPs:**
  - Method: tow hull → lay tendons on seabed → position → pull-down & lock tendons.
  - Pre-tension = 5–10% of displacement per tendon basis (total).
  - T_max and T_min must be checked — T_min > 0 mandatory for stability.

- [ ] **SPAR Platforms:**
  - Method: build horizontally → tow upside-down → upend by ballasting → moor → float-over topside.
  - Ballasted to operating draft: only top 5–10% of length above water.
  - KG very low → inherently stable in heave.

- [ ] **Subsea Installations:**
  - Use CSV with AHC (active heave compensation) cranes.
  - ROVs for fine positioning and intervention.
  - Heave compensation travel ≥ 2 × Hs.

- [ ] **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $F_b = \rho_w g V$
  - $GM = KB + BM - KG$
  - $DAF = 1 + \sqrt{1 + 2H/\delta_{st}}$
  - $T_{pre,\ tendon} = 0.05\text{–}0.10 \times \Delta g / n$ (TLP)
  - $D = W_{total} / (\rho_w g A_{cyl})$ (SPAR)

- [ ] **Vessel Types to Know:** Launch barge, HLCV (Heavy-Lift Crane Vessel), Derrick barge, AHV (Anchor Handling Vessel), CSV (Construction Support Vessel), Pipe-lay vessel, ROV vessel, Tugs.

- [ ] **Weather Windows:** Always state Hs limits — typically 1.0–1.5 m for jacket launch, 1.5–1.8 m for pile driving, 2.0–2.5 m for topside lift.

- [ ] **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** In a 14-mark question, **always include a labeled flow diagram** — examiners give 1–2 marks just for a clear diagram. The remaining 12 marks come from descriptive content + calculations.
